Original_Search_Title: Akume Advises Christian Pilgrims
Headline: Nigeria: Akume Advises Christian Pilgrims
Body_Text:
MAKURDI — BENUE State Governor, Mr George Akume, has called on Christians
performing this year pilgrimage to Israel to pray for the unity, peace and
progress of Nigeria.
Akume whose address to the pilgrims was read by the Secretary to the
State Government, Dr John Orokpo, explained that Nigeria needed prayers for
her to succeed in the various economic , social and political programmes
being pursued for the improvement of the lives of the people.
He stressed the need for the pilgrims to pay special attention to the
spiritual benefits of their journey and advised them not to do anything that
would defile the places that Christ made holy while on earth.
Governor Akume also enjoined the pilgrims to be good ambassadors of the
state and work together while in the Holy Land.
He told the pilgrims to cooperate with the Chairman of the State
Christian Pilgrims Welfare Board, Rev Fr Chris Utov, and congratulated them
for scaling through the screening.
Speaking to Vanguard during a workshop organised for the pilgrims, Rev
Fr Chris Utov, expressed appreciation to Governor Akume for sponsoring
hundreds of pilgrims to Jerusalem every year.
Utov said about 186 pilgrims had been prepared to travel to the Holy
Land on Sunday and added that the entire pilgrims from Benue State would
travel to Israel in about three or four batches.In another development
Governor George Akume is to present the 2004 budget to the State House of
Assembly tomorrow, according to the Commissioner for Information, Mr John
Uzer.
Uzer told newsmen in Makurdi that the various ministries and departments
had been meeting to fine-tune the budget and noted that the House of
Assembly has been duly notified of the presentation on Tuesday.
He hinted that the budget would focus mainly on rural infrastructural
development and completion of on-going projects. According to him, the
budget would also contain various programmes planned for the reduction of
unemployment in the state especially amongst the youths.
It was further gathered that the new projects likely to be reflected in
next year's budget are the Mbatiav and Igumale Cement Companies, the three
Cassava Processing Industries and the three Fruit Juice Factories.

Original_Search_Title: NPP in Turmoil in Kroboland
Headline: Ghana: NPP in Turmoil in Kroboland
Body_Text:
Somanya/Odumase — The two Krobo districts, Yilo and Manya, are becoming test grounds for
the New Patriotic Party (NPP) to assess its strength, as the party prepares
to go into the 2004 Presidential and Parliamentary elections.
The Yilo Krobo constituency chairman of the party, Kingsley Ampomah,
caused a stir when he signed a letter to expel 12 senior members of the
party, including the district assembly's presiding member, worsening the
boiling situation in the Manya constituency where there is an in-fighting
between the district chief executive (DCE), Andrews Teye, and the party's
failed parliamentary candidate in 2000, Demah Narh.
At Yilo, the rift in the party is about a section of the leadership
blaming the DCE, Sub/ Lt. Christian Tettey, for being too ambitious.
The protracted chieftaincy dispute between the Odumase and Dorm clans
over the paramount stool has assumed political dimensions, as Teye and Demah
find themselves in the opposing factions. Teye comes from Dorm, while Demah
hails from Odumase.
The Yilo issue has degenerated into alleged assassination plots in which
a group has accused Sub/ Lt.Tettey of involvement.
In 1996 and 2000, Tettey stood in the parliamentary elections for the
Yilo constituency but lost to the National Democratic Congress (NDC)
candidate, Daniel Terkpertey.
However, when the NPP came to power Tettey and Mathew Bawa, a tutor at
Yilo Krobo Secondary School, vied for the position of district chief
executive.
Bawa lost to Tettey and since then there has been no peace between the
two.
When the rift deepened, the constituency chairman, Nicholas Addae, and
constituency organiser, Alex Narh Adjirako, fell out with the DCE and some
patrons of the party in the constituency.
But the chairman and the youth organizer, realizing that their
popularity had waned, reportedly frustrated efforts to hold a constituency
congress to elect new executives.
It was alleged that the chairman concealed documents from the regional
secretariat, ordering the holding of the congress.
Later, nine elected members of the party held a meeting and selected
November 3, 2002, for the congress but it was reported that a day to the
congress, the two executive members made an abortive attempt to hold a press
conference at Nkurakan.
Some patrons in the constituency managed to organise a congress at Boti
Falls and the chairman and the organiser lost their positions, with Kingsley
Ampomah becoming the new constituency chairman.
According to the report, the old executive forged an alliance with Bawa
to ensure that the district became ungovernable for Sub/ Lt. Tettey.
Around the same period, the DCE was reeling under a section of the
assembly members who accused him of autocratic rule.
The former naval officer was said to have awarded a contract for the
renovation of the Old Somanya market to the Manya constituency chairman,
Mr. Padi Asime, without going through laid-down tender processes.
Again he was blamed for acquiring a tract of land at the foot of the
Krobo mountain for the cultivation of mango for export and cassava under the
President's Special Initiative (PSI), both without the consent of the
assembly.
Quite recently, the NPP held its primaries for parliamentary candidates
in which the DCE contested the Yilo Krobo assembly's presiding member, Moses
Tetteh, who lost.
Incidentally, Tetteh hails from Adjikpo, a suburb of Somanya, like the
NDC Member of Parliament (MP), Daniel Terkpertey.
Prior to the primaries at Somanya, Matthews Bawa, speaking to this
reporter, complained that the DCE was hosting the delegates to a meal at his
official residence that morning.
He said the information was confirmed when a section of the delegates
rushed to the residence and he cautioned the DCE against using his
incumbency to the disadvantage of the other opponent.
This resulted in a heated argument and Bawa alleged that the former
naval officer pulled a pistol but the DCE's wife and the NPP regional
organizer restrained him.
As he left for his house he heard that Addai was stabbed on the head
with a bottle at the DCE's residence and he was treated and discharged at
the Atua Government Hospital.
People from the Zongo community also joined in the riot that threatened
security of the area.
One of the attacking groups virtually took over the Trom junction to
Koforidua and Accra at the outskirts of Somanya, which became a no-go area
for passers-by who were terrorized and robbed.
The District Security Council (DISEC) saw the need to beef up security
by deploying soldiers to flush out the criminal elements from their
hideouts.
After the swoop, a number of the gangsters fled but some were
arrested.
Recently, a group including one Addo had gone to the media to make a
statement that the Yilo Krobo DCE and some party executives in the
constituency had engaged them to assassinate some party members including
Addae and Adjirako.
The tape on which one of them, Lala, made a statement on the abortive
assassination available to The Chronicle indicated that they did not expect
the DCE to order soldiers and police to arrest them.
According to the tape, some of them were sleeping in coffins on sale in
a bid to escape arrest.
The statement alleged that the group could not succeed on three
occasions to eliminate their victims because the victims did not turn up on
scheduled dates.
One of those who fell out with the DCE disclosed to The Chronicle that
they felt the current constituency chairman, Kingsley Ampomah was pushed to
become the chairman and that would not augur well for the party.
He said the DCE currently holds a regional executive position and wanted
to follow it with a parliamentary position, adding that these are the only
problems that they have with the DCE and his current executive.
The Akropong Divisional Police Command when reached stated that it had a
number of suspects on its wanted list, including one Addo, whose picture
appeared in a newspaper with others claiming that they were contracted to
assassinate some party gurus.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Seizes N3.02 Billion Smuggled Goods
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Seizes N3.02 Billion Smuggled Goods
Body_Text:
The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS), has within this year impounded
prohibited goods and second-hand vehicles valued at over N3.02 billion.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports that the seized vehicles,
popularly known as "Tokunbo", are those that are more than eight years old
from the date of manufacture.
A summary of the seizures made available to NAN in Abuja, at the
weekend, showed that the items seized included textile materials, frozen
chicken and turkey and used vehicles.
Others are rice, fruit juice in packs and cigarettes, petroleum
products, vegetable oil, pharmaceutical products, hard drugs, second-hand
clothes, as well as arms and ammunition.
The customs service stated that the total number of seizures nationwide
was more than 2,000.
An official of the service attributed the "remarkable haul" to the
strategies adopted by the service to ensure strict compliance with
government directives.
"We will not relent in our efforts to crackdown on smuggling activities
and to ensure that we also tackle the illegal importation of arms and
ammunition," the customs officer, who pleaded anonymity, said.
It will be recalled that the federal government in August, last year
banned the importation of sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable
oil in bulk, mosquito repellant coils, retreaded or used tyres, gaming
machines and second-hand clothes.
Others are used refrigerators and air-conditioning units, used
compressors, bagged cement, vehicles of any kind, through the land borders
and vehicles that are above eight years from the date of manufacture.
Also outlawed were frozen poultry, textiles of any kind through the land
borders, printed fabric (Ankara) cassava and cassava products, containerised
goods through the land borders as well as fruit juice in retail packs.
Government also banned the importation of table water
(sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any
type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders.
On the list of prohibited items are sugar, all kinds of canned and
bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and envelopes,
as well as Barytes and Bentonites.
The government said that it decided to ban the importation of the items
to boost local production, enhance the performance of the national economy
and safeguard the health of its citizenry.

Original_Search_Title: Harnessing Potentials of Nigeria's Air, Sea Ports
Headline: Nigeria: Harnessing Potentials of Nigeria's Air, Sea Ports
Body_Text:
Benin City — Nigeria is said to be loosing huge money in terms of excise and custom
duties due to im-proper enforcement of laws in the country even as the Edo
State Command of the Nigeria Customs Service, said it has generated the sum
of N1.7 billion betweenJanuary and October this year.
Speaking exclusively with the Weekly Trust at the Benin Airport,
Chairman of the Nigeria Economic and Financial Partners, a Non-Governmental
Organization (NGO) responsible for wooing foreign investors to the Niger
Delta, Dr. Patricia Opori said if the Nigerian borders, seaports and
airports are properly manned by the customs, the present revenue generated
by them will increase by not less than 150 per cent.
"Let me give you an example with Benin City and Port-Harcourt. When you
go to any of these cities, you realize that about 90 per cent of the fridges
you find in the markets are the imported second hand fridges that are
classified as contraband. Go to the offices of the big men who uses native
dresses, you discover that they are using the imported guinea brocade which
are also classified as contraband."
"The case of second hand vehicles is the worst. About 70 per cent of the
imported tokunbo (second hand), cars you see in the warehouses were
manufactured more than ten years ago. And I tell you one thing, all the
custom papers they give you as well as the bank tellers for the payment of
customs duty into the bank, the evidence of shipment are all fake. They have
all the required custom papers, stamps and everything in their
warehouses. When you buy a car, they go in, prepare those fake documents and
give you".
Dr. Opori said her organization consisted mainly of Nigerians with
business connections abroad who have investigated the issue and confirmed
the dubious nature of the Nigerian Tokunbo motor dealers, adding that, "of
the five vehicles bought by our members from different dealers in Benin City
and two from Port-Harcourt, none has genuine documents. Even the bank
tellers were forged."
She said if the customs can effectively man the entry points and
possibly raid places believed to be housing contraband goods, confiscate
them and punish both the smugglers, the officers who aided them to smuggle
the goods, the buyers and harbourers of the goods, customs generated revenue
will appreciate more and Nigerian local industries will be encouraged to
increase production.
She said when all means to import vehicles of more than ten years old
into the country are blocked; the importers will be left with no option but
to import those within the specifications that can be brought in through the
authorised routes thereby generating more income.
In his own contribution, a major transporter in the state and an
official of the Transport Owners Association, Deacon Bright Osasume, said in
the past, when customs officers were manning roadblocks at strategic
locations in the state, illegally acquired guns and other contraband goods
were not in mass circulation in the state.
"No transporter then will carry your goods without knowing the contents
and what the law is saying about those goods. Since the customs stopped
mounting roadblocks in Edo State, every person is now free to move anything
from Lagos to Onitsha, Warri, Port Harcourt or even Lagos to Abuja and other
parts of the East and South-South. Edo has now turned to a dumping ground of
all those things, you know you must pass through Benin to all those places."
He blamed government for dismantling the customs checkpoints in the
state which he said has given room for the massive movement of contraband
and other prohibited goods into the state which he said is responsible for
the high rate of crime in the area.
When Weekly Trust visited the major business areas in Benin City, only
Uyi Technical Company and two others were seen selling new refrigerators
with about five new compressors said to be in stock for several months
without patronage as against the numerous stores that sell second hand
fridges and compressors in the country.
A store attendant where second hand fridges, compressors, televisions,
computers and other electronics are being sold, Mrs. Franca Ezeala, told
this reporter that they sell an average of ten fridges a week and that they
sell to even local and state governments offices as well as corporate
bodies, companies and individuals.
However, at number 98C Mission Road, where new fridges are sold, Mr. Ken
Omono, the store attendant told this reporter that the last time they sold
new fridges was before the April general elections when they supplied two to
the state government. He attributed the poor sales to the massive stock of
tokunbo fridges and the poor economic state of Nigerians.
An economists in a private firm, ALSFAT Finances, Mr. Sunday Dimowo said
the major mistake government did in it's bid to curtail the entry and
circulation of contraband goods was for allowing government agencies and
officials to patronize the contraband goods themselves. "Tell me right from
the president to the governors, senators to even councillors, ministers,
commissioners and chairmen of local government, who is not using the
imported guinea brocade? Who among them is using the brocade manufactured in
Zaria or Kano? Look at their wives, who among them is using locally made
fabrics? Go to their dinning table, what you see there are imported
biscuits. The government is now buying tokunbo vehicles as official
cars. Then why should the poor man be punished for doing the same?"
Mr. Dimowo warned that the government should put it's house in order by
cleansing itself before attacking poor traders in the market whom he said,
use their life savings in buying the goods that the customs arrest and
destroy or auction.
The National President of Motor Dealers Association of Nigeria (MODAN),
Elder Benjamin Ughumu, said the issue of tokunbo vehicles should not be an
issue in this country at all, adding that "the customs have sealed up the
border against tokunbo cars and all the items they listed as contraband. You
cannot bring in a vehicle to this country now except through the seaport. If
there is anybody that brings in a vehicle illegally into Nigeria, that may
be the smugglers or customs officials. We are not smugglers."
He said it will amount to a violation of his members rights to free
trade, if the customs decided to raid their warehouses and impound any
vehicle that passed through customs into the country.
"There is no bush path or illegal route you can enter Nigeria with a
vehicle and not a motorcycle without meeting the customs. If they allow
smugglers to bring in vehicles illegally, they must not come and disturb
innocent dealers thereafter."
On the issue of motor dealers forging customs clearance papers, he said
his association does not support illegality. "I doubt if there is anything
like that happening. But if anybody is arrested for forgery and it is
established, I am sorry, MODAN may not have anything to do about that. The
person should clear himself." The Managing Director of a multi-national oil
palm producing company in Edo State, Presco Oil, and member of the
Presidential Committee on Oil Palm Development, Mr. Phil Vandebeeck, told
newsmen that the N50 billion the federal government intend to release was
for enhancement of local production of vegetable oil.
He pointed out that the oil palm producing companies in the country have
the capacity to produce enough oil for both local consumption and export. He
said Presco, which produces 50 tonnes of oil per day, is now increasing its
production to 70 tonnes per day.
He said there is no need for the importation of vegetable oil from any
country.
When contacted for comments, the Customs Area Comptroller of the state,
Dr. Hussaini Suleiman Kangiwa, said the issue of manning the country's
border by security operatives is very difficult due to the porous nature of
the border and the large size of the country.
"When people travel to border towns like Banki/Amchide in Borno State
where the two towns of Banki in Nigeria and Amchide in Cameroun have merged
together, they will understand what we are saying. There are hotels, houses
and shops built along the borderline. You fine such houses or hotels with
two doors, one in Nigeria and the other in Cameroun. When the Nigeria
authorities say no movement or business after 12 midnight, the hotels close
the gates leading to Nigeria and open the Cameroun gates and business
continues and vice-versa. How do you man such places when the people are one
and they eat from the same pot?"
He said the customs doesn't enact laws but they only enforce laws
relevant to their duties, adding, "every item listed as contraband, has a
reason why its importation is banned. The gas in those used compressors is
harmful to human health. Used Cars of over eight years are very weak and
old. At the slightest accident, you find that they are completely damaged
and that affects the passengers in the vehicle. With all the cooking oil we
produce in this country and the surplus bottled water and biscuits, what is
the need of importing other ones. We should not allow Nigeria to be turned
into a dumping ground for all sub-standard and over used products. We should
be seen to be exporting all these things".
He listed the contraband goods as rethreaded used tyres, cars of more
than eight years, gaming machines, cement in bags, vegetable oil in bulks,
used refrigerators, used air conditioners, used compressors, fabrics and
textile materials.
He mentioned other goods as poultry products, cassava and cassava
products, tooth pick in any form, table drinking water, biscuits, spaghetti,
fruit juice, paints, sugar confectionaries, exercise books, envelopes, beer
and toilet rolls.
He also warned against the exportation of maize, timber, raw hides and
skins, scrap metals, unprocessed rubber latex and humps, Artifacts and
antiquities as well as wild animals like crocodile, elephants, lizards,
eagle, monkey, zebra and lions are all prohibited.
He said most of the local industries stopped production because the
markets for their local products, which he described as goods for the
Nigerian environment, have been taken over by "unnecessary imported goods
that are mostly substandard and not good for both our economy and the
environment." He advised used motor dealers to ensure that they buy and sell
only vehicles that passed through the approved entry ports, with authentic
import and customs clearance documents and advised that the vehicles must be
within the approved specification of not more than eight years old for
cars.
"The law is there and there is just no compromise. It is left for the
dealers or smugglers or whatever they call themselves, to operate within the
ambit of the laws or have themselves to blame. Nobody will harass anyone
that does his or her business as specified by the law. When you do anything
different, it is your own business and you are doing that at your own risk
and at your own cost."
For those dealing in contraband goods, he adviced them to keep away from
such businesses and find other things doing before the law catches up with
them. "We can only advise, warn and guide before one is arrested. When you
are arrested, you must be punished according to the law and that is all."
"Look, smuggling activity alone or the illegal importation of contraband is
enough to ruin the economy of a country no matter how rich it may be. These
smugglers use the much needed hard currency to go and bring those
things. They bring them into the country illegally without the government
gaining a kobo from it. When they bring those goods, they deprive the local
manufacturers of similar goods from selling theirs. These local
manufacturers who pay tax to government, employ Nigerians to work for them
and help in developing the economy are now forced to close business." He
said that government's decision to fish out these illegally smuggled
contraband goods even from the hinterland is in the interest of both the
economy and security of the nation. "The effect of this illegal smuggling of
contraband into the country may be partially responsible for the poor
economic situation in the country since a lot of factories have been forced
to close down. Nigerians should expose them and their warehouses to the
authorities."

Original_Search_Title: Annual Inflation Drops to 19%
Headline: Zambia: Annual Inflation Drops to 19%
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE annual rate of inflation has fallen to 19.1 per cent for November
from the 21.1 per cent level recorded in October, the Central Statistical
Office (CSO) has announced.
Releasing the November Consumer Price Index (CPI) yesterday, CSO
director Dr. Buleti Nsemukila said the decline in the annual rate of
inflation was largely due to the decrease in the food index.
Dr. Nsemukila said the food index, which showed increases in September
and October, had recorded a decrease in November.
The monthly inflation rate was recorded at 2 per cent as at November
2003, representing an increase of 0.2 of a percentage point on the October
rate of 1.8 per cent.
Monthly inflation rates for the metropolitan low, high income and
non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 2.1, 2.2 and 1.8 per cent
respectively.
For the CPI, the price decreases observed for mealie meal and cereals in
the month of October have continued in the month of November.
The monthly food index was recorded at 1.9 per cent in November 2003,
0.5 of a percentage lower than 2.4 per cent recorded for October 2003.
Annual food inflation stood at 16.6 per cent as at November 2003,
decreasing by 4.4 percentage points on the October rate of 21 per cent.
The food index was influenced by the continued decline in the prices of
mealie meal, maize grain and other cereals as well as some vegetables.
Price increases were observed in meat, fish, kapenta, oils and fats,
milk and milk products and other processed food commodities.
However, these price increases were not high enough to influence the
rate of inflation in the positive direction.
There were price decreases in the cost of mealie meal, maize, rice,
flour, bread, samp, millet and sorghum, cassava meal, some vegetables and
fruits.
The non-food index recorded a monthly increase of 2.2 per cent as at
November, a percentage point higher than the rate observed in October.
The annual non-food index increased by 22.1 per cent in November, 0.8 of
a percentage point higher than the October increase.
Moderate price increases were observed in some non-food sectors.
Some non-food sectors that contributed to the slight increase in the
non-food index are increases in furniture, medical care, telecommunications
and personal care.
Price decreases were observed in the cost of air transport, vehicles and
hotel accommodation.
The drop in the cost of these services is attributed to the current
lower foreign exchange rate prevailing on the foreign exchange market
compared to that recorded in October.
Meanwhile, the average price of a 25 kg bag of roller meal decreased by
7.6 per cent, from K25,367 in October to K23,449 in November 2003, while
breakfast meal decreased by 1.1 per cent, from K31,130 in October to K30,793
in November 2003.
Maize grain measured in a 20-litre tin also registered a decrease, from
K11,993 in October 2003 to K11,537 in November, reflecting a decrease of 3.8
per cent.
The CPI covers three areas categorized as metropolitan low, metropolitan
high and non-metropolitan groups.
The metropolitan low income group covers low income households in the
urban areas while the metropolitan high income group covers high income
households in the urban areas. The non-metropolitan group covers households
in rural areas

Original_Search_Title: 16 Rescued in Gulu
Headline: Uganda: 16 Rescued in Gulu
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE Uganda Peoples Defence Forces (UPDF) on Thursday rescued 16
abductees and recovered looted goods from the LRA rebels after an assault on
a rebel hideout in Aswa River Valley, Palaro sub-county.
UPDF 4th Division spokesman Lt. Paddy Ankunda said the Lord's Resistance
Army (LRA) rebels fled in disarray towards Kitgum district.
The items recovered included dry meat, cassava chips and cooking
utensils.
Meanwhile, three LRA fighters, including LRA vice chairman Vincent
Otti's wife, surrendered to the UPDF in Kitgum district.
Area-based UPDF 503 brigade commander, Lt. Col. Samuel Kiwanuka, said
the trio were at the headquarters.
Gulu district officials said the district has been relatively peaceful
for months save for last week's LRA incursion into Aswa county.

Original_Search_Title: Congo: Military Chiefs Analyse Situation Along Border
Headline: Angola: Congo: Military Chiefs Analyse Situation Along Border
Body_Text:
Cabinda — Matters related to the current situation along the Angola, Congo
Brazzaville and the Democratic Republic of Congo's common border will be
analysed on Tuesday, in far northern Cabinda province, by the three
countries' military delegations.
To participate in the meeting, is in Cabinda city the vice Chief of the
Angolan Armed Forces, General Geraldo Sachipengo "Nunda", who heads the
Angolan team.
In oil-rich Cabinda province, the Angolan Armed Forces have been
carrying out for several months military operations against some guerilla of
FLEC, with a view to guarantee a political and military stability in this
region of Angola.
Cabinda province is bordered to the West with the Atlantic Ocean, North
with Congo Brazzaville and in East and South with the Democratic Republic of
Congo.
It has an area of 7.280 square kilometers and its population is
estimated at 170.000 inhabitants.
Oil, phosphate, uranium, wood, potassium and gold are its primary
natural resources.
The local population dedicates mostly in the cultivation of maize,
coffee, cocoa, cassava and in the production of palm oil.

Original_Search_Title: Country Selected to Export Items to US Market
Headline: Ghana: Country Selected to Export Items to US Market
Body_Text:
Ghana has been selected as the only country in sub-Saharan Africa to
export Afro-centric (locally woven garments) items to the United States
market under the African Growth and Opportunity Act (AGOA).
Ghana attained this status after the sector Ministry had sent various
samples of Ghanaian folklore garments and textiles to the United States for
verification, last year.
The items included, batik, tie and dye, Kente, Joromi, Kaftan, smock,
table mats and other textile furnishings.
Dr Gordon Asare-Kyeremateng, AGOA Adviser, announced this in Tamale on
Friday when he addressed a one-day seminar to launch the American Chamber of
Commerce AGOA Help Desk.
He said by attaining this position, Ghanaian exportable items such as
garments and textiles were likely to increase the volume of items to the US
market.
About 60 representatives from the business community in the Northern
Region took part in the seminar, which had the theme: "Taking advantage of
the AGOA initiative".
The forum was to create the awareness and opportunities of AGOA to the
business communities in the Northern, Upper East and Upper West Regions for
them to get involved in the activities of the initiative.
Dr Asare-Kyeremateng called on the business community in the Northern
Region to take advantage of the opportunities around them to explore and be
part of AGOA and derive optimum benefit from the initiative. Mr Iddrisu
Adam, Tamale Metropolitan Chief Executive called on the participants to make
good use of the seminar and position themselves in the right perspective to
benefit from AGOA.
He said, for instance, that cashew, groundnut, sheanut, cottonseed, yam
and cassava, which were produced in the region, could be exported to the
US. Mr Adam said the Metropolitan Assembly was ready to support businessmen
in the region to promote their businesses.
The seminar covered areas such as "background information on AGOA",
product coverage and eligibility", performance under AGOA" and "export
planning.

Original_Search_Title: Contraband Owners Get 2-Week Disposal Deadline
Headline: Nigeria: Contraband Owners Get 2-Week Disposal Deadline
Body_Text:
The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has given supermarkets and shop owners
in the country a 2-week deadline to dispose of contraband or risk
seizure.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) recalls that the federal government had
in August, last year banned the importation of some products, food items and
other consumables into the country.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned
items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country," a
statement issued by the NCS today in Abuja, said.
The statement, signed by the comptroller-general, Alhaji Ahmed Mustapha,
and made available to NAN, stated that the service would on expiration of
the ultimatum raid such shops nationwide.
Mustapha said that the enforcement team of the service would confiscate
the banned items "where ever found" and also prosecute the offenders.
He, directed the customs area controllers to use the local media in
their locality to educate and warn members of the public on the impeding
action.
The customs boss, also advised all customs formations nationwide to
ensure strict compliance with the directive.
NAN recalls that the service had recently raided warehouses in some
popular markets in Lagos and Kano and impounded contraband textiles and
other items valued at millions of Naira.
Among items outlawed were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum,
vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellent coils, rethreaded or used tyres,
gaming machines and second-hand clothes.
Others are used fridges and air-conditioning units, used compressors,
bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles that
are above eight years from the date of manufacture, textiles of any kind
through the land borders, printed fabric (Ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned were cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through
the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water
(sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any
type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders.
The government had also banned the importation of sugar, all kinds of
canned and bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and
envelopes, Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.
NAN learnt that the ban was intended by government to boost local
production, enhance the performance of the national economy and safeguard
the health of the citizenry.

Original_Search_Title: Christmas Fever Grips Yaounde
Headline: Cameroon: Christmas Fever Grips Yaounde
Body_Text:
Most markets now appear busier than usual as the population prepares for Christmas.
The month of December is a special period world wide, not only for the celebration of Christmas but as an end of a year. Money or no money, markets during this period are jammed with goods and people.
This festive fever is once more around. Markets in the nation's Capital get thicker each day. Buyers and sellers have intensified their activities in a bid to prepare for Christmas. Hundreds of people have engaged in businesses that are of peak demand to the population. Toys, decorative materials, fashion dresses and food stuffs are some of the most sought for goods. In Yaounde, for instance, toys and children dresses flood most markets. Affordable toys and dresses can be found in the Mokolo and Central markets. Cartons of toys are displayed in almost all corners in the market. Many buyers are seen purchasing either toys or dresses for their children.
Mokolo market is a case in point where many buyers, especially women, scramble for second hand dresses (okrikah). According to a buyer, Valerie, they are affordable because of the cheap price. Most big shops in Mokolo and the Central market have not only displayed new items in and out of their shops, but have set a festive tone with attractive music and decorations.
There is high competition amongst traders as many shop owners are doing every thing to attract customers. While some have announced discount in prices of goods, others hire young men and women to persuade customers for them. Others have even gone in for animation groups to animate in front of their shops thereby attracting customers. According to the proprietor of one super market in Mokolo, there is need to attract customers because the competition amongst traders is very high. He offers a packet of detergent to every customer who buys from the super market.
At the Mfoundi market, still in Yaounde, food stuff dominates sales as usual. One can quickly see yams, rice and chicken, etc, as the are displayed almost in all corners of the market. Most traders especially in the Melen market exceed their closing time as they sell late into the night. Here one can find very cheap under wears, dresses and shoes for less than 500francs.
With the crowd around markets, pedestrians and car owners can hardly have their way near market avenues. To this effect, police men are on guard to evacuate those selling along the road especially in the Mokolo and Elig-Edzoa markets.

Original_Search_Title: 'The Media Should Help AMA Enforce By-Laws'
Headline: Ghana: 'The Media Should Help AMA Enforce By-Laws'
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly, AMA last weekend called on the media to
help them enforce the existing by-laws that bars hawkers and traders from
selling on ceremonial streets and pavements.
The AMA for the past four weeks has initiated an 'Action Plan' which
makes Saturday's clean-ups mandatory for the AMA Task Force and
traders.
Speaking in an interview with ADM, the Acting Director of Waste
Management, AMA, Mr. Laryea said AMA has put the necessary logistics in
place to keep the Capital City clean. The only thing left is the enforcement
of the by-laws. This has been in existence since 1960. The media can
therefore play a vital role by helping AMA in championing the education of
these by-laws.
Since the interception of the 'Action Plan' AMA has made all efforts to
stop traders from selling on the said places. Twenty-one traders have been
summoned, each paid Â¢6million and Â¢2million respectively.
The AMA Task Force last Saturday inspected the Ashiedu Keteke
Sub-Metro. The Task Force indulged the traders in a clean-up exercise but
only a hand full on them turned up.
The chairman of Ashiedu Keteke Sub-Mero, Mr. Sammuel Tetteh, said AMA is
not interested in summoning traders. 'AMA summons traders to serve as
warning to others who might fall foul to the by-laws'.
The Chairman said traders selling on the streets do not pay tolls. The
traders without proper locations would be sacked from where they are.
He said Yam sellers, cooking oil sellers and onion sellers would be
relocated. Traders like those on the streets have abandoned their
locations. Action taken have not worked because 'AMA has been cowed down by
public sympathy'.
As to whether was AMA wining the 'war' on sanitation he said yes but to
the disadvantage of the general public' The chairman said traders are not
cooperating in the clean-up exercise.

Original_Search_Title: A Nation's Talent for Bungling
Headline: Nigeria: A Nation's Talent for Bungling
Body_Text:
Lagos — IN the wake of his recent bitter experience with Nigeria's sports
administrators, Englishman Bryan Robson had scathing albeit profound words
on the country. Robson, the ex-Manchester United star player had virtually
been appointed coach of Nigeria's senior soccer squad ahead of the African
Nations Cup championship next month before Sports Minister, Musa Mohammed
truncated the deal at the eleventh hour.
In the words of the "nearly" Eagles coach, until Nigeria sheds its
"Third World mentality," it will almost be impossible for it to actualise
its potentials and win a major soccer laurel.
The Englishman said his comments were not borne out of
bitterness. Maybe. He will, of course, not be saying the whole truth if he
said he was not bitter and disappointed. But even at that, any such
bitterness in him, which will be quite natural, hardly nullifies the
profundity of his observations. If it takes bitterness to say the haunting
truth about a people's legendary culture of tardiness, so be it. All it
needs to confirm the penetrating import of Robson's comment is to lift it
beyond the confines of sports. If that is done and his views still hold
relevance, then the issue of whether he spoke out of bitterness becomes
substantially immaterial.
In more ways than one, Nigeria as unably personified by its consistent
leadership and governments seem determined to cling on to haphazard and
incompetent ways. These are what Robson classified as Third World
mentality. The manifestations of this mentality pervades the country's
administrative landscape.
In its sundry dimensions, the Third World mentality finds expression in
planlessness, corrosive viciousness, myopia, primitive accumulation,
inferiority complex, lack of self worth, raw power exhibition and an
incapacitating disdain for research and scientific methods. Of course, there
are various other forms. For the purpose of this discussion here, we remain
limited to the realms of sports, soccer to be particular.
In less than one month, the African Nations Cup tournament will kick off
in Tunisia. Robson noted that all the top and best hotels in Tunis had been
reserved by other nations where teams will be at the championship. As usual
Nigeria's sports administrators (a misnomer of a tag, really) are yet to
concern themselves with such details. Eventually, the Nigerian team will be
settled in some inauspicious, dingy location; maybe in the middle of a red
light district in Tunis. Still, Nigerians will expect their team to
concentrate and contend for the trophy. Indeed, the setting is nothing
new. It has become the way of the country. In the immediate past edition of
the same championship in Mali, such other contending teams as Cameroon,
Senegal, South Africa etc were quartered by their respective sports
administrators in very secluded and conducive locations in the host
city. Not so with Nigeria's team. They were quartered in the middle of a
burstling market area, the equivalent of Oyingbo in Lagos. Such is the
attitude Robson was referring to. It hardly makes for
success. Instructively, it still takes a white coach or manager to get our
sports administrators to walk away from such a debasing, miserable
disposition. Talk of a Third World mentality!
There are, of course, the matters of appropriate feeding arrangement and
tuning up matches for the team, as well as the critical task of intelligence
on their opponents' programme. None of these obviously constitutes the
present concern of Nigeria's sports administrators. Yet you can bet that at
the appropriate time a loaded plane of federal government delegation
featuring representatives from the presidency, ministers, governors, their
wives, their concubines and sundry ministry officials and party thugs,
otherwise called stalwarts will head to Tunis, there to ginger the maligned
soccer squad to win their matches. That is the way of Nigeria, a classical
Third World attitude.
As it concerns the hiring or non-hiring of Robson, the real issue is not
that he was not eventually hired. Of course, it was hopeless to set about
scouting for and engaging a coach for a team barely two months to a major
tournament. But where was the sports minister all the while that the
leadership of the Nigeria Football Association (NFA) was negotiating the
Robson deal? Could the minister and his ministry not have moved earlier to
save the country, the NFA and all the international embarrassment of
quashing the Robson deal very late along the line? Obviously, it is the
power wielding thing, the ultimate manifestation of Third World
mentality.
Then, there is the lack of self worth or inferiority complex
dimension. In the thick of the English Premiership League at the moment
stars a young man whose name is Shola Ameobi. He plays for Newcastle
Football Club. As his name goes, Ameobi could be a Nigerian, one way or
another. It is not clear here where he was born and who his parents
are. Shola Ameobi does score goals for his club. He is a hardworking player,
but not one with any exquisite talent, the type national and team coaches go
all to get. Ameobi has been waiting for a call-up to the English national
team. Well, the call has not come. The coaches of the English national side
obviously rate him lower than what they need.
The reports in the media recently are that Shola Ameobi will be willing
to play for Nigeria. Mark that! If the wait for England fails, Nigeria can
be managed. Going by the inferiority complex that often comes to play in
Nigeria's official acts when relating with things foreign, coach Christian
Chukwu's bloated list may soon accommodate Shola Ameobi. If that development
occurs, it will only be following a pattern. It will even be far better than
the case of John Fashanu. Fashanu if you can recall, rejected calls to play
for Nigeria. He too was waiting for an England call up. John was even far
less talented than Shola Ameobi. But he had brawn. Then he was playing for
Wimbledon. Two Nigerians at heart, John Chiedozie and Tunji Banjo did answer
the call of the Nigerian national team from their base in England. In later
years another British Nigerian, Reuben Agboola followed suite and played for
the Eagles. England did call up Fashanu, but it was not a remarkable career
for him thence. But at least he actualised his dream of playing for
England.
Some years after his playing career, what did Nigerians get? First, John
Fashanu was made what they called a sports ambassador for Nigeria. Then he
was hurled into the NFA as member. Oh yes, it does get that pitiable in
Nigeria. That is the Third World mentality that Bryan Robson talked
about. It is most unlikely that any character with Fashanu's profile would
have been made ambassador of any variety, not the list in sports in say,
Cameroon or for that matter in any society with self worth. But then, this
is Nigeria. And this is why the country will never get anywhere until it
sheds its Third World mentality that does pervade its existence in numerous
forms. The pathetic thing about Nigeria is that people who watch while
cocoyam is being sown often wake the next day to expect and pray that the
yield will be a healthy yam. That sure goes beyond Third World mentality. It
is inane.

Original_Search_Title: Governor Ibrahim Idris, Please Save Koton-Karfe From Oil Thieves
Headline: Nigeria: Governor Ibrahim Idris, Please Save Koton-Karfe From Oil Thieves
Body_Text:
For nearly four years now, oil thieves and bunkerers have been operating
at Okpaka and Jamata villages both near Koton-Karfe town, bunkering oil
(NNPC) installations in these areas. It is estimated that an average of
20,000 litres of petrol and diesel are being carted away every night.
What bothers one is the consequence of oil spillage into our rivers and
fire disaster that may occur. Fire disasters with devastating extensive
consequences that can gut the entire Koton-Karfe and Gegu-Beki communities
not to talk of over 10 human settlements along the pipelines. Also about 7
petrol stations were built on the pipelines. Remember the Jesse fire inferno
where 1,000 people were burnt to death. Charred human bodies littered
everywhere like bunch of yams. In recent times there has been increases of
fire incidences at homes as a result of petrol stock at homes as transit
points.
The impunity with which the vandals operate opens the way to doubts s to
whether any effort is being made to apprehend them. This, in spite of armed
policemen on guard there.
There is active evidence that the police in Koton-Karfe and Lokoja (HQS)
are fully collaborators in these crimes. In the past vigilante groups make
arrests but promptly released as soon as they get to the police HQS at
Lokoja.
A community-based organisation, Club '98 has severally written and
visited NNPC Abaji Pumping Station and the police on these crimes but no
action. The club further organised public awareness campaign on the dangers
of these acts and attendant effect on the communities. But the illegal
trade, exploration on the "national cake" continued to wax stronger.
Furthermore, considering the dangers of oil disasters we expect NNPC to
have a fallback plan to contain emergencies such as these theft and where it
is carried out. Is it not possible that the vandals are operating as a
syndicate with the collusion of police and NNPC guards as insider? The Crime
is on the increase because there is no deterrence.
The only saving grace now is the Governor of Kogi State, Alhaji Ibrahim
Idris, appealing to take drastic steps with the federal authorities to deal,
arrest all those found. The rate at which these thieves are brandishing
their cars with stolen oil money, embarrassing, harassing and brutalising
everyone are common traits now. The Governor will be doing good by saving
innocent lives in Koton-Karfe and Gegu-Beki (where over 80 per cent of these
criminals are) from incidence associated with oil bunkering. As the govenor
used to say, that Koton-Karfe is his second home therefore please do
something to prevent the place from burning down from the activities of
shrouded criminals. The traditional rulers, elders could not exact their
orders anymore
Salisu Mamman Isa, Kogi Local Government Secretariat, Koton-Karfe,
Kogi State.

Original_Search_Title: Queen Meets Villagers in Karu, Visits BBC Project
Headline: Nigeria: Queen Meets Villagers in Karu, Visits BBC Project
Body_Text:
Abuja — Her Majesty, Queen Elizabeth II, yesterday got the feel of a typical
African village when she paid a visit to Karu Village, a local government in
Nassarawa State.
The village setting at the Local Government Headquarters was also the
site for a BBC project titled "Voices" which is a three year national public
education project made by Nigerians, in Nigeria for Nigerians in English,
Hausa, Ibo and Yoruba languages."
The Queen who arrived Karu at 10.30 am in a white Range Rover with
diplomatic registration number plates was received by Nasarawa State
Governor Abdullahi Adamu.
He was also joined to receive the Queen at Karu by other Governors,
amongst whom are George Akume and Bukola Saraki of Benue and Kwara States
respectively. Mrs. Folashade Randle, who as a four-year old Folashade Lawson
presented the Queen with flowers in 1956 during her first trip to Nigeria,
was also there to put four year old Hania Phillip Adadu through the same
steps.
Several cultural troupes including Alago from Doma Local Government,
Nassarawa State, and Jarawa from Plateau State and Swange from Benue State
entertained the British Monarch.
The Queen led by Governor Adamu walked around the village meeting locals
from Karu environs, displaying their wares including tomatoes, pepper and
yams.
Adamu in a speech at the occasion said the Commonwealth has never lost
its relevance in contemporary issues. He said the Commonwealth is becoming
stronger and continues to be a loud voice in the world.

Original_Search_Title: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Headline: Nigeria: Church Donates Gifts to Less-Privileged Homes
Body_Text:
Mountain of Fire and Miracles Ministries (MFM) at the weekend donated
gifts worth N2.5 million to inmates of less-privileged homes in Lagos.
The church made the donation through its welfare alms unit- "We Care
Ministry" and also feted the inmates with sumptuous dishes. The event took
place at MFM Onike-Iwaya Yaba, Lagos headquarters.
The inmates who had colourful wears made for them by the minister
laughed and danced across the expansive auditorium of the church. Some of
the inmates of the less-privileged homes who spoke to Eko Trust expressed
their happiness and one of them, Yinka Olaiya said, "I am happy. They gave
me two new clothes. I am wearing one and I have another one for Christmas."
The gifts include a bus to Arrow of God Home, Lekki, television, radio
sets, video CDs, cartons of milk, refrigerators, freezers, electric cookers,
bags of rice, bags of garri, tubers of yam. The statistics of the items run
into thousands.
Some of the recipients include Remand Home, Oregun, Boys Approved Homes,
Lagos State Government Little Saints Akowonyo, Boys Approved School, Isheri,
Rehabilitation and Vocational Training Centre, Isheri, The Good Shepherded
Orphanage Home, Idimu, Distressed Children Transit Home, Idi-Araba and
Remand Home, Agege.
Speaking to Eko Trust after the donation, which was witnessed by notable
church leaders, the coordinator of "We Care Ministry, Reverend Julius
Obileye, said the gesture was MFM's own way of practically ameliorating the
sufferings of the downtrodden.

Original_Search_Title: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Headline: Ghana: I Left Ghana Because of Rawlings - 1979 Victim Tells Commission
Body_Text:
Mr. Daniel Ohene Adjei, another victim of the atrocities of the 1979
mutiny started weeping even before telling his story perhaps because just as
he was about to do so he had remembered the painful ordeal.
He told the commission that in 1979, as a trader who went to buy yam
from the producing areas to sell in Accra he had brought in some one million
tubers to the Timber Market when a military vehicle pulled up with some
soldiers in it.
One of the soldiers he said asked him for the price of a tuber of yam he
had picked to which he mentioned one hundred and twenty cedis. The soldier
said it was too expensive but he tried to explain to him that transport cost
and other incidentals are factored into the price of the commodity.
One of the soldiers he said pushed him at this stage and he
retaliated. He was hit and a struggle ensued but he was overpowered by the
soldiers who bundled him into their truck after a bad beating.
In the truck too they beat him until they reached Mitchel Camp,
Tema.
He was locked up in a guardroom for three days and for two days he did
not eat he told the commission. When he was released to go home he had no
money on him and he was half naked. A woman by the roadside he said offered
him one cedi for transport back to Accra and a dress to cover his
nakedness.
He could not go to find out about the status of his tubers of yam
because his father had discouraged him from doing so he told the
commission.
He then left for Nigeria because according to him he did not want to
have anything to do Mr. Jerry Rawlings whose regime was responsible for his
predicament.
He expressed gratitude to the government for making such a commission
possible but for which those of them with bottled-up pain would not have had
the outlet of airing them.
He added that he had forgiven those who wronged him after the
narration.
Proceedings continue today with the retired Major Boakye Djan and his
AFRC mates making a spectacular appearance which is expected to be hot.

Original_Search_Title: Essential Commodity Prices Fall in Jos
Headline: Nigeria: Essential Commodity Prices Fall in Jos
Body_Text:
Prices of some foodstuff and essential commodities in Jos have fallen in
the last one month, the News agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports.
According to a market survey conducted on Wednesday in two satellite
markets in Jos, an average tuber of yam now sells at N80, down from N100 in
October.
A bag of sweet potatoes is sold for N500 instead of the previous
N700.
Similarly, a standard measure of beans which cost N90 last month now
goes for N75, a bottle of vegetable oil costs N140 as against N150, while a
kilogramme of meat remains stable at N350.
The prices of a standard measure of parboiled rice and a bottle of palm
oil are also stable at N130 and N130 respectively.
Some of the traders interviewed attributed the drop in prices to the
availability of the products in large quantities.

Original_Search_Title: Banda Chieftaincy Affair
Headline: Ghana: Banda Chieftaincy Affair
Body_Text:
The Member of Parliament (MP) for Wenchi West, Johnson Asiedu Nketsia
(aka Gen. Mosquito) has denied fueling the protracted chieftaincy dispute in
the Banda Traditional Area but blamed non-resident influential citizens of
the area for the recent clashes.
Mr. Nketsia told The Chronicle on Monday that his message to the two
rival paramount chiefs had been peace. "If there was any problem in the area
I will put 70 per cent of the blame on the so-called influential citizens of
Banda who are staying outside but go there and put fire and then come
out".
Mr. Asiedu-Nketsia was reacting to fresh allegations made by one of the
rival paramount chiefs, Nana Sakyameh Sen-Apem II to the effect that the
recent violent clashes at Banda were of the MP's making.
The chief accused him of advising his rival, Nana Kwadwo Sito I at
Banda-Ahenkro to go ahead and celebrate the Banda Annual Yam festival
despite District Security Council warnings against the celebration.
But the MP said to say that his visit on September 13 ignited the
conflict was "an infantile logic".
He admitted visiting the area on the day in question but said he did so
with the National Democratic Congress (NDC) regional chairman as part of a
planned tour of several constituencies in the area.
According to him the visit was cut short when he received a call from
Accra that he was to travel on a parliamentary team outside the
country.
Mr. Nketsia further admitted the chief mentioned the festival to him but
he told him he was traveling and would send somebody to represent him.
Other allegations made against the MP were that he had never attended
any function at Kabrono, the other rival town but always attended functions
at Banda-Ahenkro. He was also accused of shifting the venue of a programme,
which was supposed to be held in honour of the former vice president John
Evans Atta Mills, who was then visiting the Bui dam in 2000, from Kabrono to
Banda-Ahenkro.
However, Nketsia said the allegations were all false and that since his
election as MP for the area he had tried as much as possible not to involve
himself in the chieftaincy dispute. He had treated both parties with the
courtesy they deserved whenever he visited them.
With regard to the former vice-president's visit, he said there was an
occasion where some of the so-called influential citizens of Banda had gone
to use the former vice-president's visit to support one of the
factions.
Mr. Nketsia called on the factions to reconcile since such disputes did
not augur well for the development of the area.

Original_Search_Title: Is Hausa Poetry in Decline?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Hausa Poetry in Decline?
Body_Text:
Shehu Usman Dan Fodiyo (of blessed memory), was confronted with the
challenge of transforming a society that was enlightened but deeply enmeshed
in immorality, injustice and corrupt leadership.
'Malamai' then were but palace praise singers, while the masses were
left shouldering the burden of paying heavily for the lavish and loud living
of the 'Sarakuna' through taxes. Reforming such a society was to a certain
extent easy, because the people could read and write, hence literary
instruments were deployed by the Shehu and his disciples. Poetry is one of
the effective mediums used by Shehu Dan Fodiyo in making people in Hausaland
understand that the society is ill and had to be changed. Abdullahi Gwandu
and Muhammadu Bello wrote several poems. Nana Asma'u-the daughter of Shehu
Dan Fodiyo was a poet of distinction. In fact, throughout the period of the
struggle that saw Hausaland from deviation to guidance: from injustice to
justice and from corruption to transparent leadership that was the pace for
the evolution of modern Hausa society.
The 'horrible incidence,' i.e., the coming of European explorers,
missionaries who later transformed into colonizers, created a new space for
literary activities in Hausaland. Scholars wrote poems highlighting the
dangers of what became the conquest of Hausalasnd and the subsequent
emergence of what is now Nigeria and the introduction of 'boko.' Writing
gradually changed from Ajami to Roman script. The poetry of scholars like
Aliyu Dansidi, Aliyu Namangi and other notable Hausa poets became available
in Roman script.
Poetry (rubutattun wakoki) has always had an upper hand in the Hausa
literary scene. The best of Hausa writers are concentrated in the poetry
genre. Leading Hausa poets that stood out include Aliyu Dansidi of Zazzau,
Sa'adu Zungur, Aliyu Namangi, Mudi Sipikin, Akilu Aliyu, Na'ibi Suleiman
Wali and Mu'azu Hadejia. Although there were many other poets of
distinction, they were not noticed because they wrote in 'Ajami.' All these
poets were members of a generation far from the present.
The effectiveness of poetry in the Hausa literary scene can be discerned
from the impact made by Malam Sa'adu Zungur. His only collection of poems
contains four poems, i.e, 'Innovation' (Wakar Bidia), 'Welcome to the
Soldiers' (Wakar Maraba Da Soja), 'Careless Talk' (Wakar 'Yam Baka) and the
'The North' Republic or Monarchy (Arewa Jamhuriyya ko Mulukiya). Though
short, the versatility of poetic elements made these poems significant in
form and content. In 'Arewa Jamhuriyya ko Mulukiya' Sa'adu Zungur produced
an informed analysis of the position of Northern Nigeria in what is
'federalism.' With powerful imagery, Sa'adu Zungur projected the problems of
Hausaland like begging, indolence, insolvency, aversion to knowledge and too
much pride. Akilu Aliyu displayed the kind of talent that was entirely
poetic. His famous poem, 'Wakar 'Yar Gagara' (The prostitute) gave an
account of the life of a prostitute leading eventually to the point where
prostitution is a shortcut to a sad end. Na'ibi Suleiman Wali explored the
overwhelming dangers of the much cherished modernity which has promoted
immorality, while dimming the virtues of our values.
This is perhaps the period in which we desperately need more poems from
Hausa literary scenes. This is because, unlike other forms of writing,
poetry can be published and circulated early and less expensively. The decay
of almost everything is apparent in the Hausa society of today.
Developments in Hausa literary scene can best be described as,
copsided. The heydays with Kano market literature (KLM) churned out a prose
only literary era. Best of writers in this class like Bilikisu Salisu
Funtua, Bala Anas Babinlata, Ado Ahmed Gidan Dabino, Balaraba Ramat Yakubu,
were novelists. This era had the advantage of the highest number of literary
associations like Dan Haki, Marubuta Soyayyar Zamani, Raina Kama, Jigon
Hausa, Kukan Kurciya and so many others. It was in this period that we had
bookshops that also doubled as publishers.
More advanced literary criticisms in Hausa came to the fore with what
was known as, "The Great Soyayya Debate,"exposed Hausa literature to
rigorous criticisms thus drawing more attention to the richness of Hausa
literary culture. This is an era of novels.
It is acknowledged that the foundation of Hausa literature is now
isolated or even pushed out of the literary activity. This may often be
attributed to the high level of talent and the linguistic prowess required
in writing a poem especially in Hausa.
Poetry in Hausa is and has been at a standstill for so long. The
prevalence of a culture that tilts more to the screen than the text, thus
Hausa poetry becomes more endangered. This is not saying that there is
absolute dearth of talents, after all Hausa musical scene is ever thriving
with new talents coming-up all over Hausaland, performing as good as Mamman
Shata, Dan Kwairo, Narambada, Garba Supa, Aliyu Dandawo, Sarkin Taushin
Katsina, Dan Maraya, Sani Dan Indo and others.
Now that the Hausa literary scene is more or less consumed by the boom
of fina-finam Hausa with so many writers now comfortable with being
producers, there is need to avoid, 'Mamta Mafari, (forgetting the origin),
producers who were at the beginning writers doing a good service to Hausa
culture. However, the question is, shall literary activity, especially the
poetry genre be left to go away with the storm? The answer here and in the
affirmative is that there is need to sustain and ensure that literary
culture thrives.
This time, more than any other, provides the platform for new and more
'Wakokin Hausa ya rubuce." We have so many topics to put beautiful verses
and stanzas on. Politics and this democracy, education and crimes,
corruption and diseases, poesy and affluence, breach of trust and too much
emphasis on vanity!
Branches of the Association of Nigerian Authors (ANA) in dominantly
Hausa states can intervene. Instead of putting their time and energy into
writing English poems, a bit of their attention should go into putting in
place mechanisms for restoring the vitality of Hausa poetry. New talents
should be encouraged and poetry should be emphasized. The association can
also contribute by collecting Hausa poems written in 'Ajami' script around
our 'Malamai' and 'Tsangayoyi.' Transcribing them into Roman script can be a
step forward in enlightenment.
Technicality and intellectual demands are some of the discouraging
points in Hausa poetry. It takes a lot of originality to write a good Hausa
poem. But this is not supposed to discourage us from trying.

Original_Search_Title: VP Bukenya's Poverty Dilemma
Headline: Uganda: VP Bukenya's Poverty Dilemma
Body_Text:
Kampala — It has just occurred to me that people who have a very deep attachment
to cows may also have a soft spot for humans who have attributes associated
with cattle.
Now, if you like cows essentially because they are docile, giving milk
to the pigs with the same dedication as they used to give milk to Mr. Jones,
you would probably wish that they did not get much smarter; just in case
they start learning a thing or two about their tragic existence.
Fallen revolutionaries, false prophets, conjurors and miracle workers
are all more comfortable with peasants than with other social groups,
because peasants generally do not have the training and experience to
critically examine the actions and motives of dedicated "performers".
As far as their economic woes are concerned, they have not been taught
enough arithmetic to grasp the nature of the conspiracy by which the rulers,
the legislators, the tax collectors and the privileged middle and business
classes operate.
When a peasant sells his 10kg of beans, walks into a village store and
pays Shs 2,000 for a litre of paraffin, he does not quite understand how he
is helping the lords of Rukungiri, Mbarara or Soroti to build their palaces
around the country and maybe abroad.
He does not even realise that he is contributing to the five-digit
dollar monthly obscenity for Kampala-based top dogs in the petroleum
industry.
Needless to say, the idea that he is helping American and European
executives and shareholders overseas to change their golf bags sounds like a
fable.
The peasant's expertise is in following the tracks of the guinea fowl
that uproots his nuts, or judging that the state of the creeper at the edge
of the forest indicates a mature but shrivelled yam two feet
underground.
But slowly, like he learned the tricks of the monkey that eats his
maize, he is waking up to the tricks of the politicians.
I suspect that you, reading this article, are not a peasant. Neither am
I. So, you and I are probably more akin to the dog or the wolf.
Hitherto, we were told that everything was fine. We mongrels, wolves and
what have you; our feelings and our reflections were largely irrelevant,
because the Movement (an euphemism for President Museveni) had millions of
peasants at its beck and call.
The vice president, Prof. Gilbert Bukenya, was probably dramatising a
little during his conversation with the press this week, when he warned of a
peasant revolt against the elite.
But he was correct when he said that the peasants have reason to be
disenchanted with the status quo.
He confesses that "we" (presumably the princes of the Movement machine)
have been practising the politics of "exciting people", the politics of
"blinding people", the politics of "deceit".
He wants to change that, predicting that once "practical peasantry
development" has started, most of the politicians (of deceit) will "fall
away from the scene."
Will Bukenya be left standing? An intriguing question. For who are the
politicians of deceit?
President Museveni is the avowed champion of the peasants. What is more,
the president perfectly understands the arithmetic that I said was a dark
region to most peasants.
That is why he often explains (as he did this week in the US) to Western
audiences how Uganda is a big "donor", selling to the West cheap raw
materials for the benefit of their multinational processors, workers, tax
collectors and governments.
So, who are the politicians of deceit, the politicians who sing and sing
but have refused or failed to sort out the plight of Uganda's peasants and
workers?
Is it Kananathan of the AGOA fame?
No. Kananathan knows what he is doing; handing out the exact measure of
contempt he knows a morally confused political order will permit.
Which politicians have made sure that the blindness of the peasants is
preserved; that opposition politicians must not be allowed to traverse the
villages to educate the peasants about the politics of deceit?
Which politicians have turned the politics of deceit into a lucrative
industry, instead of raising Uganda to a country that "makes machines which
make machines"; the grand 1986 vision, which I am sure Prof. Bukenya now
secretly acknowledges was the politics of "exciting people"?
But Bukenya cannot eat his cake and also keep it. It is almost
impossible to demand a radical departure without confronting the architects
of the failure.
Attempting this balancing act will probably leave the professor without
sufficient intellectual crispness to persuade the elite. More important, he
will also be useless to the peasants. For, to make any significant
improvements in the lives or our peasants, the entire baggage of Movement
decay and self-perpetuating prodigal waste that grew out of the early
promise must be swept away.
It is naÃ¯ve to believe that it will just "fall away": the vice
president's preferred dream.
But will the professor concede that to be taken seriously in frontline
politics - his otherwise impressive CV notwithstanding - he must work for a
more legitimate democratic condition, with dynamism aimed at banishing the
peasant's blindness?
Or will he continue fidgeting with his contradictory thesis, constantly
mindful that the president has a strong affection for cows?

Original_Search_Title: DCE Cited in Knife- Drawing Incident
Headline: Ghana: DCE Cited in Knife- Drawing Incident
Body_Text:
It was neither corruption, nor was it bribery scandal, but purely abuse
of power, as the District Chief Executive (DCE) for Kintampo, Mr. James
Kwabena Bomfeh, has been cited in a knife-stabbing incident at Kintampo,
involving members of two factions in the Mo chieftaincy dispute.
It was alleged that Bomfeh ordered the Kintampo police to immediately
release one Bawa Peace, who had allegedly stabbed one Stephen Atta in the
neck with a knife on Thursday October 16, this year at about 8 pm at
Kintampo.
The victim told The Chronicle that after a complaint was lodged with the
Kintampo police, they only arrested the culprit briefly and later released
him on the orders of the DCE.
After the release of Bawa Peace, who is a close friend of Bomfeh, he
went to his victim's house to boast that if he had killed him no one could
arrest him because of his money and the presence of the DCE at Kintampo, and
even said after the incident he went and informed the DCE that he was able
"to accomplish the business."
But the DCE denied the allegations, when The Chronicle met him at his
office to ascertain the truth.
Bomfeh told the paper that he was not aware that Stephen Atta had been
stabbed.
He said the victim sustained the injury as a result of a blow landed on
him during scuffle between him and the suspect. Bomfeh however denied the
allegation that he ordered the police to release Bawa Peace, as alleged by
the victim.
When The Chronicle contacted the District Commander of Police for
Kintampo, Assistant Superintendent of Police (ASP) Thomas Osei, he confirmed
that a report was made to his outfit, but said because the case was still
under investigation, he would not like to comment on it.
But with the paper's further interrogation, the police commander said
the DCE never ordered them to release the suspect.
He said he is yet to call for the docket on the case because, as of
then, he had no more details on in the case, however that after he receives
the docket from the investigator, one Inspector Yeboah, he would then give
instructions on what to do.
At the time of The Chronicle investigations, about seven days after the
incident, no statements had been taken from either the suspect, or the
victim.
The police commander said initially when they heard the case they
thought it was one Mr. Dapaa, until when the victim returned from the
hospital.
Narrating the incident to the paper, the victim, Atta, said on Thursday
October 16 the paramount chief for the Mo traditional area, Nana Elarji made
announcement on a local FM station at Kintampo about his intention to
celebrate this year's yam festival at New Longoro, his traditional
headquarters.
The family head of the chief, Opanin Kwabena Nsiah, countered this
announcement, saying Nana Elarji is not a chief and as such has no
prerogative to celebrate the festival, Atta told Chronicle.
He said the family head further warned that any one who would go to New
Longoro for the festival, goes there at his own risk.
He said due to the volatile situation of the issue, the DCE and the
police commander threatened to arrest the chief if he dared celebrate the
festival.
Atta told The Chronicle that later Nana Elarji brought a letter from the
Brong Ahafo Regional House of Chiefs, which indicated that he could go ahead
to celebrate the festival as the paramount chief of the area.
The message of the letter was announced on the same local FM
station. The victim said following this announcement they came to the
chief's palace at Kintampo to have a discussion, because the next morning
they would proceed to New Longoro for the festival.
After the discussion, he and some friends decided to go to the house at
about 8 pm. On their way, according to him, they met Bawa Peace, with one
Kwame Krah, Issahaku and Koo Nkwantah.
Here, Atta said, Bawa Peace insulted them saying "Look at the slaves,
they call themselves chiefs, we shall see whether you are chiefs."
He said one of his friends called "The Boy" also replied him, saying "we
are not slaves." He added that he Atta advised him not to mind Bawa
Peace.
Later "The Boy" and the other friend left to the house, leaving him
alone. After they left, Atta said he saw Bawa Peace coming toward him with a
knife and Kwame Krah started shouting "Bawa stop that! Stop that!" But Bawa
would not mind him.
Atta said all of sudden Bawa approached him with the knife and raised
his hand to stab him, but he managed to dodge the knife and fell into a
gutter. Before then, Issahaku and Krah were chasing him and all those with
him could not come to his aid because of the knife.
"When I fell into the gutter, Kwame Krah came and held me from behind
and put me on the ground and that gave chance to Bawa Peace the chance to
stab me with the knife."
Immediately after he stabbed his victim, he quickly ran away from the
scene, Atta told The Chronicle.
Later, the victim said he went to the police and reported and he was
issued with police medical form to attend the hospital.
He continued that when he went to the Kintampo hospital he was referred
to the Holy Family Hospital at Techiman, where he was admitted and
discharged him the following day after treatment.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Stuff Soar in Jigawa
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Stuff Soar in Jigawa
Body_Text:
Two days into the Ramadan period, prices of food commodities have risen between 20% and 100% according to investigations in Dutse, Shuwarin and Hadejia markets.
According to Daily Trust investigations, prices of vegetables such as pepper, spinach, "Yakuisi" cabbage, etc have been increased by certain percentages, while pepper, tomatoes etc increased by about 20%.
For instance, in Dutse markets, vegetables formerly sold for N20 is now N60.
Additionally, prices of fruits have considerably risen by almost 100%. The survey indicates that a piece of pineapple sold at N100 is now N300, while a bag of orange sold at N3000 is now N32,00.
Further checks revealed that prices of pawpaw, banana and other fruits have risen by about 50%.
Vegetable oil however varies between N100 and N110, while eggs now sell at N15 instead of N12 per one.
Daily Trust also gathered in Shuwarin and Hadejia, that prices of grains remained stable, but that of other commodities such as Irish potatoes rose from N200 per unit to N250, even as yam tubers also increased by about 20%.
According to a trader, Mallam Kabir Sakwaya, the increase originated from wholesale traders in Kano. He blamed Southern traders for the increase, and called on market leaders to intervene and ensure the stabilisation of prices.
Scholars however, frowned at the development Chief Imam of Dutse government house, Ustaz Habibu Mohammed, enjoined Muslims to be rational and engage in actions that would make people's life easier at the period of holiness.
He said anybody that makes life difficult or uncomfortable through profiteering will certainly face the wrath of Allah.
Ustaz Habibu added that those that engage in positive actions should await Almighty's good tidings.
Several people interviewed in the market described the development as disturbing, while calling on traders to fear Allah and be considerate in their dealings.

Original_Search_Title: Ogoja People Cry Out Over Bad Roads
Headline: Nigeria: Ogoja People Cry Out Over Bad Roads
Body_Text:
Cross River — The poor distribution of food items from the local areas to the urban
cities has been blamed on poor road network in some parts of the country,
particularly in the South East.
These were some of the views expressed by some community leaders in
Mbock area of Ogoja in Cross River State, at a function organised by the
sons and daughters of Ogoja in Lagos.
One of the community leaders who identified himself as Anthony Mabin
said, though the efforts of the Federal Govern-ment towards road repairs
could not be faulted to a great extent as it has been highly felt in some
areas, he however wondered why some highly commercialised roads such as the
one which linked Cross River and Eboyin states have not been repaired.
He said the roads and bridges have clamed so many lives in the recent
past.
"Majority of those who may have died on these roads through accident are
traders who may be on their way to sell some of their products in other
parts of the country" he said.
Mabin, a Police officer attached to the computer department of the
Nigeria Police, Oduduwa Street, Ikeja, further stated that part of the
reasons why food items, especially cocoa, yams, timber, rice and cassava
seem to be scarce in places like Lagos could be traceable to the bad
condition of the Federal roads in the country.
"I wish to appeal to the Federal Government, on behalf of the good
people of Ogoja to look into the state of roads connecting Eboyin and Cross
River States as it is presently the only quickest route from Cross River
state to Eboyin" he added.

Original_Search_Title: Banda Chief Demands Probe Into Shooting Incident
Headline: Ghana: Banda Chief Demands Probe Into Shooting Incident
Body_Text:
Sunyani — Following the shooting incident at Banda-Ahenkro in the Wenchi district of the Brong Ahafo Region on September 15, this year, the paramount chief of the Banda traditional area, Nana Kwadwo Sito I, is pointing accusing fingers at the DCE for Wenchi, Mr. Joe Danquah, as the brain behind the incident.
The shooting incident in which 16 people were wounded, including the paramount chief, took place when the people of the area were celebrating their annual yam festival at Banda-Ahenkro.
The Kabronu clan, a faction in the Banda chieftaincy dispute, allegedly attacked the chief and his people as he was riding in a palanquin, on the way from the secret grove of the Banda stool.
But Hon. Danquah in an interview with The Chronicle said the chief was even lucky that he was not killed, because the security personnel had cautioned him not to visit the secret grove, because it is located at the area of the other faction at Kabronu, near Banda-Ahenkro.
In a petition addressed to the Minister of Justice and Attorney General, Nana Sito prayed that an committee be set up to investigate the matter.
