According to the paramount chief, he has no confidence in the District Security Council (DISEC), because the DCE has a greater influence on the members of the DISEC.
Nana Sito in his petition stated that immediately after the shooting incident, it was reported to the police at Banda Gbao, but no arrest had been made and those who sustained injuries were not even invited for their statement to be taken.
He attributed the police inability to take action or cause the arrest of his assailants to the influence of the DCE on the police.
He stated that before the festival, he informed the Gbao police about his intention to celebrate this year's yam festival from September 12 to 17 and he was given the green light.
He added that even on the day of the shooting incident, the police were following them and saw the shooters, yet they failed to effect any arrest.
He said even though the DCE is aware that the Supreme Court had ruled in favour of him, he has refused to recognize him as the Paramount chief of the area.
He lamented that the DCE, being a Banda himself, has accepted the loser in the Banda chieftaincy dispute as a legitimate one.
The chief explained that on May 16, 2001, the Supreme Court gave its ruling on the Banda chieftaincy dispute and declared him the winner and the paramount chief of the area.
Later in interview with the paper to throw more light on the incident, Nana Sito disclosed that Hon. Danquah on several occasions prevented some of his sub-chiefs from responding to his invitations.
Nana Sito accused Mr. Danquah of posing as the paramount chief of the area and even resorted to signing some social responsibility documents supposed to be signed by he the chief.
He cited an instance when the DCE prevented members of an ad hoc committee on the proposed Tain district to be carved out of Wenchi district from attending a meeting at his palace at Banda-Ahenkro when it was the turn of his paramountcy to host the committee.
The said meeting, according to him, was held at Banda-Buasi. The reason for preventing the committee, as revealed to The Chronicle, was that the DCE warned them of possible clash in the town if they dare to attend the meetings there.
The DCE in a telephone interview with the paper denied the allegation, but said the chief was advised not to visit the secret grove, because of its location.
According to the DCE, the security personnel had foreseen the dangerous situation ahead of visiting the site, hence the warning.
On the Supreme Court ruling, Hon. Danquah said that there are many confusing documents from various factions in the Banda dispute, indicating that the court had ruled in favour of them. But he however referred this reporter to the Regional House of Chiefs to find out who is the paramount chief of Banda.
Hon. Danquah lamented that Nana Sito is playing politics with the Banda stool, which does not augur well for the people of the area, adding that the people of Banda need development, but in a place of conflict, it is impossible for development to take place.
The DCE advised the chief to refrain from politics to pave way for development at Banda.
On the chief's claim that the DCE is posing as the paramount chief in the area, Hon. Danquah said he is not interested in the Banda chieftancy, adding that "I am not belonging to any of the two royal families in the area, so I don't support any of the factions."
On the social responsibility that the chief accused him of signing, the DCE told the paper that he only appended his signature on the document as a witness and not as a chief.

Original_Search_Title: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Headline: Nigeria: Refuse Takes Over Karmo Market
Body_Text:
Karmo market which is one of the busiest and cheap commodities market in
the Federal Capital Territory (FCT) has become a dumping place for refuse
especially after the weekly Tuesday market when the area is patronised by
all categories of traders and buyers from other surrounding villages and the
city.
With the site of the market in a village which lacks social amenities
and good roads, the market stretches along both sides of the road from the
last gate of Karmo daily market to the bus stop of old Karmo covering a
distance of two and a half kilometres.
The market, due to the density of the population of the village and the
number of visitors may be the dirtiest market in the city since traders not
only throw waste materials around the locality, but abandon their rotten
food stuff after the market, thereby creating stench and health hazards for
the residents of the area.
Apart from the motor park, a small space in which commuter buses
commonly called "molue" park after the daily business with residents, other
parts of the market is nothing to rejoice with in terms of sanitation and
plan.
The motor park which is mostly occupied by food sellers in carelessly
swept every Wednesday even as unsold food stuffs are piled under broken
vehicles at the park making the scene an eyesore especially after a heavy
rain.
While some visitors blame authorities for the deteriorating sanitary
condition of the market, others say the traders, most especially food
sellers are responsible for dumping left over foodstuffs along the
roadsides, though wheel barrow pushers abound on market days.
One of the visitors, Mrs. Jenet Ogbeche who said she lives in Area one,
Garki, not only expressed displeasure over the dirty condition of the market
but also complained of the food stuffs, the refuse and the sellers who
according to her, are not "hygienic" physically irrespective of the kind of
food they sell to people.
"It is a pity that the food sellers seem not to know what is
healthcare. They put their stuffs on refuse and sell to the people. But
unfortunately, people don't even mind from where they buy their items. Even
some of the sellers are so dirty that one would not doubt their food items
are unhealthy. Some even wash their yams, potatoes and fruits in the dirty
waters of gutters and drainage's," Mrs. Ogbeche observed.
Another woman who comes from Gwagwalada, blamed the traders for the
harzards even as she wondered what the money collected from traders was used
for.
"If the authorities, don't clean the market, does it mean the traders
can not clean where they put their commodities. If every trader cleans his
or her surrounding for the good health of the customers the market will
always be tidy," she opined.
But the authorities should do something better and faster. The market
has existed for decades and is supposed to be one of the most patronise in
the city since the rich come to buy their weekly needs from here," the woman
said.
Karmo market is not only famous for cheap commodities but also for
assorted materials because traders on weekly bases come from different
states of the country to sell and buy.

Original_Search_Title: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Headline: Uganda: Gov't to Gazette Nakivubo Swamp As a Protected Area
Body_Text:
Kampala — PROSSY Nalubwama was under siege. Her belongings were in ruins after the
floods poured into the house as she slept. She held on to a floating empty
jerrycan until she was rescued.
Her ordeal is not strange to the neighbours. They know that her house is
one of those that are worst hit by floods.
This scene in Kalerwe is common in Kampala's suburbs, which are prone to
floods. As the rainy season approaches, the tenants shift to escape the
menacing floods. New unsuspecting tenants usually replace them in the dry
season.
More than half a million people in Kampala, especially the low-income
earners, have been suffering quietly as a result of floods.
"We know about the plight of the people, but it is their fault," says
Robert Wabunoha, a senior environmental lawyer of the National Environment
Management Authority (NEMA).
Wabunoha says many people in Kampala have over the years built houses in
the valleys where water naturally flows. Because the water has nowhere else
to go, it floods people's homes.
Wabunoha says government has come up with new environmental laws to
protect the ecologically sensitive areas such as wetlands.
As the wetlands, which used to hold enormous quantities of water become
no more, the city has begun witnessing a catastrophe.
"It is becoming a routine to lose lives when floods hit the city," says
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in charge of wetlands in the
Ministry of Water, Lands and Environment.
Mafabi says most wetlands in Kampala have been cleared for human
settlement and industries.
In a recent tour organised by Shelter and Settlements, an NGO, the
low-lying Kifumbira slum that separates Mulago hill from Bukoto was found to
have countless pools of water. The pools provide a breeding ground for
mosquitoes and create a dirty environment that favours cholera. The area
suffered most from past cholera outbreaks.
This is because the latrines are built above water streams. During rains
the area residents usually open a hole to release faeces from the
latrines. The rain then washes away the faeces to streams, from where they
fetch water.
However, not many people have access to toilet facilities. Some defecate
in polythene bags, which they throw into the stream. This has earned buveera
the name: "flying toilet."
There are also heaps of unclaimed garbage because the crowded houses
leave only tiny passages, which can not be accessed by garbage
collectors.
Rwandume Mugizi, the KCC environment inspector, says Kampala has
swallowed up the greenery that once covered the empty hills and
valleys.
"We have a concrete jungle, which does not allow water filtration in the
soil and most of the water runs above the ground," he says.
Mugizi points out that most of the land in Kampala belongs to
individuals. This makes it difficult for the city council to plan and ensure
that houses are built to the required standards.
"The authorities plan for land that is not in their hands."
He says the lack of policies on housing has led to haphazard development
in Kampala and its suburbs. Mugizi also blames old laws that did not
recognise wetlands as important ecological areas. It is only in 1995 that a
law was put in place to protect wetlands. Henceforth it became illegal to
encroach on a wetland.
When the floods hit Kampala early this year, the former minister of
environment, Dr. Kezimbira Miyingo, issued a directive that all houses in
wetlands should be demolished.
But Phoebe Gubya, the KCC environment officer, complains about the
directive saying there is need for compensation. He says landowners claim
that they did not know they were building on wetlands.
However, Mafabi says there is no need for compensation for the houses
built after 1995 when a law was put in place to protect wetlands.
Mafabi says some of the wetlands such as the Nakivubo swamp, purify
waste water before it enters Lake Victoria. In so doing they protect the
lake from pollution.
"Soon the authorities will be mining water from filth as the mouth of
the lake is located a stone's throw away from Ggaba Water Works," he
says.
He says efforts are in advanced stages to gazette the Nakivubo wetland
as a protected area, and to stop fresh encroachment on it.
He says that after gazetting the Nakivubo swamp, the team will move in
to save Kinawataka wetland.
The war to protect wetlands has seen government taking contradictory
positions. As the government prepares to evict over 10,000 yam growers from
the Nakivubo wetland, a few rich owners of houses in Bugolobi who should be
evicted, have been left intact.
In a special report in The New Vision of April 25, it was reported that
the new proposed boundaries were being changed to favour the rich owners of
the houses.
Even the recent directive by Kezimbira came at a time when a
controversial shopping mall, Shoprite, was being put up at Lugogo play
grounds, which is a water way.
Mafabi says some politicians have abdicated from the noble cause of
protecting wetlands because they fear to make unpopular decisions.
"We are making a new law on wetlands to close the pitfalls," says a
source in the ministry of environment.

Original_Search_Title: PDP to ANPP: Don't Politicise Okadigbo's Death
Headline: Nigeria: PDP to ANPP: Don't Politicise Okadigbo's Death
Body_Text:
Abuja —  Kinsmen to consult oracle
Peoples Democraic Party (PDP) yesterday reacted to the raging
controversy over the death of former Senate President and vice presidential
candidate of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) in the last general
elections, Dr. Chuba Okadigbo, as it advised the deceased's party not to
politicise his death.
PDP's reaction came as the ANPP members in the House of Representatives
also yesterday alleged that Okadigbo died as a result of killer chemical
contained in the tear gas inflicted on him by the police during the party's
rally in Kano last Tuesday.
Also, kinsmen of the politician, the Iguedo clan in the Omambala area of
Anambra State have said they would consult the "Iguedo Oracle" to unravel
the circumstances that led to the death of the late politician.
But the Anambra State government, through controlled by the PDP,
yesterday promised to accord Okadigbo state burial and bear all the expenses
relating to the burial and funeral ceremonies.
The PDP whose reaction was given by its national secretary, Prince
Vincent Ogbulafor, noted that, "government cannot send anybody to go and
kill."
Ogbulafor was reacting to the allegation by ANPP that PDP was
responsible for the use of tear gas by the Police to disperse ANPP members
during their rally in Kano.
The National Director of Publicity of ANPP, Alhaji Ibahim Modibo had
accused PDP of instigating Police against its rally and described the Police
as "the Peoples Democratic Police."
But Ogbulafor in defence of PDP said, "It is very unfortunate that any
time any thing sad happens, they point accusing fingers at the party (PDP)
and the government."
Ogbulafor added that, "government cannot send anybody to go and kill, it
is not done. It is unheard of."
Ogbulafor described death as, "a necessary thing that will come when it
will come, that's why we are all afraid of death, because we don't know when
it will come. We should not politicize his death. It should not be
politicized at all."
Addressing newsmen yesterday at the National Assembly complex, the
caucus leader, Hon Ahmed Salik said he was with Okadigbo at the venue of the
Kano rally when the substance he described as "colourless, odourless and
pepperish in the eye" was sprayed on them. He stated that the chemical made
him (Salik) feel like fainting before he beat a retreat to board a bus where
he said he stayed till the end of the rally.
He said that prior to the spraying incident, the police had cordoned
every entrance to the venue, which caused a surge on the entrance before the
"chemical" was sprayed on the crowd.
Also explaining further during the briefing, a member of the ANPP House
caucus, Hon Ethelbert Okwaranyia said indication that what was sprayed on
Okadigbo was not ordinary teargas, was that the presidential candidate of
the party, General Muhammadu Buhari and Dr. Nnia Nwodo also nearly fainted
when they inhaled the same substance whose effect was different from that of
teargas.
The ANPP legislators therefore asked for a full scale inquiry into the
incident.
Salik who said the party was issuing the statement as a gift on the eve
of the independence day celebrations also congratulated Nigerians for
working for democracy but regretted that they were not getting any good deal
due to the style of administration by the PDP under President Olusegun
Obasanjo.
"The leadership of this country has since 1999 been the worst ever
witnessed on the surface of this earth. The leadership has been
characterised by ineptitude, incompetence, incoherence, abuse of power,
outright violation of the laws of the land as regards the constitution and
other Acts of the National Assembly.
"The leadership of this country has been non-performing, colourless,
lackluster and even primitive. While we live in the 21st century, our
leadership has remained essentially pre-historic with all the trappings of
antiquated megalomania. No thanks to bad leadership, Nigeria has woefully
failed to attain its full potentials of greatness", he lamented.
Meanwhile, the South-east Legislators and other politicians from the
zone will be meeting in Abuja Thursday to take a position over the death of
Okadigbo.
The Iguedo clan, Okadigbo's ancestral home, refers to the communities
said to be direct descendants of Iguedo, the only known daughter of "Eri,"
the progenitor of the Igbo race. It include such communities as Ogbunike,
(Okadigbo's home town) Umuleri, Aguleri, Awkuzu and Nteje. They also
domicile within the Anambra East, and West local governments as well as
Oyi.
The president general of Awkuzu community, Chief Frank Oramulu, who
spoke to newsmen last Sunday during the New Yam festival celebrated by the
community at the palace of the traditional ruler of the town, Igwe John
Nebolisa, said the spiritualists in the clan would invoke the spirit of
Okadigbo with a view to ascertaining who or what killed him.
Oramulu, a veteran labour leader and one-time chairman of the Peoples
Democratic Party (PDP) in Anambra State, said the decision to consult the
oracle was simply traditional.
"Every group of people has their own tradition, we will invoke his
(Okadigbo) spirit and do everything to find out those who killed him and I
tell you, they won't go unpunished," he said.
Oramulu was reacting to the insinuation that Okadigbo's death was
premeditated by the tear-gas cannisters directed at his face by the police
which he inhaled during the ANPP rally in Kano last Tuesday.
"You can't kill an Iguedo son. To kill an Iguedo son is not what you
do. We will go to the Edo mother and tell her that her "Ikenga" (staff of
authority) had been stolen. But if God called him, so be it," Oramulu
said.
Igwe Nebolisa who described Okadigbo's death as "most unfortunate,"
however, cautioned that the people should not mourn as if they had lost
every hope, since according to him, "many other Okadigbos abound in
Igboland."
However, the Anambra State government, in a statement yesterday signed
by Mr. Chuks Akunna, Chief Press Secretary to Governor Chris Ngige, said it
has decided to bear the burial expenses of the late Chuba Okadigbo because,
"the late Oyi was one of Nigeria's finest breed".
The statement quoted Ngige, as saying that even though Okadigbo belonged
to a different political party, the ANPP, the PDP led administration in
Anambra State would accord him all due respect because of his immense
contributions to society, particularly in the area of political
economy.
Ngige described the death of Okadigbo as a great loss to Nigeria and to
Anambra State in particular.
"We in Anambra State have lost a political icon. The political landscape
of Nigeria has been greatly altered by the passage of our great 'Oyi of
Oyi," Ngige said.
The Anambra State government stated that it would ensure the memories of
the late Okadigbo are preserved, that the state government would be
consulting the Okadigbo family at every stage of preparations for the
burial.
In its own reaction to Okadigbo's death, the Nigeria Labour Congress
(NLC) yesterday demanded that a high power judicial enquiry be set up to
probe the circumstances leading to the death. It noted that the police being
accused of complicity in the death has no moral authority to investigate
itself.
Also, the congress warned President Obasanjo against current attempts by
the government to silence opposition. Labour also noted that the current
effort to muzzle the opposition has the capacity to truncate the democratic
process.
Speaking yesterday at the opening ceremony of the Central Working
Committee meeting of the workers organisation, NLC president, Adams
Oshiomhole said the death of Okadigbo is a sad commentary in Nigeria
political history and that the issue raised by the incident must not be
allowed to be buried like it was done with similar deaths in the past.
The NLC president noted that whether or not a linkage exist between
Okadigbo death and police use of tear gas in the Kano rally, that the police
resorted to use of such instrument in disparsing a harmless rally, should be
a source of concern to all those who struggle for democracy.
"If a man who was a leader of the Senate should be treated in such a
manner as to inhale tear-gas then the country is in trouble. It is rather a
sad commentary in our history. Labour is worried by this
development. Indeed, all of us who struggled for democracy must feel
concerned about this.
"It is also worrisome that a rally which has former Head of State,
governors, former Senate Presi-dent could be teargassed," he said.
Oshiomhole noted that this was not the first time police has deployed
excessive use of teargas on citizens who want to register their opinion
about government policies and actions. He recalled the last fuel crises in
which protesters were molested. "By the time we were through with the
peaceful protest, we were counting the number of men lost."
Labour said proper inquiry into Okadigbo's death is inevitable to save
the country from future recurrence.
"Whether police use teargas or not, whether there is linkage between
Okadigbo's death and the use of teargas, we demand for a proper judicial
enquiry into this sad episode.
"And we do not see police doing a proper investigation. Indeed, it is
wrong for police to investigate themselves in a matter they are already a
party and which they have cleared themselves of any complicity. We must get
to the root of this matter," he said.
Meanwhile, the Okadigbo family yesterday formally announced the death of
the late politician.
The announcement signed by Pharaoh Okadigbo (Son) and Dr. Edmund
Okadigbo (Brother) for the entire Okadigbo family in Ogbunike, Oyi Local
Government of Anambra State, reads in part:
"The Rt. Hon. Dr. William CHUBA OKADIGBO passed on in Abuja on 25th
September 2003. He went the way he came, peacefully."

Original_Search_Title: EBA, Pounded Yam Revive Falcons
Headline: Nigeria: EBA, Pounded Yam Revive Falcons
Body_Text:
A GREAT deal of hope appear in the horizon for Nigeria, ahead of
Thursday's second Group-A match against host USA, as team officials have
moved to solve some of the problems facing the team here.
The girls are now being served African or Nigerian prepared dishes in
the light of earlier problems of food they faced in the hotel. The process
began Monday evening after FIFA acceded to a request by the Nigerian camp to
have a supply from outside the hotel which the players have so suddenly
appreciated.
"Now I can eat eba or pounded yam which I'm used to and no more those
petty things they served since we came," said defender Kikelomo Ajayi, soon
after taking the first meal Monday evening. "I'm better now. It tastes so
well to eat something I know would make me happy and give better energy. I
was near vomitting eating the same things they served," another central
defender Bunmi Kayode said shortly after the meal.
Players had objected to being served often, the continental meals by the
hotel, with many finding the food strange and a times, refusing to eat.
"Now, we are happy that one essential need has been solved for them. How
I wish we had this from the beginning," said Sunmonu Bello-Osagie, who is
with the team in camp.
Both captain Florence Omagbemi and attacker Mercy Akide brightened up
after having the first course of the local food they knew and Omagbemi said
it felt good that this will now be made continuous as per arrangement by
team officials.
"It's better than what we faced. Players are happier with this
arrangement and we appreciate it," she said.

Original_Search_Title: One Person Feared Dead As New Yam Celebrants Clash With Police
Headline: Nigeria: One Person Feared Dead As New Yam Celebrants Clash With Police
Body_Text:
Asaba — AT least one person died and several persons were injured when a group of Asaba youths celebrating their new yam festival clashed with the police. The main police station in the town popularly called the "A" Division was sacked and vandalized while 15 vehicles were smashed in the process. The police confirmed to reporters yesterday that they had arrested 19 people in connection with the bloody clash.
Vanguard gathered that a stray bullet hit a woman when police opened fire in a bid to disperse the rampaging youths who came out in their numbers.
Addressing newsmen in Asaba yesterday, the State Commissioner of Police, Mr. Charles Akaya said "about 5.30pm youths in their thousands marched through Nnebisi road of Asaba while performing the grand finale of their new yam festival. In the process, they unleashed mayhem on innocent road users", adding that "since the beginning of the festival on 15th September, 2003 there have been reported cases of assault of people in and around Umuaji especially women usually between the hours of 7 and 8.30pm.
"These youths", the CP went on, "while marching towards Traffic Light (area) went into Police Works Department and picked some iron rods. When challenged by policemen on duty they vandalized the louvres of offices at "A" Division (police station) and damaged 15 vehicles."
He added, "after being dispersed, they met a police Inspector on their way and dispossessed him of his uniform and also set ablaze a 504 car belonging to the Ministry of Economic Planning. Nineteen persons were arrested for investigation", the Police Commissioner stated.
Akaya further warned members of the public that "the Public Order Act is still in force which demands that procession into the streets should be on police permit. It has been a common incident that during festivals in Asaba, residents are treated to violence", pointing out that "this situation will not be tolerated any longer as offenders and sponsors shall be treated as common criminals."
Commenting on the Okwe clash, the Commissioner said that "on 20th September 2003 at 10.30 am, some youths of Umuaji armed with various dangerous weapons such as guns and machetes went into Okwe and damaged some buildings and a pick up van with registration number AM 295 LND driven by one Emmanuel Ananaro was commandeered and used throughout their rampage which they abandoned while escaping.
"This", the CP said, "is a clear case of armed robbery in addition to malicious damages. They (suspects) will be located, arrested and prosecuted as such."
The Police Commissioner who told reporters that he personally came out when the youths stormed Okwe which is close to his residence to ensure that the situation was quickly brought under control added that Emmanuel, the owner of the pick up commandeered by the rampaging youths "is with us and helping us in our investigation."
Igbinedion laments deductions of $145.7m debt owed by private firm
By Emma Amaize
BENIN CITY- GOVERNOR Lucky Igbinedion of Edo State has described as unacceptable, a situation where deductions are made from the state's allocation monthly for the $145.7 million foreign debt incurred by the Bendel Feed and Flour Mills, Ewu, which had been privatized. He told Vanguard recently in Benin City that some private individuals were feeding fat from the company while the State Government and people of Edo State who received no dividends were bearing the brunt.
"We cannot accept this in a democratic dispensation. Actually, it was when we discovered that some huge amounts up to N200 million was being deducted from our allocation every month that we decided to investigate and we traced part of the deduction to a debt said to have been incurred by the company," he said.
"I will tell you that I was personally shocked at this scandal and the government is already addressing this hideous distortion which the operations of the Bendel Feeds and Flour Mill is causing the people of the state," he asserted.
The state Commissioner for Finance, Mr. Bright Omokhodion who shed more light on the matter stated that "this is the most stunning and excruciating of all the debts the state is said to be owing. Available records showed that the company, established by the State Government some years ago got an export credit guarantee of 1.08 million Australian Shillings and a commercial credit of 508.4 million Austrian shillings millions as at then."
He explained that "going by the Central Bank circular (Pr -- Spem transaction guideline of February 1988) by which all transactions (exchange rate) was to convert at 0.0390 to a Naira, the total of 1.5 billion Austrian Shillings amounted to N72 million but the Federal Government in 1991, abrogated the settlement rate, which was a concession to the industries for repayment.
"At this time," he observed, "the company was yet to start repaying the loan and because of the abrogation which meant no concession to the industries and increasing exchange rate the money started taking geometric flight."
Omokhodion asserted that "by the time the company was privatized, that was before the Igbinedion administration came into office, the settlement rate was not taken into cognizance and its indebtedness was calculated on the Central Bank rate that was abrogated in 1991." He said that those who bought over the company were now enjoying at the expense of the people of the state.
Vanguard learnt that the share structure of the company may be radically altered at the end of the day as the government was not prepared to shoulder the debt burden of a company that is in private hands or the Federal Government would be asked to shift the responsibility of paying the debt to the company. The State Government, it was learnt might even ask for the refund of the money deducted from its allocation from the company.

Original_Search_Title: Wabara, Ogbuewu, Ogunlewe to Attend Arochukwu Festival
Headline: Nigeria: Wabara, Ogbuewu, Ogunlewe to Attend Arochukwu Festival
Body_Text:
Lagos — Senate President, Adolphus Wabara, Minister of Culture and Tourism,
Hon. Franklin Ogbuewu, Minister of Works, Senator Adeseye Ogunlewe and other
dignitaries are billed to attend the grand finale of the 2003 Ikeji
Arochukwu Festival.
National Publicity Secretary of Nzuko Arochukwu, Mr. Ernie Onwumere,
said the ceremony would hold on Friday, at the Amaikpe Square, Obinkita
village, Arochukwu at 1;30 pm, to mark the final new yam festival for the
Igbo. It will feature cultural exhibitions that cut across Igbo the
nation.
"This is the day we come to celebrate culture dances and thanking God
for protecting us throughout the year. The celebration is to mark the end of
a season and the beginning of another. More than 250 communities of
Arochukwu are expected to come together for the celebration of the event. It
is more of a home coming", he said.
He said in 2002 when Aro people commemorated the British occupation of
Arochukwu between 1901-1902, many tourist attractions were opened up and
these would be open to visitors in the grand finale where visitors would be
visiting as a remembrance.

Original_Search_Title: Surinamese "Homecomers" At Elmina
Headline: Ghana: Surinamese "Homecomers" At Elmina
Body_Text:
A delegation of 102 Surinamese, on a "home-coming visit" have been honoured at a grand durbar at the Elmina Castle.
The Surinamese Minister of Transport and Communication, Mr. Guno Castelen and the Minister of Regional Integration, Mr. Romeo Van Russel, are leading a delegation that is made up of businessmen and entrepreneurs.
The visitors are to "reconnect with their roots and to commemorate the 140th anniversary of the abolition of slavery."
They were served with mashed yam with boiled eggs and were taken through the slave dungeons of the castle, as part of the welcome ceremony.
The District Chief Executive for Komenda-Edina-Eguafo-Abrem, Nana Ato Arthur, reminded Africans in the Diaspora to share their expertise with Ghanaians and to invest in the country.
"As brothers and sisters you should find time to return to your roots and share your experience and achievements with us", he said.
Nana Arthur briefed the delegation about a cultural heritage and management programme being initiated to facilitate the development of the district and called on the private sector, traditional councils, sister city linkages, especially those in the Diaspora, to help in its implementation.
Mr. Van Russel, a member of the delegation, thanked the people of Elmina for the warm reception and said Surinamese and Ghanaians are the same, but divided as a result of slavery.
He said the delegation will build and strengthen the new linkage it has recovered and ensure that it yields positive results for the two countries.
Nana Kodwo Condua VI, Omanhene of Edina Traditional Area, described the visit as a "spiritual and family reunion" that should serve as an avenue for the unification of Africans on the continent and those in the Diaspora.

Original_Search_Title: Warri War Hijacked by Criminals - Bozimo
Headline: Nigeria: Warri War Hijacked by Criminals - Bozimo
Body_Text:
Benin City — MINISTER of Police Affairs, Chief Broderick Bozimo says the Warri war
has not ended because the legitimate struggle of the people was hijacked by
charlatans, criminals, oil bunkerers, kidnappers and all manners of
people.
He told newsmen in Benin City, weekend, that "if not for the police and
other security agents, that area would probably not be part of Nigeria now
because these people are just doing whatever they like."
Chief Bozimo stated that every time the government and leaders of the
warring ethnic groups succeeded in restoring a semblance of peace to the
troubled city, "these people with vested interests, the fifth columnists,
start it all over with deliberate burning of houses and killing of the
people."
He cited an instance with the recent meeting of the entire Ijaw
leadership at Yenagoa, the Bayelsa State capital, attended by the Minister
of Science and Technology, Prof. Isown, Minister of Transport, Senator
Seikibo, himself, the Bayelsa and Delta state governors, all notable Ijaw
traditional rulers and youths.
Chief Bozimo stated that soon after the meeting where it was pointed out
that the struggle had misfired and there would be no more fight, the fifth
columnists went on a destruction spree.
He said that the "Itsekiri, Urhobo and Ijaw do not hate themselves as
the criminals want to portray them," adding "if we are not a mad people and
I know we are not mad, is it not obvious that the burning and killing of
ourselves is a case of us cutting our noses to spite our faces, if we are
not idiots and I know we are not, is it possible for us to go the way we are
going when others are building."
His words, "most of the houses burnt in Warri belong to people who have
no idea what the war is all about. In the swamps and villages, old women and
men who do not know what local government means, who have not left their
homes for centuries are killed."
Chief Bozimo stated that Ijaw, Itsekiri and Urhobo leaders were worried
by the hijack of the struggle as "even though, politically, we have
divergent views, but that our views are divergent do not mean we should go
and burn ourselves. It is the fifth columnists, the oil bunkerers who
benefit when we fight and when two goats are fighting, the third goat will
carry the yam and run away."

Original_Search_Title: The Marriage in Minna
Headline: Nigeria: The Marriage in Minna
Body_Text:
From all indications, Aisha Babangida was not the only bride in Minna
last Saturday. Nor was her lucky husband, Bashir Garba the only eligible
groom at the occasion. Brides and grooms of various shapes and hues were out
aplenty at the Babangidas' talk-of-the-town wedding.
Society weddings attract all kinds of people for all kinds of
reasons. Well-wishers, stargazers, onlookers, busybodies, freeloaders,
pickpockets, jilted rivals, irate lovers, hen-pecked husbands, distraught
wives, anxious spinsters, scheming bachelors, etc all have their place at a
big wedding, adding colour and glamour and possibly, fresh gossip
angles. Each comes with a mission that may or may not have anything to do
with the man and woman being joined in wedlock.
So, it should not come as a surprise that there seemed to have been more
than a social dimension to the well-attended event in Minna at which a
Babangida girl and an Abacha boy were joined together as man and wife. Let's
face it, there was no way that a wedding ceremony involving the child of
former President Ibrahim Babangida would have been solely or even mainly a
social event devoted simply to joining two young lovebirds in holy
matrimony. Given the emerging atmospherics of this interesting season, it
was clear even to the blind that this wedding ceremony was not so much about
matrimony as it is about politics.
For good or for ill, General Babangida is today seen by many as the man
to beat at the 2007 Presidential election. A big social event like his
giving of a child away in marriage was bound to attract all manner of
politicians.
Expectedly, many of them, cast in the mold of beautiful brides on the
lookout for a suitable groom, went to the event in the hope of striking some
interesting political liaisons. The prize catch, of course, was the father
of the bride. And many hopeful brides-to-be went out to Minna to display
their political ware last Saturday, in the expectant anticipation that they
could catch the fancy of the much-sought-after prime suitor. The spectacle
recalls a custom in my mother's village in the Urratta area of Owerri in Imo
State. Big occasions such as the New Yam festival, the coronation of a new
chief, Christmas or Easter often attract a lot of people back home from
wherever they might be sojourning. Many usually come home with the riches
that are emblems of their successes in the lands of their sojourn. Some also
come home to find suitable "unspoilt" brides with whom to start a home, as
families get anxious that their wards should not fall into the predatory
hands of big city girls. In the event, a practice soon developed amongst
mothers eager to find suitable suitors for their daughters.
What they do is that during these festivals, when the village would be
filled with all the nouveaux arrive, mothers would deck their daughters out
in their best apparels and take them on endless parades of the village,
under the guise of felicitating with neighbours. My grandmother once noted
with wry humour that such perambulations would often lead into the
homesteads of families with eligible but unattached sons. Here, mother and
daughter would linger for a while under one pretext or the other, or in some
cases mother would leave the daughter behind to return home to attend to
some urgent business or chitchat with the intended groom's mother. In the
interim, some kind of rapport is meant to develop between boy and girl. Many
interesting liaisons, not all of them leading to marriage, are known to have
resulted from these parades. Quite a few have ended in bitter quarrels and
hot family feuds, especially in cases where the male party in question stood
accused of leading the lady on by "obtaining under false pretence". In some
cases where matters get out of hand, aggrieved families are known to resort
to the desperate expediency of shotgun weddings, often with unpredictable
results. Politics is usually about marriages of convenience. And it does
appear that the season of political marriages is now upon us once again as
people gear up for the next phase of the battle for the Nigerian
presidency. The marriage in Minna was thus largely about pairing up - brides
out in search of suitors; presidential aspirants in search of vice
presidential hopefuls; and vice versa.
The curious thing about the event, though, is that the real brides
failed to show up. Of course all manner of pretenders and interlopers were
out in full flight, struggling to be noticed and courted. Some of the
defeated Alliance for Democracy, AD, governors belong in this category. They
should be quite conversant with yet another marriage-related custom, this
time from Yorubaland.
People familiar with how traditional engagement ceremonies used to be
conducted amongst the Yoruba would recall the playful drama that often
ensued at such events. After the groom's family had stated their mission,
the bride's people are required to produce the bride so that the groom would
certify that that indeed was the lady he sought. Often, the bride's family
would produce all manner of people covered in veil - from wizened old women
to heavily pregnant housewives and even infants. Creative families have been
known to produce men dressed up as women. Of course, the groom's family
would refuse to accept these unlikely "brides" until the genuine material
was produced, usually after a lot of toing and froing, and some disguised
extortion.
So where were the real brides last Saturday? They were in Ota having a
meeting with the President, who increasingly might turn out to be the most
critical matchmaker in this game as the Yoruba decide whether to go to Minna
or Yola, or neither.
The message sent by the absence of the Odu'a governors from Minna cannot
be lost on the initiated, including the father of the bride. The message is
clear: hold your territory and let those interested in your support come to
you to ask for it. Don't go offering yourself. That could smack of begging,
or worse, prostitution. Those who are now busy trying to use Babangida to
even scores with Obasanjo over the outcome of the last elections had better
be warned. They are about to miscalculate again. The man in Minna knows the
difference between genuine and fake brides.
As for Vice President Atiku Abubakar, Babangida's presumed main rival
for 2007, the work that needs to be done keeps piling up by the day. Does
Atiku have marriageable daughters? We ask this question because it seems he
needs to stage his own show very soon. Now we hear another of Babangida's
children, this time his first son, is set to marry in December in Maiduguri,
right in the heart of the North East that is said to be Atiku's
stronghold. What does that tell you? Sounds to me like carrying the battle
into the enemy's territory. For sure, things can only get more
interesting. May God grant us long life and the good health to witness it
all.

Original_Search_Title: Volta NPP Power Game Ends With Much Disenchantment
Headline: Ghana: Volta NPP Power Game Ends With Much Disenchantment
Body_Text:
Hohoe — It was a congress with a difference. There were no banners in the town
to, at least, inform people about what function it was, no invitation to the
media and no radio announcements were made.
But there were buses loads of bespectacled macho men. And many District
Chief Executives in the region- the good, bad and questionable- all showed
up from far and near; while yam sellers and other wayside traders living
right there enquired from pressmen what the drumming and horn tooting were
all about. They apparently had no prior information. For those who were
fortunate to get the programme, the venue indicated was E.P. Secondary
School, even though they were right in the premises of a teacher training
college.
Such was the organization of the Volta regional delegates conference of
the New Patriotic Party (NPP), which took place at the St. Theresa's
Training College here at Hohoe last Saturday, that the enthusiasm that was
ignited earlier in the day quickly evaporated like a morning dew.
Of course there were also allegations of fat white envelopes,
reminiscent of the black polyethylene bags described by Dr. Charles
Wereko-Brobby, the step aside Volta River Authority (VRA) Chief Executive,
at the congress grounds.
But at the end of the day, Mr. Lord Commey, National Organizer of the
NPP , managed to return to Accra with a list of nine names under his armpit
for all his "toil", thanks to the 'cooperation' of Mr. Atta Yeboah, Deputy
Regional Director ,of the Electoral Commission (EC).
And at the end of the day-long exercise, the 'big nine' names that came
up as the new regional executive of the party, have come to seal (at least
for now), the political power game, with its ethnocentric connotations, that
had rocked the region in recent times.
Mr. Emmanuel Ken-Wuud Nuworsu. finally emerged as the substantive
Regional Chairman, massively defeating his only remaining opponent, M
Emmanuel K. Asidigbe. Other new executive members are Christian Kofi Glakpe
(1st vice chairman), Madam Nancy Tsiportu (2nd vice), Emmanuel Elvis
Mesre-Boahene (secretary), Ken Ayim (assistant secretary), Nana Okyere
Mantey (treasurer), Johnson Avulete (organizer), Madam Grace Mensah (women
organizer) and John Reagan Tepretu (youth organizer).
Suspiciously, all those who won the elections were conspicuously
positioned at the bottom of each ballot paper.
This raised a lot of eyebrows in the hall. However, the disenchantment,
as noted by "The Chronicle", had more to do with the naked abuse of
incumbency and power than the poor hospitality accorded the observers,
activists, police and media men; as not even sachet water was served all
through the twelve hours of heat, sweat and noise that engulfed the assembly
hall.
At a point, one of the contestants for the chairmanship position,
Mr. Samuel Tsahey, felt he had no option than to announce his withdrawal
from the race, just minutes before voting was billed to have begun.
He cited irregularities like manipulation of the delegates' list,
saying, "The whole business was being handled by anonymous people". Indeed,
so glaring were some of the accusations that not even Lord Commey's
oratorical skills could not disabuse the minds of many a participant in the
hall.
When later The Chronicle contacted him for his views, Mr. Tsahey
explained that the list of 80 delegates who were to vote had been hidden
from him, even though he applied for it long ago; and alleged that the ones
for Ho West, Hohoe North and many other constituencies were compiled a few
minutes to voting time.
"The NPP in the region has become a private company; owned by Tommy
Amematekpor and run by Ken-wuud Nuworsu", he charged.
Mr. Tsahey argued that since Nuworsu, the incumbent, was also a
candidate, it was not prudent on the part of the national executive to have
given him the job of compiling the list of delegates; adding that there
could have been a committee for that job instead.
As if to corroborate the claims, another activist, from Nkwanta
constituency, (name withheld), said he heard Mr. Seshie Torblu, executive
member of Ho central, shouting that anyone who contested against Nuworsu
would get only 18 votes.
True to his word, Emmanuel L.Asidigbe, who vied with him, polled 18; an
indication, (according to the young man), that the game had been played in
the boardroom long before it was staged in the open.
On his part, Mr. Nuworsu refuted all the allegations leveled against
him.
He said he was not responsible for the compilation; neither did he hide
it from anyone.
"Ask anybody, it was the constituency chairmen who brought their own
lists to the regional office; and we forwarded them to the electoral
commission", he stressed.
He also contended that the EC official was simply following the list he
brought from Ho, and that there was no way he could have changed it at the
congress grounds.
Other guests of honour present were Hons. Kwasi Owusu Yeboah and Kofi
Dzamesi, Regional and Deputy Ministers respectively, Mustafa Hameed,
National Youth Organizer, Alhajis Maiga and Tanko, both of the Nasara Club,
Mr. Obed Okujeto, patron, and Mrs Agnes Okujeto.
In another development, a petition, filed against Nuworsu by Dr. Samuel
Ohene. Party member with number 0086226 is likely to throw a spanner into
the wheel of events in the near future.
The petition dated August 27, this year, and copied to the National
Chairman, Secretary and Council of Elders argued that recent publications in
"The Heritage" and "Independent" newspapers were so serious that they should
be looked into before he was allowed to stand.
According to Dr. Ohene, the same allegations, as published, have been
repeated to him directly by known NADMO employees. He said he was also aware
that the Serious Fraud Office, SFO, has completed investigations into a case
in which the new Regional Chairman and NADMO Regional Director, sold many
application forms and charged fees for them.
"The whole party will be exposed to ridicule and contempt when these
issues become subjects of criminal or civic litigation very soon- as I
believe they surely will", the petition stated.
Although the petition was dated some three clear days ahead of the
congress it is not known whether the authorities received it to consider the
contents. But whatever the case may be the concerns raised can have serious
political implications if the charges were true. What is left now is for a
litigant to prey on the "golden opportunity".

Original_Search_Title: For a Just Reform
Headline: Nigeria: For a Just Reform
Body_Text:
Lagos — THE recent decision by the National Council of State to set up a
technical committee to recommend ways by which the bastardized local
government system in Nigeria could be reformed and restructured, is no
doubt, an idea well conceived. However, in keeping with the maxim that "he
who comes to equity, must come with clean hands", this writer firmly
believes that the Obasanjo-led government should be able to convince
Nigerians at the end of the day that he and indeed his party - the
"almighty" Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), are not going to use the
so-called reforms of the local governments to play further on the already
ravaged collective psyche and intelligence of the Nigerian populace.
The growing fear of a hidden agenda that belie the so-called reforms
cannot be swept under the carpet, especially when one looks back into what
the local government system has suffered in the hands of the thirty-six
state governors in the country since the expiration of the first fourth
public dispensation in May 2002. It is story replete with bastardization,
frustration and hopelessness. You need to see chairmanship and
councillorship aspirants in the country who have been endlessly tossed,
dribbled and siphoned, and you begin to wonder why those innocent Nigerians
are subjected to such inhuman and ungodly treatments.
It is an incontestable truism that the problem with the local government
system is not caused by non-performance by elected council authorities but
it is a product of the over-bloated selfishness of power-hungry and
money-conscious state governors who have constituted themselves colossuses
in our political landscape. The deliberate shift of elections into the 774
councils in the country in preference for Transition Committees, is a case
study of the governor's blatant display of greed and avarice. This, no
doubt, puts to question the sincerity of purpose of the state governors and
their cohorts at the States' Electoral Commissions.
Unfortunately, neither the States' Houses of Assembly - bodies
constitutionally charged with the direct legislation over the Councils - nor
the Obasanjo-led Council of State that has just woken from its slumber,
could ask questions, intervene or constitutionally stop the governors from
making further caricature of the local government system. But what do you
expect from most Houses of Assembly that have become stooges and toothless
bulldogs in the hands of state governors? Does the kettle call the pot
black? Or, would Obasanjo swear that he does not know before now the
illegalities that have bedeviled the local government system? Who is
cheating who?
The claim in some quarters that non-performance on the part of Council
Chairmen is the cause of the planned reforms, does not hold any water. The
non-performance in the Council areas is unarguably caused by our state
governors. While this writer does not intend to hold brief for the Council
Chairmen, after all they are no saints, it remains obvious that the
large-scale corruption going on in our local government areas is
masterminded by the state governors who have seen in the local governments,
veritable avenues for siphoning public funds. In fact, in some states, the
local governments are extended ministries and parastatals that are at the
mercy of wolves in the garb of governors. To say that the governors see in
the local government councils, veritable goldmines, is not an
overstatement.
Whoever mooted the idea of Joint Account for states and local
governments. The so-called state and local governments' joint account system
serves as the final deathblow on the financial independence of the local
government system. Elected authorities of the local government areas are now
at the mercy of the governors who play god on them. Yes, agreed that section
169 of the 1999 constitution as amended in 2001 provides in subsection 6 for
the running of a "State Joint Local Government Account", the same
constitution, in sub-sections 7 and 8, empowers the states to distribute the
amounts standing to the credit of the local governments accordingly.
But what do we witness in our states? - a brazen display of executive
recklessness. The truth is that our governors have gone-haywire and no one
appears ready to stop them. They appear to be wielding enormous powers in
the control of the finances of the local governments such that an
institutionalized confederation of looting appears to be taking place. Yet,
the respected Indian nationalist, Jawaharlal Nehru had forewarned that "evil
unchecked grows, evil tolerated poisons the whole system." Who should
therefore, blame Council Chairmen if they emulate their "looting
masters".
Before the expiration of the tenure of the immediate past local
government councils, attempts were made in some states by council
legislators to remove council chairmen found to have violated constitutional
provisions but their efforts proved abortive, no thanks to strong backings
received by this offenders from their "partners in crime-governors". Yet,
any keen followers of events in the local governments will agree with this
writer that capital projects, and indeed meaningful developmental
programmes, stopped in so many councils as soon as the so-called joint
account system came into operation. The governors proverbially hold the
knife and the yam. They decide which local government area gets what. The
rest gets into their private pockets and businesses.
This writer happens to come from one of the local government areas in
Abia State where in the past one year or thereabout, the local government
authority has not embarked on any capital project. The very last capital
project executed by the local government council was the one done by the
elected council chairman whose tenure ended in May 2002.
Restructuring or reforming the local government system is not a bad
idea. However, methinks that the reformation should start first by taking
away the management of the council from the state governors. If the councils
cannot be made autonomous then, they should be returned to the Federal
Government for proper management. In addition, the Joint Account System
should be abolished as the aim has been defeated. Allocations should come
directly to the councils from the federal government via the Revenue
Mobilization, Allocation and Fiscal Commission. The governors are hawks and
wolves, who see in the Councils, ready preys to feed on. They should no
longer be allowed to manage our local government areas.
The National Assembly on the other hand, should be guided by national
interest, put the Supreme Court ruling at the back of their mind and give
appropriate legal backings to the afore-mentioned suggestions. Again, there
is nothing wrong with the 1976 reforms and the Ibrahim Babangida
modifications. Any attempt to scrap the council areas will not only be
retrogressive but will certainly breed uncontrollable social chaos.
Imah wrote in from Mbala-Isuochi, Abia State.

Original_Search_Title: Banks And Sierra Leone Border Reopen
Headline: Liberia: Banks And Sierra Leone Border Reopen
Body_Text:
Monrovia — Small signs are emerging that life in the Liberian capital Monrovia is
beginning to return to normal following the signing of a peace agreement on
18 August aimed at ending 14 years of civil war.
Banks have begun to reopen for the first time in nearly three months and
the government is talking of reopening schools on October 1.
Overland travel to Sierra Leone has also become possible for the first
time in six months, even though this involves a precarious 90 km trip
through territory controlled by the Liberians United for Reconciliation and
Democracy (LURD) rebel movement.
The main border crossing at Bo Waterside reopened to pedestrians last
Saturday, although it remains closed to vehicles for the time being.
Commercial banks in Monrovia closed on 6 June as the Liberians United
for Reconciliation and Democracy (LURD) rebel movement launched the first of
three attacks on the capital. However, the state-owned Liberian Bank for
Development and Investment reopened its doors this week following the
signing of a peace agreement on 18 August.
Information Minister Reginald Goodridge told IRIN the Central Bank of
Liberia (CBL) would reopen on Monday. The two other main banks in Monrovia,
Ecobank and Tradevco Bank and International Bank, were expected to follow
suit soon after, he added.
The closure of the banks hampered what little commercial activity could
still continue after LURD overran the northwestern suburbs of Monrovia and
pounded the city centre with mortar bombs and rockets. Relief organisations
were forced to fly in large quantities of dollars in cash to continue paying
their staff and buy in essential services.
Education ministry officials told UN and relief agency representatives
on Wednesday they were hoping to resume normal school classes on October 1,
two weeks before acting president Moses Blah hands over power to a new
broad-based transitional government.
However, the reopening of schools, most of which have been turned into
temporary shelters for thousands of people displaced from their homes by the
recent fighting in Monrovia, has not yet been officially announced.
Aid workers quoted education ministry officials as saying that schools
would be reopened in phases because of the still volatile security
situation. Those in Monrovia, where the presence of a West African
peacekeeping force affords better public safety, would be the first to
resume classes.
The bridge over the Mano river to Sierra Leone at Bo Waterside was
reopened to pedestrians even though the territory between the frontier and
Monrovia remains firmly under LURD control.
Jacques Klein, the UN special envoy to Liberia met Sierra Leonean
President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah in Freetown earlier this week to ask that the
border be reopened to vehicle traffic as well to facilitate humanitarian
access.
The United Nations is keen to bring food and other supplies into Liberia
overland from Sierra Leone and would like to resume the repatriation of the
13,500 remaining Sierra Leonean refugees in the country by road.
Until now the refugees have been repatriated by sea. But the
Guinea-registered ferry that was being used, the MV Overbeck, suffered
engine failure at sea on Sunday, shortly after leaving Monrovia with nearly
200 people on board. It was finally towed into Freetown on Thursday after
drifting on the high seas for five days.
Sierra Leone closed its border with Liberia in February after LURD
fighters seized control of all of western Liberia, pushing several
demoralised hundred government troops into Sierra Leone to seek refuge.
But last Saturday border guards began allowing pedestrians to cross the
international bridge freely in both directions on Saturday.
An IRIN correspondent who visited Bo Waterside on Thursday was told by
Liberian and Sierra Leonean officials that more than 500 Liberian refugees
had returned to Liberia since then.
At least 75 came home on Thursday carrying mattresses and other
personnel belongings.
Varney Tombekai, one of the returnees, told IRIN: "I am very happy to
return home. It is better to live in your country than being a refugee. I
can now see peace in Liberia. "He said many of the Liberian refugees
sheltering in the nearby Sierra Leonean towns of Zimmi and Jenema were eager
to return.
Liberia's northern border with Guinea remains inaccessible from Monrovia
because of recent fighting near the LURD-held town of Gbarnga which
straddles the main road to Guinea. However, many people have begun to
transit through Sierra Leone to Guinea instead.
On Thursday, a few Liberians were simply crossing into Sierra Leone to
purchase food and other commodities like soap, milk, and sugar among
others.
"Thank God, we can now do our businesses again. I am appealing to the
Sierra Leonean government to open the borders to cars so we can bring more
goods and food to Liberia and our people can visit some of their relatives
inside Sierra Leone", a female trader said.
Food remains scarce in the LURD-controlled towns and villages along the
90 km road between Monrovia and the Sierra Leone border. Several villagers
told IRIN they were living on yams and cassava growing wild in the bush, but
these were becoming difficult to find.
Relief agencies are gradually establishing a presence in the
area. Medecins Sans Frontieres has opened a mobile clinic in the town of
Tubmanburg, 60 km northwest of Monrovia and the UN children's Fund (UNICEF)
has resumed the immunisation of children in the area.
UNICEF and its partners have mounted a seven-day campaign targetting
42,000 children, who have had no access to immunization facilities since
February. Many of them have been displaced with their families to Tubmanburg
from other parts of western Liberia.

Original_Search_Title: KCC Razes House Built in Wetland
Headline: Uganda: KCC Razes House Built in Wetland
Body_Text:
Kampala — KAMPALA City Council (KCC) has demolished a building in Bugolobi to halt
encroachment on the Nakivubo swamp, which filters wastewater before it
enters Lake Victoria.
KCC law enforcement department and the Police also arrested the site
engineer, Waliggo. He was charged in a city court. The building was earlier
razed twice.
Phoebe Gubya, the KCC environment officer yesterday said Nakawa division
carried out the demolition of the house without her endorsement.
She said a recent ministerial directive to protect Kampala's vital
swamps could not be executed because it did not provide for compensation of
the land and house owners.
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner in-charge of the Wetlands
Inspection Division (WID), blamed KCC for laxity in enforcing the law. He
said KCC continued to approve building plans in the wetlands.
"We will break down more houses. But we have to begin by discouraging
new encroachment on the swamp," Mafabi said
He said plans were underway to gazette the Nakivubo swamp as a protected
area for purification of water.
Mafabi said the swamp was being destroyed by industries, human
settlements and growers of yams.
The new swamp boundaries have been adjusted to favour rich property
owners who have encroached on the natural boundaries of the swamp at
Bugolobi.
He said the Nakivubo swamp had suffered encroachment over the years and
its distance from the lake has declined from 4.8 kilometres to three. "If
nothing is done, we shall soon be mining water from filth because Ggaba
Water Works is three kilometres from Murchison bay," Mafabi said.

Original_Search_Title: Labour Leader Commits Suicide in Ado-Ekiti
Headline: Nigeria: Labour Leader Commits Suicide in Ado-Ekiti
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Tragedy struck in Ado-Ekiti weekend when the Chairman of the newly
inaugurated Trade Union Congress (TUC) in Ekiti State, Mr Olu Johnson,
allegedly committed suicide by hanging.
Mr Johnson who was the deputy director of the Public Complaints
Commission in Ekiti State, was sworn in along with four other officers last
Tuesday to run the affairs of TUC, an alternative union to the Nigeria
Labour Congress (NLC).
The new chairman, according to reports, hanged himself inside his
bedroom in the early hours of Saturday after eating a meal of pounded yam
prepared by his wife.
Late Johnson, a father of six was living in a flat around Omolayo area
of Ado-Ekiti with his second wife, after he divorced his first wife who had
four children for him.
THISDAY gathered that the deceased on the fateful day had kept the rope
used to draw water from the house well in a secret place and later sneaked
into the bedroom to hang himself with it, when his wife and children were
having their bath.
The wife on returning to the bedroom after her bath found the dangling
body of her husband on the rope tied to the ceiling fan. It was gathered
that the police were immediately contacted after which the body was removed
and rushed to the state Special Hospital, Ado-Ekiti where he was confirmed
dead.
Several posers have been raised over what could have pushed the Deputy
Director who is presently on level 15 to hang himself.
None seemed to offer satisfactory explanation for the Ado Ekiti tragedy;
as suicide was a very uncommon crime in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Caging Anti-Democracy Friends
Headline: Nigeria: Caging Anti-Democracy Friends
Body_Text:
Benin-City — Once again, Nigeria_s countless efforts at cultivating a stable, civil democratic rule is heading for the crucibles. The indicators for this avoidable looming abortion of democracy are familiar. In the past, these indicators conspired to rub this emerging great nation of good governance, democratic rule, social justice and enforcement of citizens_ fundamental human rights. Indeed, the force of poverty, marginalization, corruption, arrogance of power, lawlessness, hunger, violence, thuggery and assassination are again roving the political airwaves, and leaving on the tracks ominous signals, which if not addressed in the true spirit of social justice and the rule of law are capable of jeopardising the sustainability of democracy in the country.
It is against this background that every stakeholder in the polity has a self-imposed responsibility to call the Ngigegate scandal by its very name; civilian coup which is a treasonable felony. Such grievous crime against the on going democratisation process should not be swept under the carpet, without recourse to the rule of law and the spirit and letters of the constitution and ensure the safeguard of the rule of law and not the rule of man or rule of some pristine crude and uncultured godfathers, it behoves on the State to immediately arrest and prosecute all the suspects in the failed coup attempt to force Dr. Christopher Ngige out his office as the Executive Governor of Anambra State.
The urgent need to prosecute all those involved directly in the civilian coup attempt is not necessarily for the purpose of identifying the victors and the victims in the _Ngigegate scandal_ but for the sole purpose of establishing the fact that Nigeria as in the rest parts of the world where decent humanity lives, it is the rule of law that prevails and not the rule of man. It is for this objective re-establishing the fact that rule of law governs humanity that the Nigerian State together with the rest of society should insist on enforcing the law and arraigning all suspects in that saga before a competent law court as specified by the constitution. To do otherwise is to encourage subterfuge and lay the groundwork for the subversion of the fourth republic, and by extension the democratisation process.
Thus, the recent declaration by Chief Audu Ogbeh, the National Chairman of the ruling Peoples' Democratic Party that the dramatis personae allegedly linked to the failed coup in Awka, who the party had earlier expelled from its fold can be pardoned and re-absorbed into the party as soon as they apologise for their alleged crime against the Nigerian State is rather begging the question, and indeed very unfortunate. The PDP and its leadership stand to garner flourishing credibility if the rule of law prevails on this matter, and surely the party and its leadership will be helping to build a virile, credible and formidable democratic Nigeria. It is not a matter of some persons, who have money, playing godfatherly roles in the business of politics. There is nothing absolutely wrong in godfatherism in any secular venture. Even in the advanced democracies of the world, i.e. the United States, Britain, Canada, Germany, France etc, political gladiators do not fly in from the sky. They are products of the societal melee that create those who have mega bugs, who sufficiently generate interest in politics, which is the art and science of the sharing and allocation of societal collective resources. And so, for the common good of the godfather, the godson or goddaughter and the entire society, godfatherism blossom to recruit and create new political (ruling) elites, who then occupy positions of authorities, when they become holders of the knives and the yams. So, really, the issue is not godfatherism as such. The issue in the Anambra political debacle is that desperadoes on both sides of the divide are involved in a ruffor-ruffor fight for the soul of Anambra state. These desperadoes, in the process of sorting themselves out infringe on the constitution and laws of the body-polity. This is the crux of the matter. The law should take it course. And let heavens fall.
The advanced democracies are where they are today because of the high premium given to their rule of law. When the rule is on course, societal miscreants and deviants are likely to be caged and restrained. But where the rule of man or group prevails, the contradictions are bound to explode as soon as possible to endanger the entire society.
To therefore treat the matter with kid gloves, and claim that it is a PDP affairs is turning logic on its head, and consequently, endangering the nascent democracy. AIG Raphael Ige, Chris Uba, and all identified actors in that show of shame on July 10, 2003 in Anambra State should be prosecuted. The Nigerian State needs to do this to reassure the world and the entire humanity that civilized, cultured and enlightened people live in Nigeria. To demonstrate that yes, we may have missed the opportunity in the past to democratise but now we are surely on course and there is no holding us back. In this efforts to democratise the Nigerian polity in all facets, there cannot be sacred cows, and halleluja people who do not care about patriotism and nationalism. These guys are simply unfriendly friends within the polity. They need to be sanctioned.

Original_Search_Title: Silver Medal Family
Headline: Nigeria: Silver Medal Family
Body_Text:
Lagos — SOLDIERING is associated with brewery and discipline even as its
practitioners are known as strong defenders of territorial boundaries. But
despite these good characteristics, people still have a way they look at
solders: perhaps with a touch of scorn, contempt and disdain.
This belief arose, maybe because soldiers are seen as unfriendly people,
people solely trained the hardway to shoot and snuff life out of others,
blood-thirsty and ruthless personalities, a people without any touch of
fellow-feeling and a people without a trace of compassion.
In other words, the profession has an associational ring with violence,
killing, maiming, brutality and ruthlessness.
Civilians, especially politicians in the saddle of power dread them a
lot, academics don't fancy them either, so do parents not want their wards
to be identified with men in this 'fraternity' not to talk of their
daughters getting married to them.
Nevertheless, whereas people hold this opinion, these smart looking men
in starched uniform think otherwise. In their view they are like other
mortals - flowing with blood in their veins, inundated with the milk of
human kindness as well as refined sensibilities.
Really, how else do you explain away the fact that a profession full of
diverse professionals comprising among others doctors, engineers,
journalists and lawyers are not imbued with men of substantial humanity; how
do you explain that these men, physically strong, with well-balanced
proportions between their trunks and limbs are not full of humanness.
Little wonder like bees to honey, women go after them. Yes, a flurry of
beautiful women always tug around them, after all, they have all it takes to
keep a home: they are educated, knowledgeable and strong.
Think of such beautiful wives of former Generals like Mrs. Maryam
Babangida, Mrs. Maryam Abacha, Mrs. Ronke Ayuba and a host of others. For
those in the military circle, they understand the meaning of the four letter
word - love - more than other professionals and this effectively explains
why they treat their wives with honour and dignity contrary to people's
expectations.
This assertion was so much corroborated by no other than a retired
one-star General and former Director of Army Information, Brig. Gen. Fred
Chijuka who said the profession is synonymous with respectability.
The General who married as a teacher prior to joining the Army in 1971
said he has since positively altered his relationship with his wife,
Josephine.
"I think I was even more reckless with her before. But since I joined
the Army, I think I have been a little more sympathetic and a little more
careful not to offend her.
"Before I have always tried to 'play hard' on her at times when I am
rally put off, but I think I softened up more when I joined the Army. I
discovered that in the military, we respect women a lot, particularly our
wives," he said frankly.
Speaking to Panache last Tuesday about his marital life that has lasted
35 years, the teacher-turned-soldier narrated with ease how the journey
which began in 1967 culminated in marriage a year after.
Recalling how the journey started, its vicissitudes, the eventual
marriage, and its twists and turns was no big task for the former Army
spokesman who from every indication remembered the game-plan and strategies
he employed to woo his wife with enthusiasm and a measured clarity.
There was this day, he said while he was on holidays that he saw this
girl, slim, beautiful with her hair neatly pushed behind and with a small
belt on her waist. She was just passing through his village road when he saw
her and something in him like a wayward yearning told him to inquire more
about her.
So he asked his mother about the girl, but the answer he got was not so
satisfactory to equate to the organic sense of love that almost immediately
seized him.
In truth, in the brief but fleeting moment he saw her, he felt in him
something strong, even as a feeling of love, hope and optimism thoroughly
enveloped him. Somewhere in the recess of his heart, he had begun to nurse a
feeling that maybe something could happen between them.
To put this feeling, this desire into reality, he continued the inquiry
and this took him to the girl's village as well as to her school, then a
Catholic Teachers' Training College in Ughelli.
At the college, he sent that she be called and on sighting him, she ran
and ran to embrace him, an action she told Panache was done innocently.
"We use to see him, so when I now saw him in far away Ughelli I ran and
embraced him, you know as somebody from the same town. That was all I had in
mind.
"I just embraced him because we were from the same area. I never knew
that the action meant another thing to the young man.
"Later he started asking me questions - ah! ah! so this man has
something up his sleeves," she stated.
For Mr. Chijuka, then a young graduate from the University of Ife, the
warm reception was more than meets the eye. More than anything, it prepared
him over and over on the path of love and confidence.
The action not only satisfied his initial promptings and excitement that
maybe something would come out of this, but it gave him the needed push to
put across his request. Insofar as he was concerned, the deal has been
struck and he was ready to hit the bull's eye in the military fashion.
"Then I said thank God since she embraced me like that it means she
likes me. So I now went home and told my mother I have seen the girl and
that her school was very close to where I was teaching.
"So she now asked are you interested in her. I said yes. She then said,
well if you are interested, you shouldn't worry just go back to your
station. Maybe by the time you come in December, she would be around then we
would open up the matter," he noted.
True to type, everybody returned in December and his father took the
proposal to the girl's family. Ordinarily, it would have received the
readiest acquiescence taking into consideration his pedigree and that of his
family except that Joe as he fondly calls his hearthrob was looking forward
to becoming a reverend sister.
Indeed that was the only snag, so her family demanded for time. Besides
this, he said his family cashed in on other interests like using her auntie,
relatives and relationship. Eventually she jettisoned the idea of becoming a
reverend sister and agreed to marry him.
But did the courtship last, Brig. Gen. Chijuka said no, pointing out
that what prolonged it to 1968 was the Nigeria Civil War.
"When the war was not too bad in our area, we started very serious
arrangement and by November 9, 1968 we had the wedding," he said.
Shortly after, the union produced the first child, Isioma, but not
without the accompanying expectation and anxiety.
"Then my wife was very slim and I was very excited that a slim person
could be pregnant," he said. At this remark, Joe who was busy pretending to
be computing some figures with the calculator, quipped, "say whatever you
want to say." And her husband retorted MTN the best connection.
Their talking to Panache, no doubt, elicited in them the urge to
remember their initial love life, to tell the story the way it is and to try
to make their feeling known. It was a feeling that made them look like a
newly wedded couple and the excitement in their modest business office at
the Tafawa Balewa Square (TBS) was thick.
"Even with her big tummy, she pounded yam for me. She will do all the
house chore without complaining. She was really very strong.
"Like the day she delivered, I was just informed that she was in labour,
but before I could reach home and come back, she had delivered," he said
full of hysteria.
The Catholic family above all has been blessed with six children - four
girls and two boys. Isioma who is resident in United States with her
husband; Ikenna, Ekene, Ifeyinwa, Nneamaka, Onyineye.
Brig. Gen. Chijuka while examining what has produced and sustained the
going family, said it was God, compatibility, love and commitment of all
family members.
"The secret is trust in God. There is no other thing than trust in God
and He has be with us.
"Also, we make these children realise the importance of hardwork. For
example, Ikenna who read Marine Engineering made a second class upper. Ekene
who studied Chemical Engineering made a second class upper, Nneamaka who
read Economics also made a second class upper. I hope the last one,
Ifeyinwa, who is still in school will make a second class upper. So it has
been a combination of all these factors, but mainly God's help and love for
us," he said with humility.
Despite having an understanding wife and parading children with fine
credentials and designations, among other attendant streaks of successes,
Brig. Gen. Chijuka noted that there has not been any major obstacle for the
past 35 years.
Since his children and wife were quite healthy, the only thing he
considered a set back was lack of money.
"God has been of special favour to us because none has ever been so sick
that one would say that this is abnormal. It does not mean that at one time
or the other that they have not gone to the hospital, but there has never
been any serious ailment that would make us to say why is this happening
this way.
