"So we have not had any serious obstacles except perhaps money. If you
are looking at my pocket for plenty of money you won't find it. But the
miracle is that we keep going," he stated jokingly.
Reacting to the activities of inlaws and interlopers, Afresco as his
wife calls him, said the family had never witnessed their intervention.
But almost immediately, he paused and with his left hand, touched his
head in an attitude of meditation. And what caused this prayerful outlook
was not far-fetched as he reminisced.
"I remember even as a young man when you marry, there is always these
funny ideas that you would want to be out every time, not looking for women,
but keeping the same company you have been keeping before.
"And then as a teacher, I had habit of coming late especially after
teaching English and Literature and marking certain papers that would almost
get you mad. So at times after marking, I would be out drinking with my
friends in the town.
"So there was this day, she flared up and accused me of coming back
late, so I told her I was going to report her.
"I remember she said, no, let us not take any of our disagreement to
anybody. Young as she was I was highly impressed. So we have never given
anybody room to know what we are doing in our family," he said.
His wife who was still computing some statistical figures abruptly
stopped what she was doing and looked at her husband, her mouth slightly
opened, indicating that she was all along indirectly engrossed in the
discussion.
She herself concurred to this even as she enjoined younger couples to
always have patience.
Patience, Mrs. Chijuka said was the watchword, counselling that
matrimonial problems should not be taken to a third party.
"Women should not be carrying their problems, matrimonial problems to
their friends. Stay in the four walls of your room and discuss whatever the
problem is. Don't tell your friends your husband gives you N10 because she
will tell you her husband gives her N100. Na lie.
"I always tell them stay with your husband and discuss whatever you
feel. Don't discuss your problems with outsiders," the retired teacher
insisted.
The veteran, yes, in marriage, who acknowledged that the family has come
a long way, said she would re-marry her husband time and time again.
Her husband, she said, has got some beautiful qualities.
"Of course, of course, I will go for Fred any time, any day. Nobody
else, no, no no. It must be Fred. So that is it.
"Well you have heard him talk and the majority of Fred is goodness. He
is good. He is a nice man," she reiterated in a measured but laden voice of
enthusiasm.
For the Chijukas, it has been 35 years of little rough edges, but so
much happiness. It has been a relationship of unending understanding and
affection. It has been a sing-song of love and satisfaction.
No doubt, the web of associational memory, especially the symbolic
embrace is still fresh on their mind even as they look forward to more
blissful years ahead.
For the sweethearts, it has been 35 days, nay 35 years of compatibility
and joy.

Original_Search_Title: Eloquent Testimonies
Headline: Nigeria: Eloquent Testimonies
Body_Text:
Last week, The British Council (Nigeria) celebrates its 60 years of
existence as several Nigerians count their blessings from the Council's
stable akeem lasisi At 60, The British Council has many stories to tell. If
it were a human being, no matter how successful it had been, having seen six
decades would have meant that it would have been hosting the aged.
But because it is an agency that symbolises an ideal, a culture and a
dream, the British Council is only waxing stronger with the rolling
seasons. That is why its authorities rolled out the drums in Lagos, last
week, showcasing its achievements since it opened shop in Nigeria in
1943.
Members of the diplomatic corps, civil society, artists, community and
beneficiaries of its projects and programs thronged the Lagoon Restaurant,
Ikoyi, venue of the Diamond Jubilee celebration. They were treated to live
music, dance (drama) and buffet that, quite symbolically, included pounded
yam.
From the British High Commissioner in Nigeria, Mr.Phillip Thomas, to the
Council's Director, John Richards, and other senior officials, came words of
fulfillment and promises of renewed vigour and expanded focus at the
celebration. But their own testimonies were not even as eloquent as those of
Nigerians whose lives have been touched, over the decades, by the
Council.
Telling the British Council's story one after the other, some had gone
as far as declaring the council delivered them from what could have been the
blind alley of their lives.
Accomplished dancer, Arnold Udoka, who told the audience that the
British Council is like a mother to him. In 1985, the Council had sponsored
his advanced training in dance abroad, thus "making me what I am
today. "Having created me" added the international dancer, "I have also
given birth to so many dance children, and these children knew their
grandmother" According to Mr John Momoh, owner of Channels Television, "you
can't talk of Channels Television and its success without talking about the
British Council." The Council has been supporting him since the early stages
of his life. At a time he travelled to see all television stations in the
United Kingdom, on the bill of the British Council. This experience, he
says, procreated his media outfit.
At another level, the British Council scholarship program has
contributed immensely to the educational development of many Nigerians. One
of its beneficiaries, Dr. Anthonia Adindu, who saluted the British
government for committing millions of pounds to the program, noted that over
700 Nigerians have benefited from it since 1984. Chief Medical Director,
Aminu Kano Teaching Hospital, Dr.  Suleiman Wali, shared Adindu's
feelings. Wali's first contact with the British Council was in 1953, when he
was in the primary school.  The Council's library used to organise drama and
debates, apart from providing books for use. When he eventually went for
post-graduate studies in England, the British Council arranged "for
everything for us - including accommodation" Celebrated writer, Cyprian
Ekwensi, and reigning artiste, Bisade Ologunde a.k.a. Lagbaja, also mounted
the stage to tell the British Council story, ostensibly on behalf of
Nigerian literary and entertainment clan. Ekwensi's testimony was laden with
wise cracks that kept the audience laughing and saluting him
intermittently. 'The 82-year-old author of People Of the City, Jagua Nana,
The Drummer Boy, among other novels, had his first contact with the council
in 1946. It was around the time he first met the Council's first director,
Lloyd Williams, "Then, they were more inclined to Literature than
Choreography," Ekwensi said in reference to an international dance project
the council is prosecuting at present. Williams had helped organise the
Scribblers' Club, in which Ekwensi was active. The Council had a nice
reception for he and his fellow Nigerians in England in 1951 as well.
Lagbaja was at the center of entertainment at the Lagoon bash. He is
happy that there is continuity of the adventures of the British Council in
Nigeria. Among all the artistes the Council had brought from England,
Lagbaja confessed, one was "a very big impact" on him - the Itchy Fingers, a
jazz group.
As the United Kingdom's international organisation for educational and
cultural relations, the British Council had existed for about two decades
before it came to Nigeria in 1943. In the 1920s and early 30s, the foreign
office in Britain realised the need for an organisation responsible for the
promotion of British culture, education, science and technology in other
countries, along the lines of existing French, German and Italian cultural
organisations. "This led to the setting up of the British Committee for
Relation with other countries in 1934. The name was later changed to the
British Council for Relations with other countries and then shortened to the
British Council.
The work of the early British Council included support to British
Institutes and societies, and English schools in other countries;
recruitment of university lecturers, support to students and English
teachers; supply of books and periodicals for libraries, lecture tours,
music performances and arts exhibitions. Priority was given to the countries
of the Middle East and Mediterranean region, Europe and Latin America.
Apart from contributing to the growth of the artistes, when some of them
make landmark achievements, the British Council rises to celebrate them. It
did this for Helon Habila, when he won the $15, 000 Caine Prize for
literature. Recently, again, to assist young writers, it flagged off a
mentoring program in which it intends to link some of the budding writers to
established ones abroad. It is equally planning a residency program that
Emecheta is proposed to anchor. The occasion of the diamond jubilee
celebration also proved auspicious for the British Council to roll out a new
set of projects and programs. They include the Jubilee Literary prize:
Jubilee Dance Project; Jubilee Music Showcase; Arts Manager's Conference;
Writer's Workshop; British Film Project and Telling Stories.

Original_Search_Title: Foday Saybana Sankoh as I Got to Know Him During the Sierra Leonean Peace Talks in Lome
Headline: Sierra Leone: Foday Saybana Sankoh as I Got to Know Him During the Sierra Leonean Peace Talks in Lome
Body_Text:
I have just heard about the untimely passing away of former RUF rebel
leader Foday Saybana Sankoh, alias "Okuruba" or The Warrior.
I got to know the legendary Foday Sankoh during the Ecowas-sponsored
Inter Sierra-Leonean Peace Talks in LomÃ© in May 1999.
As a correspondent for the BBC Focus/Network Africa programmes it was an
inevitable professional obligation for me to establish a working
acquaintance and personal relationship with the RUF rebel leadership, as
well as the Sierra-Leone official Government delegation led by the legal
luminary, Solomon Berewah, now Vice-President, in order to facilitate
unimpeded access to the two opposite divergent views of the argument.
It was in the course of this peace process that I got to strike a
harmonious chord with Corporal Foday Sankoh at close quarters.
Corporal Saybana Sankoh was indeed an African traditional warrior, but
he struck me as a real defiant, resolute Bush Man who will go to any lengths
to get what he wants. Damn the consequences. I will illustrate this with an
unforgettable incident which nearly cost me my job and my reputation.
It happened at the prestigious Hotel Corinthia 2 Fevrier in LomÃ©. The
rebel leader confessed to me he was ill-at ease and fed up to the neck with
the monotony of the menu and the glittering life of luxury at the
comfortable five-star hotel in LomÃ©.
"Ebow," Sankoh said on that fateful day while I was nosing round for a
scoop from the RUF rebel High-Command, "I am feeling nostalgic for a return
to bush life. This daily menu of steak and chips, rice and chicken is
becoming too much of a predictable ritual," he confided in me.
Foday Sankoh said he wanted to live in a natural environment of the bush
where one could easily secure fresh fruits, pepper and other basic needs of
life in their pure unadulterated form, with birds chirping on the trees and
cool water flowing freely from the streams.
"I am beginning to feel home-sick of the pure green vegetables, the
appetising fresh red bush meat which you can just shoot down, prepare on
fire, and consume in their unpolluted delicacy," the rebel leader said.
He expressed regrets that even though he enjoyed the liberty to move
freely in LomÃ©, ironically, life in the hotel looked somewhat to him like a
glorified prison.
At this point, I suggested that we could have dinner together in my
house if he wanted. I promised him real, traditional 'kontomire,' vegetable
stew with fresh meat, fish, and crabs with cooked rice, and yams as a
gastronomic complement.
Without hesitation, Foday Sankoh jumped for the bait like fish caught on
the alluring hook.
I saw his epiglottis galloping rhythmically with saliva. His appetite
was whetted beyond the wildest imagination. But at the appointed time, in
the evening, I sensed trouble.
The Togolese security guards refused to allow me access to Foday Sankoh
in his hotel room.
A shouting match ensued, and this brought Foday Sankoh out of his
rooms. He was told by the Togolese security that the dinner could not take
place in my house that evening because we had not made the necessary
security protocol arrangements.
Hell broke loose. Foday Sankoh flew into tantrums. And I later found
myself playing a role of the mediator between him and the men assigned the
duty of ensuring his safety.
When calm was restored, he warned that he would leave the hotel in
protest if he was not allowed to go out and eat whatever and wherever he
wants.
I went back home, for I thought the matter was settled.
It was therefore with trepidation when my mobile phone rudely woke me up
at 2 o'clock in the morning, only to hear the angry voice of Foday Sankoh at
the other distant end.
"Is that Ebow Godwin of the BBC?" Foday Sankoh asked. I
responded. "Well, I have left the Hotel 2 Fevrier in protest over the
security refusal to allow me to eat my dinner outside the hotel," he said
nonchalantly.
To me, it was like a bombshell. I saw myself gradually, but unwillingly,
drawn into the web of diplomatic controversy, and I detested the gloomy
prospect.
"But Foday Sankoh, you cannot do that. Besides, the security men are
right, we have to arrange things properly," I barked out in indignation
without being aware of it.
Yet Foday Sankoh was calm, unmoved. "Is the dinner still intact? If it
is, I have got the authorities to agree to let us have it served in your
home with state protocol in attendance," he said.
By noon time, Corporal Foday Saybana Sankoh was chauffeur driven in a
convoy of limousines under tight Togolese security for the dinner that later
turned out to be another supper.
As Foday Sankoh scooped a mouthful of sumptuous green vegetable stew, he
whispered discreetly into my ears, "President EyadÃ©ma was unhappy about the
refusal of the security guards to allow me to go out of the hotel for my
dinner last night." Apparently President EyadÃ©ma had ordered that the
situation must be rectified immediately.
That was Foday Saybana Sankoh for you. A man whose resolute and defiant
character brought him very close to the promised land, Canaan, in
Sierra-Leone, but paradoxically failed to reach there.
Nevertheless, while out of the bush war, Foday Sankoh never lost eyes
for the good things of life, including beautiful women.
In fact he got himself traditionally "married" to a charming Togolese
lady from Sokode in Central Togo during the Sierra-Leonean Peace Talks.
Unconfirmed reports say that the Togolese "wife," Bataba Victoire, was
believed to have been with Foday Sankoh on the fateful day the tragic
shooting incident turned his political fortunes upside down in
Freetown.
But up till now, all efforts to locate Bataba Victoire have ended in a
fiasco. Now that Foday is dead, the fears of many worried Togolese is that
the mystery surrounding the whereabouts of his Togolese wife may yet remain
buried with Foday Sankoh.
Foday Sankoh told me that he was born in 1936 in a village called
Massang in Moyorsokolifo kingdom in Tonkelele district, northern
Sierra-Leone.
"It is not on the map," he wisecracked. He fondly remembers his mother,
the first in the line of four wives of his father.
"My mother's society name is Yinnosserie or the beautiful woman called
Yinnor," he told me in an interview in LomÃ© during the peace talks.
"I think I was lucky enough to be sent to school, at the age of ten (10)
I started my primary school in Rokes, that is Yohibana area, those with a
railway line," he said.
But while in my child-hood I liked going to the bush to set traps for
animals, fighting with my brothers with whom we used to engage in wrestling
contests. And they used to call me 'Okuruba' or the warrior in our
language," he said.
Foday Sankoh said the language was Timne. He reveals that his name Foday
Sankoh originates from the Poro Secret Society where men learn the secrets
arts of survival.
He told me that the period of initiation into manhood could last for
four years. "Say-Say Bana or Big is my Society name. And it means BIG. But
when you come to see the man, he is a small man. Foday Saybana Sankoh is a
BIG name given to a small man," he explained.
From school, Foday Sankoh briefly worked with the Electricity Department
in Maburka, first as a messenger, then as a meter reader from where he rose
to become an engine attendant.
Later he joined the Water Works Department where an expatriate engineer,
one Mr. Boston, who was an ex-Serviceman, encouraged Foday Sankoh to join
the Army during a recruitment exercise in Maburka. He began military
training in Daru in the Moa barracks.
Foday Sankoh told me that he was posted to Freetown in 1956. After
training, he rejoined the First Battalion where he claimed that he did three
months advanced course at Wilberforce Barracks from where he was deployed to
Tower Hill, First Squadron, then Military Headquarters in Freetown. He
qualified as a signals man.
Foday Sankoh claims that some of his course mates were former Defence
Minister Hinga Norman against whom he fought during the Sierra-Leonean
conflict.
Afterwards, Foday Sankoh took to photography which he claimed he learnt
while he was briefly in Britain to continue with his training in
signals.
It was photography that served as a turning point in his life as a
revolutionary. In 1982, he used his photo studios for undercover activities
to secretly recruit militants for his bush war, which he began in 1991.
When I reminded him about the injustice and atrocities committed by the
RUF rebel movement, he denied them flatly. He in turn accused the local
Kamajors, of committing those atrocities. I knew he lied.
During the LomÃ© negotiations, Foday Sankoh narrated to me the saga
behind his arrest and subsequent detention in Abuja, Nigeria in 1996.
"I do not trust anybody except God, but I am prepared to negotiate with
the devil if only that will bring peace to the people of Sierra Leone," he
told me.
Foday Sankoh recalled how in 1996 he decided to go to Abuja in Nigeria
after the RUF had signed the Abidjan Peace Accords "as part of my plan to
tour the West African Sub-Region to solicit support for the peace plan, and
to halt the hostilities between the Kamajors and the Army."
Foday Sankoh remained bitter till his death about the Nigerian military
dictator, General Sani Abacha.
"Upon my arrival in Nigeria, I was arrested, chained, beaten, and
detained for no apparent reason whatsoever," he told me in LomÃ©. "Abacha
later handed me over to Tejjan Kabbah in Sierra-Leone. Do you call that
justice? He asked.
Foday Sankoh claimed that the late General Sani Abacha confiscated a
large sum of foreign currency, which he allegedly carried on him. When the
news of Abacha's death was announced, Foday Sankoh remarked, "He deserved no
sympathies because Abacha played God. Nobody plays God."
Independent sources say that the monies seized by Abacha were later
refunded to Foday Sankoh by the Nigerian Government under President Olusegun
Obasanjo.
But now that the rest of the story has been cut short by the passing way
of Foday Sankoh, one cannot easily tell whether it is 'Okuruba' or General
Abacha who deserves no sympathy from humanity.

Original_Search_Title: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Headline: Nigeria: A Hero Called Charles Oguntoye
Body_Text:
IN an era ruined and run by skepticism, it is so simple to cast Charles Oguntoye in the bin of Nigerians due for condemnation. Some are calling for his head, others given a chance would strip him of his citizenship, though he must have been smart enough after so many years to acquire a British passport of convenience. If I know the likes of Oguntoye, he would be surprised that simple business decisions that followed expected patterns have resulted in more publicity that he would have asked for all his life.If he is one of those who never mind whether publicity is good or bad (publicity is publicity, they say) he would not be in the least affected by the emotional outburst about his dealings with Nigeria and Venezuela. He is not known to speak Spanish and we are not giving him any credit for bringing the Venezuelans into this game that dominated sports reports for the better part of the week?What did he do? The now famous British-based, Nigerian businessman, sports promoter extraordinaire, patriot, a football lover with Nigeria's Super Eagles as his niche, a man of action, a man of integrity and an astute match bargainer asked the two countries to play a game in London.
There is nothing wrong with all these attributes. I believe they were what attracted the Nigeria Football Association to him, in the first place. This would not in anyway suggest that the NFA sets out to do business with any serious considerations in mind. Oguntoye could actually sue the NFA for defamation of character, assassination of character, disclosure of business secrets, breach of contract (as in arriving late to the match venue, a factor he could have blamed for the poor attendance) and improper disclosure of the meals that the Super Eagles took before the game.Did they not know that they had a choice to board the next plane back to Nigeria? I even heard that was not a decision they could have made in a hurry. The team could not afford the bus fare back to the airport. The hotel that had their unwanted presence was a last bus stop of sorts. If the owners had insisted on the Super Eagles taking a room or hitting the streets, coaches and pla6yers might have ended up in the kitchen watching plates for their upkeep. Would that not have been befitting training for a team that did not practice?What fault of Oguntoye's was it that the Super Eagles would spend hours on the corridors of a hotel? I considered them lucky - some hotels would not allow you to hang around for more than a couple of minutes. It is not good for business, especially black faces. Luggage strewn all over the place would not be the perfect example of a hotel in business.Extra luck was on their side. It was not winter and the hotel bore their presence with remarkable patience, all 10 hours of it. Coach Christian Chukwu ached from transferring his bowed head from one hand to the other, players watched in dismay, as their coach had no clue about where Oguntoye was. What business did he have with them? His business was with eth NFA, which certified the terms acceptable for the Eagles to depart for London.
Things got better as the hours crawled by. At meal time they knew that they were on their own. Is it not totally dumb to expect that a man who did not bother to provide a roof over your head would feed you? Oguntoye had a game plan, he followed it through, leaving the teams complaining in English and Spanish.When they were lucky to get some fast food, they complained that they had expected something more solid after hours of waiting for a bite. Imagine how ungrateful a team can be! They thought that Oguntoye was going to import some pounded yam to please them? As the owner of the business (called match) in question, was it not within his competence to decide that the Super Eagles could do with some trimming? In response to that allegation, their excellent host served them sandwiches before the match that was postponed for hours as Oguntoye turned his bargaining charms on. He would race from one hotel to the other intriguing the other team with details of his faithfulness to the agreement to a match that was finally played without any of the teams knowing why they had to play.Oguntoye got them to believe that if they did not play, the other would gain a walk over. He must have made it sound like the losing team was giving up the World Cup final without a fight.
Could it be the attraction of travelling to London that made the NFA get into a match without knowing its expectations from the promoter? Nobody could blame Oguntoye on that one. I have it on good authority that the Nigeria-Irish pre-World Cup game that is paraded as his major credential went on smoothly because the Irish FA took over the organisation. Days to the match, worse signs than what happened in London were showing.Nigeria was in London to play against Venezuela, at the instance of Oguntoye and the NFA, which is trying very hard to prove that it can get matches for the Super Eagles. There is no doubt that the farce that took place in London was one of the great A games that the NFA has secured for the national team.Hard as I tried I found out that Oguntoye did the Super Eagles and the NFSA a greet service for which eh deserves our praise. So many of us complain about the slow progress that the Super Eagles make on the monthly FIFA rankings. Oguntoye has given FIFA a good reason to take us at least one notch when the next rankings are released. What else do you expect of a hero? Who among the complaining lot has lifted a finger to help Nigerian football? How many of them can get the Eagles a classy opposition like Venezuela, a South American team? Are we complaining because a Nigerian promoted the match?Many are those who would want this matter top be blown out of proportion. They are talking about eth sanctity of the national team and the discretion that it suffered in London. We are trained to expect worse things. Bad as Oguntoye was, he provided tickets for the team to travel, a hotel room was made available, courtesy of more concerned Eagles supporters, a meal of sandwich was better than nothing we all can admit.No longer would anyone ask who is Charles Oguntoye. He has made his name. He has made his mark. If we cannot get him a national honour in October, the entry may be late, we have ample time to do so next year, by which time he would have organised a couple of other games for Nigeria, with expectedly more finesse along the lines of the experience of the one in London.The NFA has to start accepting responsibility. How could it have sent off Chukwu and the Super Eagles without a penny on them? How much did it know about Oguntoye's excellent match organisation? What role did the secretariat play in accepting a game for which it had not been paid anything? Where did the NFA expect to get the money to pay winning bonuses, or was Nigeria meant to lose the game so that no bonuses would be paid?Chukwu should be the last man to really complain about anything.
He must have had his fill of solid meals since his return. Oguntoye has also aided the resurrection of his slipping career. His hecklers cannot say that he did not beat a Latin American team. The facts are there that he won the match 1-0, at a time of flagging performances.Thank you Oguntoye. From your great handling of this assignment, we have discovered that the food that we give the Super Eagles is wasted. The good accommodation that we provide for their camping is an avoidable drain on scarce national resources. Have they won matches with the same ease as they did in London when we fed them well?I think that we should quickly set up an inquiry on this matter. The sole term of reference would be to establish how the conditions in London made the Super Eagles to win the match against Venezuela. Some people are sounding as if they are sad that we won. They also want to blame Oguntoye for that. Unable to raise convincing reasons to diminish the game, they have taken on Oguntoye and made him their whipping boy, as if he stuck the contract into the throat of the NFA and the Super Eagles.What a country!PS: The next time the Super Eagles are invited for a game (I heard that would be a long time) important items that would be negotiated in the contract would include details of food items to be served. Sandwiches would be forbidden. We would be quartered in villas instead of hotels with waiting areas that a promoter would think is befitting for the national team of Nigeria. Promoters beware or consult Oguntoye, whose next line of business would be a consultancy on how to ruin international games.

The facts are there that he won the match 1-0, at a time of flagging performances.Thank you Oguntoye. From your great handling of this assignment, we have discovered that the food that we give the Super Eagles is wasted. The good accommodation that we provide for their camping is an avoidable drain on scarce national resources. Have they won matches with the same ease as they did in London when we fed them well?I think that we should quickly set up an inquiry on this matter. The sole term of reference would be to establish how the conditions in London made the Super Eagles to win the match against Venezuela. Some people are sounding as if they are sad that we won. They also want to blame Oguntoye for that. Unable to raise convincing reasons to diminish the game, they have taken on Oguntoye and made him their whipping boy, as if he stuck the contract into the throat of the NFA and the Super Eagles.What a country!PS: The next time the Super Eagles are invited for a game (I heard that would be a long time) important items that would be negotiated in the contract would include details of food items to be served. Sandwiches would be forbidden. We would be quartered in villas instead of hotels with waiting areas that a promoter would think is befitting for the national team of Nigeria. Promoters beware or consult Oguntoye, whose next line of business would be a consultancy on how to ruin international games.

Original_Search_Title: Those Whom the Gods Want to Kill (1)
Headline: Nigeria: Those Whom the Gods Want to Kill (1)
Body_Text:
What happened in Anambra would only have taken place in
Nigeria.....
IT was one Thursday in August 2000, the citizens of Aguata Local
Government Area of Anambra State gathered at the LG Secretariat for their
first ever new yam festival and cultural extravaganza. All the traditional
rulers with their cabinet chiefs, top private and public sector operators,
members of federal and state executive and legislature from within and
outside the state, past and present governors and all the distinguished sons
and daughters of the local government as well as their friends were in
attendance. I was there in my capacity as a member of the king's cabinet of
my town. It was indeed a distinguished assembly. While Governor Mbaninuju
was breaking the kola-nut (and that is at the core of every Igbo occasion),
there was some commotion as a siren blaring convoy led by heavily armed
policemen, drove with the recklessness of Jehu into the arena and a frail
looking young man alighted just in front of the governor. And while I was
wondering who had the guts to enter so riotously and arrogantly, the
governor abandoned the kola-nut ceremony and started singing praises of the
new entrant, introducing him as Chief Chris Uba (Eselu), and as one of the
most distinguished sons of Anambra without whom nothing would work in the
state! Everything was suspended until he settled down. He not only made a
nonsense of official protocol, he interrupted the kola-nut breaking by the
governor which was an alu (abomination) in Igboland. I noticed how helpless
the governor was, and how from then on, everything revolved around him with
everybody trying to catch his attention. That 'triumphant entry' was my
first and last time of seeing Chief Uba, an unbearable arrogant man who
plays god with reckless abandon, who does not give a damn about crushing
people toes, and who uses his power to humiliate and belittle men, whom
ordinarily, he is not worthy to clean their shoes.
Stupendous wealth
But I have heard about him before then. They said he was stupendously
wealthy, very powerful, well connected (especially at the source) and
influential but nobody told me how he came about his wealth or what he does
and I do not know whether he acquired his wealth through the power and
influence or vice versa. I also heard that he could fix anything and that he
was among the three musketeers that hoisted Mbaninuju on the unlucky people
of Anambra State in 1999. He also emerged as the political leader of Old
Aguata Union, and no one could explain how that was possible in an area that
boasts of people like Dr. Alex Ekwueme (former Vice President), Okwadike
C. P Ezeife (former Governor), Prof. H. Nwosu (former Electoral Commission
boss) and Dr. A. A. Agwuncha (of Eastern Mandate Union). And like the man
who never dreamt of taking an ozo title, he tied the symbolic thread around
the neck, where everybody would see it instead of the normal position, the
ankle where it is not very visible.
Before long, Uba acquired a larger than life image particularly in the
affairs of Anambra State where he has been mentioned in connection with
everything happening there and none of those events are noble. These
included the crises between Mbaninuju, his deputy and the state Assembly,
the Mbaninuju/Offor face-off, the abdication and failed removal of Aguata LG
chairman, the circus-show-like PDP primaries, and the recent election fiasco
in Anambra State. Encouraged by his past 'successes', the repressive state
machineries at his disposal, the much touted and abused Abuja connection,
show that he is in charge in Anambra State, he moved against Ngige. Surely,
the gods wanted to kill him and as it is in their character, they first of
all made him mad-man with power; mad with money; mad with a spirit of
invincibility; and mad with a rabid desire to be worshiped. And because a
dog destined to die ignores the hunter's whistle, he moved against Ngige
even before he had settled down to work, without perfecting his plans and
overlooking the fact that Ngige may be a doctor but he is certainly no
Mbaninuju.
What happened at Awka on Thursday, July10, 2003, would have been
laughable but for its grievous nature. But it did not start that day. It
started when Uba 'single-handedly installed' all the political office
holders in Anambra State (that confirms that there were no elections and
that they were not elected) and they in return publicly pledged
unquestionable, zombie-like loyalty. And being a man without an iota of
humility, he made repeated public shows of the installation and the
resultant allegiance. After a fierce battle that probably started before
inauguration day, May 29, all bordering on direct and indirect 'resource
control', the godfather struck. It was a coup achieved through
civilian-police collaboration. If you think it was not a coup, compare the
statement by Vrutus-the deputy governor- with those of the coup masters, Joe
Garba or Dogonyarro. This is in addition to the invasion and abduction. And
there probably would have been a summary execution but for that phone call
that was also providentially broadcast on Radio Nigeria Network News. But
that was not the first police-civilian coup in the state. The PDP primary
was a coup and so were the victories of Ngige and PDP, the National Assembly
show of shame in which people who did not contest emerged winners, and the
emergence of Mrs. Azodo as the Speaker. Other sordid details of high-handed
political brigandage and chicanery are already public knowledge.
Pockets a state
The wind has blown and we have seen the anus of the fowl! An individual
pocketed a state in the federation, boasts about it and employs the unholy
trinity of fraudulent manipulation. What happened could only have taken
place in Nigeria where every thing is possible, where some people are above
the law, and where police 'services' are available to the highest bidder; in
Anambra where the 'men of timber always look the other way while the rest
are busy chasing containers, where the civil servants' loyalty is as cheap
as that of Judas, where money maketh a man, and where there is always a good
supply of mercenary politicians; and in the PDP which is always its own
opposition, where nothing is ever done transparently and where there is
always a leader in addition to a chairman.
Ngige fought a good fight; he displayed intelligence, courage and tact
but he has a share of he blame. Why and how did he devaluate himself to that
level; why did he politically castrate himself and thus become the first
ceremonial state governor in Nigeria in whose domain appointments,
deployments and dismissals are made without his knowledge! How did he allow
himself to be manipulated to such an extent and how was he able to bear this
humiliation for so long? Surely, he gathered ant-infested firewood and
should ordinarily expect the company of lizards. Also, he who must go to
equity must do with clean hands.

Original_Search_Title: Land Dispute - Youth Dare Nkoranzahene
Headline: Ghana: Land Dispute - Youth Dare Nkoranzahene
Body_Text:
Kintampo — For what they termed as a crucial meeting, the Mo Traditional Council (MTC) and the youth have met at Kintampo to deliberate on the utterances made by the Nkoranzahene, Okatakyie Agyemang Kudom II against their paramount chief, Nana Takyie Elaje II.
Reacting to the statement of Okatakyie at the recent visit of the Brong Ahafo Regional Minister, Nana Kwadwo Seinti that Mos are taking part of his land, the youth in particular dared the Nkoranzahene or any of his chiefs to come to Kintampo to make such a statement.
The meeting which was attended by 13 sub-chiefs under the MTC including the paramount chief himself, condemned the Nkoranzahene's utterances, which also described the Mohene as a self-styled chief.
At the meeting, the Mos admitted that Nana Elaje was not gazetted but since they are concerned as a state, he is their chief.
According to them, since rituals were performed to enstool Nana Elaji as the Omanhene of the Mos, they would solidly rally behind him to fight a good cause for their state.
It would be recalled that Chronicle in its 23rd June edition, published a story on its back page headlined "Imminent clash over boundaries".
The paper quoted the Nkoranzahene as saying that the Mos are taking his land and also described the Mohene as a self-styled chief in the Mo traditional area.
The chiefs and their subjects regretted that some seven disgruntled persons, spearheaded by the district chief executive for Kintampo, Mr. James Kwabena Bomfeh, are pushing the family head of the omanhene to stand against him.
The Mos called on the government to caution the DCE, himself a Mo, in his involvement in the Mo chieftaincy issues.
They warned that if Mr. Bomfeh is interested in the Mo chieftaincy, he should relinquish his position as district chief executive.
The MTC recalled that during the honourable regional minister's visit to the Kintampo District, the DCE made the Mohene and his sub-chiefs organize a durbar in the honour of the minister, but the minister failed to come to them.
The minister and his entourage, according to the traditional council members came to New Longoro, the seat of the Mohene, but never stepped at the durbar grounds on the advice of Mr. Bomfeh.
The MTC in a unanimous decision called on the government and the Brong Ahafo Regional House of Chiefs to bring the Nkoranzahene to order, since a statement of such nature should come from some one like him.
They recalled that if the Nkoranzahene knows that the Mohene is a self-styled chief why was he invited to attend the Nkoranzahene's yam festival last year at Nkoranza.
On the demarcation of boundaries between the Mos and the Nkoranza traditional area, the MTC has agreed that the government should look into it quickly to avoid any future clash.
The Mos said where the Kintampo market is located belongs to them, but the Nkoranzahene's representative at Kintampo, being the Kyeremakomahene has been taking royalties from the traders at the market, which they said must be stopped with immediate effect.
The warned that if it continues they may run out of patience it is important that the government take a critical look at it. The MTC also deliberated on issues affecting their culture and Deg Pedia festival, which is slated for October 28 this year.
According to the omanhene, the Mos have a lot of cultural activities that need to be showcased to the world. Nana Elaje said the Pedia festival, which means yam festival, would attract tourists to the area, in order to create revenue for the district.
To get the Mo traditional area to develop; the MTC has appealed to all Mos far and near to come together to build a formidable Mo state.

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Pay More for Foodstuff
Headline: Nigerians Pay More for Foodstuff
Body_Text:
The recent hike in the price of petroleum products from N26 to N34 by
the Federal Government has begun to spill over to the prices of food stuffs
in the country, even as 80 per cent of the populace are living below the
poverty line, i.e., on less than US$1 per day.
Vanguard findings showed that the prices of different brands of 50 kg
bags of rice ranged between N4, 200 - N4, 600 while Tin sales of N1, 100 -
N1, 200.
On a similar note, 100 kg bag of brown beans sells for N7, 500 while the
same size of white sells for N6, 000. Tin of both brown and white sells for
N950 and N800 respectively.
The bigger size of the Abuja species of yams which is the most common in
the market sells for N400, while the medium and small sizes sells for N300
and N200 respectively.
A 100kg bags of gari sells for N4, 500, while the Tin sells for N550. A
module (olodo) of eguisi (melon) is N1, 000 while the hand peel-made sells
for N1, 200. Also, a module of Ogbono, is sold for N2, 000, while a derica
cup is sold for N550.
Specifically, Vanguard surveyed traders at various markets on Lagos
Mainland. The foodstuff dealers say they are still trying to come to terms
with the additional hardship the petroleum product prices have spilled on
food items. They blame the rise in cost of food stuff on transportation
which has more than doubled following the increase in pump price.
A majority of the traders who spoke to Vanguard warned that Nigerians,
especially, the masses, should be prepared to adapt to the situation of
things because " nobody is sure of what the future economy has in the store
with the high prices of goods and services."
According to them, one fact we cannot run away from is that the cost of
living has gone up for the masses.
A spokesman for the dealers, Mr. Genesis Okorie said that after the
strike action the market prices of commodities have not been
stable. According to him, dealers are not yet sure of the next rate of
increases on purchases.
The reason for the fluctuation he said is that majority of the dealers
additions to the old price depend on the rate at which goods were purchased,
and the cost of transportation.
He complained that government is not helping matters for the survival of
the Nigerian masses. He added that for them in the business, things have
been low key in the past months of the political campaign, coupled with
government coming with an increment in petroleum products, then the
strike.
Mr. Barnabas Odife, at Iddo market said that the action of the
government in increasing petroleum products prices is a sure signal of the
masses to depend less on the government for their survival.
According to him, if the government has the interest of the masses at
heart, it would have considered the negative implications on the poor masses
before effecting any increase in less than a month of assuming office. The
commodity dealers appealed to the federal government to help the masses by
ensuring that its policies are fine-tuned to lessen their negative
impact.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Market: Its Health, Traffic Menace
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Market: Its Health, Traffic Menace
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Mile 12 market along Ikorodu-Ketu road, Lagos State is known for its
cheap fresh fruites, vegetables and other food items. But many are not aware
of the deplorable environment and the danger it poses to free flow of
traffic, writes Agha Ibiam
If one takes a trip around Nigeria to take a census of markets,
especially food stuffs, Lagos would probably rank the highest despite the
fact that 80 per cent of the food consumed are produced outside the
state.
The population explosion in the state, which became more noticeable in
the early 1980's, has also aggravated the demand for the explosion of the
markets. But the most disturbing about having these pockets of markets is
the conversion of the roads as markets. Reasons adduced by the traders are
not far-fetched. They range from the high cost of renting shops and payment
other sundry bills from the local government authorities. Other reasons
include high level of poverty, lack of job opportunities and the tendency to
care for the ever-increasing extended families.
The hazards the conversion of roadsides to markets constitutes to
motorists can not be over-emphasised. Most of the traffic jams experienced
by motorists on major Lagos roads could be traced the dangers of these
roadside markets as vehicles no longer use normal lanes to discharge and
pick passengers.
The Mile 12 International Market, located at the out-skirt of Lagos is a
case in point. It is the major market that supplies the Lagos residents and
its environs consumable goods.
Established in 1975, the market was established to argument the
existence and reduce the traffic jam along the Iddo Market.
The scanty population then, made it unnoticeable until 1983, during
President Shehu Shagari's tenure when it became fully established. Before
this period, the Mile 12 market was said to be situated at Maidan market
named after the village. Stretching from Iyana School Bustop, extending to
the nooks and crannies of Kosofe and to Mile 12 under-bridge.
The foodstuffs come mostly from the northern, eastern and middle belt,
(Benue) parts of the country. Yam for instance is brought from Abuja, Zaria,
Ogbomosho, Abakaliki and Igbura in Benue State. While other perishable goods
like tomatoes and pepper are brought in from Jos, Plateau State; Abeokuta,
Ogun State; Ede in Osun State and Ajase in Benin Republic. Palm oil comes
mainly from Ekiti-Pupa, Ekiti State and Umuahia, Abia State.
The problems of Mile 12 Market are of two folds. One is the problem it
constitutes to the environment. The second reason is the traffic jams the
market is causing as a result of its location. THISDAY checks revealed that
most traders prefer using the service lane and the first few lock-up shops,
located just opposite the pedestrian bridge to sell their goods. Even the
bridge is now a market on its own as pedestrian scarcely makes use of
it.
Apart from two or three lock-up shops opposite the bridge, the rest are
not adequately used. THISDAY reliably gathered that miscreants perpetrate
virtually all manners of evils there, especially at nights. Most of the
shops built by various individuals during the time of Col. Olagunsoye
Onyinola (rtd) as governor of Lagos State are dilapidated due to
abandonment. Most of the shops are uncompleted and poorly planned.
Interestingly, some of the abandoned shops have been turned into
residence. They have also become a hideout for miscreants, used as joints
for smoking Indian Hemp, abode for criminals. Others are also used as cinema
houses and brothels. It was reliably gathered that the markets are sometimes
used as petrol depots, especially during scarcity of petroleum product
Cocaine business equally thrive at the abandoned shops.
Mile 12 market can be a gory site. Even pigs known for dirty places will
detest being nurtured at the Mile 12 Market environment. It therefore
borders health officials how consumable foods items like fish, meat
tomatoes, vegetables, onions, pepper amongst others are displayed and sold
openly there. During and after the rain season, the market remains a no-go
area. The epidemic the entire market is causing to its environs, according
to some residents cannot be explained.
For many, the entire market is an eye sore. THISDAY could not hold his
breath while touring the market as repugnant odour oozed out from different
directions. The swampy, muddy ground is about two to three feet deep in some
parts of the market. Observers have therefore wondered how traders manage to
carry out their commercial activities in such a place. It is therefore a
strange sight seeing people wearing rain boots. It also creates economic
activities as young girls and boys wait to wash people's feet for a token of
about N5 to N10.
THISDAY asked one of the traders why the market is in such a messy
situation. He quickly said that the Lagos State Government and the council
should be held responsible. Government, he said makes a lot of money from
the traders and the trailer drivers through all sundry taxes and levies
without develop the market. It has appeared as if most of the traders are
accustomed to the dirty environment. The bad terrain is also a problem to
trailer drivers. The trucks do not have good and spacious packing spaces,
thereby using service lanes, which compound traffic problems.
According to a medical doctor who operates within the axis of the
market, who spoke to THISDAY on condition of anonymity, said most health
hazards exist as a result of its closeness to residential quarters. Some the
health hazards include road accident, cholera, malaria, typhoid fever and
other water borne diseases.
He noted that road accident is frequent at Mile 12 Market. And when a
case is beyond their treatment, it is referred to Orthopedic
Hospital. Cholera, the said is also very rampant due to wastes dumped
indiscriminately. The decomposed wastes emit odour that causes stomach
disorder and other undiagnosed ailments.
"Most of the people when admitted, do not even know what their problems
are. You have to subject them to serious interrogation and test before
getting a clue. But as medical practitioners, we understand that the
environment contributes to most sicknesses they suffer," the doctor
said.
About the traffic jam, the Iyaloja of Mile 12 Market, Alhaja Iyabo Ahmed
argued that it is not the fault of the traders who use the service lane, but
blamed it on commercial buses that stop incessantly to drop and pick
passengers.
She said the local government officials apparently because of the petty
levies they are charged permit the traders that sell at the roadside. This
she said has equally encouraged arrogance on the part of the authority's
officials. "Both my team, the Hausa and the Igbo Yam Sellers Association,
have in the past attempted to solve the problems of the traffic jam. But the
more we try to proffer solutions, the more unrecognised we become to the
council. They prefer levying the people who sell along the service lane, toe
trailers that are packed indiscriminately and in the process make huge
amount of money for their selfish interest", she said.
The man-hour and loss of lives recorded along the Ikorodu--Ketu road
cannot be quantified. There are some instances the traffic jam stretches
from Owode-Onirin spare parts market. At times, motorists spend about two to
three hours between that short distance. This is mostly witnessed when
trailers bring food items from various states.
THISDAY had observed a situation where a young sick girl of about 14
years almost lost her life in an attempt to conveying her to a hospital in
Lagos. Perhaps her consolation was the consistent words of sorry she
received from her mother and co-passengers. There have been other worst
cases.
Weekends are considered as worst days on that road. There are some
Sundays when ordinarily the road suppose to be free, but to the chagrin of
the road users there will be unprecedented traffic jam. The situation is
made worst when the military men, LASTMA officials and the traffic warders
who supposed to control traffic, concentrate only in extorting money from
bus conductors. The uniform men often use miscreants to make this brisk
business.
A senior police officer in Ketu division who again appealed for
anonymity explained that the traffic jam at the market is as a result of the
inaccessibility of the service lane to motorist, especially the commercial
buses.
This he said has compelled traders and trailers to take over the lane to
sell and discharge their goods. But of note he added, is that most drivers
are impatient, pointing out that if the service lane had been made
motorable, their effort of controlling traffic, especially the commercial
drivers would have been more easier.
The officer did not however absolve his men of extortion. "Our effort to
rid the police of bad eggs is our obligation. We are aware the N20.00 they
collect from motorists, we will do something in that regard", he
stressed.
The other problem of Mile 12 Market, the Iyaloja said is the acute lack
of water, light, toilet facilities, security and inaccessible road due to
the swampy nature of the place. Like other markets in Lagos, her expectation
is to see the market built like the Tejuosho or Iponri markets. Even as
traders pay the council ticket, ranging from N5.00 to N10.00 daily, while
shop owner's pay about N200.00 monthly, the Iyaloja said there was nothing
to show for it.
She rather argued that government could decongest the market by making
another place available to them.
An official of the Nigerian Market Women and Men Association of the
market however proffered a solution to the traffic hold-up. He suggested
that the trailers could be made to pass through Obanta road, linking Maidan
to Dada Street. Another route is from Dada Street to Adebinpe Street, which
takes them off to Kosofe-Iyana school and off to Ketu, enroute to the
express.
The Lagos State Government recently inaugurated mobile courts within the
metropolis to prosecute traffic offenders. The Federal Road Safety Corps
(FRSC), the report said would concentrate on federal roads, while the ones
raised by the government would concentrate on state within the
metropolis.
But it appears that the more laws are made, the more motorists disobey
it. They argue that the state should brace up to that challenge as more
lives and man-hour is lost on Lagos roads.

Original_Search_Title: Petrol Costs N400 Per Litre in Port Harcourt
Headline: Nigeria: Petrol Costs N400 Per Litre in Port Harcourt
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt — The on-going nationwide industrial action called by the Nigeria Labour
Congress (NLC), yesterday in its eighth day crippled economic and social
activities in Port Harcourt, Rivers State capital as state government and
private offices remained shut.
Only few vehicles plied the roads because of scarcity of fuel.
Since workers of all petroleum stations in the city joined the strike
action, premium motor spirit (PMS) popularly known as petrol and other
petroleum products became very difficult to come. Investigations by THISDAY
showed that the commodities could be found with black market operators.
As a result, a litre of petrol yesterday sold for N400 in the black
market.
Prices diesel and kerosene also went up by as much margin a situation
which forced many motorists to pack their vehicles.
People who depended on electricity generating sets for power supply to
their houses or private businesses were forced shout of operation as a
result of fuel scarcity.
The acute scarcity of petroleum products, apart from forcing a few
commercial vehicle operators to double their fares also pushed up prices of
food stuff like garri, rice and yam beyond the reach of many people.
A tuber of yam that used to cost between N100 and N150 now sells for
between N200 and N350 while a cup of rice that use to cost N100 now sells
for N200.

Original_Search_Title: Mistaken MP Ken Lukyamuzi Flees Own Rally
Headline: Uganda: Mistaken MP Ken Lukyamuzi Flees Own Rally
Body_Text:
Kampala — RUBAGA South MP John Ken Lukyamuzi on Sunday fled from a rally he had
called at Nateete Police grounds in Rubaga Division Kampala, when he saw a
team of Military Police pass by, apparently on a different mission.
Lukyamuzi, clad in a cream suit, abandoned other MPs and Justice Forum
leader Kibirige Mayanja and fled with part of the crowd and hid in a nearby
yam garden. He suspected the Military Police had come to arrest him.
The Military policemen were chasing Joseph Lubega, a driver of taxi UAE
458T which was involved in an accident with an army vehicle on Masaka
road.
They ironically caught up with him a few metres from the public
rally. The embarrassed Lukyamuzi re-appeared after 30 minutes, with his
cream suit heavily caked in mud. He said experience had taught him to become
wary of security at such gatherings and would not take chances.
"As a leader, I have to lead even in running for dear life. But today, I
was going to mobilise security for my guests," a jocular Lukyamuzi told the
crowd that was slowly regrouping after the fiasco.
Present was Mbarara Municipality MP Winnie Byanyima, who told Ugandans
not to be coerced into endorsing the third term.
"I am back to work with other Ugandans to block a constitutional change
that will give Museveni another term," she said amid cheers.
Aswa county MP Reagan Okumu, Jacob Oulanyah (Omoro County) Ssebuliba
Mutumba (Kawempe South) and Nsubuga Nsambu (Makindye west) also
attended.
Lukyamuzi told the gathering that he was going to spearhead a motion
demanding President Museveni to present a comprehensive programme on how he
would effect peace talks with LRA leader Joseph Kony.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Goods, Services Up in Adamawa
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Goods, Services Up in Adamawa
Body_Text:
Yola — PRICES of goods and services have jumped up by about 200 per cent in
Adamawa State few days after the Federal Government announced a hike in
price of petroleum products. Vanguard investigation in Yola, the state
capital and its environs indicated that transport fares on intra and inter
state routes have risen by between 100 per cent to 150 per cent depending on
the distance.
For instance, Yola to Jalingo which use to cost N250 has now risen to
N400 while Yola to Numan, a distance of about 54 kilometers which hitherto
cost N80 has now been increased to N150. Yola to neighbouring Gombe which
hitherto cost N300 has now been increased to N450 by motorists. Long
distance journeys were mostly effected by the hike. For instance, Yola to
Kano, Yola to Jos which use to be N1,500 and N1,100 have now attracted
N2,000 and N1,500 respectively. The worst hit are prices of food
items. Prior to the increase of petroleum products, foreign rice which was
being sold at N4,500 per 50kg bag has now jumped to between N5,000 and
above.
Prices of other staple foods, like garri, yam, cassava, beans and even
vegetables have also recorded sharp increases thereby making life more
difficult for the ordinary Nigerian. Speaking to Vanguard over the fuel
hike, a trader Mallam Hamisu Modibbo described the increase as
unrealistic. "By this increase, the PDP led federal Government has buried
Nigerians alive as no ordinary citizen can be assured of his or her future,"
he feared. Some civil servants who spoke to Vanguard on the issue regretted
that the new hike has further compounded the plight of the average Nigerian
worker as salaries and other take emoluments have been rendered
useless.

Original_Search_Title: Business Returns to Eagle Square, Federal Secretariat
Headline: Nigeria: Business Returns to Eagle Square, Federal Secretariat
Body_Text:
Business has fully returned to the Eagle Square and parts of the Federal
Secretariat after traders were evacuated prior to the presidential
inauguration ceremony at the Eagle Square.
Abuja Trust visited the two government establishments and can report
that food sellers and other traders originally evacuated have resumed
business fully.
The food sellers mostly women were busy attending to their customers
while some of them told our reporter that they were afraid that they may not
be allowed to resume their business after the inauguration.
"We were very afraid that they may not allow us to come back but they
had assured us that we would resume business after the inauguration and they
kept their premise" one of them said.
Some of them whose wooden food boxes were damaged during the evacuation
have built new ones while the shades are now better organised and well
demarcated.
Abuja Trust also observed that the mini-market had returned. The
mini-market provides almost all civil servants could buy in a typical
Nigerian foodstuff market ranging from garri, oil, yam, meat, vegetable,
tomatoes and okro.
Our reporter gathered that most civil servants now patronise the mini
market after work and go straight home instead of passing through the
popular Wuse market or the Bakassi market to buy things.
"Your see, sometimes, one want to buy meat, tomatoes and vegetable which
are perishable and due to constant power failure people find it difficult to
keep them at home all the time and so to get them you more from the office
to market.
But now you just leave your office come down have and get what you
want.
Some ogas even send their boys in the office to come and buy things for
them," a lady told our reporter.

Original_Search_Title: Kidnappers Invade Gwarimpa
Headline: Nigeria: Kidnappers Invade Gwarimpa
Body_Text:
Residents of Kado village in Gwarimpa were last week forced to assess
the security of their children, as the crime of child kidnapping is rearing
its ugly head in the area.
Checks by our reporter revealed that two boys, of between nine and ten
years of age, whose names were not revealed for security reasons, were said
to have gone into a nearby bush to play.
One of the boys, who was a victim of kidnap was said to have wandered a
little further into the bush to ease himself.
Unknown to the boy, a kidnapper was lucking in the bush and watching for
the appropriate moment to make a move.
Unfortunately for the man, he did not know that the boys were two and
had quickly come out of his hiding and grabbed the boy and put him in a sack
which he lifted and started leaving.
The second boy who was watching was amazed and quickly raised an alarm
to the astonishment of some passers-by who immediately decided to
investigate the strange alarm which the little boy had raised.
The boy lamented that the man had put his friend into a sack and was
running away with him.
The passers-by immediately intervened and accosted the man who was seen
a little distance away carrying a sack behind his back.
When the little group had finally caught up with the man, they asked him
what was in the sack and he said it was some yam tubers and firewood which
he bought, but the boy who had followed him insisting that it was his
friend.
The group forced the man to open the sack and almost immediately, the
man tried to escape but was caught and beaten before he was handed over to
the police.
A reliable police source at Gwarimpa informed our reporter that the
police had later discovered that the man who was a member of a syndicate
that was into kidnapping people for ritual purposes.
The man was said to have confessed that he was sent by a woman to bring
a young boy to her. "She did not tell me what she wanted to do with the
boy," the man claimed.
Meanwhile, the residents have the problem of ensuring security of their
children to grapple with in addition to incessant robbery attacks, water
scarcity and erratic power supply to the estate.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Stuff Rise in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Stuff Rise in FCT
Body_Text:
There has been steady increase in the prices of foodstuff in Abuja the
Federal Capital Territory. A survey that took Abuja Trust to Wuse market and
old market and Karmo market revealed that the prices of a set of yam tubers
has increased by almost 150 per cent in the last two months a set of yam (5
tubers) which used to sell for N300 about 2 months ago, now sells for
N800.
Other stuffs like garri, white and red beans have also recorded price
increase. Red beans which used to be sold N90 per mudu now goes for N120 per
mudu, while the white one moved from N70 to N90. Rice also had its price
increased per mudu from N110 as at 2 months ago to N140, while a bag of it,
depending on the brand, ranges between N4,100 to N5,000.
The price of palm oil has equally risen per 20-litre jerican form N3,100
to N3500 while groundnut oil has increased N2,500 to N3,200. This wholesale
increment has also affected the marginal price of oil per bottle.
There have also been recognizable increase in the prices of meat. For
example, a cow, which used to be sold for between N30,000 - N35,000, now
goes for N90,00 - N100,000. According to Malam Gasbar Aliyu, a butcher at
Wuse market, the increment has been due to cost of transportation and police
duties. "We normally convey cow from Kano and Borno to Abuja, and this used
to cost us N2,000 and N4,000. Now, we spend N5,000 and N7,000 respectively,
to convey cow from Kano and Borno to Abuja. Beside, we pay policemen very
heavy amount of money on the highway as bribery."
Other traders with whom Abuja Trust spoke also attributed the increase
in prices of foodstuff in FCT to cost of transportation.
However, Malam Audu Adamu, a fruit seller told Abuja Trust that seasonal
charges have also contributed significantly to the rise in foodstuff
prices. "I however believe that in matter of 2-3 months from now, some food
items will begin to record prices fall, while some will stabilize and others
increase also."
A concerned resident, Musa Salihu told our reporter that unless
government shelves the proposed increase in prices of fuel, the
uncontrollable increase in prices of food items would provoke the poor
people to rise against law and order.

Original_Search_Title: Percy Leads Gospel Singers to Lagos Festival of Praise
Headline: Nigeria: Percy Leads Gospel Singers to Lagos Festival of Praise
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Kaduna State-based gospel musician, Panam Percy Paul, is due in
Lagos for a worship concert tagged: "Festival of Praise".
As banners of the three-day variety show are being hoisted on the
streets of Lagos, the General Overseer of the Gospel Ministries
International (GMI), organisers of the concert, Rev. Moses Kayode Omodunisi,
said praise was an unequal weapon of attracting God's favour.
Under the theme: "Bringing Down His Glory", Omodanisi said the event was
one of the Church's numerous programmes aimed at sustaining the nation's
nascent democracy and attracting God's power upon the devil's mission of
death and destruction characterised by various afflictions of the people in
this end time.
He tasked Christians to understand the dynamics of worship to be able to
understand soul lifting music, which pleases God.
"God does not eat pounded yam. His food is praise. God is so faithful
that our existence is just by His grace and as a person, when I pray, 70
percent of my utterances is praise. I am not a good singer though, but I
praise Him by His titles, I like to call him by His names. Jehovah,
Elshadai, Jehovah Shammah, Jehovah Jire and so on. Beside, we have always
had what we call 'Judah Night', otherwise known as 'praise night' once in
every two months, but more of an internal affair. This time around, we
intend to have it big and so we have invited Dr. Panam Percy Paul to
minister to the people along others", he said.
He said the minister has been so chosen because of his unique
acceptability as a gospel artist and the maturity of his music.
The festival, he said, was one of the Church's techniques of spreading
its divine ministration round Lagos, saying that this year's shower of
praise, holding at its Lagos headquarters Mechanic Village, Aswani, off
Osolo Way, Isolo, would begin with an all night praise from 10pm, Friday,
June 13. It would be followed Saturday at 3pm with a seminar to be directed
by Dr. Panam Percy.
The events, he said, will be rounded off with a praise service on
Sunday, June 15, at 8.30 am.
He gave the names of other members of the festival committee to include,
Host Pastor H. Omotayo, Pastor Bolade Maxwell Osazenhen, Choir Master and
Pastor Gabriel Ukpeh, among others, while support songs, he said, will be
rendered by the GMI Mass Choir.
Reverend Omodanisi enjoined men and women with traits of barrenness or
impotency to come and receive their deliverance at the Lagos Headquarters of
the Church every last Saturday of the month.
He said no one was created by God to be barren, but for the devil and
the sinful ways of men, advising that all those with such problems trust in
God.
He spoke about his calling, saying that his vision was to equip the
young with spiritual life and make them live in the ways of God.
He said the only way the vision of the church would be manifested was
for it to invest in human resources development, cum spiritual development,
stating that the Church has already concluded plans to establish Nursery,
Primary and Secondary schools to take care of the educational needs of
Church's pupils. One of the Church's mission, being to train the youth for
an assured future, Omodanisi said the Church would eventually float a
University of Technology.
Reverend Moses Kayode Omodanisi was a senior lecturer at the department
of Mechanical Engineering, Kaduna Polytechnic. A trained engineer, he
obtained his Masters degree in Mechanic Engineering from the University of
Detroit, Michigan State, United States of America (USA).

Original_Search_Title: Volta Region Security Problems Send Minister to Parliament
Headline: Ghana: Volta Region Security Problems Send Minister to Parliament
Body_Text:
The Volta regional minister, Nana Kwasi Owusu- Yeboah, will today appear before Parliament to respond to two urgent questions concerning the region.
The questions which dwell on the security situation in the region, are expected to be asked by the member for Ho West, Francis Aggrey Agbotse, and Dr. Kwame Ampofo for South Dayi.
The former would attempt to quiz the minister on the causes of the numerous conflicts in the region and what the regional security committee (REGSEC) is doing to ensure peace, while the latter would also ask the minister what necessitated the heightened security situation in the Peki-Tsito disputed land area and measures taken to restore peace in the area.
The conflict areas are Nkonya/Alavanyo, Nkwanta, Peki-Tsito and Abutia Kpota/Abutia Agordeke which disturbances did not only claim many precious lives, but also grossly impeded progress and development in those areas.
The region, lying across the Volta Lake, is heterogeneous in nature with over 200 dialects. It stretches from the Gulf of Guinea in the south and joins the Northern region at Damongo, some 400 kilometres away, and is without doubt referred to as the yam basket of Ghana.
The Volta region which used to be called Trans-Volta Togoland (TVT) before independence can boast of the largest man-made lake in the world and has the highest human settlement in the country at Amedzofe standing at 2,800 feet above sea level, which makes it the coolest spot in the world.
That is not all; the region also has the highest fresh waterfall in the country, Wli Falls, as well as the highest mountain, Afadzato, all in the Hohoe district.
Recently, a member of the Ho district assembly, suggested to the government to take over the Peki-Tsito disputed land area for the establishment of a factory or university or military camp to end the conflict.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Hits Abuja Badly
Headline: Nigeria: Inflation Hits Abuja Badly
Body_Text:
Lagos — Beans, one of the staple food in the country, is among the major food
items, whose prices have risen in the last few months.
A News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) survey of major markets in Abuja showed
that the prices of beans, rice, yams, cooking oil and other condiments have
increased following the onset of the rainy season.
A measure of white beans, which sold for N65 in February, now goes for
N85, while brown beans, which sold at N80, now costs N100.
A measure of various brands of rice, such as Golden Caprice, Caprice,
CIC (white rice), "Tomato" rice, which were sold for N120 and N110,
respectively now cost N150 and N140.
A 50 kilogram bag of the brand, which usually sold between N3,800 and
N4,000, now goes for N4,100 and N4,300 respectively.
A measure of locally produced rice, which was sold for N100, now costs
N120, while a 200kg bag of the same rice is now sold for N14,000.
NAN reports that the prices of cooking oil have also increased, a bottle
of palm oil, which was sold at N110, now costs N130, while the same measure
of vegetable oil, which was sold for N100, now costs N140.
Locally produced groundnut oil, which was sold for N120 now sells for
N150, while a 20 litre of cooking oil now costs between N3,200 and N3,400
respectively.
A crate of egg, which was sold for N350, in recent times now sells for
N400.
A kilogram of beef, which was sold for N350, now costs N400.
A measure of flour, which was sold for N70, now costs N80, the 50kg bag,
which was sold for N2,200, now costs N2,700.
A small tuber of "Onitsha" yam, which was sold for N50 now sells for N80
and a medium size of "Pepa" yam, which was sold for N90, now sells for
N140.
NAN however reports that the price of garri has remained stable, with a
measure of "white" and "yellow" garri still selling for N50 and N60

Original_Search_Title: Police Warn On Circulation of Fake N500 Notes
Headline: Nigeria: Police Warn On Circulation of Fake N500 Notes
Body_Text:
The Nigerian Police over the weekend warned residents of the Federal Capital Territory of the circulation of fake N500 notes in the city, advising them to be vigilant in transactions involving exchange of N500 notes.
According to a statement from force headquarters, the police has discovered that some residents mostly in Karmo, Idu and Gwagwa have been alleged to be in possession of large sums of fake N500 notes.
The police stated that such notes were often mixed with genuine N500 notes during transaction especially for buying of fuel and GSM recharge cards.
It therefore warned fuel attendants and GSM recharge card dealers to be wary of such notes and ensure that such fake notes are do not circulate.
The statement asked patriotic Nigerians to report suspects involved in the illegal dealing to the police.
The police assured the residents that it was working hard to get those involved.
The police warning is coming on the heels of complaint over fake notes in circulation resulting in fist-cuffs in some cases between passengers and conductors.
Our investigations revealed that, following the alarm, some residents have refused, outright, to transact business involving N500 notes.
"Like some of these women selling yams, they don't accept the notes if they are to give you some balance. Except you buy exactly N500 worth or N1000 naira, they will not collect the notes" a resident told Abuja Trust.
According to him, the people are aware that such people using the fake notes buy goods worth N50 to N100 and pay with a N500 note expecting to get genuine money worth N450 or N400 as the case may be.
Abuja Trust spoke with some conductors who complained that the development has often resulted in argument between them and their passengers.
"The other day somebody boarded a bus from Idu to Karmo which is just N20 and gave men a N500 note. I told him that he would have asked me if I had change before entering. He started arguing only for us to discover that the money was fake," a conductor told Abuja Trust.
According to him, most of the conductors can distinguish between a fake note and genuine one.

Original_Search_Title: N26m Property Destroyed in Communal Clash
Headline: Nigeria: N26m Property Destroyed in Communal Clash
Body_Text:
Lagos — The clash between Inyimagu and Agbaja communities in Izzi clan of Ebonyi State has left at least 931 persons homeless and over 170 houses burnt with yam barns and other property valued at about N26.6 million.
This was disclosed by the director of Ebonyi State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA), Mr. Hyacinth Ote. Ote spoke with newsmen against the backdrop of his agency's involvement in rehabilitating the victims of the crisis.
The SEMA boss disclosed that only two lives were lost and about 10 injured victims still hospitalised, adding that calm had since returned to the feuding communities following police intervention and that of elders of the two communities.
Ote said that SEMA which was recently inaugurated in the state is facing its first test with crisis, stating that the agency has so far made detailed documentation of the destruction in the area in addition to individual presentation of losses filed by the victims.
He disclosed that relief materials would be supplied to the victims by the Federal Government through the National Emergency Management Agency NEMA as well as from the Ebonyi State government.

Original_Search_Title: UN Awards Journalists And Media Institutions for Information Development in Africa
Headline: Africa: UN Awards Journalists And Media Institutions for Information Development in Africa
Body_Text:
New York — The United Nations Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) and its partners today presented Awards to prominent media practitioners and organizations that have made significant contributions to the development of information and communications technologies (ICTs) in Africa.
An initiative of ECA, the African Information Society Initiative (AISI) Media Awards aim to promote journalism which contributes to a better understanding of the information society in Africa. They are given to individual journalists and media institutions based in the continent.
ECA said the sustainable build up of an information society in African countries requires the development of information resources to reflect the needs of each and every sector and stakeholder. The information society also requires that knowledge be disseminated and used by stakeholders, the public at large and disenfranchised groups such as women and the poor to make rational choices and exercise human rights.
Internationally recognized African journalists, media researchers, and experts on ICT for Development issues selected this year's winners: ICT Focus from Ethiopia, Radio Afrique Espoir from Benin, Association Yam Pukri from Burkina Faso, Realities Magazine from Tunisia, Women of Uganda Network, African Languages Technology from Nigeria, Etienne Tasse from Cameroon, Salif Sanogo from Mali, Takawira Musara from Zimbabwe, IT and Telecom Digest from Nigeria, Bianca Wright from South Africa and Brenda Zulu from Zambia.
The Open Society Initiative for Southern Africa (OSISA), the Open Society Initiative for Western Africa (OSIWA), the International Development Research Centre (IDRC) and the International Institute for Communication and Development (IICD) sponsor the awards.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Sellers Clamour for Shops At Proposed Markets
Headline: Nigeria: Yam Sellers Clamour for Shops At Proposed Markets
Body_Text:
The Chairman of the Yam Sellers Association at the New Central Market (Bakassi), Alhaji Suleiman Abubakar has appealed to the authorities entrusted with the allocation of shops at the proposed Wuye and Garki markets to include his members.
The chairman made the appeal in a chat with Abuja Trust expressing his dissatisfaction with the allocations in which none of his members were included in the ongoing distribution of stalls at the proposed markets.
According to him, despite the immense contributions his members made to the growth and distribution of the present market, they are still discriminated against in many facets of the market activities.
Alhaji Suleiman cited an instance by saying, "it is unfortunate that we are treated as second class citizens. Recently, butchers in this market were provided with a loan to support and improve them their financial base, but none of my members has gotten the loan."
