Alhaji Suleiman said the authorities should realize the significance of their trade adding that yam is the most patronized item in the whole of new market and as such they should be considered in allocation of market stalls.

Original_Search_Title: Beyond Election Malpractices And Irregularities
Headline: Nigeria: Beyond Election Malpractices And Irregularities
Body_Text:
Lagos — One Yoruba proverbial saying is that, if you do not because of yam take oil, you must because of oil take yam. The relationship between yam and oil is such that it cannot be strained without having some negative effects for both sides.
In the same vein, the application of the Yoruba idiom to the current environmental conditionings of Nigeria's 2003 general elections clearly shows the organic relationship between election malpractices and irregularities, on the one hand, and national cohesion and security, on the other. However, the discussion of the 2003 elections has not underscored the need for national unity, security and development. This is most unfortunate.
Some international election monitors, as well as some defeated candidates have complained that the elections have so far been characterized by electoral malpractices and electoral irregularities. As rightly pointed out by Prof. Ben Nwabueze last week Thursday (The Guardian, May 8, p.8), "electoral practices are to be distinguished from mere electoral irregularities, which relate to noncompliance with prescribed procedure at election, like late commencement of polling, polling outside the statutorily stipulated time, late delivery of electoral materials. Electoral malpractices and electoral irregularities differ not only in their nature but also in their legal consequences; whilst the latter do not in general, invalidate an election, the former emphatically does".
Perhaps more importantly, Prof. Nwabueze said election rigging is a subversion of the constitution, it is treason, robbery of the right of the people to participate in their own government, and the "gravest offence that can be committed against the constitution and people of Nigeria". Consequently, it is a "mocking irony, that a call should have been made for the arrest for treason, not of those guilty of the massive rigging being complained of but of those making the complaint and calling on the people to protest against it".
In other words, there is nothing wrong with complaining about electoral malpractices and irregularities. I cannot agree more here. However, I have problems with the manner of the complaint, with the way it is expressed and with the intended effects. The chief complainant, General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd), threatened national cohesion by calling for "mass action" and by asking the international community to derecognise the Obasanjo government come May 29, 2003.
Without any scintilla of doubt, the constitutional method of complaint in the context of election disputes is to seek redress in the court. By asking the international community not to relate with the Government of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, the ANPP flag bearer, General Buhari, has ab initio assumed that the court would rule in his favour. There is nothing to suggest that the variables being considered by General Buhari are those that will retain the attention of the learned men of the court. Men of the legal profession are indeed learned because they create new bases for the adoption of new laws by interpreting laws in unexpected ways and loopholes in the interpretation and application of laws in such a way that complainants also become the accused or respondents.
Besides, most of the countries being called upon by General Buhari have a policy of not recognizing regimes or governments but states. Although in international practice, a nonrecognized government may not be able to enter into official relations with the Government withholding recognition, there are several factors explaining why the international community would not want to derecognise the Obasanjo government.
Chief Olusegun Obasanjo is an accomplished international player and a seriously needed ally in the implementation of the principle of "responsibility to protect" in international relations. His experience as a military man and as a statesman is needed in the maintenance of regional security. No matter the way electoral malpractices and irregularities is perceived, the need to protect some supranational interests, especially in terms of global peace will always make the international community to condone whatever electoral excesses we may be having in Nigeria. In fact, Europe and America are the international headquarters of election rigging. The only point of difference, when compared with that of Nigeria, is that election rigging in developed countries is very scientific and devoid of violence. Rigging is always fair and not excessive there.
There is also the factor of individual personality. General Buhari did not have good image internationally when he was Head of State. His foreign policy was confrontational and that was why he had diplomatic rows with several countries. Even when he engaged in a verbal war with Britain over Umaru Dikko when he was created as a diplomatic baggage, Margaret Thatcher, then Prime Minister, simply intimidated.
General Buhari by threatening to uncover all the names of Nigerian leaders, especially the military, having questionable accounts in Britain, if General Buhari did not promptly stop his "bullying". That ended the story.
In fact, Europe and America are not at all interested in a disorderly Nigeria. Geopolitically, Nigeria is very important to them in subSaharan Africa. Nigeria plays host to their economic investments. In their thinking, a friendly Nigerian leader is what is needed. If Europeans and Americans were asked to make a choice between Obasanjo and Buhari, the choice cannot but be clear: Obasanjo. This is why, in spite of the electoral complaints and the recourse to election tribunals, world leaders have already taken their final decision, which is to recognize the reelection of President Obasanjo and to give the election international legitimacy by congratulating him and pouring encomiums on the people of Nigeria for generally eschewing violence during the election.
It is also important to put the understanding of malpractice in election and irregularity in implementation of the Electoral Act No.4 of 2002 in the general context of election petitions. First, Part III of Chapter VII of the 1999 Constitution provides for the establishment of Election Tribunals with original mandate to determine petitions brought before them and to the exclusion of any other court. The responsibility of the election tribunals is to determine the validity of an election into the National and State House of Assembly (Section 285). As regards the determination of the validity of the election of a president or his vice, Section 239 gives the jurisdictional competence to the Court of Appeal.
In this regard, Section 134(1) of the Electoral Act No.4 of 2002 provides thus: "an election may be questioned on any of the following grounds, that is to say (a) that a person whose election is questioned was at the time of the election, not qualified to contest the elections; (b) that the election was invalid by reasons of corrupt practices or noncompliance with the provisions of this Act; (c) that the respondent was not duly elected by majority of lawful votes cast at the elections; and (d) that the petitioner or its candidate was validly nominated but was unlawfully excluded from the election".
The main problem here, as noted by Babatunde Fashanu, a Senior Advocate of Nigeria, is that the non qualification of candidates to contest elections has been the main object of dispute in the courts and that the courts have been "emphatic in holding that election tribunals or courts have no jurisdiction to delve into aspects relating to nomination of candidates as electoral body's decision is final on this". Thus, what is the proof that complaints would be admissible for determination by the courts? On malpractice how easy will it be to prove? Can it also be done quickly?
That election monitors and defeated candidates have complained that the elections were marred by malpractices and irregularities constitute only one side of the equation. The other side, which is even more important, is the implication of the complaint, the way it is being expressed, for national unity and security. Put differently, a school of thought advocates "mass action" and "violence". This school posits that Nigeria be made ungovernable come May 29, 2003. Will a new foundation for the destabilization and disintegration of the country not be laid with the adoption of a violent approach to alleged election rigging?
Another school of thought has suggested the establishment of a transition government while a third school has called for a government of national unity. Will the establishment of a national government or a transition government change political attitudes in Nigeria? Are politicians truly committed to a united, virile and developed Nigeria?
In conclusion therefore, there is need for greater caution in the handling of election petitions June 12 1993 election was adjudged the most free and fair in Nigeria's political history, yet the results were annulled. The 2003 elections were more than 50% free and fair, some Nigerians are not only call for the outright cancellation of the election results but also the dismissal of returning officers and the reconstitution of the INEC as conditions for peace in Nigeria. This is a wrong approach to the problem. Rather than destroying Nigeria, defeated and aggrieved politicians should be strategizing now on how to prevent future occurrence of election rigging in Nigeria. They will become better politicians by so doing.

Original_Search_Title: Lesotho Host Regional Military Netball Champs
Headline: Lesotho Host Regional Military Netball Champs
Body_Text:
Maseru — The Lesotho Defence Force (LDF) host the second International Military Sports Council (CISM)/ East South Africa Liaison Office (ESALO) Netball Regional Military Championship games ending on the 9th May 2003.
Speaking at the opening ceremony, LDF Chief of Staff, Major General Sam Makoro mentioned that the commanders of the forces should commit their good will for sporting activities so that the duty of the player should match their responsibility as sportsmen. He stated that the commanders should also strive to make games genuinely regional in terms of countries participating in the tournaments.
According to the LetÂolo Kholoane, the Vice President Lesotho Military Sports Council, the purpose of the championship is to foster military friendship through sports in the international forces. He said the success of the games would depend on the commitment of the individuals, organising committee, score keepers, team managers and empires by providing a conducive environment for competitors.
He mentioned that CISM/ESALO should be used as a tool to promote peace, happiness, physical fitness, and cooperation among the military regionally, continental and global thus illuminating suspicions and mistrust within the army.
"We Lesotho Military Sports Council are dedicated to sporting activities, not only within the LDF but also by taking part in the nationally organized sports activities with our Military Athletes," he said.
The CISM-ISALO Representative Lieutenant Colonel Yam Steinbache said Netball provides opportunity for entertainment and unity among sports personalities and the forces and teamwork within the army. He said the high level of sports could prevail within the forces only if the commanders could commit themselves to sports by taking part, and encouraging sportsman.
He said the Lesotho Netball Championship games should be taken as a unifying factor for future generations and the international forces. He mentioned that sports recruits highly disciplined people within the army and their communities.
The Botswana Defense Force cancelled its participation in the challenge. Lesotho plays against Republic of South Africa in the opening game.

Original_Search_Title: Maitama FHA Occupants Condemn Structural Design
Headline: Nigeria: Maitama FHA Occupants Condemn Structural Design
Body_Text:
Occupants of the Federal Housing Estate in Maitama have condemned the structural design of the apartments as built by the authorities, saying, the design is bringing basic facilities into decay.
The occupants, while narrating their ordeals, urged the Federal Housing Authority (FHA) not to repeat the design of apartments built in Maitama anywhere in Abuja.
Investigation by Abuja Trust revealed that most of the apartment built by FHA are now in deplorable condition.
Some of the affected occupants told our reporter that the untimely decay of the apartments in the area was due to lack of foresight by FHA at the initial stage of construction.
Though FHA had sold out the facility to private firms that are now responsible for its maintenance, some of the staff of these organisations lamented that construction materials used by FHA were substandard coupled with conduit wiring and running of water pipes inside walls which consequently weakens structure.
According to a staff of National University Commission, Mr. Ridwan Abdul Salami, who spoke with our reporter, FHA should be blamed for structural defect of most blocks in the area, adding that the connection of water system among flats was in bad condition which usually caused blockage, resulting to gushing out of water on the walls.
"The water system or pipes were passed inside the building I don't know how Federal Housing Engineers at the initial stage planned these building," he said.
Mr. Redwan added that in order to prevent blockage of water system, some of the organisations that bought the FHA buildings in the area were from time to time taking necessary steps to bursting walls as a way of bringing out water pipes and cables for proper functioning.
However, apart from the lack of foresight by the designers, Mr. Ridwan, who has been residing in one of the blocks in the area for the past eight years pointed out congestion of people in a flat, pounding of yam among others as some factors that are contributing to the weakness of the facility.
In a similar reaction, Mr. Lateef Salami, who works with Ministry of Petroleum Resources and who leaves in one of the affected blocks, said FHA had one time admitted its fault during a meeting with occupants but said that it could no longer demolish the blocks after construction.
Mr. Lateef further stressed that FHA was no longer in control of any block except for the monitoring of available spaces and illegal construction in the area.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Food Items On the Increase -Survey
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Food Items On the Increase -Survey
Body_Text:
As Christians get set to celebrate Easter, prices of most staple food items in Abuja have risen.
Surveying some markets in the city indicated that items affected by the price increase included garri, beans, palm oil, groundnut oil and chicken.
A bottle of groundnut oil now costs N150 as against the former price of N120 while a small keg of groundnut oil, which sold for N 600, is now N 650.
A bag of beans now sells for N7,200 as against its old price of N6,800 while a measure of the same commodity is now N80 and N100 for the white and red beans as against their former prices of N50 and N70 respectively.
Live chicken which was purchased for slightly lower prices now sell for prices ranging from N600 to N900 while a kilogramme of frozen chicken which sold for N220 now sells for N270.
A kilogramme of beef, which was hitherto N300, now goes for N400 at the Wuse and Garki village Monday markets.
A big tuber of yam now sells for between N250 and N300, medium size, between N180 and N150 while the small size tuber is sold for between N100 and N120.
According to the survey, condiments such as tomatoes, pepper and onions were also affected by the increase.
Prices of ingredients for salad such as a small size cabbage which sold for N60 as at last week, now goes for N100 while the big one which sold for N120 now costs between N170 and N200.
A bunch of lettuce which sold for N10 now sells for between N20 and N30.
The survey also showed that a measure of rice now sells for N160 even as the price for a 50kg bag ranges from between N4,200 and N4,800, depending on the brand.
According to our further reports, the markets were however, unusually bustling with women and children trying to make last minute purchases.

Original_Search_Title: South West : Appeal Court President Denounces Petition Writing
Headline: Cameroon: South West : Appeal Court President Denounces Petition Writing
Body_Text:
Buea (Fako) — The President of the South West Court of Appeal, Buea, Chief Justice Lucy Asuagbor has told the judiciary that "every benefit carries along an equivalent burden of responsibility". Chief Justice Asuagbor was responding to her colleagues of the South West Judiciary corps recently during a new-year wishing ceremony at the Buea Appeal Court premises.
The ceremony, second of its kind in Buea after last year, saw the presentation of new year-wishes to the Appeal Court President and the procureur-general by Magistrates, registrars, Sheriff-bailiffs, barristers and the auxiliary staff.
Responding to demands of better treatment for emoluments, mission allowances and security of magistrates, chief Justice Asuagbor promised to act within legal means to improve on their lot. She, however, admonished her colleagues to desist from petitions and to embrace dialogue in sorting out their differences.
The Appeal Court president listed out some of the achievements made in the South West judiciary including the erection of the Menji (Lebialem) Court of First Instance and High Court with plans to equip them. The acquisition of computers, photocopiers and vehicles for enclaved jurisdictions was re-echoed by chief Justice Asuagbor as a booster for the South West judiciary.
The Appeal Court president announced some six seminars on Francophone OHADA (Business law) intended for the various Anglophone judicial corps this year. The seminars, she explained, would lay to rest the question of the applicability of OHADA law that had lingered long in one of the two Anglophone provinces of Cameroon. Some of the laws that have remained a headcache to the judiciary west of the Mungo include the CIMA law on Insurance, the CEMAC Maritime Code in addition to the OHADA law on business. Legal experts had noted their gradual process of harmonisation in the recent years as yielding fruits.
In an earlier statement on behalf of his colleagues, the vice president to the court of Appeal, Justice Macaulay Moma urged the Procureur-general and the Appeal Court President to "eat with a fork and not a spoon so that we can also enjoy what is left over". He referred to their bosses as having the yam and the knife to make the judicial family happy or not.

Original_Search_Title: They Won, They Lost
Headline: Nigeria: They Won, They Lost
Body_Text:
Lagos — Last Saturday's election threw up new faces and shatteed the ambition of
others. In addition to those who outrightly lost their seats in the National
Assembly, other political leaders equally lost their bases to new
godfathers. The pattern of victory by the three main political parties has
re-drawn the political map of the country.
While the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) ate deep into states controlled
by the Alliance for Democracy (AD) , the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP)
made in roads into PDP areas leaving the AD as the net loser. With the
exception of Lagos state, the PDP swept virtually all the other AD states
even as the PDP itself lost grounds in key Northern States like Kano and
Kaduna to the ANPP. The main opposition party made serious incursions into
Plateau while conceding part of its hold on Kwara State to the PDP.
Victims of the upsets included Senators Olabiyi Durojaiye,Wahab Dosunmu,
Seye Ogunlewe, Femi Okunrounmu and others in the South-West. Former governor
Obong Akpan Isemin also lost his bid for the senate. Senator Bob Nwanunu who
was in the outgoing senate representing Abia central senatorial district on
the platform of the PDP switched camps to the United Nigeria Peoples Party
(UNPP) and lost. One of the political godfathers whose hold is slipping is
Senator Jim Nwobodo in Enugu state. Nwobodo who claimed to have made Dr
Chimaroke Nnamani Governor in Enugu State in 1999 has more or less lost
grip.
The Winners...
Olusegun Obasanjo
Former United States Vice President, Nelson Rockefeller, once said no
politician can claim to be worth the name except he has worked with the
local people and won elections with their help. Perhaps, it is for this
reason that people dismiss President Olusegun Obasanjo when they talk of
politicians of the moment. Though the man is an elected President, his party
did badly in his home base.
However, with the result of last Saturday's National Assembly elections
in which the President led his party into taking 13 out of 18 senatorial
seats in the South West, nobody can now dismiss him as a floating
politician. President Obasanjo has eventually arrived as a politician with a
base, a man who controls the grassroots in his area and one whose name can
be invoked to achieve victory. Incidentally, Obasanjo's PDP took over in the
South West at a time when his closest rival, Muhammadu Buhari looked set to
deliver the whole of the Northwest to the All Nigeria Peoples Party
(ANPP). The President has therefore created a base from which he would
operate. He now has a solid platform of support with national assembly
members who can help him in times of trouble having won the election with
his name.
Muhammadu Buhari
But for the entry of Major General Muhammadu Buhari (rtd) into the
presidential race, it would have been a walk-over for President Olusegun
Obasanjo. His entry not only electrified the political atmosphere, it has
helped to shore up the popularity of the ANPP in the Far North. Last
Saturday's National Assembly results was a testimony of sorts. Nigeria's
democracy has benefitted from Buhari as he has injected credibility,
character and a sense of keen contest into the presidential race and the
polity at large.
Don Etiebet
The easiest prediction to make some two years ago was that the All
Peoples Party (APP) was on its way to the political gallows. That was
correct. With defections to other parties and a party leadership that was
nowhere to be found, APP actually died. From the phoenix came a change of
name and leadership. The All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) was born and
Governor Attahiru Bafarawa of Sokoto State was appointed to head the
Caretaker Committee.
The party rose from the dead - literally. By the time the presidential
convention was held in January, the party had become a new creature - old
things gone, new things on board. It produced Chief Don Etiebet as National
Chairman and a new and vigorous campaign was launched to wrest the
presidency from the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). Although, Etiebet did
not deliver his state or the South-South to the ANPP, the general
performance of the party in the National Assembly elections, especially in
the Far North, is a clear indication that the PDP, for the first time, has a
match. It won in Kano and Kaduna, and took control of some PDP seats in
Jigawa, Sokoto, Borno and Plateau States. But it lost Kwara.
Audu Ogbeh
As National Chairman of the PDP, Chief Audu Ogbeh could easily be said
to be a winner. He assumed the mantle of leadership of the party at a time
of turbulence, when the party was torn by internal strife and mutual
suspicion. Ogbeh, at the time he came brought some measure of credibility to
the party, such that those who wanted to quit, had a rethink. Although the
party became a victim of self-inflicted crisis in the manner it imposed
candidates after the primaries, that it was able to put its house in order
is a credit to Ogbeh.
PDP
The ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) lived up to its pre-election
boast that it would sweep the polls and even poach the states held by the
rival Alliance for Democracy (AD). Although many are protesting the nature
of the landslide in the South West in particular, PDP prepared the ground
for what happened last Saturday by entering into an agreement with AD. While
AD relaxed its guard, PDP went on the onslaught and left AD vulnerable. If
the PDP will overcome the protest that would trail its victory at the polls,
the party would have consolidated. For the victory it has recorded so far,
the party deserves to be a winner.
Olusola Saraki:
"Too close to call." That was the verdict of most political analysts
ahead of last Saturday's legislative elections in Kwara State. Oloye's iron
grip on the state, many thought, has been pried loose by the incumbent
governor, Muhammed Lawal, who had combined with Senator Ishaq Salman of the
ANPP and other political stalwarts within the party in a battle to
'demystify' the Oloye. The battle was fierce, and the stakes high. However,
at the end of voting on Saturday, Saraki was able to prove that in Kwara he
is still the indisputable political grandmaster. His daughter, Gbemisola,
won by a landslide to put an end to Salman's restive stay in the
Senate. However, the elder Saraki still has one last battle to fight. If his
son, Bukola, wins on Saturday, his opponents have every reason to begin to
sing, 'long live the king.'
Bola Tinubu
Few days before the elections, only a few people would be willing to bet
against AD's victory in the southwest. Despite the crisis that has rocked
the party in the last four years, many believe that the Afenifere factor is
still strong enough to keep the party on top. But the results proved
bookmakers wrong. At the end of Saturday, AD had lost all its states, except
Lagos, which remains tightly under the party's control. For this, the state
governor, Asiwaju Bola Tinubu has been able to prove that he is really on
the ground and in charge despite the stiff challenge by the PDP.
Chimaroke Nnamani
The Son becomes a man. Before last Saturday's election, Chimaroke
Nnamani could be said to be fighting the battle of his life. Up against two
formidable forces: his erstwhile political godfather, Senator Jim Nwobodo,
who is also the presidential candidate of the UNPP, and the Catholic
institution in the state. Late 2001, a band of youth invaded the Catholic
Adoration Ground in Enugu. The church leadership believed the invaders were
sent by the governor. For this 'crime', the church believes he does not
deserve a second term. Last week's elections were to be the 'day of
judgement' for the governor. But in the end, the governor's party, PDP,
clinched two of the three Senatorial seats in the state. With this, Nnamani
was able to prove that he has grown to become his own man. Next weekend's
election will however be the final test.
INEC/Guobadia
Before now, the INEC could not be said to be popular among
Nigerians. Its failure to conduct the local government elections last year,
its refusal to register new parties until compelled by the Supreme Court,
its inability to comply with the 2002 Electoral Act were seen as deliberate
acts to favour the government in power. All that has changed with the
orderly and peaceful elections. Not even late voting and pockets of violence
in some states like Rivers, Bayelsa, Delta and Edo could detract from the
success of the polls. And as confirmed by several commentators and foreign
observers and monitors, the Abel Guobadia-led INEC has done a good job
considering the fact that historically, conducting civilian- to-civilian
elections in Nigeria has never been successful. However, voices of protests
are being raised predictably from the AD and ANPP. Is this the usual
Nigerian style of not accepting defeat gracefully or was the election really
flawed?
Tafa Balogun/ Police
The police as led by Inspector-General, Tafa Balogun, deserves
commendation for the success of the poll. Against earlier fears, there were
no incidents grave enough to mar the credibility of the elections.
That the police would do a good job was not unexpected given its
excellent performance during the parties primaries in Abuja earlier in the
year. Tafa Balogun deserves special kudos for his impartiality considering
the nation's experience in 1983, when the then I.G was accused of deploying
the entire police machinery to aid the ruling party to rig the
elections.
The Voters: Keeping Faith Despite All Odds.
Nothing, perhaps, accords an election integrity more than huge voters'
turn-out. And that is precisely what the electorate gave last
Saturday. Regardless of the stature or strategy of candidates and the forces
behind them, there would be no elections if the people didn't go out to
vote.
The enthusiasm of the voters was not spurred by the performance of those
they voted for in 1999. The incumbent National Assembly spent more of its
time and energy on wranglings with the executive and staging one drama after
the other about scandals within the chambers. Last year, for instance, the
national budget could not be passed by the lawmakers.The campaign of those
struggling to supplant them was drab. Some never even bothered to
campaign. Yet the voters came out in their numbers to give the process
legitimacy. They are the ultimate winners.
The Losers...
Umar Na'Abba...
For a long time, the man has been fighting a battle of his life. He has
been described severally as a cat with nine lives. That he is still Speaker
of the Federal House of Representatives may be a surprise to himself and
everybody close to him. It appears his opponents finally waited for him at
the polls. One lesson Na'Abba may have learnt by now is that it is not easy
to fight against the establishment, no matter how strong you are.
In the days leading to the election, the PDP in Kano State pulled the
last sling. Many thought that as usual, the shot was going to miss him. But
he bowed this time around - a loser.
Jim I. Nwobodo
It appears that the fight between Jim Nwobodo and his "political
godson", Dr Chimaroke Nnamani has been won and lost. What will happen on
Saturday may be the final rites. Early in the Administration, the duo parted
ways and declared war on each other. Jim took charge of the greater number
of the State Legislators and made the house unmanageable. Everyone had
thought that the final showdown was going to be this general election as
both sides had on several occassions threatened fire and
brimstone. Surprisingly, Nwobodo took to the costly mistake of election
boycotts. A cleaver way to save face?
Emeka Odumegwu Ojukwu
The self-styled Igbo leader has lost another war. Everybody had thought
that Ojukwu's entry into the race was going to generally change the
political equation in the South Eastern States. Many thought he was going to
be the proverbial god that has the yam and the knife. Results at the last
election have shown that Ojukwu could only be a crowd puller for rallies,
without the knock-out punch. He has this coming Saturday to clear his
image.
AD/AFENIFERE
The AD/Afenifere is the greatest loser in last Saturday's polls. In all
the South West States, with the exception of Lagos, the PDP made a clean
sweep of the seats.
The erosion of the South West traditional base of AD/Afenifere will lead
to the emergence of a new power block in Yorubaland under the leadership of
younger men. Is this the end of the politics of herd mentality where all
Yorubas troop into one political camp on the instruction of a powerful
leadership clique? They emerged flagbearers through a selection process
conducted by a corterie of Yoruba leadership under Chief Abraham
Adesanya. Their election was more or less a diktat from the Pan Yoruba
organisation, Afenifere.
AD Governors
In the past four years, the AD, the political puppet of Afenifere was
sphintered into two implacable factions. Chief Bola Tinubu of Lagos, more
than any south-west governor, symbolised the split, he on one side, the
Afenifere leaders on the other. He thus became the test case.
While he resisted the PDP onslaught successfully, his four colleagues
crumbled completely. Is this a foretaste of things to come this Saturday?

Original_Search_Title: Forced to Go Home: Rwandan Immigrants in Tanzania
Headline: Africa: Forced to Go Home: Rwandan Immigrants in Tanzania
Body_Text:
Kigali — In early March this year, over 2,300 Rwandans arrived at the Kibungo
transit camp, a centre that receives Rwandan refugees returning from
Tanzania.
These were not the usual refugees. They were Rwandans living within
the local community near the border and had never been in the camps.
When the governments of Rwanda and Tanzania had signed an agreement
to repatriate all Rwandan nationals living in Tanzania as refugees,
these people had not considered themselves part of the target group.
Over several weeks in early March, they had been identified and
evicted as illegal immigrants.
"Most of them left Rwanda in the 1980's, some of them were born
there," Odette Nyiramirimo, the Secretary of State for Social Affairs
in the Ministry of Local Administration told Internews.
When we arrived at the camp, the returnees were placing their
complaints before Nyiramirimo and James Kimonyo, the governor of
Kibungo province.
Internews spoke to some of them.
"I was grazing livestock; they came and beat me up. In the confusion,
I was taken one way and the livestock in another. They took the money
I had in my pocket, and told me, 'Rwandan go home'. They took us to a
school compound and when we were many of us they put us in a pick up
and brought us to Rwanda," a bewildered young man told Internews.
"I do not know Rwanda, I was born in Tanzania, I do not know what to
do," he adds.
The President of the National Repatriation Commission, Sheik Abdul
Karim Harerimana told Internews that the move by the Tanzanian
government caught more than the evictees by surprise." We had not
anticipated this. We asked them to stop the process for some time so
that we can talk with them and work out the modalities of how it
should be done," Harerimana explained.
Internews asked the Tanzanian Ambassador to Rwanda, General Martin
Mwakalindile, the rationale behind the evictions. He explained that
the forcible return of persons categorized as illegal immigrants to
their countries of origin was a national exercise not limited to
Rwandans alone.
"We have very many refugees, Rwandans, Burundians, Somalis and
others. Like all other countries in this region we also have a lot of
illegal immigrants.
Unfortunately, these illegal immigrants are often linked to criminal
activities. They take advantage of the presence of genuine refugees.
In this case, we had Rwandans who were here illegally, and they had
to go home. We are going to continue with this campaign,"
Mwakalindile stated.
At the camp, the evicted Rwandans had barely enough cover from the
rain that pelted them during our visit. Few had umbrellas or warm
clothes.
The reception centre where they were gathered consists of two main
tents and two large open air shelters used for item distribution. On
one side are several make-shift toilets.
Inside the tents women are preparing the midday meals. Most pots have
yams mixed with bananas, or maize and beans. The fires have to be lit
outside, or in a small area in one tent with earth dug cooking
stoves. Due to the congestion, most of the food has to be tended in
the rain.
"There is a lot of pain here; there is also a lot of confusion. The
people here feel that they have been treated as criminals. The
eviction was not handled very well, so we are talking to the
Tanzanian government to see how to handle this better next time,"
Nyiramirimo told Internews.
If the adults are upset, the children seem to be oblivious about it.
They play in the mud and rain, while others queue up at the United
Nations High Commission for Refugees (UNHCR) truck to fetch water.
These are the lucky ones.
"I left behind two children. One is six and the other is five. My
husband was away. He had gone to see a family who were recently
bereaved. I do not know where he is. When they picked me up I had
gone to fetch water. I want to go back and see if the children have
rejoined their father, I do not know what has happened to them," one
woman told the officials.
She is not alone. Several other women in the group have also lost
children and some have been separated with their spouses.
Nyiramirimo tries to reassure them. "Please register yourself, we
talked to the Tanzanian authorities and those of you who left young
children and property will have a period in which you can go and
check on your property.
Those of you who want to come back can come back, those of you who
would prefer to stay in Tanzania must get the proper papers allowing
you to do so," she explained.
Mwakalindile admits that the forced repatriation may not been handled
appropriately.
"We are investigating the allegations that some people were
maltreated. It is possible that some of the officials treated the
people badly for whatever reason; these are going to be punished," he
promised.
He however explains that there are cases where the allegations made
are untrue. "Some said they had lived in Tanzania for over 20 years,
but it was later found that the papers they had were not in order.
What we have told them is if they want to stay in Tanzania they have
to go through the legal process and get their papers. There were
allegations that naturalized Rwandans have been forcefully
repatriated, but I think that these are few mistakes; most members of
the group were illegal immigrants" he added.
The evicted Rwandans were given plastic sheeting, blankets and
cooking pots as well as food to last three months by UNHCR although
they do not fall under the organisation's mandate. They will be taken
to their original districts where the local officials will resettle
them. But that poses a problem. Those born in Tanzania have no idea
where to resettle. Moreover in a small country facing population
pressure, they fear their return could spark land conflicts.
"I have lived out of Rwanda for so long, I am afraid that if I show
up at home and try to ask for land from my brothers we will end up in
a conflict, please advise me what to do," one evictee asks
Nyiramirimo.
"For now, try to talk to your family members and if that fails then
go to the district officials and see if there is communal land that
they can assign you. But even if you do not get land you can find a
way of earning a living. There is a lot of work to be done in
Rwanda," she tells him.
"Some people tell us that we should leave those who are outside to
stay there since we are a small country and our resources are meager,
but we do not agree with them," Harerimana tells Internews. "Every
Rwandan has a right to come back home, Rwanda may be small but there
is space for all of us," he asserts.
In a country where most land holdings are less than an acre, those
returning will have to jostle just to get space to build a house.
Harerimana says that in any other country the returnees would remain
refugees; people who have to ask permission to live there, but in
Rwanda, however difficult their situation, they would be home. One
has to wonder if that is enough.

Original_Search_Title: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Headline: Nigeria: PDP, ANPP And Nigeria's Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — The focus of this article is on the winning prospects of the two main political parties (PDP and ANPP) and their flag bearers, based on the anticipated vision plans of each for Nigeria during the April 2003 general elections.
This intellectual exercise is being undertaken even though we are fully aware of the uncertainties that have so far, engulfed the political campaigns with unpredictable future. We are no doubt aware of the wave of political violence involving assassinations, arson and the desperate posture of the incumbent for the second term re-election at all levels. - Very importantly, there are doubts as to the independence, transparency and credibility of the poorly funded INEC whereby it may not be in the position to comply sticky with the provisions of the electoral laws of the land. Besides, the foregoing, there are questions being raised about the timing of the 2003 election day that has coincided with the Christian Holy week of Easter. Finally, there is the silence that pervades the future of local councils elections that have been kept on ice as if the state and Federal Governments are immune from the four (4) years term as provided by the constitution as the life span of these tiers.
What happens if elections do not hold at appropriate dates to enable the emergence of elected government at the Federal and state levels? The aim here however is that the elections shall hold. In the circumstance, the real struggle for power especially at the federal centre. is between the PDP, the incumbent and ANPP the real front runners . Here we shall reflect on the performance of the incumbent party and what the PDP views as the-future Nigeria. On the other hand we shall consider the challenge of the incumbent's performance by ANPP and consider ANPP's vision plan for the future Nigeria. In both uses, the focus and pronouncements of the flag bearers of the two parties shall be in the reckoning.
Let me begin this analysis with. the truism that an individual's life can only be truly exposed and comprehended, at the moment; the individual seeks elective office on the platform, of a political party. To me, this truism holds valid in respect of all those seeking elective office at all the tiers of governments in Nigeria today, especially, the presidential candidates who all want to effect change for a better Nigeria.
At this stage it appears, PDP and ANPP through their flag bears have rounded up their plans for the Nigeria of tomorrow: about how to keep it up, get it fit or strangulate it under the environment of poverty, corruption, dependence or self reliance, rekindle its hope or leave it to languish in despair, or uplift it to become an affective player in world affairs or allow it to become allergic to internal insecurity and disputes in circumstance of hunger, mono economy and "monopoly money" called the Naira amidst disputed privatisation scheme. Or further still, change to good governance as a precondition for NEPAD or be ready for a future that we fear?
In most instances the campaigns for election 2003 had been less about policy issues that lead to progress. Until recently did Nigerians see public notices placed by ANPP calling for campaign based on issues rather than personalities, that tended to dominate the PDP addresses at public rallies. With time fast closing on us, the focus on issues and personalities may not mean much for the choice that Nigerians shall be called upon to make on April 19th. From our close observation of the ongoing campaigns, it appears PDP government does not like change of whatever nature. This explains the party's philosophy of continuity at all costs. According to PDP, the president must continue, the Vice President must continue, stagnant Nigeria must continue, the lack of infrastructure must continue, the governors must continue, corruption must continue, privatisation amidst public outcries must continue, Nigeria police must continue, ASUU strikes and labour unrest must go on. To the disbelief of the good people of Nigeria, the Corporate Nigeria with its style of PDP funding even if the funds come from unclaimed dividends, must continue. The forces of history that shaped him, must continue. The return of PDP presidential candidate to power after 20 years makes PDP to think that Nigeria exists as a constant: constant of violence, constant unchanged and unchanging values, a constant warehouse economy, and a country under one God that brought him back to power to accomplish his work. This is why the man enjoys his pounded yam meal with okoro and bush meat without minding even if ordinary Nigerians persist in hunger. How much was the cost of a tuber of yam in 1994 and how much is it today?
On the other hand is it not a valid position to observe that the ANPP maintains that the only constant phenomenon of life is its unremitting motion. In existence, don't we move with change in the security, stability and prosperity of Nigeria and adopt the style of making deliberate efforts for moderating these changes in the interest of the nation. If in the evolution of these scenarios the PDP approach runs off key, ANPP syntax the might to be oscillating towards perfection. In this policy orientation, if ANPP's candidate outshines PDP's flag bearer in the election 2003, Buhari's victory will only be the manifestation of the extent to which his party had articulated a supreme vision plan for future Nigeria.
At the start of their search for leadership of Nigeria, each had a supreme decision to make. How much change would they bring about in themselves and to the country? This entailed the extent to which each would be exposed to enable Nigerians to decide. For the PDP candidate the decision, easy. He has been the incumbent (this has its burden). He had been the offspring of sympathy that had earned him pardon and nationwide choice in 1999. His performance in some areas spoke for him and in some cases incumbency worked against him, in some parts of the country like in Benue and Odi Niger -Delta region, where he is yet to convince the people that he did not kill their kith and kin on premeditated agenda. His steady repositioning and desire to seek re-election was some how delayed due to a long consultation with his associate and family members and in particular his desire to receive approval from the almighty God. At the end of the consultations he had a safe feel that he would continue as president, with the vision plan for bigger changes. This is the basis for the slogan that "continuity shall secure over democracy".
The ANPP on the other hand focused on what its flag bearer calls "project Nigeria". The project focuses briefly on among other fundamental issues, achieving the following priority goals:
(i) Stability: through provision of equal opportunity to all Nigerians with emphasis on gender equality, mutual trust and the protection of human rights including freedom of speech, freedom of religion and freedom of expression and make Nigeria to be an effective member and player in international affair.
(lI)Security: - to tackle the entire problems that threaten Nigeria and foreign interests by ensuring comprehensive security in all its dimensions.
(llI)Prosperity: to evolve diversified dynamic and self-reliance in a nation with well-developed infrastructure.
The ANPP flag bearer prides himself of the success he made of the PTF assignment before the arrival of PDP government on the scene and scraped the PTF without any substitute. He beats his chest of the presence of PTF vehicles in most government institutions, pharmaceutical drugs in government hospitals and instructional materials in most schools and tertiary institutions across the nation. He feels that the Niger Delta region shall be freed from the PDP government neglect, through inaction, corruption and insensitivity at the top. This is why Buhari accuses PDP of insincerity in dealing with the Niger Delta problems including the desire to review onshore - offshore dichotomy bill. This is also why,' the ANPP predicts that, PDP shall abandon that bill that has become a subject of intimidation of the good people of the region. As the ANPP Chairman promised at the Port Harcourt rally, ANPP government shall address the Niger-Delta on the basis of justice and equity in the best interest of the people of the region and the nation as a whole.
Even though election 2003 is very close, it appears PDP has dual campaigns: one by the candidate's handlers and the other by the flag bearer himself. This underscores the failure of the candidate to respond to the issues raised at his solo TV presidential debate. At that debate, the candidate did not just avoid answering questions, he dismissed ASUU as being self- seeking without the interest of the student, he failed to explain why the Auditor General of the Federation was sacked when for the first time ever, such a report was produced. The candidate lacked background facts for the clarification of issues, which makes the PDP a party without any plan for bridge-building between the past and the future.
What has come to work against the PDP and its flag bearer has been the decision of the Alliance for Democracy (AD), the western geopolitical zone dominated party to endorse the incumbent, the flag bearer of PDP, a Nigerian who hails from the western section of Nigeria. This action by the AD has been criticised particularly by those who seemed to be linking such endorsement with the kind of endorsement of Abacha by political parties in the l990s They also link such a decision to ethnic politics that has been the bane of Nigerian politics since independence in 1960. Here it must be recalled that never in the history of Nigeria has the westerners voted for another Nigerian other than one of their own kith and kin, for the highest office in the land.
Audu writes from Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Scarcity: Fares, Food Prices Go Up in FCT
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Scarcity: Fares, Food Prices Go Up in FCT
Body_Text:
Abuja — Transport fares in the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), Abuja, have shot up significantly as a result of the fuel scarcity prevalent in the nation. Also, prices of most staple food items like rice, garri, yam, fruits and vegetables have increased.
A trip from Abuja to Kubuwa and Abuja to Gwagwalada, which used to cost N40 and N60 now cost between N60 and N80 respectively. Taxis and other private 'kabu-kabu' vehicles plying the metropolis have also hiked their charges from N100 per drop to about N250 while city buses now collect N30 and N40 as against N10 and N20 previously charged.
At the popular new Garki Wuse, Karimo and other markets around the city, a cup of rice (mudu) now costs between N140 and N150 as against the previous N120, five average sized tubers of yam, which normally costs N300 now goes for N450, while fruits and vegetables such as tomatoes, pine apple, oranges and paw-paw are now scarce due to the transport fare situation.
THISDAY checks reveal that the fuel supply situation in the FCT has worsened in the last few days, with several motorists virtually reciding in the petrol stations in a desperate effort to purchase the commodity.
At the NNPC Mega Filling Station in the Central Area of the City, queues of vehicles waiting to buy fuel now stretches to about three kilometres. The same applies to the two fuel stations opposite the NNPC headquarters in Abuja owned by Total and National oil companies.
Many motorists interviewed in the city expressed despair and anger at the situation and called on the Federal Government to take decisive action to address the crisis before it gets out of hand.

Original_Search_Title: Garment Dealer Meets Museveni
Headline: Uganda: Garment Dealer Meets Museveni
Body_Text:
Kampala — MRS. Emily Lee of the US-based garments company, Emily International,
was in Kampala last week to place orders with Apparels Tri-Star
(Uganda).
According to a press release, Mrs. Lee was also introduced to President
Yoweri Museveni at State House Nakasero, by Tri-Star managing director,
V. Kananathan.
Museveni said Africa has many opportunities and potential for economic
growth, but lacked enough entreprenuers to exploit the abundant
resources. He said Uganda is self-sufficient in food production, but needed
capital goods like machines and manufactured items like medicines.
Emily International is one of the biggest dealers in the global garment
industry. Among its customers are several US retail stores, including Dollar
General, Family Dollar, Fred's Store and Bealls. The company also supplies
grments to Wal Mart Germany, Hannes and Mauritz in Sweden and N.D. in
Switzerland.
Mrs. Lee was accompanied by Abraham Lee and Yam Kwok, both with her
company. Tri-Star, which is based at Bugolobi, will be making a variety of
ladies'wear for Emily International.
Museveni thanked Mrs. Lee for opening up business links with
Uganda. Ends

Original_Search_Title: Queenmums Must Support Aids Victims - Mother Theresa
Headline: Ghana: Queenmums Must Support Aids Victims - Mother Theresa
Body_Text:
Odumase — The First Lady, Mrs. Theresa Kufuor, has commended the Manya Krobo Queenmothers Association (MKQA) for their role in supporting HIV/AIDS victims.
She has further urged queenmothers in the various traditional areas to emulate the example of the MKQA in ensuring the well-being of HIV/AIDS victims.
Mrs. Theresa Kufuor made the appeal when she paid a visit to the MKQA HIV/AIDS orphanage at Odumase Krobo on March 27, this year.
The First Lady, who is also the vice president of African First Ladies Against HIV/AIDS, said every effort should be made to reduce the high degree of stigmatisation against people living with HIV/AIDS.
She urged women infected with the disease not to give up on their situation, but rather seek treatment.
In an address, the project co-ordinator of MKQA, Madam Esther Kpabitey, said the association - by its traditional orientation - prefers to maintain the orphans in homes rather than keeping them in orphanages.
She said by being given home-based care, they quickly overcome stigmatisation and assimilate well into society.
The project co-ordinator appealed for more resources and a permanent structure as their current building belongs to the Presbyterian Church of Ghana.
The MKQA, which consists of 71 queenmothers, has 586 orphans under its care.
The First Lady presented tubers of yam, rice, boxes of chocolate, milk and loaves of bread to the association.
She later visited the Atua Government Hospital to interact with patients and also to acquaint herself with efforts being made by Family Health International in controlling mother-to-child HIV/AIDS transmission.

Original_Search_Title: VLTC Suspends Fuel Trans As Lake Level Falls
Headline: Ghana: VLTC Suspends Fuel Trans As Lake Level Falls
Body_Text:
The Volta Lake Transport Company (VLTC) has temporarily halted
conveyance of petroleum products on the Volta lake from the Akosombo port to
Buipe in the north because of the low level of water in the lake.
Consequently, the company will be losing about Â¢1.753 billion in
transport charges that could be earned between March and August this year,
the period when the operations will be on hold.
This reporter's findings, supported by management sources, have it that
bulk cargo carriers making the 415-kilometre south-north trip, are not able
to go farther than Debre, 35 kilometres to Buipe, the last destination.
This is because as the lake dries, rocks surface and therefore threatens
vessel movements.
On March 2 this year, a quantity of 1.4 million litres of petroleum
products on its way to the north was returned.
However dry cargo in the form of cement was discharged at Debre for
onward transportation by road to Buipe. Information gathered has it that
transportation of the products on water is not only cheaper by 40% but also
safer as it is devoid of the rampant accidents and damages associated with
the road transport system.
The VLTC operates four ferry crossings at Kete Krachi, Dambai, Yeji and
Adawso with the Yapei Queen moving from Yeji to Akosombo carrying livestock,
yams, dried fish, exportable products and passengers.
Two other vessels, Buipe Queen and Volta Queen also float on the lake
alongside ten others, some of which are crying for rehabilitation. The VTLC
is a subsidiary of the VRA.

Original_Search_Title: Osun Chief Judge's Reprieve
Headline: Nigeria: Osun Chief Judge's Reprieve
Body_Text:
A FORTNIGHT ago, the Chief Judge of Osun State, Justice Fasasi Ogunsola during an official duty tour of prisons in the state ordered the release of some inmates in Osun prisons.
Among those released were two teenage girls sentenced for stealing two tubers of yam by a magistrate court. At the time of their sentence, the girls, Rafiat Mufutau and Rukayat Rashidi were 15 years old. Five other inmates namely Adebayo Adeyemi 19, Muri Alimeke 22, Sikiru Wasiu 30, Yisau Akano 35 and Alaba Adewale 40 were equally set free after having spent about five years in prison without trial.
Justice Ogunsola condemned the judgement of the magistrate who convicted the teenagers without considering their age. We agree with him. The teenagers' imprisonment, as minors cannot be sustained in law. The magistrate ought to know better. It is trite law that minors cannot be imprisoned the way the learned magistrate did in the case of the two teenage girls.
Rather, the teenagers should have been tried under the Children and Young Persons Act. The penalty under the latter would have been reformative.
As much as we deprecate stealing as past time amongst our teenagers, it is important to equally remind our courts to act withiA Ogunsola had spent close to five years in prison without trial. This is sentence and punishment without conviction. We are nonetheless aware there are more thousands of unfortunate Nigerians who have spent more than that number of years awaiting trial that is not in view. More inmates are still suffering the same fate in other states of the federation.
We commend Osun State Chief Judge for invoking the power conferred on him by Section 1(1a) of the Criminal Justice (Release from Custody) Special Provisions Act, Laws of the federation 1990 to free unconstitutionally encumbered Nigerians.
The ordering of the release even though a routine legal thing is a fundamental aspect of a judge's judicial responsibility.
The amnesty granted the inmates is laudable because it reminds us that some of our judicial officers have not been doing their jobs diligently . Also, the release re-emphasize the need to drastically re-organise our judicial process since there are so many Nigerians who are Awaiting Trial Inmates who are being unjustly kept in custody.
As an organisation with a firm belief in the rule of law, we call on the government to quickly look into the matter of Awaiting Trial Inmates, setting free those who should be set free, while speedily putting on trial those who have cases to answer.
We call on other chief judges across the federation to emulate the Osun Chief Judge's example.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Judge Seeks Better Interpretation of Law
Headline: Nigeria: Chief Judge Seeks Better Interpretation of Law
Body_Text:
Osogbo — For sentencing two Juveniles over allegations of stealing two tubers of Yam, the Chief Judge of Osun State, Justice Fasaasi Ogunsola, yesterday berated an Okuku magistrate for not interpreting the law accordingly.
Rafiyat Mufutau and Rakiyat Rashidi, both 15 years old have spent six months in Ilesa prison in Osun state for allegedly stealing two tubers of yam.
The two girls regained their freedom on Friday when the Chief Judge visited the prison on an assessment tour.
Justice Ogunsola, after been briefed about the case of the inmates ruled that the juvenile offenders ought not to have been imprisoned by the lower court. He said the magistrate court should have tried them under the children and young persons act which would have been reformative.
Among those who also regained their freedom was a female convict, Bolaji Odelana, who was delivered of a baby girl few months after arriving in the prison in 1998.
Odelana pleaded with the Chief Judge to set her free in the interest of her five year old-daughter.
Justice Ogunsola also set Adebayo Adeyemi, Muri Alimeke who were said to have been mistakenly arrested along with some armed robbery suspects in Ila Orangun in July 2002, free when security operatives raided the town.
Also Wasiu Sikiru, Yisau Akano and Alaba Adewole were freed based on the submission of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) that there was no information to link them with the conspiracy and murder charge for which they have been detained since 2001.
Releasing the inmates, Justice Ogunsola charged all stakeholders in the administration of criminal justice to ensure that accused persons are not put behind bars unjustly.

Original_Search_Title: N10m Goods Razed in Market Fire
Headline: Nigeria: N10m Goods Razed in Market Fire
Body_Text:
Osogbo, Osun — More than N10 million worth of goods were destroyed when fire gutted the Atakunmosa Market in Ilesa West Local Government, in Osun State.
The incident occured in the early hours of last Wednesday destroying 25 stalls in the market.
The cause of the fire was not immediately known but reliably sources blamed it on electrical fault.
Among the goods destroyed were food stuffs, including bags of rice, beans, garri, several tubers of yam, cassava and fruit items.
Also destroyed were 20 grinding machine used for commercial purposes.
Chairman, Transition Committee of Atakunmosa Local Government, Hon. Wale Webster-Esho, who visited the scene, lamented the level of destruction.
He appealed to the victims to remain calm. Webster-Esho gave the assurance that the local government would come to the aid of the traders by re-constructing the destroyed stalls and providing cash to put the victims back in business.
He called on the state government to provide infrastructural facilities for the traders in the market.

Original_Search_Title: Relief As Food Prices Drop
Headline: Nigeria: Relief As Food Prices Drop
Body_Text:
Rivers — Port Harcourt residents may heave a sigh of relief as prices of food
items have dropped considerably, compared to their prices same time last
year.
According to a News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) survey in Rumuwoji Market,
Diobu, a bag of rice, which sold for N4,500 last year, now sells for
N3,900.
Meanwhile, half a bag of the same item has fallen from N2,250 to N1,850
since the beginning of the year.
NAN reports that a bag of beans, which previously sold for N6,500, now
sells for
N6,000, while a bag of pepper has dropped from N19,000 to N16,000.
The survey showed that a bag of garri has dropped from N7,000 to N4,000,
while a basin of the commodity, which sold for N1,000 last year, now goes
for N850.
According to the survey, the price of palm oil also dropped from N3,300
to N1,900 for a 20-litre container, while a 20-litre container of groundnut
oil has remained at N2,500.
A bag of Ogbono, which cost N28,000 last year, now sells for N24,000,
while the price of a bag melon has dropped to N19,000, down from the
previous N20,000 last year.
NAN reports that of all the commodities, only tubers of yam have
recorded an increase, with a heap of the produce selling for N22,000, up
from the N18,000 for which the commodity was sold for last year.
A rice dealer, Mrs Ngozi Orji, attributed the fall in prices to the fact
that no major festival had ever been celebrated in the state since the
beginning of the year.
She attributed the seasonal increase in the prices of food items to
festivities, during which almost everyone shops for food items.

Original_Search_Title: Fuel Scarcity: Prices of Food Items Go Up
Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Scarcity: Prices of Food Items Go Up
Body_Text:
Lagos — As the fuel scarcity persists in Lagos, prices of food items have gone up considerably.
A check by correspondents of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) in some markets in Lagos showed that prices of food items like yam, garri, tomatoes and meat have gone up by about 100 per cent.
At the Tejuosho Market in SuruLere area of Lagos, a tuber of yam that was sold between N200 and N250 last week, now goes for between N300 and N350, while a basket of tomatoes that was previously sold for N600 now goes for N1,200.
Mrs. Titi Okolawo, a trader at the market, blamed the situation on the scarcity of fuel, saying that she paid about N600 to transport 100 tubers of yam from mile 12 market as against the previous price of N300.
Another trader, Lasisi Raji, a meat seller at Oyingbo market, said the prices had risen significantly compared to what obtained last week.
He said that an average size cow that was sold for between N35,000 and N40,000 last week was now going for between N45,000 to N60,000.
Raji also complained of low sale as a result of the high cost brought about by the scarcity of fuel, saying: "I used to sell about 70 pounds of meat per day, but now I sell only about 25 pounds".
At the Oyingbo Market, Mrs Felicia Otu, a restaurant owner said that she was now operating skeletal services because of the drop in patronage and the increase in cost of food items.
NAN investigation, however, showed that the prices of food items, such as rice and palm oil have either gone down or remain stable as a 50 kg bag of rice is still being sold for between N3,800 and N4,500, while the price of palm oil has gone down to N2,400 as against the previous price of N2,800.

Original_Search_Title: The Senate: The Change We Want
Headline: Nigeria: The Senate: The Change We Want
Body_Text:
As the nation prepares for another election, Ndigbo wants a Senate that can produce some dividends of democracy.
It is rather too unfortunate and shameful that while other senatorial districts of other geopolitical zones count their gains as the first term of our new democratic dispensation draws to an end, the South East States of the Igbos count their losses in terms of lack of Federal presence due to the under representation of our interests at federal level of government, especially the senate. For instance, while the South-South Senators smiled home with the NDDC and imminent abrogation of the on-shore off-shore dichotomy, with the former bringing in its wake a commendable wave of socio-economic development, such as the Niger Delta University, the people we sent to Abuja have only the stories of their fight-to-finish battles of supremacy, protracted bad blood, galore of senate presidency coups, infamous furniture allowances and often misdirected and self-serving bouts with the presidency to tell us. At their home state level, most of them can only boast of instigations of melancholy, confusion, chaos, unwarranted wars against their home governors, undiluted political gansterism, sponsorship of malicious junk journals, plus irresponsible busy-body, poke-nosing and rude intrusion in the running of their state governments. They can only boast of bicameralizing their states legislatures, massive importation of menacing, trigger-happy, red-eye policemen, provocative display of federal opulence, stage managed demonstrations, disorientating and mesmerizing of some ill-informed masses, as well as arrogant display of federal might in the negative.
In view of all these, I, for one, have always postulated that rather than place the blame on Obasanjo and crying over spilt milk, we of the South-East should start re-engineering our fate and repositioning ourselves for more federal benefits and peaceful states. And the first step is by ensuring that only mature minds with reliable and respectable antecedents are elected into our federal legislatures, especially the Senate. It is therefore a thing of joy that we of Enugu East senatorial district have made the pace-setting and all important first step in the right direction with the emergence of Owa Enugu, Chief Ken Nnamani as PDP senatorial flag bearer, having suffered a peculiarly dark period of gross under representation in the just-ending four-year season. The emergence of Owa (Igbo word for the moon), is very symbolic in the sense that the moon is not only an element of light in Igbo, but also enjoys a pride of place in the socio-cultural lives of the Igbos. It is not for instance, by accident that the Igbo traditional calendar depends entirely on the moon. The same with the Igbo Festivals (such as the new yam festival) and even certain sacrifices and rituals which must either take place during a crescent, half or full moon. But very important is the fact that the moon is a powerful uniting force, bringing the old and young together; and that it is a traditionally sacred and gentle phenomenon which neither hurts nor scorches. Owa Enugu has displayed these qualities by taking up the role of a father, a uniting and stabilising force in the volatile terrains of Enugu politics. And this mature, charismatic and galvanising quality is what the South East needs at the federal level if we must not count losses again in 2007.
Campaign/Promises
Also, we need a senator whose promises we can count on. Being the season of campaign, it is not unusual for the political office seekers to come to bamboozle the electorate with a recitation of their age long, crammed promises in order to steal our votes. They would promise to build bridges, tar our roads with silver and gold, build Eldora do hospitals where our wives would deliver their babies without the slightest labour pain, make our educational system soar till it kisses the third heavens, turn our rural areas into New York city, as well as drill three boreholes in each compound, from where we shall fetch water, wine and honey. Then our unemployed would be richer than the drug barons of the South Americas. No doubt, these are wonderful promises. Yet, at this point of my life, experiences have taught me to take a politician's promises based on what he was able to give to the society before seeking any political office. This is because it is only a man who has helped himself that I can trust to help me, and a man who has served his immediate society that we can trust to serve the wider society.
For example, when Owa Enugu's campaign leaflet emphasizes on a science and technology-based education and building of more technical colleges, I believe it because he has offered five university scholarships to deserving youths between 1986 and 1990 and because he too is an accomplished scholar with chains of higher degrees from the best of American universities, not one of those who either go to buy or lobby universities for honorary doctorate degrees. When it says Chief Ken Nnamani will come up with legislations aimed at revitalizing the industries and carry out an urban renewal of Enugu which has lost its glory as the cradle of modern civilizations in the East, I believe it because he has constructed 50 HT NEPA poles for the extension of electricity from Ebony substation to his town, Amechi-Uwani at the cost of N5.8 million in 1994. He also donated a 1.5 million 500 11/0.415 Kva transformer to his people in 1986, thereby saving the community from a long time epileptic rural electrification. He further sponsored the extension of NITEL underground cable for 100 digital telephone lines to Amechi-Uwani in 1998 to the tune of 1.3 million. I will have no doubts at all because he had as well built an ultra modern civic centre for his people in 1986. Again, when the campaign leaflet promises he is to push for legislations that will revitalize the ailing industries and attract new industrial projects, I need not doubt such because he is an industrialist himself, an accomplished international businessman who is also a director and managing director/CEO of chains of local and foreign companies. In my own candid estimation, I believe that if a man can serve his people so selflessly with his own limited personal resources, it is such a man that the people of Enugu East senatorial district can trust to deliver. The logic is that a toothless child who chewed palm kernel with its gum will definitely chew an iron rod to flour if given the opportunity to grow. May it also suffice to say that the house of sins and scandals which the Senate had reduced itself to since the inception of this dispensation is largely attributable to the preponderance of unpatriotic politicians, ex-convicts and prisoners, fraudulent contractors, progenitors and genitors of corruption and either retired military maladministrators or those who had collaborated with the military in looting the nation and unleashing blatant anarchy on the land- as ambassadors, ministers, directors, and even as master-planners and executors of the infamous five-million march for Abacha. The Senate then becomes a house in dire need of the restoration of dignity and gentle men who would not resort to fisticuffs to settle minor disagreements, thereby desecrating that which is held sacred and in high esteem. We need in the senate, men who can bare their clean palms to the sun and have the moral justification to fight corruption to finish.

Original_Search_Title: Why Obasanjo Should Not Be Re-Elected, By Nwobodo
Headline: Nigeria: Why Obasanjo Should Not Be Re-Elected, By Nwobodo
Body_Text:
Abuja — United Nigeria Peoples Party (UNPP) presidential candidate, Senator Jim
Nwobodo, has given reasons why Nigerians should not re-elect President
Olusegun Obasanjo iduring the forthcoming presidential elections,
emphasising that Obasanjo's a second term would plunge Nigeria into deeper
socio-economic and political problems.
Nwobodo, who unfolded a 14-point agenda, if elected President, urged
Nigerians to discountenance the slogan "continuity will secure our
democracy," saying no single individual can lay claim to being an embodiment
of democracy.
Addressing a world press conference yesterday in Abuja, the UNPP
presidential candidate described 1999 to 2003 as a "most traumatic period
for Nigeria," and wondered why a President with such a dismal performance
would choose to seek re-election.
"His approach to governance is imposition of siege and brutal
destruction of the peoples' psyche. When a man cannot elevate his nation to
a position of honour and prosperity, he has no basis whatsoever to seek
re-election. A leadership that cannot lay foundation for true nationalism
and patriotism must be adjudged irrelevant and anachronistic," Nwobodo
stated.
He accused Obasanjo of lacking the will to "nurture in the minds of the
led, such virtues as national pride, conviction and the will to carry on in
the spirit of a people with one destiny."
Nwobodo averred that Nigerians were today left in a state of doubt as to
whether or not their condition would improved going by the socio-economic
problems facing the country.
"In 1999 a bottle of coke cost N20, today it is N40; in 1999 a bag of
rice cost N1,850, today, it is N4,300; before 1999, a tuber of yam was below
N100, but today, it is over N200, and so it is for other consumable items
such as garri, beans and others."
"In 1985-1992, Nigeria's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth average was
seven per cent; by 1996-1999, it was 3.5 percent; in 2003, it had dropped to
1.9 percent. Although the per capital income increased nominally but what is
the value of naira to dollar? In 1999 the dollar was N85 but today the
dollar is N140. What this means is that the inflation and exchange rates
have wiped out any gains from the per capital increase.

Original_Search_Title: I Pump Iron And Lift Weights - Igbinedion
Headline: Nigeria: I Pump Iron And Lift Weights - Igbinedion
Body_Text:
CHIEF Lucky Igbinedion, Edo State Executive Governor is an amiable personality no matter the situation he finds himself.
A hard worker and sports enthusiast, he cuts the picture of the perfect role model to the up and coming.
In this brief interview, he speaks about his other side when he is not entangled in the matters of state and governabce. Excerpts:
"IT is quite a difficult task to outline my daily routine especially now that the heat of politics is on and electioneering campaigns are at fever pitch.
There are days I do not sleep until around 4.30 am, to be exact any day I get as much as four hours sleep, I consider myself privileged.
Basically, I rise as early as the job dictates, usually around 6.30 am if I went to bed late, and 5.30 am if I slept early.
I perform my personal early morning devotion after which I tune on the radio for foreign and local news. My favorite stations are the BBC, VOA and Edo State broadcasting Service (ESBC ).
I am a regular listener to the daily programmes of the ESBC especially the one titled Facts You Must Know. Afterwards I move over to the gymnasium for a thorough work-out.
I pump iron and lift weights after which I head for the bathroom. By 8.30 or 9.00, I am at the office. I love to stroll down, but I often drive down. I love to stroll since it has to do with fitness..
My day at the office is that of the typical chief executive. I do so much consultations, have meetings and dialogue to settle differences. There are also several travels to be done, to Abuja, Lagos and all the LGAs.
I believe it is God that gives good health, all we can do is try our best to stay healthy. I do everything in moderation - no smoking, no drinking, no excesses. Usually I skip breakfast but I never forgo lunch. In actual fact it is my main meal for the day.
My favorite meal is pounded yam with any good soup. In the evening, I may be content with hot fresh fish pepper soup and I am OK for the day.
For my leisure, I love sports naturally. I am a sportsman in all totality, that is, to the core. I play football, tennis and squash. I am also into weightlifting and boxing. I am a strong believer in general body fitness and I work out every morning and evening. In addition to sports,I love music especially Jazz.
Nigerian music also appeals to me irrespective of its origin - Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa or Edo. I listen to music a lot particularly while traveling by road since it keeps me cool, calm and collected.
One pastime I have never really come to terms with is watching the movies even though I am an ardent fan of action films.
But since I became governor, I have never had the time to watch even one movie."

Original_Search_Title: Armed Robbers Kill Suspected Informant
Headline: Ghana: Armed Robbers Kill Suspected Informant
Body_Text:
Accra — Police on Wednesday said a 45-year-old man who was shot dead by an armed gang at Flamingo Junction, near Russia, in Accra on Tuesday, could be a victim of vengeance by the robbers.
Police told the GNA in Accra that the man whose name was only given as Mufu must have been gunned down following the arrest of a hardened armed robber, Philip Gbotsui.
The armed robbers suspected the man to have informed the police on the hideout of one Gbotsui, who is about 25 years old and was believed to be behind many armed robberies in Accra.
Gbotsui, who was described as a "top class armed robber", was arrested on Monday night near Gloryland Hotel, Odorkor, after a struggle with the police.
The Police also believe that Mufu was gunned down by a group of armed robbers who thought he was an informant, but police deny that he was an informant.
The Police sources said at around 9:00pm on Tuesday a Police Patrol team heard that there had been a shooting incident at Flamingo.
Upon reaching the scene they saw Mufu lying in pool of blood with empty bullet shells by his side.
The sources said eyewitnesses said Mufu, in the company of a lady, was buying fried yam at the Flamingo Junction when an unregistered car with five men suddenly pulled up.
One of the occupants got out of the vehicle and tapped Mufu on the shoulder. Another occupant with a Pump-Action gun fired at Mufu while two others also pulled pistols and fired at him.
They got back into the car and sped off leaving Mufu dead and one Daniel Amenu, who was also in the company of Mufu with a shot in the thigh.
The Police sources said they believe the car, which was ash in colour, is either an Opel Astra or a Hyundai Excel.
Meanwhile the lady in whose company Mufu was before the incident has "vanished into thin air".
Police also said the armed robbers are planning to kill two other people they suspect are informants, but declined to give their names for security reasons.
