Police said they are meanwhile continuing with investigations into the robberies believed to have been committed by Gbotsui.
Police said they have arrested one Adamu, another notorious armed
robber, at the Kaneshie Overhead upon a tip-off.
He managed to throw away a weapon to some accomplices before his arrest.

Original_Search_Title: Niger Police Kill 5 Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Niger Police Kill 5 Robbers
Body_Text:
Minna — Men of the Niger State Police Command have killed five robbers in separate incidents in Niger and Edo States.
Briefing the press in Minna recently, the acting Police Commissioner, Mr. Cyril Njirize, said the first robbery took place at Aviele in Edo State in a luxurious bus travelling from Minna to Onitsha.
An escort attached to the bus, he said, noticed the suspicious movement of a passenger in the bus and apprehended him.
The passenger, according to him, was seen by the police when he bent down to remove something under the seat and withdrew frantically. But the escort curiously conducted a search under the seat and discovered three tubers of yams.
It was then discerned that one of the yams had been clearly sliced into two and a space enough to accommodate a pistol was hollowed out where a locally made pistol was hidden.
The discovery, Njirinze said, eventually led to another yam where some bullets were similarly concealed.
When the bus was stopped, the passengers noticed that three persons who were suspected to be accomplices of the robbers wanted to escape and two of them were shot dead while the third one ran away, he said.
In a similar case, which happened along the Kaduna-Suleja road, he explained, four people had joined a Peugeot vehicle at Suleja, along with others going to Kano when they observed that other passengers were carrying huge sums of money.
The acting police commissioner said when the vehicle reached Sabon-Wuse, a few kilometres from Suleja, one of the four men pulled a gun at the driver and a struggle ensued.
Unfortunately for the bandits, the Operation-fir-for-fire squad emerged abruptly and clearly blocked them from their escape bid.
Three of them who jumped from the car into the bush were shot while the remaining one was arrested.
The suspect who claimed to have come from Anambra State for the robbery operation gave their names as Azubike Aneke, Cosmas Eze, Uche Chioma and Sunday Okafor. Also recovered from them were a pistol and a single barrel gun.
The police, said the police commissioner, are still investigating the case of the escaped robber at Aviele in Edo State.

Original_Search_Title: Audit Report: Business As Usual?
Headline: Nigeria: Audit Report: Business As Usual?
Body_Text:
We continue our serialisation of the Auditor-General of the Federation's report recently submitted to the Clerk of the National Assembly.
Federal Ministry of Education (Continued from yesterday)
The examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Kaduna, revealed the following:-
(a) Amounts totalling N1,423,770.00 were paid to some contractors and members of staff for the supply of various items and as refunds of out-of-pocket expenses incurred between May, 2000 and January, 2001. The relevant supporting documents such as invoices, receipts and Stores Vouchers were not attached to the payment vouchers. It is therefore difficult to accept the expenditures as legitimate charges against public funds.
(b) Amounts totalling N4,261,935.00 were paid between May, 2000 and January 2001 to some members of staff for the settlement of the college expenses. A scrutiny of the payment vouchers showed that the actual total amount signed for and collected by the payees was N3,068,513.00 resulting in an overpayment of N1,193,442.00 which should be accounted for.
(c) An amount of N42,779.00 was paid to a contractor in January, 2001 for the supply of food items. The payment voucher was supported with a photocopy of an old invoice for N150,000.00 dated 25th October, 1999 and store receipt voucher dated 21st October, 1999. This fictitious payment of N42,7799.00 should be recovered from the officer who authorised such payment and recovery particulars furnished for audit verification. In addition, all officers involved in the processing of the payment should be appropriately disciplined.
(d) 2 cheques for amounts totalling N250,000.00 were issued in April and June 2001 in favour of the Principal without raising payment vouchers contrary to the Financial Regulation which stipulates that all payment must be properly vouched before payment is made. The total amount of N250,000.00 should be refunded by the Principal.
These irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Kiyawa, it was observed that:
(a) An amount of N498,000.00 was paid to the Principal in November, 2001 for the purchase of an equipment to the Home Management Department. The equipment should have been procured through the use of Job Order or Tendering procedure. Moreover, the equipment could not be traced into the stores records or produced for my verification. Consequently, I cannot accept such payment as a proper charge against public funds.
(b) Another amount of N689,000.00 was paid to the Principal in November, 2001 for the purchase of adding machine, typewriter and duplicating machine. Audit investigation revealed that the duplicating machine was yet to be supplied as at the time if audit inspection. The Principal should please refund the cost of price of the duplicating machine not purchased.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose response is being awaited.
During the examination of the accounting books maintained at the Federal Technical College, Lassa, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 9 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N7,850,058.81 were raised and purportedly paid for hiring of labourers to clear the college premises. The physical inspection of the college revealed that no clearing was effected to justify such colossal expenditure. Consequently, I cannot certify that the expenditures were legitimate charges against public fund.
(b) 3 payments totalling N2,821,000.00 made to the Principal, during the year under review, for the printing of Treasury and other forms were wrongly classified to the Sub-head for Personnel Costs, contrary to the Financial Regulation which stipulates that expenditure will be classified in accordance with the Estimates and votes must be applied only to the purpose for which money is provided. Furthermore, there was no authority from the Accountant-General of the Federation for the printing of the Treasury Forms in accordance with the Financial Regulations.
(c) Similarly, 2 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,506,800.00 were raised and paid from the Personnel Costs vote to members of staff for alleged Duty Tour Allowances. The narration for the payments was not specified on any of the payment vouchers. It is therefore difficult to accept the expenditures as legitimate charges against public funds.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose reaction is being awaited.
The examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Maiduguri, revealed that irregular loans for amounts totalling N3,587,253.71 were granted to Officers of the College, Federal Pay Officer, NITEL's Territorial Manager and the Publisher of the College's magazine between April, 2000 and November, 20001 from the Domestic, Feeding and Capital Accounts.
As at the time of audit inspection in December 2001, the loans were yet to be recovered contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
This irregularity has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary and his comments are being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Nise, it was observed that:-
(a) An amount of N3,223,783.80 was paid to a contractor as part payment for a contract valued at N12,520,000.00 in respect of the Perimeter Wall Fencing of the College. The audit inspection of the project revealed that the work was shoddily executed even though the interim payment certificate was signed by the Project Officer. The cement used was poorly mixed, thereby causing major cracks on the wall and the blocks to be wearing off. The contractor should therefore be made to do a satisfactory job or refund the money so far collected.
(b) Six payment vouchers for amounts totalling N1,661,473.75 paid to various contractors between February and July, 2001, were not accompanied with supporting documents such as receipts and invoices contrary to the Financial Regulations.
These issues have been taken up with the Permanent Secretary and his reaction is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Ohafia, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 10 (Ten) payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,246,271.00 paid between February and April 2001 for various contracts were not supported with the relevant documents such as Contract Agreements, Job Orders, and the contracts did not pass through any competitive tendering procedure in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations. Furthermore, amounts totalling N112,313.55, representing the mandatory 5 per cent withholding tax, were not deducted from the amounts paid to the contractors resulting in a loss of revenue to the Federal Government.
(b) Out of the balance of N7,604,171.92 in the Capital Account Vote as at April, 2001, amounts totalling N2,900,000.00 were expended between February and April, 2001 for various supplies and construction without recourse to contract procedures as stipulated in the Financial Regulations. Moreover, the amounts were not posted into the cash book and vote book. Consequently, it is difficult to accept the payments totalling N2,900,000.00 as proper charges against public funds.
(c) A payment voucher for N500,000.00 paid in March, 2001 to a contractor as part payment for furnishing the Principals's Office was not supported with the necessary documents such as - Contract Agreement, Store Receipt Voucher and Store Issue Voucher. This irregular payment was made from the Domestic Account of the college without any authority in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
All the issues have been taken up with the Permanent Secretary whose response is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Okigwe, the following observations were made:-
(a) A total amount of N3,625,833.32 under the Capital Allocation Vote in 2000 was paid to the Principal without reference to any payment voucher number or service rendered instead of spending the amount on Capital Projects.
(b) 36 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N6,523,224.45 paid between December 1999 and December 2000 were not produced for my inspection. Consequently, I cannot certify that the expenditure on them were just and proper charges against public funds.
(c) 33 part-time teachers were employed by the Principal between November 1999 and April, 2000 - 6 months, without any authority from the headquarters and a sum of N998,930.45 was paid to the teachers for the period. However, there was no evidence to confirm that the part-time teachers signed and collected their salaries. Furthermore, the amount paid to the teachers in the last 3 months - February to April 2000 was N169,287.55 more than the amount paid from November 1999 to January 2000. The issue should be investigated and the amount totalling N998,930.45 should be recovered from the Principal.
(d) A colossal amount of N4,000,000.00 was paid to 50 casual labourers in December 1999 and July 2000 for cutting of grasses and clearing of students hostels. It is amazing that a college that has some labourers on its payroll could engage outside labourers for everyday from January 1999 to May, 1999 and April 2000 to be cutting grasses.
There was no evidence to authenticate that the casual labourers signed and collected their wages during the period. Furthermore, the payment of N400,000.00 in July, 2000 would appear to be duplicated. Consequently, I cannot accept the expenditure of N4,000,000.00 as legitimate charges against public fund.
(e) Amounts totalling N570,500.00 were paid as loans to 37 members of staff between October 1999 and December, 2000. None of the loans has been recovered from the beneficiaries as at the time of audit inspection in February 2001. Furthermore, the loans were not recorded except on the payment vouchers which the beneficiaries signed and collected contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
(f) A sum of N3,057,538.00 was paid to various officers of the college as refunds out-of-pocket expenses. However, lit was observed that all the necessary attachments or supporting documents were not attached to the payment vouchers to confirm the authenticity of the transactions. It is therefore difficult to accept the payments as proper charges against public fund.
(g) A total amount of N1,747,110.00 was paid to the Principal and some members of staff as refunds of out-of-pocket expenses. Only N886,776.15 could be accounted for leaving a balance of N861,146.85 to be accounted for.
(h) 171 payment vouchers for amounts totalling N2,528,821.42 paid between January and September, 2000 were not posted into the Cash Book in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
(i) 33 payment vouchers amounting to N756,430.00 paid between January and September, 2000 were not signed by the Principal, who was the Officer Controlling Expenditure, in accordance with the provisions of the Financial Regulation.
(j) An amount of N250,000.00 was paid for the purchase of 5000kg of yams in February, 2001. Although Store Receipt Voucher was raised apparently to satisfy payment requirement, the items could not be traced into the Store neither were they accounted for.
(k) A sum of 200,000.00 was paid to the Bursar in February and May 2000 from the Domestic Account to enable him purchase some unspecified domestic items and renovate his apartment. Audit investigation revealed that the bursar was not living in government quarters and no domestic items were purchased for the kitchen.
The irregularities have been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government College, Otobi, the following observations were noted:-
(a) 27 Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 0002953 -0002979 issued in acknowledgement of receipt of school fees totalling N40,500.00 on 30th January, 2001 were not posted into the cash book.
(b) The total collection of N75,000.00 vide Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 01201-01250 was posted into the cash book as N7,500.00, resulting in a balance of N67,500.00 to be accounted for.
(c) Collections vide Domestic Account Receipts Nos. 005401-005423 and Nos. 005434-005450 amounting to N57,330.00 and N22,154.24 respectively could not be accounted for during audit inspection.
(d) Bank Reconciliation Statements have not been prepared for the bank accounts being operated by the school since April 2000 contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations. Zit is therefore difficult to ascertain the true periodic balances of the accounts and this could lead to non-detection of possible fraudulent practices.
(e) The debit closing balance of N160,819.26 in the Other Charges Cash Book as at 28th December, 2000 was brought forward as N61,678.19 on 2nd January, 2001. Similarly, an amount of N120,259.43 being closing balance (credit) as at 28th December, 2000 was brought forward as N6,601.83 debit balance in the Boarding Account cash book on 2nd January, 2001.
In the absence of the Bank Reconciliation Statement, the irregularities could not be explained.
(f) Cash advances totalling N640,358.00 granted to 7 members of staff between May 2000 and April, 2001 still remained unrecovered as at the time of audit inspection in June 2001. Some of the officers collected advances more than eight times without retiring the previous ones negating the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
The attention of the Permanent Secretary has been drawn to the above-mentioned irregularities and his comments are being awaited.
At the Federal Government Girls' College, Sagamu, it was observed that a personal advance of N141,490.00 was granted to an officer in February, 2001 out of which an amount of N110,590.00 has been retired leaving a balance of N30,900.00 to be retired as at the time of audit inspection in December, 2001.
The Permanent Secretary has been requested to recover the outstanding advance furnishing me with recovery particulars. His response is being awaited.
During the audit examination of the accounting records maintained at the Federal Technical College, Shiroro, the following anomalies were noted:-
(a) An amount of N189,000.00 was irregularly withdrawn from the school's Domestic Account. This was done by detaching the documents that were used to support the payment of the same amount in the year 2000 and attaching them to another payment voucher that was raised on 31st January, 20001 to withdrawn the amount of N189,000.00.
(b) A sum of N358,000.00 was paid to a member of staff on 27th December, 2001 to settle what was termed "outstanding liability on foodstuff purchased for the college". This sum was paid from the school's Domestic Account. The originals of the payment vouchers and the relevant attachments showing details of the liability paid, were not produced for audit inspection.
The Permanent Secretary has been requested to recover the amount irregularly withdrawn and comment on the disciplinary action taken against the culprit; and also furnish me with the details of outstanding liability that was paid. His response is being awaited.
While examining the accounting records maintained at the Federal Government Girls College, Umuahia, the following observations were made:
(a) Cash advances totalling N2,677,470.00 granted to 3 members of staff of the College between September 2000 and January 2001 for the purchase of foodstuff and fuel had not been retired at the time of audit inspection in March, 2001. Incidentally, one of the officers had been granted 19 advances, which had not been retired.
Furthermore, those services might have been better rendered through the award of contracts after competitive tender procedures or through the issuance of Job Orders.
(b) Amounts totalling N276,122.00, being Withholding Tax due on 9 contract payments totalling N5,522,444.00 and effected between January and December, 2000 were not deducted, thereby resulting in a loss of revenue to the Federal Government.
(c) Monthly allocations for overhead and capital costs were withdrawn en bloc from the Federal Pay Officer's Account through payment vouchers raised in favour of the Principal without the necessary supporting documents and deposited into the Domestic Account. The amounts deposited were then spent through the Domestic Account Cash Book while the Treasury Vote Book indicated on en bloc amount. This irregular procedure would not indicate the actual payee, amount on each voucher, the sub-head and purpose of payment contrary to the provisions of the Financial Regulations.
The irregularity has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary and his comments are being awaited.
At the Federal Government, Wukari, 14 copies of revenue receipts issued between 28th September and 8th December, 2001 for amounts totalling N4,673,334.83 could not be produced for my verification. It is therefore difficult to ascertain the correct amount of revenue collected for the Government during the period.
The issue has been brought to the attention of the Permanent Secretary whose comments are being awaited.

Original_Search_Title: Those Who Killed Bola Ige Will Not Go Scot-Free - Ajayi-Obe
Headline: Nigeria: Those Who Killed Bola Ige Will Not Go Scot-Free - Ajayi-Obe
Body_Text:
Lagos — Chief Phoebe Chiadikobi Ajayi-Obe, SAN, the second female to be conferred with the prestigious rank of Senior Advocate of Nigeria is publicity shy. JUDE IGBANOI tracked down the unassuming Senior Advocate in her chambers at Ibadan last Friday and she had a lot of advice for younger lawyers, women and even our belligerent politicians
You were conferred with the coveted rank of Senior Advocate of Nigeria in 1989. Being the second woman to be so honoured, how did you receive the news?
I was very, very happy. My family was very happy. I knew that it carried a lot of responsibilities and I was prepared for it.
What would you say marked you out for this award?
I wouldn't say that I am better than anybody. I believe that at that time it was on merit. They looked at your character generally, your performance at the Bar and your contribution to the development of the law.
As a matter of fact, the Chief Judge of your state must write something positive about you. The Justices of the Court of Appeal where you practice must have their input. The Supreme Court as well, they have to assess you all round.
Not only in your performance at the Bar but you must be generally accepted as suitable to be elevated to that coveted position.
There seems to be something peculiar about law practice in the city of Ibadan. Producing the first two female SANs is something unique. What is the secret or can we pass it off as a mere coincidence?
It is not a coincidence. The standard of practice in Ibadan is very high. Distractions in Ibadan are very few, unlike Lagos or any other city.
In Ibadan if you want to practice law, you practice law. You don't mix it with any other thing, like business, e.t.c When we go into practice in this Ibadan, we are in practice, though the money is not there in Ibadan as it is in Lagos. So, we live moderately here and we have good practice. That is the only secret I can attribute to it. The social life in Ibadan is moderate and healthy compared to Lagos and other cities. That gives us time to do our work. Most importantly, a stable home. If you have a stable home it will help in everything that you do.
Success comes easily when you have a stable home. When you have time to look after your children, you plan properly, your husband is understanding and accommodating, so you are free to do your work. Of course, you will achieve success.
Up till date, there are only five female Senior Advocates of Nigeria. There is no woman at the Supreme Court and there are just a few at the Court of Appeal. Would you say the Nigerian Bar and Bench have been fair to the women folk?
So far, they have not been fair. The question is, who takes the decisions?
Mostly men. Women are trying, but the society is not helping. I wouldn't say that only five women are qualified to be Senior Advocates of Nigeria. But some of them will reach the last stage, and when the result comes out their names won't be there.
It's not as if they are not qualified. Many Nigerian women are qualified to be in the Supreme Court, to be Senior Advocates, but there are only five female SANs in the whole of Nigeria. I know it is not fair. We deserve more.
As for the Supreme Court, how do you get there? Women cannot nominate themselves. We can't send ourselves to the Supreme Court! We are even lucky that we have a few of us in the Court of Appeal.
I remember when Honourable Justice Bello, CJN, was about to retire, he came here and I asked him, when are we going to have a woman in the Supreme Court? He said to me that left to him as a person, we would have had one. Even at that time, we were to have a female Chief Judge in Kaduna. He told us that he couldn't do that because the Moslems would not accept a female C.J. That the Alkalis will not accept her as their head. Fortunately, he had to make her the first female Justice of the Court of Appeal to compensate her. So you can see the obstacles that women face.
As a word of advice to younger female lawyers in Nigeria, what does it takes for a woman to reach the peak in a male dominated profession like law?
In every profession a woman starts from minus zero. I advise them to start from there to the top. Before a woman is recognised, she was to work harder and excel and do much more better than her male counterparts.
It's only when she does that she will be recognised. If she is at par with men she doesn't stand a chance.
So, I advise them to work very hard. they should believe that the sky is the limit. One day they will be recognised.
The issue of declining standards in practice is one that has been of concern among senior members of the bar in recent times. What was practice like in your earlier days and how can it be improved upon now?
Well, in our earlier days a lawyer was looked at as learned, respectable person in the society. Law was his life, his profession and he lived on it. If he worked very hand he got results.
Today, law is not as lucrative financially as it used to be. There are some, a few that find it very lucrative. But you find that some lawyers mix law practice with other professions to make ends meet. Of course, the law itself must suffer. This is because you have to prepare your cases very well, prepare your address very well, interview your clients and witnesses in your chambers.
You must prepare before you go to court. When you get to court you must deliver.
If you are preparing a document, you must prepare that document looking at precedents and authorities and strive to make it a masterpiece.
That gave us joy in those days.
Today, to some lawyers, what gives them joy is how much they have got out of it. Of course, if that is the case the standard must come down.
But we are doing our best to see that the standard does not go lower. In Ibadan, the standard of practice is higher than any where in this country. When you talk of lowering standards, Ibadan is still much better and we are doing our best to ensure that the young ones who want to practice law really practice law. We have juniors and we make sure we bring them up to continue in that direction.
Coming back to the women issue, women empowerment and women in politics. The first female Speaker of a State House of Assembly in Nigeria was frustrated and hounded out of office in Benue State. Now, the only female Deputy Governor in the present dispensation has just suffered the same fate in Lagos State. Currently, the only female Presidential aspirant, Mrs. Sarah Jubril is not taken seriously. What is really wrong with the womenfolk in Nigeria? What is your assessment of the Nigerian woman?
Well, I can say that it's not women themselves that are the problem. It is the society. For instance, if a woman is a Deputy Governor, if her party does not give her the support, if her governor does not give her encouragement and there is no rapport, you find that she will be a lone ranger among men who are interested in ousting her.
I am not holding brief for the Deputy Governor that she is a perfect person. But she is not the worst Deputy Governor in the country. I think, when you find that two people are unable to work together, it won't be exactly because this is a woman. But it's worse because she is woman and, of course they will ask 'in the first place, how did she get there?' So, she can stand a chance with them.
You see, I will advise women, if you want to go into politics, go into politics on your own merit. If you get there, look at the office and do your best to carry out the responsible that office imposes on you. Another thing you have to look at is that we are advocating that women should support women in the coming elections. We are advising that women whenever we find a woman capable, we should support her. We are advocating that.
We are not saying that we don't have other women who don't rate women high. But we are trying to educate ourselves. For instance, when we had our Zonta meeting recently, we invited Professor Sowunmi to talk on this issue. It generated a lot of debate. Part of the debate was that we women are our own enemies. We advocated that we set out to educate women that we need to support ourselves. If we don't support ourselves, nobody will support us. We can only be stronger if we support ourselves. Men who will want to pull us down will not succeed. We are at that and by the special grace of God we will get there.
The death of Chief Bola Ige was quite traumatic for both the legal profession and the government. Now, the suspects are undergoing trial here at Ibadan. You, being based here, must be conversant with the goings on. Given the political dimensions that the case has assumed, would you say that justice would be done at the end of the day?
You see, I want to believe that a court will do justice any time. The moment we suspect that justice will not come from the court, the common man is finished.
I want to believe that a judge once called a judge will do what justice demands in every matter that comes before him. So, I won't say that justice will not be done. Justice is not just who wins or who lose.
Losing a case may still be justice and winning it could be justice. So, I want to believe that justice will be done in the end. Of course the judge can make a mistake. Why do we have the Court of Appeal? Why do we have the Supreme Court? If a judge at the High Court makes a mistake, you go to the Court of Appeal. If the Justice there makes mistakes you go to the Supreme Court. If they confirm the judgment there, you leave it to the final arbiter.
God is the final arbiter. But I believe that the people who killed Chief Bola Ige will not go scot-free. They will not! Even today, they are suffering for it. Even if they are not the people facing trial, the Bible says, what you sow, you will reap.
There is no way they can be happy and joyous and live joyfully for the rest of their lives with that on their conscience.
I observed that you have a Christian Fellowship here in your chambers. Now, there is so much God - consciousness in Nigeria today. We have churches in every nook and cranny of our cities, yet there is still so much crime, violence and corruption in Nigeria. Is it that we are not praying hard enough? What exactly is our problem?
The way I see it is that if we had not turned to God, the situation would have been worse. Definitely, the situation would have been worse.
God has been answering our prayers. Look at the time that Abacha wanted to translate into a civilian president. God intervened. Look at all other instances. If we had not turned to God, this country would have gone to pieces. What we are going through in this country and what we are getting away from, other countries would have gone to war to destroy themselves. Look at the way things are in the country but we still have two or three meals in a day. I think God is still in control. The children of God who are real children of God have our prayers answered. That is why things have not got worse. But I believe that things will inprove.
You know that some of these killings, robberies and so on is as a result of unemployment and hunger. Hunger! The only thing we are complaining about is the killing of innocent people.
Again you find that before they go into these operations they are no longer human beings. They have taken something that removes them from humanity, so they behave like animals. Can you imagine somebody robbing you of your car and still killing you?
It makes no sense! It's just because the man is not himself. But I believe that with the number of Christians praying everyday God is at our rescue. We will not give in. We will continue! I believe that God will come to our aid. God has been with us, that is why this country is still one.
It has been said that there is this age old mutual mistrust between the Igbos and the Yorubas which makes the proverbial handshake across the Niger a mirage. As an Igbo married to a Yoruba, how have you been able to strike a balance between your ethnic and cultural leanings, being married to a Yoruba and building a stable home in the heart of Yorubaland?
When I first came to Ilesha, my husband's uncle used to call me Kobokobo! After a year or two, I was passing through the front of his house and he called me his daughter.
He said, 'You are my daughter, you are no longer Kobokobo'. He went into his house and brought some yams and gave to me.
I think the differences between the Yorubas and the Igbos are strictly political. Outside of the politics, the two tribes have very good rapport. There are very many Igbos married to Yorubas, just like you have many Yorubas married to Igbos.
Disagreements only come in through politics. I listened to the Primate (Mbang) last month. He said that the problem of Nigeria will be solved when the South unites. I took him seriously because the present politicians will not be there forever.
The younger people know what the problems are. If you go outside this country, Nigerians are Nigerians. They don't say, 'this one is Yoruba or this one is Igbo! I believe that its a question of time and God will answer our prayers. I also believe our leaders should remove self and think of country first. That is their problem. Self. They want to satisfy self first I want to rule, I don't want to support a Yoruba man. An Igbo man must not get there. That is self.
I really believe that it's a question of time, it will all be well. My father-in-law loved me very much. I got away with a lot of things because I am not Yoruba woman. So, that made things easy for me.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuffs Remain High
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuffs Remain High
Body_Text:
There has been a steady increase in the prices of foodstuffs after Sallah, Christmas and new year festivities in the Federal Capital Territory.
A market survey of the two main markets in Abuja conducted by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) showed that prices of foodstuffs have increased, as opposed to the expectation that they would crash after the festivities.
NAN found that a "Mudu" (measure) of yellow gari which sold for N50 during the festivities last December, now costs between N55 and N60, while a bag which costs N2,500, now shows a N700 increase.
A "Mudu" of white gari which sold for between N45 and N50 last December now sells for N55, while a bag now costs N1,800 up from N1,700.
A "Mudu" of imported rice which sold for N110 during the festivities, now costs N120, while a bag, which was sold for N3,300, now costs N3,750 and N4,100, depending on the brand.
Brown beans which was sold at N80 per "Mudu" last December now costs N100, while a bag, which sold N8,300 now costs N14,000.
White beans which costs N60 per "Mudu" is now N80, while a bag, which was sold for N10,500, now costs N12,500.
A bottle of vegetable oil was sold for N100 last December, but now costs N120, while that extracted from "Kulikuli" which was sold for N110 now costsN140.
A bottle of palm-oil which was sold for N90 is now 140.
Twenty litres of vegetable oil, "Kulikuli" oil and palm oil now sell for N4,200, N5,500 and N5,600 respectively.
A large tuber of yam which was formerly sold for N70, now costs N150, a medium size tuber, which was N50, now costs N100, while the tiny ones which sold for N40 a piece, now costs N70.
The price of a kilogram of meat, however, remained unchanged at N350.
Most traders attributed the increases to the high cost of transportation and increased demand for foodstuffs since the festive period.
Consumers who spoke with NAN, however, complained about insensitivity on the part of the traders.
A civil servant, Okey Chima, said the traders ought to have known that a very large percentage of Abuja residents were civil servants who had spent their money during the festivities.
"They should have sympathy for us as we have not been paid any salaries since Christmas. Coupled with that, many of us have school fees to pay," he lamented.
Another resident, Mrs Juliana Usman, said she was optimistic that prices would fall by the time demand reduces.
"Nobody has money to throw around at this time of the year and demand is bound to fall," she said.
Visits to some other markets on the outskirts of the metropolis showed that businesses were picking up as more traders returned from the Christmas and new year vacations.

Original_Search_Title: Checking Fire Disasters in Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Checking Fire Disasters in Abuja
Body_Text:
Last Friday at about 6:30 pm, some parts of the Wuse Old Market in Abuja went up in flames recording a second incidence of fire outbreak in a major market in less than one month.
In the last week of December last year, barely three weeks ago, the new Wuse market had its share of fire, which the market has become notorious for. According to reports from the Federal Fire Service, on the day the fire outbreak occurred at Wuse new market, all the stations of the service had gone to quench one fire outbreak or the other before the new market incident brought them together later. What this means is that about four cases of fire incidents occurred that day in the Abuja city centre.
Unlike the Wuse new market, which has had over 17 (seventeen) cases of fire outbreaks, the old market has not been known for five disasters. The only recorded of fire outbreak that occurred there before that of last Friday was around Easter period in 2001 but it was only restricted to a shop which got burnt completely.
Last Friday's fire, Abuja Trust gathered started at 6:30 pm at a gas shop and spread to over 48 shops. There are speculations in some quarters that another 40 to 45 attachment shops were also affected.
The whole stations of the fire service turned out to the venue. The zone 3 Wuse, Garki Area 10, Federal Secretariat all other stations came with their tanks except the Nyanya station which has been off for the past one year due to breakdown of their appliance, which is said to be under repair.
The fire was later brought under control after over five hours with the help of NNPC fire service. The Julius Berger (Nig) Plc refused to send their tank or mobilise their fire service because, as Abuja Trust gathered, the management had instructed that the tank should not be released.
Since December 2001, Abuja has been inundated with fire outbreaks, which the fire service put at an average of three every week.
It all started early in December 2001 when the AIT/Ray Power lost its Abuja centre to fire.
The office, which was situated at the top floor of the Labour House, was gutted by fire, which was said to have broken out as a result of fluctuation in power supply to the station.
The fire was said to have originated fromthe TV studio where the network of cables caught fire and extended to the whole of the office.
Modern broadcast equipment worth millions of naira were lost in the inferno.
In less than two weeks after the incident, the Wuse new market was gutted by fire. According to a source, the fire broke out in a boutique where a seller was trying to put on a faulty generator. The attempt to bring the generating set to life resulted in a spack, which then ignited some of the material on sale leading to spread of the fire.
The fire did not only render so many people who lived in the market homeless but their means of livelihood were completely lost to the inferno. Those who could not contain the reality of the event were said to have jumped into the fire.
Last year, fire incidents which seem to have been restricted to harmattan periods and dry season occurred several times. On the 13th of February 2002, the new building that is to serve as the headquarters of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs under construction caught fire.
The wooden scaffold and other wood works got burnt completely. But for the arrival of firemen at the scene, more harm would have been wrecked on the building.
Abuja Trust gathered that the fire was suspected to have originated from the kitchen of the laboures at the building who once in a while roast yams and eat same with red palm oil.
On 14th February 2002, one of the floors of one of the three storey buildings at plot 1954 Cotonou Crescent, Wuse zone 6, popularly called Nigerian Social Insurance Trust Fund (NSTF) Wuse estate was lost to fire.
At about 9:30 am, that fateful morning, thick smoke started coming out from one of the flats at the top floor. Then there was fire coming out of the roof, people began to gather outside especially at the express road overlooking the building. Cars passing by started parking to catch a glimpse of what the fire was all about. It took the combination of efforts of the fire service, the NNPC servicemen and those of Julius Berger to bring the fire under control.
According to sources at the fire service, fire outbreaks occur regularly in Abuja. Last year many fire outbreaks occurred but the irony about them is that people don't know about them; people only know about fire outbreaks when markets are involved. Places affected include MTN Network station Wuse II, SCC (a construction company) building in Wuse II, the homes of General T. Y. Danjuma Minister of Defence and that of Mamman Yusuf.
On March 27, 2002, the Edo State Governor's lodge was gutted by fire right in the presence of the governor, Chief Lucky Igbinedion.
There were also fire incidents at the American Embassy and the police workshop in Gaki II. Abuja Trust gathered that the police have acquired fire fighting appliances, which have not been mounted. It seems the fire service command in Abuja anticipated the current fire incidents in Abuja which may have prompted it to recruite over 100 firemen in March 2001 and sent them for training in Lagos.
The training ended in July 2001, were it not for the additional fire fighters, the story would have been different in most of the fire outbreaks in Abuja.
In this 2003, just three weeks into the year, there have been three major incidents of fire in Abuja. The first was in Kado, near the estate where a life was lost.
Abuja Trust gathered that the Kado fire incident was unfortunate as the victim was said to have been locked inside on his own request. His friend whom he visited was going out and the boy requested that he should be locked inside to avoid being disturbed since he wanted to sleep and was not planning to go anywhere. His burnt bones were gathered at the foot of the door to the bedroom. It was the first time in two years according to official reports, that somebody was to die in a fire incident in the city.
On Thursday, 16th January, a part of the Supreme Court quarters was gutted by fire only for the Wuse old market to be visited by the same fate the next day Friday 17th January 2003.
Abuja Trust gathered that the men of the fire service are always incapacitated in the event of fire outbreak because of either lack of equipment or the obsolete nature of the ones available.
"When we go to the fight fire you see onlookers applauding, "Berger", because they often think that Julius Berger fire fighters are doing better than us but in most cases we are the people who make use of the equipment" one firemen who pleaded anonymity had told Abuja Trust.
According to him the service is greatly hampered by the unavailability of fire fighting equipment: "even at that, we are often the first to arrive on any scene of fire outbreak" the fire service man said and asked government to equip the fire service with modern fire fighting equipment.
Abuja Trust investigations revealed that the four fire service stations in Abuja situated in Wuse Zone 3, Garki Area 10, Federal Secretariat and Nyanya have only 4 trucks.
Wuse Zone 2 has truck number FG 443 B20 with a capacity of about 2000 litres, which takes only about 5 to 7 minutes to discharge in the event of fire outbreak. That is why more time is lost in going back to refill them when fighting the fire.
The Garki Area 10 station which is also the headquarters has truck number FG 438 B20 with a capacity of about 1000-1500 litres, which takes less than 5 minutes to discharge in the process of fighting fire.
The Federal Secretariat has truck FG 445 B20 with the biggest capacity of 8,500 litres and takes about 30 minutes to be discharged using hose reel or 15 minutes when delivery hose is applied. In most fires the delivery hose which has greater pumping pressure and quantity of water pumped out is often used.
The Nyanya station has not been functional in the last one year but Abuja Trust gathered that repairs are being effected now on the equipment. Abuja Trust gathered that a new station would soon be opened at station house, Abuja.
In most fire incidents in Abuja, the NNPC firemen, Julius Berger and sometimes the with Airport firemen join hands to bring such fire under control. One of the NNPC fire trucks with number AH 436 BWR has about 10,000 litres capacity bigger than any of the ones possessed by the fire service. Julius Berger sometimes comes with a truck serving as reservoir to the main truck.
However, Abuja Trust gathered that despite efforts to contact Julius Berger to help in the Friday fire outbreak, the construction company refused because management had instructed otherwise. This made the situation worse especially when Garki truck was said to have had a flat tyre in their second round of going for refill (it went for refill four times). Wuse station truck was said to have ran out of diesel leaving only the NNPC truck and that of Federal Secretariat station.
Going by the quantum of losses incurred by individuals and even government to fire and sometimes to thieves during such incidents, it has become imperative for government to equip the fire service very well to enable them tackle any outbreak.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Pushes for Debate
Headline: Ghana: Minister Pushes for Debate
Body_Text:
...over constitutional clause barring gov't interference in chieftaincy matters
The Western Regional Minister, Hon. Joseph Boahen Aidoo, has called for a debate and possible review of the portion of the 1992 constitution that bars the government and its officials from meddling in chieftaincy matters in the country.
He said this particular clause was inserted into the 1969 constitution by its framers because of the autocratic nature of then CPP government when the president or any of his officials sitting in Accra could just get up to say that a particular chief has been destooled.
According to him, currently no sitting president or his minister under the fourth republican constitution can just get up to say that a particular chief has been destooled and therefore there is no need for such a clause to remain in our constitution for some of the chiefs to exploit to their advantage.
Speaking to the Western press corps at the end of the year party he organized for them at his official residence in Sekondi last December, Hon.
Aidoo said his decision to call for the review of that particular clause in the constitution stemmed from the fact that some of the chiefs are using it to prevent government officials from intervening in chieftaincy disputes that threaten life and property in their traditional areas.
Hon. Aidoo, who is also the Member of Parliament for Amenfi East, said the chieftaincy institution has its own rules and regulations which must be followed by chiefs and their subjects, but some of them sometimes either deliberately or not do flout these rules and regulations resulting in misunderstanding which usually leads to disputes in their traditional areas.
He regretted that anytime chieftaincy matters would raise their head in some of these traditional areas and a government official responsible for the well being of the people tried to come out with measures to ensure peace and unity, he is immediately accused of meddling in chieftaincy matters.
"When some of these chieftaincy disputes start occurring in areas under your jurisdiction you cannot sit down for people to lose their lives. Definitely you have to go in but this laudable idea is misinterpreted to mean that you are meddling in chieftaincy matters," he told the journalists whose questions prompted him to make the comment.
Hon. Aidoo, who is seeking re-election to represent his people in parliament, made a special reference to Bamiako, a town in the Nzema East district of the Western Region, where one person was shot dead and several others injured in a chieftaincy dispute that rocked the town in December, last year.
According to the regional minister, when Awulae Angama Tua-Gyan, the omanhene of the Bamiako traditional area, announced the date for celebration of his annual yam festival, he received intelligence report from the security men on the ground that the festival was allowed to come on as planned there would be fatalities.
The regional minister further told the reporters, who kept on asking more questions on the seemingly unending chieftaincy disputes in the region, that after he had received the intelligence report he also informed the Nzema East security council and asked them to take up the matter.
He said the district Security Council also met all the feuding parties, including the Omanhene, Awulae Angama Tua-Gyan, on how to ensure an incident free festival.
As part of the security measures, the Omanhene was also asked not to ride in a palanquin during the day of the festival, which he agreed.
Hon. Aidoo however regretted that having agreed earlier not to ride in a palanquin as advised by the security men, the Omanhene reneged on his promise and allowed himself to be carried in a palanquin during the day of the festival and this resulted in the violence that occurred that day, which also led to the shooting to death of one person and injuries to several others.
The minister admitted that the Western Region had been bedevilled with a number of chieftaincy disputes but said if what the chiefs themselves have told him is anything to go by then the disputes may soon be over.
"In fact, I have met the chiefs and they themselves have assured me that the problem will be brought under control," he told the reporters, who were given a gastronomic treat by his outfit.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Now a Traditional Export Commodity
Headline: Ghana: Yam Now a Traditional Export Commodity
Body_Text:
Accra — Fresh yam has since November 20, 2002 been classified as a traditional export commodity, the Ministry of Trade and Industry said on Tuesday.
A notice to exporters and the general public by the sector Minister, Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku said the use of the Ghana Export Form for export of Non-Traditional Goods would no longer apply to yam export.
The notice said all yam exporters should therefore use the Bank of Ghana Exchange Control Form A.2.

Original_Search_Title: Food Cooked in 'Kaveera' Bad
Headline: Uganda: Food Cooked in 'Kaveera' Bad
Body_Text:
Kampala — IT is lunchtime, the local shed where local dishes are sold is packed to
capacity. A customer calls out and makes his order, loud enough for everyone
to hear. The cook, with beads of sweat trickling down her face, lifts the
cover of one of the saucepans and proceeds to unwrap the huge polythene bag
covering the food, picks a plate and piles on some steaming matooke.
She then uncovers another saucepan, undoes the polythene covering, wipes
her hand on her apron, picks a sweet potato and lays it on the plate with
the matooke. The cook then opens the lid of another saucepan and serves some
groundnut sauce before walking through the benches to deliver the order to
the customer.
Has it ever occurred to you that such meals could lead to illness for
the rest of your life.
In many of the local food stalls, the food is covered with buveera as it
cooks. The 'logic' behind this method of cooking is that the food gets ready
faster and is kept steaming hot. Once in a while, there have been complaints
of the food assuming the colour of the buveera, but this has not stopped
people from going ahead and eating it.
However, research has revealed that under certain temperatures,
polythene can disintegrate and release chemicals that are detrimental to
human health.
Polythene is made up a combination of carbon and hydrogen and thus is a
hydrocarbon. It is a tough, translucent, waxy and thermoplastic (can be
repeatedly softened by heating).
According to Andrew Othieno, the senior environmental inspector of
National Environmental Management Authority (NEMA), when polythene bags are
made certain properties with different compounds are added to make them suit
the purpose for which they are made.
One of the components that are added include chlorine. Buveera
disintegrate under temperatures of less than 1,000 degrees centigrade and
begin to release some toxic fumes.
These toxic fumes contain chemical compounds known as dioxins and
furans. They are also called unintentional by-products because they are
released by mistake as a result of combustion. These chemical can also be
created by car exhaust fumes, and emissions from the incineration of
hospital waste, municipal waste, hazardous waste and wood.
Dioxins and furans have been blacklisted internationally because they
pose a serious threat to human health and the environment.
These categories are known as Persistent Organic Pollutants
(POP's). There is scientific evidence that exposure to even low doses of
POP's can lead to cancer, damage to the nervous and immune systems,
reproductive disorders and interference with normal infant and child
development.
Thus many countries have come together to put an end to the production
of this unintentional combustion by-products, together with 10 other POP's
including pesticides and industrial chemicals.
This has led to the (POP's) Stockholm Convention, a treaty that sets out
control the production, import, export, disposal and use of POP's.
In Uganda, efforts have been made both by NEMA and Government to try and
reduce the use of polythene for instance in the last budget, a high tax was
imposed on buveera to serve as an economic disincentive. Alternative
measures have been proposed such as using paper bags or totally banning the
use of polythene.

Original_Search_Title: Rebuilding Bridges to Reunite the Country
Headline: Angola: Rebuilding Bridges to Reunite the Country
Body_Text:
Luanda — As new bridges are laid in Angola, replacing those broken or blown up
during the war, previously isolated communities are slowly being opened
up.
The quickly-built military bridges are not only a short-term solution to
allow the delivery of humanitarian aid, they also open the door to trade and
long-term development in many rural areas.
The bridge over the Quissafo river, 65 km north of N'dalatando, the
provincial capital of Cuanza Norte, was blown up during the 1980s. Driving
between N'dalatando and the provincial capital of Uige further to the north
required a detour through the bush and the use of a small, temporary bridge
- a route that was only passable during the dry season.
The new bridge and road will provide tens of thousands of people with
access to World Food Programme (WFP) aid, and the markets in
N'dalatando.
"The area has recently been made available to returnees. We need the
bridge to get through during the rainy season - without it, people would be
without food for between three and six months," Uladip Onayemi, WFP's base
manager in N'dalatando told IRIN.
The Quissafo river bridge is the last of nine key bridges around the
country to be built by the Swedish Rescue Service Agency (SRSA) on behalf of
WFP.
"It was hard to live here during the war. If you look closely at the
trees, you will find bullets in them. This whole area was a
battlefield. Many trucks, especially the ones carrying food, were ambushed
and the drivers were killed," said Francisco JoÃ£o Diogo, one of 10 local men
from the National Institute for Roads in Angola (INEA) helping the SRSA to
build the bridge.
His colleague, Pedro Manuel Antonio, is a mechanic in INEA. "It is hard
work, but the bridge is an important step forward for the development of the
region. It will help us to get in contact with other people again, to
develop a market and get easier access to food. During the rainy season we
have always lacked food - corn, vegetable oil, salt, fish," Antonio
said.
Ten casual workers from the surrounding villages also work on the site,
receiving food and US $5 every day. They arrive early in the morning,
wearing torn clothes and light sandals, in contrast to the men from INEA,
who wear helmets and boots.
After the bridge was blown up, the forest took over the road. Some 250
metres had to be cut down to reach the site. Before the work started, a
de-mining team from Norwegian Peoples' Aid cleared the area.
When IRIN visited, the workers were putting together a much larger and
heavier 36-metre bridge to span the river. The air was intensely hot and
humid and the workers' faces and bodies were wet with sweat.
The three-by-three metre frame sections were quickly put together and
the metal skeleton slowly stretched over the river towards the other
shore. It was anticipated that it would take six or seven days to complete
the bridge.
Tomas JoÃ£o Neto is one of the casual workers. He was born in one of the
villages close to the river but, 12 years ago, when he was seven years old,
he moved to the capital, Luanda, to escape the encroaching war. Four months
ago he came back with his three brothers, three sisters and his mother. His
father died just before they returned.
He usually works in his field where they grow sweet potatoes, beans,
onion, manioc and maize. "It's a nice change to work here, and the money is
important. I am the only one in my family who earns money. Five dollars is
not much, but I think I will be able to buy some clothes and food in
N'dalatando," Neto said. It is 60 km from his village to N'dalatando, and he
will have to take a bus to go there. A return ticket will cost him more than
$10.
Neto thinks the bridge will make a big difference to the village. "When
it is ready, many people will return. This used to be a crowded area, now
only 300 people live here. In two years there will be a lot more people here
again," he predicted.

Original_Search_Title: Maganjo Cleared for AGOA
Headline: Uganda: Maganjo Cleared for AGOA
Body_Text:
Kampala — AN inspection team from the Whitaker Group visited the Kawempe-based
Maganjo Maize Millers early this week.
The Whitaker Group, a consultancy firm helping locals exploit the Africa
Growth Opportunity Act (AGOA), is owned by a former assistant US trade
representative, Rosa Whitaker.
Maganjo is one of the four companies that have been cleared by the firm
to benefit from the AGOA initiative. AGOA allows for duty/quota-free access
to the US market.
Whitaker's Susan Linye, said they had identified five Maganjo products
that could compete in the US domestic market.
These include Soy drink, Kwik instant porridge, Maganjo high protein
baby porridge and sweet potatoes.
Linye commended Maganjo for their packaging, but advised them to improve
on labelling.
Maganjo currently exports 200 five-kilogramme packets of maize flour
every month to the US by special arrangement.
"We send 200 packets of flour to the United States and 50,000 packets of
two and five kilos to John Whirler trading company in the UK every
month. Our brands compete fairly in East London," Alex Luggya, the sales and
marketing manager said.
He said: "We are ready for the challenge though we do not know how big
the US market is. They saw our competing brands in the UK that is how they
identified the company."
The visitors were led by Susan Muhwezi, the special presidential advisor
on AGOA and Wasswa Hashim, a commercial advisor.

Original_Search_Title: There's a Zulu in My Soup, And He Looks Tasty
Headline: South Africa: There's a Zulu in My Soup, And He Looks Tasty
Body_Text:
The fourth annual V&A Waterfront Winter Food Fair got off to a frivolous start last night as chefs from five city restaurants were given the opportunity to showcase their culinary know-how and artistic flair.
Called Food as Art and hosted by Giggling Gourmet Jenny Morris, teams from Balducci's, Jewel of India, Balducci's Royal Sushi Bar, Baia Seafood Restaurant, and the Commodore Hotel were given an hour in which to slice and dice their raw food and vegetables into a delectable artwork.
The judging panel, which included the Cape Argus's Andrew October and Bianca Coleman, had a difficult time choosing a winner as the chefs pulled out all the stops to impress.
Kerry Correis and Raynor Damons of Balducci's prepared a "gamey" piece, which they said reflected the restaurant's philosophy.
"We used mainly venison, because Balducci's has an African and wildlife theme," said Damons.
Jewel of India presented a more conservative piece, with chefs Arun Shinde and Rajendra Singh presenting favourite North Indian dishes like pulav rice and butter chicken.
Jason Sun of Balducci's Royal Sushi Bar, created a sushi garden with a South African sushi flag as the centrepiece.
Gert Biedenhann and Nabeelah Kriel of Baia, opted to use only fruit and vegetables for their display.
"This is the first time we've done something like this and it was fun," said Biedenhann.
But the design that attracted the most attention and was the eventual winner, was the Commodore Hotel's Zulu, made from seaweed, lemon grass, ginger, spring roll pastry, chillies, sweet potatoes and a host of other vegetables.
Chefs Markus Ferreira and Chris Wheeler said they had not created anything like it before. "What can be more African than the Zulu," said a delighted Ferreira.
The fair, which will run until Sunday at the Marquee on Market square, is a celebration of the best food and cuisine that South Africa has to offer.
With more than 35 exhibitors from across the province, the fair offers the ideal chance for small businesses and gourmet food producers to show their wares.
A highlight of the three-day jamboree will be the celebrity cook-off tomorrow that will see a local politicians take on sports celebrities. This will be in aid of Feedback, a charity that distributes excess food.
Other gastronomic delights include the Food Theatre; a top-class demo kitchen; gourmet food producers' stands; a wine bar; tea tastings; a pub with coffee and cake and a big screen; and a Kids Corner.

Original_Search_Title: Miner Crashed to Death
Headline: Zambia: Miner Crashed to Death
Body_Text:
A Twenty-eight year-old miner died on the spot yesterday after being run over by an underground loader.
Copperbelt police chief Grapheal Musamba confirmed that the miner, a machine operator of Mpelembe Drilling, died at Mindolo north shaft on level number 950 after being run over by an underground loader.
Mr Musamba named the dead miner as Jesper Kabasa of 1/73 Mindolo township.
He said the late Kabasa sustained multiple body injuries and died on the spot.
Mr Musamba said the driver of the loader ran over the miner because of poor visibility after a blast.
He said police were still investigating the matter to determine whether there was foul play.
And a woman is admitted to the Ndola Central Hospital after her husband hacked her with an axe on the back of her head.
Ndola police chief Moses Chishimba confirmed the incident which occurred on Monday afternoon.
Mr Chishimba identified the victim as Helen Chanda of Chipulukusu township.
The assailant who was detained at Ndola central police station would be charged with attempted murder, police said.
The man was believed to have been on separation with his wife for 11 months.
Eye-witnesses said they were taken by surprise when a man produced an axe and hacked the unsuspecting woman inflicting a deep cut on the back of her head.
Mr Chishimba said the woman was at the time selling sweet potatoes at Mulungushi bus station in the town centre. She was rushed to the hospital where she was immediately admitted.
An instant justice mob almost lynched the assailant before police whisked him away.
"The man could have been killed by the instant justice mob which was angered by his action but he was saved by officers patrolling the area," Mr Chishimba said.
Meanwhile, one of the drivers who was injured in the Mtendere bus station rumpus at the weekend is still admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) in the University Teaching Hospital (UTH) in Lusaka.
UTH acting public relations manager Francis Manda described the condition of the driver as serious.
He said the other two victims were treated and discharged.
Police are holding a call-boy to help with investigations over the wounding of the three mini-bus drivers after a clash over levies.
Bus Drivers and Motor Taxis Association (BDMTA) vice-chairman Mwape Mumba said the call-boys were demanding K5,000 and K3,000 per load for big and small buses.
A check at Mtendere bus station yesterday found that police officers had since been deployed to maintain law and order.

Original_Search_Title: Genuine Traditional Fast Food Comes to Town
Headline: Zimbabwe: Genuine Traditional Fast Food Comes to Town
Body_Text:
IMAGINE a city sleaker, clad in an expensive three-piece suit, briefcase in hand, pulling up at a fast food outlet to ask for tea and mbambaira (sweet potatoes).
Or better still, sipping from a gourd of mahewu while he waits for sadza and peanut-buttered relish. Such a menu can only make your mouth water if it is read in Shona or Ndebele.
Imagine a menu that reads: Dapura Hunanzva reSadza ne Machikichori Akasiyana-siyana.
This is the new revolution in fast food which has come to Zimbabwe with the opening of Gogo's-a fast food outlet specialising in traditional cuisine in the central business district of Harare.
The new outlet can only be described as a true taste of Zimbabwe's rich food heritage which has long been looked down upon because of the influence of Western diets.
Even in free and proud Zimbabwe, very few people, especially the young and rich, would want to be heard saying they just had sadza for lunch; they would rather talk of buying chicken and chips and other expensive Western food when local food is cheaper and often more nourishing and tasty.
The way local languages were once despised by the educated and 'sophisticated' and relegated to the peripheries of syllabuses in school, is the same way traditional cuisine has been treated - more than two decades after independence.
The opening of Gogo's, which is soon to go national, should prove how much Zimbabweans do enjoy their own traditional dishes just as the Chinese, the Indians, the British, the Americans and others enjoy theirs.
The revolution also comes with the local invention by William Gwata, of the "Gwatamatic" machine, which cooks sadza enough for a thousand people in 45 minutes.
This invention brings a turnaround in sadza cooking, as one can programme it to mix all the necessary ingredients. The end product is sadza which no-one can complain to be too thick or anything else.
"Our vision is to be the African fast food brand of choice. We envisage making Gogo's a haven for those who are nostalgic about traditional cuisine, a cool place for those young at heart and a place where one gets a homely meal in a cut throat working environment," said one of the directors and Chairman of Gogo's Livingstone Gwata.
"Our menu, which is essentially based on local varieties of sadza ne mupunga, is significantly enhanced by our wide spectrum of exotic ethnic relishes and delicacies such as guru remusana, Barbecue free-range chicken, mufushwa, mukaka une ruomba, derere and muriwo unedovi," he said.
Deserts include fruits and delicacies in season like mawuyu, tsubvu, nhunguru, masawu, madora, mazhanje, mutetenerwa and maranjisi.

Original_Search_Title: Nine Killed in Road Accident
Headline: Zambia: Nine Killed in Road Accident
Body_Text:
NINE people died on the spot in Kitwe when a Charalambous Transport
truck collided with a Mitsubishi vannette on Chingola-Kitwe road.
Copperbelt police chief Graphael Musamba said the accident occurred on
Tuesday around 19:00 hours. He said the collision took place near Garneton
turn-off when the truck registration number ACG 2018 coming from Mufulira
rammed into the van as the truck driver attempted to overtake another
vehicle.
Mr Musamba said four passengers were in a critical condition in Kitwe
Central Hospital.
The vannette registration number ACB 1703 was being driven by Nicholas
Mumba of Kawama township in Kitwe, who is among those in hospital. There
were 13 passengers on the van.
The truck driver, Eliton Simbeye, is being questioned but has not yet
been charged. The dead, who were yet to be identified, were seven women and
two children. The vannette was laden with sweet potatoes and bags of
maize.
Meanwhile, police in Kabwe have summoned former first lady Vera Chiluba
Tembo to report to central division headquarters on Friday next week. Ms
Tembo last week knocked down Davy Mulawo near Chibombo turn-off as she was
driving to Lusaka from Ndola. The pedestrian died later in Liteta
Hospital.
Central Province police chief Auxensio Daka said his men had
communicated with the former first lady over the matter.

Original_Search_Title: Memorable Night As Artistes Pay Tribute to Late Mutuma
Headline: Zimbabwe: Memorable Night As Artistes Pay Tribute to Late Mutuma
Body_Text:
FRIDAY 21 February was a beautiful day for Zimbabwean music. They were all there the young and the old, the good and the bad, those who have arrived and those who are still on the way.
From the electrifying and well-choreographed Africa Revenge to charismatic Oliver Mtukudzi, the cool Summer Breeze, vintage Jabavu, effervescent Amagents, prodigal (and perhaps repentant) Andy Brown, lively Busi Ncube, and the good old Pied Pipers an almost Who's Who of Zimbabwean music all there to honour a man who on that night showed that even in death, he had friends real friends.
Seeing them give their all for free when they could have been making money elsewhere, one could not help but feel that this was the beginning of good things for Zimbabwean music in general and jazz in particular.
The place was Jazz 105, the home of Zimbabwean jazz. The occasion was the Jonah Sisonke Benefit Concert.
And the stage was set for a night to remember, a night to celebrate jazz and the life of one of our own. Some danced to remember, others to forget.
By the time I arrived at the venue Summer Breeze were already doing their thing. Fari Sumaili was a joy to watch and listen to on guitar. His intricate jazz chords, accompanied by the subtle melodies of Moses Kabubi's keyboards, serenaded by the riffs of a well-handled bass guitar and generous helpings of a saxophone sound set the stage for an unforgettable night.
Jazz 105 is not the biggest venue around, and the temperature was already beginning to rise. By the time Summer Breeze left the stage, we were all quite warm musically and it was apparent that such a disciplined and mature performance could only be the beginning of auspicious things.
Africa Revenge did not waste time in living up to the night's promise of fulfilment. Comfort Mbofana, who wore an appropriate skullcap and so effortlessly performed his duties as the master of ceremonies for the night, fittingly introduced them as "a band that is going places". Tazz and Willis, those fine and focused young men from the City of Kings, would have made Mutuma proud to be from Bulawayo, for with the able and youthful assistance of Ruth on backing vocals, Zex Manatsa's son on lead guitar, the two Mparutsa brothers on drums and keyboards, Master Pablo Nekapa on bass guitar, they sang beautifully and danced so breathtakingly, their harmonies soaring and and soaking Mutuma's night and, above all, talent. They gave us songs like Emawemi, Sihlobo Ungaqaqazile Mfo Wethu, uSomandla Ukubhekile among others.
And then it was time for Too Open. Yes, this band is called Too Open, and they played their own brand of jazz driven by the wizardry of their enterprising lead guitarist and inspired by the presence in their ranks of some of Mutuma's former band members in Sisonke.
Sisonke fittingly took to the stage to rapturous applause. And by this time the drink was beginning to have an effect on me and those others who were affording it the chance to o so, which was more or less everyone.
The sequence of events correspondingly became livelier, apparently not only to me because there was a lot more swaying of bodies and tapping of feet around me. But I remember that this Sisonke that drove and was driven by Mutuma, brought him right back that night. And those of us who saw him bade him goodbye.
Sisonke set the mood by playing a beautiful instrumental of one of Mutuma's songs from their album Sisonke Mdala.
Jairos Hambahamba, that effervescent keyboard player, immaculately dressed in flowing West African regalia, gave us a beautiful rendition of the title track Sisonke Mdala and we sang along.
But when he sang Pafunge, we raised our voices even higher threatening to blow the roof off with echoes of Pemberai Pemberai! Ndinotenda, Pemberai Pemberai!
Patience Musa was there too, her soulful Zvirinani urged on by the guitar playing of Jimmy "Dludlu" Buzuzi.
Then there was Bob Nyabinde, that irrepressible headmaster from Kwekwe, making what I thought was a surprise appearance, having been missing from the original line-up. Accompanied only by his guitar, he strode onto the stage, looking a little unsure, I thought. But the people cheered him on and his anguished delivery of Swing Low, Sweet Chariot was to me both a prayer and a send-off for our Jonah.
Earlier Nyabinde had said something quite sad but which also bore testimony to the love of Mutuma: "When I played in Harare for the first time, it was with Jonah at the Terreskane."
Then along came muzukuru Brown. As he took to the stage he coaxed his guitar with his usual dexterity and broke into the song Ndoenda. First, Busi Ncube joined him on backing vocals, then Mary Bell, then Sam Mataure on percussion, followed spontaneously by various other musicians.
And when Potato hopped onto the stage and shook the house with his gravelly tribute to Mutuma, the crowd went wild.
Jabavu were on fire! Those veterans of jazz were really hot. Singing with such flaming passion, Mukoma Philip Svosve delivered another beautiful song, Rudo Chete. Dumi Ngulube and Amagents were there too. Ngulube himself was inspired on keyboards, churning out some new material and older tunes such as Echoes From the Mountain and Shungu Mwanangu among others.
I did not watch Mtukudzi, due to urgent business that could not wait. In fact, I went out just as he was taking to the stage and tuning his guitar. But I have no regrets because I know he did justice to his name and to his presence, which on its own would have been enough without a performance. Chirikure Chirikure, Friday Mbirimi, and the Pied Pipers probably performed after I left. If they did, I know they too gave a good account for themselves.
For me, the point had been made there was no need to belabour it.
Over $500 000 had been raised. The truth had been well told. Mutuma had friends. And they all came. Some to give speeches, like Oliver Hawadi who, turned out immaculately in a white tuxedo, gave moral support just as he had done during Mutuma's living years.
Admire Taderera, whose love for jazz is well known, also gave a moving speech.

Original_Search_Title: Good Old Tastes of Home a Must for 'Fussy' Ozzies
Headline: South Africa: Good Old Tastes of Home a Must for 'Fussy' Ozzies
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — THE Australians are not only the most difficult cricket team to beat on
the field - they are also the most demanding in the dietary stakes.
The reigning Cricket World Cup champions have kept hotels on the hop
with their requests for imported beer, sandwich spread and no oil or fat in
their meals.
By contrast, 1999 Cricket World Cup runners-up Pakistan, India, England,
New Zealand and South Africa are proving a roll-over, in the hotel stakes,
at least. Heinz Kerber, executive chef at the Sandton Sun, said this week
the Australians were the "fussiest" of his World Cup guests.
The Sandton Sun is part of the Southern Sun group of hotels, which is
the official host of all the cricket teams.
Said Kerber: "The Australians are the most strict by far. Their
breakfast is not allowed to have any fat or oil in it." The team also
requests lots of cereals, fresh fruits and yoghurt.
Team members turned up their noses at Marmite and local beer, insisting
only their local beer, Fosters, and the Australian equivalent of Marmite,
called Vegemite, would do.
"They told us right from the start that they do not like the taste of
Marmite, so we had to import Vegemite especially for them," said
Kerber.
"They also were not very keen on our local brew and we had to find
Fosters for them."
He said the team had also brought along their own energy drinks, which
were stocked next to the Fosters in the fridge in the team's common
room.
Jonathan Rose, media manager for Australia, confirmed that the team had
strict dietary requirements, saying that every player's diet was set by a
dietician.
He confirmed team members had asked for Fosters beer and Vegemite. "Home
is where the heart is," he said.
Kerber said Pakistan was the only other team that had special dietary
requirements, but these were relatively simple to fulfil.
"The meat has to be sourced at halaal butcheries.
"Aside from this, we have had to make sure we do not ever serve team
members pork," he said.
Kerber said the hotel had also stocked up on exotic teas to please its
Asian guests.
"When it comes to the Asian sides, we know they like their teas, so we
have made sure we have good Ceylon and Jasmine teas available at all times
in the team rooms," he said .
Hotel staff members had treated all the teams equally, Kerber said.
However, they couldn't help showing a little favouritism towards "their
boys", the South African players.
The hotel assigned South African cricket captain Shaun Pollock the same
bedroom in which he stayed previously and also made sure that his favourite
sweets, Jelly Tots, were in a jar in his room, even though he had not
requested this.
The other team members were also given rooms they had occupied
previously.
But despite this "favouring" of South Africa, all the teams said they
were happy with the service they were receiving.
The New Zealand cricket team said they had been bowled over by South
African food.
"The food here has been great. The only thing we make sure they give us
lots of is protein and energy-replacement bars.
"But something the team loves the most is the double-thick chocolate
milkshakes. They have become a real favourite with team members since we've
been here. The steakhouses have the best milkshakes we've ever tasted," said
cricketer Daryl Tuffey.
He said the team rooms in the different hotels had also proved a
winner.
"Table tennis has been a real winner with us. We've had
mini-championships among ourselves. Some of the guys even brought their
PlayStations and X-Box sets and have been playing non-stop in their spare
time," said Tuffey.
Team manager for England Andrew Walpole said his team had been glutting
themselves on the golf courses and were also enjoying the food.
"A lot of the guys love a game of golf and South Africa has beautiful
courses. So we have been doing a lot of that in our spare time," he
said.
"The food here has been great and adventurous. The pap is interesting,
but we still prefer potatoes. But we've enjoyed the game dishes, as well as
the braais."
Pakistan team manager Shaharyar Khan said his team were having no
trouble at all finding food, despite their dietary requirements. " There is
no problem because we have found a lot of established Asian restaurants in
