whichever town we have visited," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Politicians Angry at UN Plan to Make Kenyan Commander of Peace Force
Headline: Nigeria: Reps Ask FG to Rebuff Okonkwo's Replacement As Ecomil Commander
Body_Text:
Abuja — THE House of Representatives Committee on Army has urged Nigerian defence authorities to stop the planned replacement of General Felix Okonkwo, the Nigerian commander of the ECOWAS Military Force in Liberia (ECOMIL) with a Kenyan General.Chairman of the House Committee on Army, Rep. Ado Dogo at a press briefing in Abuja yesterday, also revealed the discomfort of Nigerian troops in Liberia with the American imported meals they have been feeding on. The troops, he said, want local foods such as garri, yam.Mr. Dogo who just returned from an assessment visit of the Nigerian contingent in Liberia also disclosed logistical constraints facing the troops, notably finance and transport.
Observing that Nigeria's contribution of 1,580 officers and men to ECOMIL's total strength of 3310 officers and men should give the country a commanding role in the peace keeping operation, Mr. Dogo said:" Presently, General Okonkwo is the ECOMIL Commander in Liberia. He is expected to surrender such leadership to General Obande of Kenya when UN takes over the activities of ECOMIL in October 2003."The delegation learnt that General Obande is not in anyway better than General Okonkwo and moreover, Nigeria has the largest contingent in the peace keeping operation, why cant the country lobby for the retention of General Okonkwo as the Force Commander?"
Observing the discomfort of the Nigerian troops with American imported meals, the Committee chairman said: "The food presently supplied to NIGCON comes from the U.S. and most members of the contingent are usually not at home with it. They instead prefer home food such as garri, yams, apu etc. "Rep Dogo also disclosed other operational constraints facing the Nigerian delegation in Liberia such as transport and poor finances, but assured that the country's troops were giving a good impression of the country and its military even in the face of the limitations.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Gives Importers of Contraband Goods 2-Wks Notice
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Gives Importers of Contraband Goods 2-Wks Notice
Body_Text:
Calabar — THE Nigeria Customs Command in Calabar, Cross River State, has warned
all traders of imported items that have been banned by the Federal
Government to dispose them off within two weeks or risk being confiscated
and destroyed. Prominent on the list of the items are refrigerators, used
tyres, cars beyond eight years of manufacture, cement in bags, fruit juice,
wheat, sorghum, sugar, toothpick, toilet rolls, vegetable oil,
gamingmachines, mosquito repellant, drinking water, spaghetti noodles,
poultry products and many others.
Speaking to Vanguard in Calabar, yesterday, the Area Comptroller of
Customs in the state, Alhaji Shehu Umaru, said the action had become
necessary in view of the continued large influx of the banned items to the
state.
Umaru announced that his men would make sure that those items "were no
longer brought down here because they have been banned by the Federal
Government" adding that, it was inconceivable for items that have been
banned "to continue to flood the market with impunity."
On how he would actualize his plan to clean the markets and streets of
the items, he said, his command had its plan close to the chest and that
those found to have contravened government's directive would be
prosecuted.
He pointed out that "the society must not condone lawlessness", saying
"local industries must be protected from the activities of these smugglers"
also that, each of the items had its danger, such as the health hazards
associated with used refrigerators and the danger inherent in patronizing
used tyres.
"Government is concerned about the health of the people and anything
hazardous to the well-being of the people must be dispensed with and we
don't mind going from shop to shop, street to street to seize these
items. We shall burn them when they are confiscated". Umaru said.
On what becomes of seized cars, he said, "that will be determined when
it happens", but that when the papers of the cars contravene the
government's directive of not more than eight years, "we will definitely
impound such cars" and treat them as illegal goods in the country.
Speaking on the efforts of his command to boosting the nation's economy,
he said the target revenue allotted to the command had been surpassed,
stating that it had generated over N500 million, even though it still had
about over a month to the end of the year.

Original_Search_Title: Eagle Beer Wins Mercantus Award
Headline: Uganda: Eagle Beer Wins Mercantus Award
Body_Text:
Kampala — EAGLE Beer, Uganda's popular new local brew made from locally grown
sorghum has won the internationally recognised Mercantus Award.
Eagle Beer won the honour for being the best in the 'consumer insights'
category. The award recognises Eagle as a brand that meets the demands of
consumers right from the process of sorghum growth, brewing, and revenue
contribution to Government.
The Mercatus Awards is an annual event organised by the SABMiller group
to reward marketing excellence.
The South African based SABMiller is the world's second largest
brewer. The winners were announced in London on the 12th of this month by
SABMiller CEO. Graham Mackay.
"It is a huge achievement," said David Case of Moringa Ogilvy in
Uganda. "It is not just a little beer from Uganda but a globally recognised
beer."
Moringa Ogilvy handles the Public Relations and Marketing work of Nile
Breweries, the brewers of Eagle Beer.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Gives 2-Wk Deadline On Contraband
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Gives 2-Wk Deadline On Contraband
Body_Text:
Abuja — The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has given supermarkets and shop owners nationwide a two-week deadline to dispose of contraband or risk seizure. The Federal Government had in August, last year, banned the importation of some products, food items and other consumables into the country.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country," the NCS said in a statement in Abuja yesterday.
In the statement, Comptroller-General of the NCS, Alhaji Ahmed Mustapha said the service would on expiration of the ultimatum raid such shops nationwide. Mustapha said the enforcement team of the service would confiscate the banned items "whereever found" and also prosecute the offenders.
He directed Customs Area Controllers (CACs) to use the local media in their areas to educate and warn members of the public on the impeding action. The Customs boss also advised all formations nationwide to ensure strict compliance with the directive.
Among items outlawed were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellent coils, retreaded or used tyres, gaming machines and second-hand clothing. Others are used refridgerators and air-conditioning units, used compressors, bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles above eight years from date of manufacture, textiles of any kind through the land borders, printed fabric (Ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned are cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water (sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders. Government had also banned importation of sugar, all kinds of canned and bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and envelopes, Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Customs Begins Seizure of Contraband Nationwide
Headline: Nigeria: Customs Begins Seizure of Contraband Nationwide
Body_Text:
Lagos — ALL supermarkets and shop owners in the country who still stock and sell
contraband goods have been given two weeks, beginning yesterday, to dispose
of the products or risk prosecution.
Government had in August last year banned the importation of some
products, food items and other consumables into the country.
But, Comptroller-General of Nigeria Customs Service (NCS), Alhaji Ahmed
Mustapha, in a statement yesterday and made available to the News Agency of
Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja, warned that at the expiry of the deadline, such
goods will be seized.
Some of the items outlawed are used fridges and air conditioning units,
used compressors, used vehicles and those above eight years from date of
manufacture, bagged cement and others.
"We are inundated with reports of flagrant display and sale of banned
items in supermarkets, shops and market places across the country, Alhaji
Mustapha said.
Mustapha said the enforcement team of the service would confiscate the
banned items "whereever found" at the termination of the deadline and also
prosecute the offenders.
He directed the Customs Area Controllers to use the media in their
locality to educate and warn members of the public on the impending
action.
The Customs boss also advised all formations nationwide to ensure strict
compliance with the directive.
The NCS had recently raided warehouses in some popular markets in Lagos
and Kano and impounded contraband textiles and other items valued at
millions of naira.
Among items banned were sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable
oil in bulk, mosquito repellant coils, retreaded or used tyre, gaming
machines and second-hand clothes.
Others are textiles of any kind through the land borders, printed fabric
(ankara) and frozen poultry.
Also banned were cassava, cassava products, containerised goods through
the land borders, fruit juice in retail packs, table water
(sparkling/non-sparkling), toothpicks, spaghetti, noodles, biscuits of any
type and drugs and other regulated products through the land borders.
Government also banned the importation of sugar, all kinds of canned and
bottled beer, toilet rolls, confectioneries, exercise books and envelopes,
Barytes and Bentonites with effect from July 1, 2003.
The ban government had said, was intended to boost local production,
enhance the performance of the national economy and safeguard the health of
the citizenry.

Original_Search_Title: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Distribution of Food to Sudanese Refugees Begins
Headline: Chad: UNHCR Briefing Notes: Distribution of Food to Sudanese Refugees Begins
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
We have begun distributing emergency food aid along Chad's eastern
frontier, where thousands of Sudanese refugees, mostly women and children,
are encamped in a dozen sites after fleeing the rebellion-torn Darfur region
in western Sudan. A UNHCR relief convoy offloaded supplies of sorghum and
oil on Monday at Toumtouma, 42 kms north of the border town of Adre, center
of our emergency operations. On Wednesday it proceeded to Kolkol, 25 kms
away.  This supplementary diet is for the most vulnerable group of refugees
numbering around 1,000 in the two sites. Chadian military escorts went with
the convoy.
We had earlier handed out plastic sheeting for shelter, blankets and
jerry cans to these refugees. Local refugee committees were told to bring
carts and donkeys to transport the food supplies from the World Food Program
to their encampments in the semi-arid border region. Each family group of
six received three sacks of sorghum and four jerrycans of oil.
The Red Cross, our main implementing partner, will handle general food
distribution. We are awaiting the deployment of the Red Cross staff in the
region where security is one of our major concerns. There are land mines
scattered in the area and there have been reports of cross-border
raids. However, further delays in the general food distribution would
prejudice the fragile health of the refugees and their peaceful coexistence
with the local population during this fasting month of Ramadan.
The first Sudanese refugees came into Chad in April following an
uprising by the Sudanese Liberation Movement (SLM) in Darfur state earlier
in the year. By July, the Chadian government said some 65,000 Sudanese had
fled into Chad.  On Sept. 4, the Khartoum government and SLM hammered out a
40-day cease-fire while peace talks were being conducted.
Early this month, UNHCR launched an appeal for $16.6 million dollars for
emergency assistance to the refugees. The amount would cover provision of
shelter, health services, water and sanitation facilities through the
yearend in a remote region where there is little basic infrastructure.

Original_Search_Title: Zeltis Turns Corner With R10m Boost to Profit
Headline: South Africa: Zeltis Turns Corner With R10m Boost to Profit
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — INVESTMENT holding company Zeltis says it is turning the corner and
expects to post a profit in its next set of year-end results.
The group, which specialises in providing financial services, has taken
a lot of stick in the media because of its poor performance.
Zeltis MD Rodney McLeod said yesterday there was a R10m improvement in
profit in its results for the year to June.
While revenue for the year dropped from R148,5m to R141,4m, Zeltis
turned a R2,4m operating loss into a R3,2m profit.
Its net loss came down from R12,9m to R745000.
McLeod said the improvement in its results was on the back of its
recruitment agency, Denverdraft, which was restructured to lower costs.
Long term debt was up from R510000 to R3,2m, while cash was down from
R3,2m to R971000.
McLeod said next year's results would show an improvement in the balance
sheet.
Although the group was turning around, McLeod still was not happy with
the way it was structured.
He said that Zeltis had created two empowerment recruitment operations
and was close to introducing another that would enable it to operate in the
northern parts of the country.
He said he was happy with the way the empowerment operations had
performed.
The group's dispute with Awethu Breweries the second-largest sorghum
beer and mageu brewer in SA was still continuing.
The dispute began over an amount owing from the sale by Awethu of RMD
Financial Services to Zeltis.
McLeod said Awethu was under a lot of stress and could not afford to
give Zeltis its holding in RMD Financial Services.
The results were delayed because the group was hoping to conclude a
dispute with one of its debtors.
McLeod said the dispute would be most likely be concluded before the end
of the year.
Zeltis closed unchanged at 2c with no trade taking place in the group's
shares on the JSE Securities Exchange SA yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Malnutrition Claims 40 in Bulawayo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Malnutrition Claims 40 in Bulawayo
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — More than 40 people, the majority of them children under the
age of five, have died of malnutrition in Bulawayo in the last two months as
hunger begins to take its toll on Zimbabwe's urban population.
According to a council report released last week, a total of 43 deaths
due to malnutrition were recorded in the months of August and September
alone in the city, Zimbabwe's second largest town.
The Bulawayo City Council's Health, Housing and Education Committee says
of the total number of deaths, 12 people died in August while the remainder
- 31 people - died in the month of September alone.
The report further states that of the total number, 23 children aged
between one and four years died of malnutrition in September compared to
seven in August, making the category the most affected age group.
The deaths come hard on the heels of reports of hunger stalking
villagers countrywide and reports that many families in the most remote
parts of Zimbabwe were going for days without food.
In some instances, families in Bulawayo say they have resorted to having
porridge with only lemon juice but no sugar or bread for lunch while both
lunch and supper are combined and usually taken at dusk.
Zimbabwe is facing its worst humanitarian crisis in years with more than
six million people in dire need of food aid countrywide.
However most food stuffs are gradually getting back onto supermarket
shelves but most of the basic commodities are now far beyond the reach of
many families as prices are sky-rocketing on a daily basis.
Currently, 2 litres of cooking oil and a loaf of bread have gone up to
$19 000 from $7 000 and $1 500 from $1 000, respectively.
The increases mean that a family of five now needs between $250 000 to
about $650 000 per month to survive, which is far higher than most
salaries.
The indiscriminate price increases have forced the government to
reconsider its position on price controls. Price controls were scrapped
officially in May because they were believed to be the cause of the
unavailability of products on the market.
Through the Market Assistance Pilot Programme, the United States Agency
for International Development (USAID), threw a lifeline to urban dwellers
last month when it put up 20 000 tonnes of cheaper sorghum meal onto the
market.

Original_Search_Title: Poverty Hinders a Hunger to Learn
Headline: South Africa: Poverty Hinders a Hunger to Learn
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — In a rural corner of South Africa, a grandmother is raising eight
children on R700 a month - and her biggest worry is paying school fees
IT'S 6.30am. Suzan Dolo sniffs her snuff, with her right hand clutching
a hand-made grass-broom. At 64, she still sweeps her sandy 100m' yard before
sunrise - the first activity every morning for the women of Vergenoeg
village, 80km northwest of Mokopane in Limpopo.
"You lot are going to be late," she screams at five of her eight
grandchildren. "Ms Makgotlho is already on her way."
They don't have any means of measuring time. "Our wireless [radio] is
not working any more," says Cylia, 16, a Grade 9 pupil at Nakonkwetlou
Secondary School. Thus Perpetoa Makgotlho, principal of Modisha Primary
School, is their only chronometer.
Salome and Pula didn't sleep at home the previous night. Pula, 21, the
truant and delinquent, hasn't been to school, where he is doing Grade 10,
for two days. Salome, 20, has been asked by a relative to babysit for two
days.
N ine-year-old twin sisters Elizabeth and Francinah are bathing in a
plastic wash basin. Cylia and Suzan, 14 - named after her granny - are the
first to get up, prepare the fire and sweep the mud veranda before having
the basin to themselves. Manoko, 11, is next in the basin queue.
Manoko is combing her hair , using a piece of a broken mirror to look at
her dark-skinned face and beautiful eyes. "I would like to become a nurse,"
she responds to my question, while shyly hiding her face behind her
hands.
They all wash in the tiny, weather-beaten, thatched-roof hut. It smells
of sweat, sorghum, the cow dung used to polish the mud floor and smoke from
a makeshift fireplace.
All of them, including Salome's daughter, depend on their granny's R700
monthly state pension grant.
"Please eat, you cannot go to school on an empty stomach," says
Dolo. Their breakfast is black tea without bread. "I can't give them
anything. I don't have a cent to buy dry bread. They will have to go on like
this until I get paid on Monday," she says, sneezing continuously from the
snuff.
Food is the least of Dolo's problems - school fees are her biggest
worry.
The mother of seven of her grandchildren is in Tembisa, on the East
Rand, looking for a job. She has left the children under the guardianship of
Dolo. The granny is not prepared to say anything about their fathers.
Pula's parents died and he has been living with Dolo ever since.
Her husband died in 1979 and she has known nothing but poverty for the
greater part of her life. However, the two schools still demand about R500
in fees a year.
"Every time we get paid, I have to settle my debts at the local store
for groceries I took the previous month," she says. "I pay about R260 each
month."
This is for a bag of mealiemeal, sugar, washing powder and tea. She
spoils her grandchildren by buying them a chicken on payday - the only time
they can lick their fingers besides Christmas.
After she pays R10 to a burial society she buys other basic groceries
amounting to about R250. A n electricity recharge card costs R30, even
though they only use it for lights to allow the children to do their
homework.
On weekends, Dolo supplements her pension with the sorghum beer she is
brewing. "If business is good, I might rake in around R150 and I will buy
tsotsi [a 15kg bag of mealiemeal] and pay more fees," she says.
A brownish liquid is leaking from a big brown clay pot in the hut, "a
sign that the beer is maturing", she says.
"All that I want is to see my grandchildren doing something for
themselves in future and not ending up like me and their mother," she
says. "I want them to be nurses, teachers or to be like you [a journalist]."
A large portion of her income goes on school fees. "If I don't give them
[the schools] anything, I'm likely to meet my grandchildren at the gate
returning from school. The schools send them home every payday to get
money. They say they must not return until they get money. If I don't pay
until the end of the year, they won't get their report cards.
"I tried to tell the principals that I'm struggling but they say I'm not
the only one struggling."
However, Makgotlho says her school doesn't chase away children but "just
sends them home to remind their parents of the fees".
Such incidents are common in rural areas where principals take advantage
of illiterate and ignorant parents such as Dolo, says Limpopo Education
spokesman Freddy Greaver.
Asked whether he has told parents about exemption for those who can't
pay, Nakonkwetlou principal Malesela Lekalakala says parents are frustrated
by the bureaucracy involved in applying for exemption and end up not
applying.
He adds that the government does not supply enough teaching aids and
that funds have shrunk, which means the school has to depend solely on
fees.
Solly Mabusela, chairman of the Global Campaign for Education, says this
is the reason why the campaign's international lobby group has been calling
for the total scrapping of user fees.
"The state does not have [enough] machinery to monitor the situation on
the ground," Mabusela says. "There will always be such incidents and
loopholes."
It is a warning to Dolo's grandchildren that their education might be
limited as tertiary institution's fees rise higher and higher.
Alternatively, says Firoz Patel, acting deputy director-general in the
Department of Education, they might have to wait until a new funding policy
is implemented. "We want to ultimately ban school fees in poor schools," he
says. "This could be as soon as 2005."
In the meantime, Dolo will have to share her house, her pension and the
income from her sorghum beer with her grandchildren - and hope they will
somehow rise above the poverty that restricts their lives.

Original_Search_Title: Better Working Conditions of Workers
Headline: Ghana: Better Working Conditions of Workers
Body_Text:
The Minister of Private Sector Development, Mr. Kwamena Bartels, has
urged manufacturers to consider the working conditions and health of staff
of Ghanaians.
He said that would prevent tragedy, like the one which happened recently
at the Pioneer Food Processing Company Limited.
In a speech during the launching of the Bakers Inn Bakery, Tuesday, the
minister cautioned the company to work closely with the Factory Inspectorate
Division of the Ministry of the Manpower Development and Employment to
ensure that industrial standards and safety are maintained.
He noted that "profit considerations should not be the driving force for
venturing into this kind of business," but that the company is to use the
right raw materials to produce to specifications of the standard weight and
adherence to lifespan of its products on the shelves of supermarkets.
He was pleased that the management of the company has come to complement
the efforts of local bakeries with their special brand of bakery
products.
But Mr. Bartels was fast to say that; "it should therefore not be
construed as an attempt by this company to edge local producers of bakery
products out of business."
He advised that the company, when it establishes itself on the market,
should explore the possibility of teaming up with Ghanaian research
institutions to undertake research into the use of other local staples, like
maize, millet and sorghum, in the production of bakery products.
The Deputy Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Kwadwo Afram Asiedu, also
cautioned the company not to indulge in unauthorized capital flight which,
invariably, would touch the nerve center of the country's revenue collecting
agencies.
He said the company should strive to fulfill its promises and operate
within the confines of the laws of Ghana and help to improve the lives of
workers as well as satisfying other social and civic responsibilities.
According to him one feature of the company's business that gladdens his
heart was its proposal to introduce the mechanism of franchise in
Ghana.
This, he submitted, "will be a golden opportunity for the Ghanaian
business people to embrace new mechanisms in modern day trading, such as
franchises, mergers, acquisitions and joint ventures or cooperatives."
He advised that Ghanaians discard their individualistic attitudes in the
way they do business since that has been a veritable bane to the enhancement
of the nation's business culture.

Original_Search_Title: Up Close And Candid: JAK and JJ: Reconciling the Irreconcilable?
Headline: Ghana: Up Close And Candid: JAK and JJ: Reconciling the Irreconcilable?
Body_Text:
"...as far as the apparent differences between President Kufour and
Ex-President Rawlings are concerned, we need to be less schizophrenic and
dig a little bit deeper into the background and philosophy of the two
gentlemen in order to understand why things are what they are now."
When it is said that Ghanaians are peace-loving people it is a fact not
a clichÃ©. It is this preoccupation with maintaining peace that must be at
the core of recent calls by civil society organisations such as the Ghana
Bar Association (GBA) and the Christian Council of Ghana (CCG) for the
current president of the Republic, Mr. John Agyekum Kufour, and the
immediate past president, Mr. J.J. Rawlings, to put aside their differences
and work together for the sake of the nation.
At its last general meeting held in Elmina, the GBA passed a resolution
in which the Association noted "with serious concern the mistrust, animosity
and unhealthy relations existing between His Excellency President John
Agyekum Kufour and Ex - President Rawlings which needed to be tackled. If
the two cannot sit together and take decisions together (sic) then it means
that there is something wrong".
I support any efforts that will consolidate the fledgling democratic
governance in the country.
However as far as the apparent differences between President Kufour and
Ex - President Rawlings are concerned, we need to be less schizophrenic and
dig a little bit deeper into the background and philosophy of the two
gentlemen in order to understand why things are what they are now. Is it
indeed worthwhile to reconcile two people whose worldviews are as different
as day and night? We need to know what the two sons of the soil stand for to
guide us to fashion a strategy to 'reconcile' them.
I do not pretend to have any great insight into or understanding of the
two very important men of our country. We will delve into their
philosophical positions and policies.
Mr. Rawlings by all accounts is a populist, a gun-totting pseudo
revolutionary who burst onto the Ghanaian political scene first in May 1979,
then June 1979 and again December 1981, promising to make Ghana a corrupt
free society.
For good measure and as recent revelations at the National
Reconciliation Commission (NRC) have confirmed, hundreds of innocent
Ghanaians either lost their lives and/or properties/livelihood. His
conversion to multi party democracy since the early 1990s does not hide the
fact that Mr. Rawlings believes in using violence as an instrument to effect
change in society.
Under his administration, especially during the PNDC years, Ghana
recorded one of the worst human rights abuses in her short history. The
abduction and killing of the three judges and the ex Army Officer were duly
calculated to undermine the rule of law and to intimidate those who dared to
challenge his administration.
Mr. Rawlings is miffed that no due recognition is given to what he
perceives as major achievements under his government.
Yes, it is true that between 1981 and 2000 some major programmes were
initiated. For example, the structural adjustment programme (SAP) was aimed
at removing distortions in the economy and positioning the country to embark
on market driven economic development. His own government through reckless
spending and excessive corruption undermined the initial gains, resulting
from the SAP.
There were massive infrastructure development beginning with the
modernisation of the road network in Accra, rehabilitation of some major
roads such as Tema-Akosombo, Kumasi-Sunyani, Kumasi-Mampong,
Yamoransa-Anhwia Kwanta, Anwhia Kwanta - Kumasi, Kintampo-Tamale and
Aflao-Ho.
His administration implemented a radical local government reform
programme. Overall Mr. Rawlings' record is not impressive. There was no
appreciable growth in national wealth and in personal incomes.
So it is utterly strange that Mr. Rawlings will lead a crusade to vilify
the Kufour administration, calling it the worst government the country has
ever had. On what basis did he make such an assertion one may ask? President
Kufour is well educated, suave and politically sophisticated.
He cut his political teeth in his early 20s and has been politically
active even during the rough and tumble of military regimes. He is a liberal
democrat by conviction and subscribes to the principles of the rule of law,
respect for human rights and human dignity and a strong believer in free
enterprise. He is an excellent political strategist who continues to be
under-estimated by his opponents.
He does not pander to populist demands. His decision to implement the
highly indebted poor country programme (HIPC) and the astronomical increase
in the price of petroleum products did not endear him to some Ghanaians. But
those decisions had to be taken to enable the country embark on sustainable
growth and development.
President Kufour's commitment to good democratic governance has led his
administration to initiate an impressive array of reforms such as the repeal
of the Criminal Label Act, the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS), the
proposed Freedom of Information Bill and the Whistle Blowers Bill should
further deepen democracy in our country.
The amendment to the Bank of Ghana Act, the proposed Information and
Communications Technology (ICT) policy, the Presidential Initiatives for
Cassava, Salt, Textiles, Sorghum, Cotton, Oil Palm as well as the creation
of the Ministry of Private Sector Development should go a long way in
deepening market reforms and in strengthening the development of free
enterprise and modernisation of the economy generally.
The Kufour administration has initiated several infrastructure projects,
including road construction such as Tema-Sogakope, Accra-Kumasi, Tetteh
Quarshie interchange, Circle- Ofankor six lane road and many others. And
very soon work will start on Mallam- Kasoa-Yamoransa road.
Mr. Rawlings' method of governance may have worked in the 1980s and
parts of 1990s but it has long been discredited. He ought to accept that he
has played his part. He should therefore sit back and allow others to take
the country to another level of development.
This is the sort of message the GBA and the CCG should carry to him!

Original_Search_Title: Experts Worried Over Strange Disease
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Worried Over Strange Disease
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ten-year-old Susannah Jackson is dying of what may be the world's newest and oddest disease. An illness so rare and mysterious that science has not yet come up with an official name for it.
Sitting outside her family's mud hut, near the small town of Lui, Sudan, Susannah is gripped by a series of brain seizures which force her neck to arch forwards, down, and then up again.
The disease is puzzling to doctors and scientists. No wonder people in this isolated corner of southern Sudan call it "nodding disease."
"We have no clue as what is causing this. It's like a detective novel and a murder mystery, because it's fatal," Dr Mickey Richer, a tropical disease specialist from UNICEF said.
So far, almost 300 children are known to have caught the disease in one small region of the country.
Bizarrely, the seizures normally occur when the sufferers start to eat, or when it is particularly cold.
When Richer asked for a bowl of sorghum to be placed in front of Susannah, the "nodding" began almost immediately, and stopped when she finished eating.
Curiously, Susannah does not react if she eats unfamiliar food such as a chocolate bar for instance.
Susannah's eight-year-old brother, Jacob, is now in Lui's crowded hospital with the same disease at a more developed stage.
He shows signs of being mentally retarded and physically stunted.
During a particularly fierce seizure he threw himself into a fire, and is now being treated for severe burns on his leg.
Two other children in the same ward have almost identical stories.
An hour's drive from Lui along a rutted track lies the village of Amadi, a silent cluster of huts trapped in a forest of thick green grass.
Amadi is now considered to be the epicentre of the disease - 12 per cent of children here are affected.
About 12% of children in Ramadi are affected. Last year, experts from the World Health Organisation did neurological scans on some of the children, enabling them to confirm that this is a specific and unique condition.
Twelve-year-old Ruben Nicholas stands in the middle of the village, gripped by a sudden seizure.
It looks as though someone is forcing his chin down onto his chest.
"He is nodding three times a day," says his father, Nicholas Lado.
"The disease stops him from growing. His brother died from the same disease, in August."
So what could be causing this horrific outbreak? If this had happened in western Europe, it seems likely there would be at least some answers by now. Here, in poverty-stricken southern Sudan, families still do not know if they should be trying to quarantine their children. Courtesy: bbc.com

Original_Search_Title: Parliament Raises PAYE Threshold
Headline: Uganda: Parliament Raises PAYE Threshold
Body_Text:
Kampala — Members of Parliament yesterday pushed government to raise the Pay As You Earn threshold from Shs 130,000 to Shs 235,000.
The chairman of the Committee on Finance, Mr Bright Rwamirama, told Parliament that the PAYE threshold has been at Shs 130,000 for long.
"Pay As You Earn has been fixed at Shs 130,000 for almost seven years.
As previously reported, other variables like price and inflation have considerably changed," Rwamirama said while presenting the committee's report on the Finance Bill 2003.
PAYE is a tax payable by every worker who earns a salary beyond a specified threshold. In this case the threshold has been Shs 130,000.
MPs also passed a provision they said would be flexible to car importers. According to the provision, which the Minister of State for Investments, Mr Sam Kutesa, endorsed, car importers/buyers on first registration of their vehicles can now choose to pay for four months, eight months or one year.
MPs argued that some car importers/buyers pay in instalments. They said that if importers are compelled to take out a year's registration, it might inconvenience them.
Rwamirama also said that in order to discourage the importation of locally available raw materials, government should tax barley and Gum Arabica (used for making chewing gum).
Parliament recommended that barley should attract a 7 percent import duty and 4 percent Comesa duty to protect the local sorghum.
Barley is one of the raw materials used in brewing beer.
MPs declined to impose tax on extracts, essences, and concentrates - raw materials used in the manufature of soft drinks such as sodas. Kutesa said that if taxes are imposed, soda prices would increase.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni Thanks Teso Residents for Action Against LRA
Headline: Uganda: Well Done On LRA, Teso
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Yoweri Museveni on Wednesday addressed residents of Teso region on The Voice of Teso radio about the recent invasion of the region by rebels of the Lords Resistance Army (LRA). Below, we reproduce the Presidents full address
"I greet you all the people of Teso. Yoga. First of all, I am very sorry about the suffering you have gone through in the last two months on the account of thugs of Kony who have been causing so much damage to the people of northern Uganda and they came here to try to spread their killing.
As I told you, the other day, at a public rally, these bandits' coming to Teso was not because they were growing in strength. It was actually because they were losing in Acholi. They therefore thought that if they came to Teso and Lango that should force us to de-concentrate the forces we have concentrated in Acholi area.
We have got two whole divisions there reinforced by a lot of LDUs. These are the 4th and 5th divisions. These are quite large army groups. These are permanently dealing with the Kony problem in that area. They (rebels) thought that by coming to Teso and Lango, we would re-deploy or deploy away the 4th and 5th division from the north into Teso and other areas of southern Uganda. We did not do that, because we brought other forces from other parts of the country. These are the ones we have deployed.
Fortunately, for the case of Teso, we had a very strong support from the people. We also had the support from the Arrow Group; meaning volunteers. Of course the terrain in Teso is also good. It is not sustainable for banditry activities against a well-organised force, which UPDF is as of now.
Unlike the first insurgency in Teso in the 1980s, the UPDF now is much better equipped than it was at that time. So there is no way anybody can sustain an insurgency in an area like Teso because the terrain is open. It has no mountain and has no forests. It is not possible for anybody to sustain an insurgency here. Therefore these bandits thought they could come and disturb Teso as they have been disturbing Acholi. It shows how bankrupt they are. They are not even able to plan for their criminal activities.
The army was of course not here in Teso when these people made their incursion. We didn't have UPDF units in this area. We had only some LDUs and some ASTU in Obalanga and some ASTU in Kapelebyong. These are the ones, who initially did not perform well. However, as we brought in more and more troops, it was clear that it would take us a short time to clear these bandits especially using the support of the "wananchi". That is exactly what is happening now. After a few encounters, first of all with the air cover units of the air force, mainly the helicopters that we have been attacking these bandits in a number of places like Abarilela and some other places.
You see I am beginning to learn some of these names of these places where the helicopter managed to get the bandits. Then some of the infantry battalions have also got in touch with these bandits. Like the other day; a few days ago, one of our battalions, the17th battalion which is normally based in the 2nd division in Mbarara; but which we have now deployed in this area got in touch with these bandits ata place called Apopong. Apopong is to the northeast of Amuria towards Adacar.
They really gave them (bandits) a good beating. The journalists were yesterday (Tuesday) taken to see the 31 bodies of the bandits who remained there, including some of their leaders. But many many many went with injuries. Some of them will die. And there were also a few other contacts. I mean clashes. That is what we call it in military language a contact. But you should not take it in the ordinary sense. We had contacts in a few other places and as a consequence of these, all the bandits have fled Teso areas. By yesterday (Tuesday) all the organised groups of the bandits had fled the Teso areas.
They were in Otuke in Lango and some of them are even nearing Pader. I have heard of a few splinter groups, which got separated, from these main groups when we attacked them. like I heard of a group that is in the direction of Kapelebyong. They were about 10.
I also heard of another group. They were a few people near Orungo. But there is even a group north of Amugu. That is in Lango now, which yesterday ambushed some of our soldiers who were a bit careless. But otherwise the bandits have fled. It will not be easy for them to come back. If they come back, they will die. That is what I am here to ensure. That any bandit who comes to Teso will die because it is easy to operate in this area because of the clear terrain.
Therefore in about a week, I will be coming to you or through the division commander; I will be able to tell the people to go back to their villages. However, I want you to give us about a week from now so that we put in more forces in the area. We want to put in more forces and more equipment so that once the people go back to their villages; the terrorists do not disturb them again.
I would like to thank the people of Teso for their maturity and for their strong support for the army. This is an example of patriotism and commonsense. When you allow bandits to infiltrate your area you ruin your own development opportunities.
The people of Teso since the other insurgency have started making big strides to develop their area. I can see the Government has built a lot of schools and has brought a lot of boreholes. But more importantly, the people themselves have started developing homestead incomes.
I hear you now grow sorghum called Epuripur. That sorghum that was developed by our researchers in Serere and this is now being used in making modern beer. This beer in the past used to be made from (pause).. What do they call them. sorghum there in Europe..called.. Eerr.. barley. So the people of Teso are now saving us from squandering money in importing barley to be used in making beer, because it can be made from sorghum. This we had known all along, but it is good you have implemented it. But what is important is that it brings incomes to your homes.
The people of Teso started growing potatoes and sending them to Kampala and they got money out of that. I even saw somebody who built a very good hotel in Kumi town from potatoes.
That is why I stopped the other day when I was coming here. I stopped to wash my hands and have a rest. It is a very nice hotel. The people of Teso have also grown fruits.
I visited a number of people. One in Katakwi and another one here in Serere who is growing fruits. And incidentally, this can be a lot of money; because fruits, especially the oranges have got not only the regional market but attracts international markets as well. Now that man, like the one I visited on Serere side is getting quite a bit of money from oranges. Therefore the people of Teso are making very big strides and I am not surprised that they do not want to be disturbed by fools like Kony. People who do not have any purpose; killers; criminals like Idi Amin who died the other day. We are going to ensure that these criminals are crushed once and for all.
We are going to mobilise the Arrow Group into battalions. We shall be having one battalion in every three sub-counties in the sub-counties, which are affected. But if it becomes necessary, we can even have a bigger number.
There is a lot of enthusiasm to join this battalion of LDUs, which we now call the Arrow Group. But we have to think of the budget. We must give them some uniform, we must give them guns and we shall give them some allowances. They cannot work for nothing. They have to get allowances. Because of that we shall have controlled numbers.
But we have selected a number of sub-counties, which could be vulnerable to banditry, when they come back to these areas or have been vulnerable to the Karimojong rustlers who have also been disturbing the border sub-counties in Katakwi. All these we shall arm these LDU battalions so that they protect the people against cattle raiders from Karamoja and also in case some of the bandits try to come back; although they (LDUs) are not going to be alone.
There is also going to be a strong contingent of UPDF here with mobile elements including tanks, which will never allow these bandits to come to this area again. I myself after staying here (in Soroti) for a few days, I will move on to follow the track of those bandits and ensure that we eliminate them from Uganda completely. I would also like to inform you that we have forces in Gulu and Pader. In fact Gulu is now quite peaceful because of the work of the 4th division, the whole division of the army reinforced by the LDUs in that area. The problem has been with Pader. There had been quite some activity there, including Kitgum. We are now going to concentrate in those areas after we have cleared Teso and Lango.
The purpose of my talking to you today was to tell you that I hope you will be moving back to your homes in a week.
The bandits are largely cleared out of the Teso area as I speak.
But in case there are some few remaining and in order to allow us to bring more equipment to the area and to ensure that we guard against re-infestation of the area, I would like you to give us a week so that we can completely ensure the safety of this area. Eyalama noi. Thank you very much.
'You let bandits infiltrate your area, you ruin your development opportunities'

Original_Search_Title: Museveni to Flush Kony Out of Teso in One Week
Headline: Uganda: Museveni to Flush Kony Out of Teso in One Week
Body_Text:
Kampala — PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni (right) has asked the people of Teso to give
him only one week to flush out LRA rebels from the region.
He said the people displaced by the rebels should be able to return to
their homes within a week, report Kenneth Oluka and Nathan Etengu.
"I would want you to give me just one week so that we put in more forces
to guard against a re-infestation of the area by the bandits," Museveni said
in a 10-minute address on Voice of Teso radio station.
He did not take any questions. He said all the organised rebel groups
had by Tuesday fled towards Otuke in Lira and Pader.
He said they had suffered heavy losses during battles with the UPDF and
the Arrow Group, a local militia.
"The area, as I speak now, is free of bandits. But in case there are a
few remnants and in order to allow us bring in more equipment to the area
and ensure we are guarded against a re-infestation of the area, I would like
you to give us a week so that we can ensure the safety of this area,"
Museveni said.
He said he was in Teso to ensure that any bandit who tried to
destabilise it was killed. "In a week's time, I will be coming back to you
and I will be able to tell the people to go back to their villages,"
Museveni said.
He said the Teso terrain was conducive for fighting any form of
insurgency. Museveni said more troops were to be deployed in the region. He
said the Government would support the recruitment of more volunteers to join
the Arrow Group.
He said the people of Teso had started making big strides to develop
their area. He thanked the Iteso for developing homestead incomes
particularly for undertaking the cultivation of the Epuripur sorghum used in
the brewing of Eagle beer.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Ahead of Budget
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Ahead of Budget
Body_Text:
DELTA chief executive officer Joe Mutizwa says volumes in the three months to June 30 held up very well particularly lager and sorghum.
"Lager volumes, in June alone, were 5% up on last year and 14% ahead of budget and overall margins increased to 33% from just under 30% last year," Mutizwa said.
He said despite the depression in soft drink volumes due to the cold weather, he was hopeful soft drink volumes would recover in the remaining period to year-end as the warm weather returned.
Soft drinks contributed only 6% to group bottom line in the just-ended financial year to March 31.
Turnover in the three months under review increased to $50 billion compared to $103 billion achieved in the whole of last year.
The chief executive said sorghum volumes were almost 12% ahead of budget for the three months under consideration.
Recovery in the packaging division, MegaPak Zimbabwe, was very strong and volumes were ahead of budget and up on last year, he said.
Megapak volumes for the year ended March 31 were slightly over 4 000 tonnes converted with turn-over increasing to $6,7 billion which the company said reflected problems in the country with fuel and water supplies.
Mutizwa said this division's major challenge was to expand capacity so as to meet growing demand and plans were underway to address this.
Megapak Zimbabwe incorporates Megapak (injection moulding), Megapet (PET bottle blowing), Blowmoulders and Megatank (large plastic tank manufacturing).
Mutizwa said market response on the new Castle Milk Stout in terms of penetration was gratifying and ahead of expectation.
"We are very bullish about the Castle Milk Stout brand and we are glad that although it is too early to say much, market response has been good," said Mutizwa.
Export volumes for barley malt were in line with budget while lager beer exports were 19% up on prior year though off a small base.
New markets for barley malt, lagers and plastic packaging are being developed in the growing markets of Angola and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mutizwa said.
"Initial indications are promising although it will take a while to exploit the full potential of these markets," he said.

Original_Search_Title: 'Customs Does Not Aid Smuggling of Textiles'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Customs Does Not Aid Smuggling of Textiles'
Body_Text:
The Nigeria Customs Service has refuted a recent allegation by local textile manufacturers that it was aiding and abetting the smuggling of printed fabrics (Ankara) into the country.
"It is an unfair allegation against us because we are fully implementing the federal government's prohibition order on the importation of some items into Nigeria and this is earning us commendations from President Olusegun Obasanjo and other stakeholders in the textile industry," Customs spokesman, Wale Adeniyi, said at the weekend in Abuja.
Reacting to the allegation by the Nigerian Textiles Manufacturers Association, Adeniyi told the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) that the service had in recent times seized contraband worth millions of naira.
The seizures were made at the land borders in Katsina, Seme and Idi-roko and some of the seaports not designated for importation of some items, he said.
"Only recently, we impounded two large containers of textile materials at Onne Port for illegal importation through that port," Adeniyi said.
He, however, explained that government had allowed the importation of other types of textiles through Apapa and Tin Can Island ports in Lagos, noting that "anybody caught contravening the law will forfeit the items, as well as face legal action".
He also said the Customs had last month established an enforcement strike force to tackle smuggling nationwide.
About 300 officers, the spokesman said, were undergoing intensive training at the Police College, Ikeja, to prepare them for the task of dealing with smugglers.
Part of the anti-smuggling campaign has also been the mounting of a campaign to educate importers and other Nigerians on items banned from coming into the country, as well as on the dangers of some of the items to the health of the nation, Adeniyi said.
He appealed to Nigerians to always avail the customs of information on activities of smugglers along the country's entry points.
"We are ready to make good use of such information, so as to decisively deal with the smugglers and those circumventing government's fiscal policies".
NAN recalls that the government had, in August last year, banned the importation of sorghum, millet, wheat flour, gypsum, vegetable oil in bulk, mosquito repellant coils, used tyres, gaming machines and second-hand clothes.
Other banned items are used fridges and air-conditioning units, used compressors, bagged cement, vehicles of any kind through the land borders, vehicles older than eight years, textiles of any kind through land borders, printed fabrics (Ankara) and frozen poultry, among others.
NAN reports that smuggling has, however, continued, with the perpetrators attacking customs officers at several border points with dangerous weapons.

Original_Search_Title: EuronAid Launches a Triangular Cereal Purchase Operation
Headline: Ethiopia: EuronAid Launches a Triangular Cereal Purchase Operation
Body_Text:
EuronAid has purchased 24,387 MTs of sorghum from the Sudan with the
help of funds donated by the European Commission [EC] to Ethiopia. EuronAid
launched the triangular purchase operation according to arrangements made by
the euronAid Headquarters, following negotiations among EC and
Ethio-Sudanese authorities. The tripartite negotiations addressed such
issues as customs and border formalities and procedures of passage of
trucks, among others.
According to EuronAid officials, the purchase operation aims at the
repayment of relief grain borrowed by EC's partner NGOs in April 2003 from
government reserves to ensure the timely supply and distribution of relief
food.
EuronAid's triangular purchase operation has won the support of the
officials of the Ethiopian and Sudanese governments. EuronAid officials say
the operation, which has come at the backdrop of quiet favorable bilateral
ties, is going as planned mainly due to the strengthening relations between
the two countries.
Ethiopia and the Sudan, who share an over 1000 km border and a
longstanding relations, have recently further reinvigorated their ties by
embarking upon various bilateral engagements that have brought them close to
each other. The current operation, which marks the start of cross-border
initiatives negotiated by EuronAid and Ethio-Sudanese authorities, is
believed to boost existing trade and investment between the two
countries.

Original_Search_Title: Annual Inflation Drops
Headline: Zambia: Annual Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Lusaka — ANNUAL inflation rate has reduced from 21.9 per cent to 20.2 per cent
during the month of July.
The Central Statistical Office (CSO) census and statistics director
Dr. Buleti Nsemukila disclosed that metropolitan low, high income and
non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 20.8, 21.8 and 18.8 per cent
respectively for July as compared to 23.2, 23.5 and 20.2 per cent for the
month of June.
Dr. Nsemukila explained that the downward trend in the food index was
largely due to the significant lower prices of mealie meal, maize grain and
vegetables.
"Additionally, price decreases occurred in dried beans, shelled
groundnuts, sorghum and fresh kapenta," Dr. Nsemukila said. He also
disclosed that the monthly inflation rate registered a reduction of -0.1 per
cent for the month of July.
Dr. Nsemukila disclosed that monthly inflation rates for the
metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were recorded at
-0.5, -0.1 and 0.1 per cent respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Heineken Surveys New Openings
Headline: Ghana: Heineken Surveys New Openings
Body_Text:
A DELEGATION from Heineken International, parent company of Ghana Breweries Limited, has visited the country to undertake a study of new business opportunities in the country.
In particular, the delegation is conducting a survey into the possibility of setting up a multi-million cedi project in Kumasi for the training of Ghanaians in Microsoft Certificate (MSC) and other information, communication and technology (ICT) related courses.
This was disclosed when the delegation held discussions with the Vice-President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama, at the Castle.
The Heineken International delegation included Mr. Jean-Francois van Boxmeer, Executive Director in Charge of Africa; Mr. Thomas de Man, Managing Director, Africa Department; and Prof. M. Looijen, Emeritus Professor of the University of Leiden and Consultant to the proposed MSC/ICT training center project.
Those who led the delegation to the meeting with the vice-president included Dr. Sir K. Donkor Fordwour, Member of the GBL board, Mr. Ishmael Yamson, Chairman of Unilever Ghana Limited; Mr. S. O Adebanji, Managing Director of GBL and a number of other GBL executive.
Alhaji Aliu Mahama commended Heineken International on its decision to undertake the Kumasi project. He further urged Heineken and GBL to study the prospects of expanding into fruits and food processing as well as the cultivation of Sorghum as a raw material for GBL products.
Such moves, the vice-president said, would help create jobs and conserve foreign currency.
Mr. Boxmeer described the Ghanaian investment climate as favourable and said Heineken International remained open to all ideas that would promote the welfare of its companies and the environments in which they operate.

Original_Search_Title: Indian Brewer Not About to Sell Its SA Sorghum Interest
Headline: South Africa: Indian Brewer Not About to Sell Its SA Sorghum Interest
Body_Text:
Cape Town — United Breweries of India was not planning to sell its stake in United
National Breweries (UNB), the SA sorghum beer company, and press reports in
India that speculated on the sale were inaccurate, UNB president and chief
operating officer Rajan Ranganathan said yesterday.
He was commenting on a report from the Economic Times in Mumbai in March
that United Breweries planned to sell the SA business because of a decline
in sorghum beer consumption .
Two weeks ago The Telegraph of Calcutta reported that United Breweries
planned to dispose of UNB, among other assets.
AK Ravi Nedungadi, chief financial officer of the United Breweries
group, was quoted in the article as saying it was looking to divest control
in the company and would sell its 75% stake in it "if they get a reasonable
price".
After a capital restructuring last year, in which United Breweries
bought out R46m of minority shareholders, United Breweries holds 74,99% of
UNB and Marriott Merchant Bank 25,01%.
Ranganathan said SA management had discussed the reports with its
holding company . What was true was that UNB was looking for a black
empowerment partner and held discussions with a number of groups but had not
found a suitable partner.
UNB has turned around in the past year and is now making profits, so it
would not be a good time to sell it. UNB management believed it could
increase profits further, Ranganathan said.
This was not because sorghum beer consumption was growing, since it was
a mature market, but because there were more efficiencies to be wrung out of
the business.
According to UNB's recent submission to public hearings on the Liquor
Bill, the sorghum beer market in SA is estimated at 1,7billion litres a
year.
Ranganathan said UNB's sales revenue was about $70m a year.
United Breweries is India's biggest brewer but its position is being
challenged by the new joint venture between Shaw Wallace, India's
second-largest brewer, and SABMiller, which has brought its six regional
breweries into its partnership.

Original_Search_Title: No Health Facilities in Rebel-Controlled Abyei County
Headline: Sudan: No Health Facilities in Rebel-Controlled Abyei County
Body_Text:
Nairobi — An NGO survey of rebel-controlled Abyei county has found that there are
no health services available to a population of about 32,000 people, forcing
them to walk for between two and three days to access medical care.
About 60 percent of people relied on traditional healers and "spear
masters" who performed witchcraft, the Irish charity GOAL reported, with
about 20 percent opting for formal health care in Abyei town, where there is
a hospital, or neighbouring Twic county, which has primary health
clinics.
A lack of money and the distance involved determined where people went,
GOAL reported, adding that those who were unable to walk had to be carried
on stretchers.
Community leaders had set up "emergency health posts" in rebel-held
Abyei three years ago, but there were no medicines and no salaries available
which meant they were unsustainable, GOAL programme officer Martina Collins,
told IRIN.
Malaria, diarrhoeal diseases, respiratory infections, malnutrition and
measles were the main causes of mortality in the area. Two thirds of people
had no blankets and slept around fires, exposing themselves to mosquitos and
malaria as the fires died down and the smoke - which acts as a repellant -
thinned out, GOAL reported.
Despite a food surplus in the region, almost one quarter of the children
surveyed appeared to be malnourished. This was due to a poor diet of
sorghum, lacking protein content, which young children were being fed from
when they stopped breast feeding, said Collins.
There was also no immunisation available for children apart from
polio. The only children who had been immunised - eight out of 116 surveyed
- were returnees from northern Sudan.
Abyei has suffered several decades of conflict between the Ngok Dinka in
the south and the Misseriya Arabs to the north. Although it has been
peaceful since late 2002, thousands of Dinka remain displaced. Five camps in
government-controlled northern Abyei house an estimated 70,000 people, with
a further 50,000 scattered throughout the province of Bahr el Ghazal.
The Abyei Community Action for Development (ACAD), a local NGO,
estimates that 1,300 households have returned to southern Abyei so far this
year, while another 750 people are expected within the next week. The
returnees, mostly from Khartoum, were visibly better off than local people
who had not been displaced, Reuben Haylett, a medical coordinator with GOAL,
told IRIN.
Many others who had returned, had chosen not to stay due to the lack of
services, and moved on to neighbouring Twic county or to the IDP camps in
northern Abyei, Deng Mading, director of ACAD, told IRIN.
A regional conference held in Agok, Abyei county, in early June
expressed "outrage at the subhuman conditions" in which the IDPs were
living.
The over 700 conference participants - no government representation was
present - called on the international community to guarantee "free movement
and adequate protection and security" for the repatriation of the IDPs. They
also asserted that they were "part and parcel" of southern Sudan and
identified the Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army (SPLM/A) as their
"legitimate representative" in any negotiations with the government.
The status of Abyei is currently one of the most contentious issues in
negotiations for a national peace agreement in Sudan. The SPLM/A insists on
the right to hold a referendum in Abyei on whether to join southern
Sudan. In theory, this would precede the main southern referendum, on
secession, to be held six and a half years after a peace agreement is
signed. The government has so far insisted that Abyei is a local issue and
does not require negotiation with the SPLM/A.

Original_Search_Title: Kwazulu Liquor Bill May Grant Sunday Sales
Headline: South Africa: Kwazulu Liquor Bill May Grant Sunday Sales
Body_Text:
Durban — The KwaZulu-Natal provincial government is expecting widespread debate
over new liquor law proposals, following the release at the weekend for
public comment of a draft bill which will also open the door for Sunday
trading.
In terms of the constitution, the licensing of liquor retailers and
micromanufacturers, and the licensing of sorghum beer manufacturers and
retailers, are provincial competencies.
A national Liquor Bill was introduced to the National Assembly in 1998,
but past president Nelson Mandela referred it to the Constitutional
Court.
The national bill, aimed at replacing the existing "outdated and
inefficient" law, is being reworked. It currently contains a default
provincial bill should provinces not enact a provincial act within a year of
the national Liquor Act being passed.
KwaZulu-Natal economic development and tourism MEC, Roger Burrows,
released the draft provincial licensing bill for public comment on
Friday. Following consideration of written comments, the draft will be
submitted to cabinet and then to the provincial parliament. Burrows expected
the law to be in place by December.
He said he hoped the new legislation would bring unlicensed
establishments on board, thus "levelling the playing field and making the
industry easier to police". These included the shebeens, whose value Burrows
placed at billions of rands.
However, he expected "considerable debate" over proposals to grant
licences in terms of the retail sale of liquor for consumption on and off
the premises.
This would cover buying alcohol at a restaurant, but taking home the
unopened bottle. Burrows said this category "took into account what was
already happening".
The legislation also opens the door for Sunday trading, with Burrows
saying the provincial liquor licensing authority would consider the
restrictions on trading days and hours. However, there would be provision to
prevent outlets selling alcohol in socially unacceptable places, such as
next to a church during services, or next to a school.
Burrows also said applicants needed written consent from their local
authority for a trading licence before a liquor licence would be
considered. This covered shebeens opening in residential areas, where the
surrounding residents were unhappy with its existence.
Burrows criticised the current law, dating to 1989, as "outdated and
extremely inefficient in its practical application". He said governments
over the past two centuries have successfully used liquor laws for
separation and suppression.
The draft bill proposes reducing the current 25 liquor licence
categories to six. These include onpremises retail consumption; offpremises
retail consumption; onand-off-premises retail consumption; retail sales in
terms of a special licence (alcohol sales in a temporary beer tent);
micromanufacturing (small breweries and wineries) and the manufacture and
sale of sorghum beer.
Public comment on the draft bill must be submitted to the department by
August 15.

Original_Search_Title: Wheat Imports Dock At Mombasa
Headline: Kenya: Wheat Imports Dock At Mombasa
Body_Text:
Nairobi — More wheat imports continue to arrive at the port of Mombasa to replenish the country's dwindling grain reserves . A conventional cargo vessel MV Glorious docked at the harbour last week with 21365 metric tonnes of wheat, and so did Mv Kyma under the agency of Barwil Bat Haf Agencies with 31,000 metric tonnes of bulk wheat.
The two vessels are discharging their cargo at the Grain Bulk Handling Services (GBHL) facility adjacent to berth Number One.
MV Maersk Arizona under the agency of Seaforth Shipping (Kenya) is sailing towards Mombasa carrying 4357 metric tonnes of bagged sorghum.
Another shipment of 13200 metric tonnes of relief cargo arrived aboard MvWilson on Tuesday. It is under the agency of Seabulk Chatering and Shipping (CBULK).
More cargo imports are expected to dominate at the port with Mv Jolly Gialo , a vessel under the agency of Interocean East Africa Limited (IOEA) bringing in 100 car units.
Mv Modern Peak whose agents are Diamond Shipping Limited (DSS) brings in the largest vehicle unit imports of 420 tomorrow.
Mv Grand Quest also under DSS docks at the port on Friday with 162 units. On the same day, Mv Orange Horizon will bring ashore 270 car units.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Beer Trade in the Eastern Cape Largely Illegal
Headline: South Africa: Sorghum Beer Trade in the Eastern Cape Largely Illegal
Body_Text:
Bisho — More than 90% of the outlets for sorghum beer in the eastern Cape are
operating illegally.
This was revealed yesterday (subs: Fri) by the senior vice president
of the United National Breweries (UNB) Paramjit Gill during the
company's presentation to the public hearing on the Eastern Cape
Liquor Bill of 2003.
Gill said there were about 150 000 illegal traders of sorghum beer in
the province. Many of these were also producing sorghum beer. The UNB
brewery in Butterworth was the only licenced producer.
He said that there were "basic problems in treating sorghum beer like
other liquor products". He recommended that the sorghum beer trade be
kept out of the preview of the proposed Liquor Act for the first ten
years.
He said the UNB was "willing to assist" the Liquor Board to assist
current and prospective traders to comply with the proposed Act.
He suggested that the province introduce a "simple permit system
instead of licenses." He said that the application form should be
easy to fill in because there was "a lot of illiteracy" in the
business and the fee should be "nominal".
Standing committee chairperson Andre de Wet said the UNB seemed to be
"willing to participate and to bring some form of legality to a place
where illegality is rife". He urged the UNB to make "concrete
proposals" for the legalising of the sorghum beer trade.
However, chairperson of the Eastern Cape Liquor Board Mnyamezeli
Ntshiba pointed out that the sorghum beer trade "does not have more
than 5 licensed" outlets in the province.
"You (UNB) sell to the outlets that are not licensed," he said.
Ntshiba added that an "outcry" from communities over illegal shebeens
prompted the inclusion of a clause in the bill empowering inspectors
to enforce adherence to the rules.

Original_Search_Title: Special Report On the Northern Crisis
Headline: Uganda: Special Report On the Northern Crisis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Since Ugandan President Yoweri Museveni came to power in 1986, Uganda has been the envy of many developing nations, achieving high economic growth rates and posting encouraging social development indicators, including stemming the upward trend of HIV/AIDS infection rates in the country.
But these indicators are primarily a reflection of the development of the southern and central areas of Uganda. In the north, a very different situation prevails.
For the last 17 years, residents of the northern Acholi subregion (comprising Gulu, Kitgum, and Pader districts) have lived under the terror of killings and abductions by the Lords Resistance Army (LRA); an insurgent group, whose leader Joseph Kony is seeking to replace Museveni's government with one ruled by the biblical Ten Commandments.
The LRA insurgency has made children its main target in the war against Museveni's government, and has committed some of the worst atrocities against civilians. The group routinely attacks villages in 'Acholiland', killing civilians and abducting children to be forced into its ranks or to be used as sex slaves for commanders. Yet, due to Uganda's overall macroeconomic successes, the LRA insurgency remains an under-reported conflict, and its toll on the Acholi society has continued to be underestimated.
The horrors of captivity
Children who have managed to escape from LRA captivity tell horror tales of the extreme physical and psychological violence with which they were initiated into the ranks of the rebel army.
Peter Ochan, 14, was abducted while asleep and made to walk through the night and the next day, before he and other abducted children were finally allowed to rest. During the subsequent ten months he spent in LRA captivity, the clubbing of children to death by rebel commanders became a daily ritual in the bush.
Ochan, now accommodated at the Gulu Support the Children Organisation (GUSCO), a rehabilitation centre set up to help children returning from captivity, tells of how he and others were forced to carry the decomposing body of a boy killed for trying to escape.
"We carried the body on our shoulders everywhere we went. It was smelling very badly. We were told it was part of our training. The commander then told us to scoop out the brains from what was left of the body and show around to others," he said.
Sixteen-year-old Tony Ulanya was also abducted when he was only ten and stayed in Kitgum district for only three months, after which he was made to walk to southern Sudan in a group of some 300 abductees, where he was forced to undergo military training. Of the three hundred abductees, only about 50 survived. The rest died of diseases or lack of food, or were murdered by rebel commanders, he says.
Ulanya has met Kony several times and describes the rebel leader as a "possessed" man. "He would preach to us on Sundays for many hours. He always told us that he spoke with the angel who told him that he would one day be the president of Uganda and the young people he has abducted, and the children born in captivity would take over the country. Sometimes he leaves people and stays on his own for a long time," he said
Opportunities to escape from captivity are extremely rare for many of the abductees. Many do not dare attempt escape, fearing reprisals against themselves or their families should they be re-abducted. A few have managed to do so when under intense attacks by the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF), but many are killed in the crossfire.
It is even more difficult for child mothers to return because as 'wives' of commanders they are much more closely watched. Their movement is also restricted by the need to care for their children, who cannot survive weeks of walking in the bush with little food or water.
Patrick Komakech, who is at Kitgum Concerned Women Association (KICWA) - another rehabilitation centre - was lucky to escape two weeks after his abduction from school in Kitgum early in 2003. But many children who tried to do so were recaptured and killed as the rest watched, as a "lesson on what would happen to us if we tried to run away".
"Two boys tried to escape. They were beaten to death with sticks. A child whose feet were too swollen to walk any further was tied up and left behind to die. He was left there and told to eat soil," Komakech said.
Rehabilitation
Those who run the rehabilitation centres for returning abductees such as GUSCO and KICWA say most of the children who escape are often emaciated, weak from diseases, and severely traumatised.
In the bush, good food like chicken and goats is reserved for commanders, while children only eat sorghum or wild fruits. "In Sudan, we had to kill people, steal their animals and take stored grains in order to survive," Komakech said.
Returning abductees are required to first report to the Child Protection Units set up within the UPDF, before being taken to rehabilitation centres such as GUSCO and KICWA. While under the control of the UPDF, many said they had been under pressure to join the army.
Fifteen-year-old Denis Nyero, who was rescued by the army, told IRIN he had refused to give in to the pressure, but many boys who were with him at the child protection centre had agreed to remain behind and join the army.
The rehabilitation centres are, however, just a temporary respite for returning abductees. Once return to their families has been facilitated, the children continue to remain under constant risk of re-abduction, which would mean almost certain execution at the hands of the LRA.
There are also children in the centres who were born in LRA captivity, and have no parents or relatives to care for them because they were either orphaned or separated from their parents in the bush.
One positive aspect of the human tragedy in the Acholi subregion is that the community usually welcomes child returnees, despite the atrocities they may have committed against their own people. Even where children's parents are dead, the extended families will always accept them back without attaching much stigma to them, according to the KICWA centre manager. "I do not remember of any children who have been rejected by their families," said Christopher Arwai, who heads the KICWA centre
The problem of acceptance has occurred mostly with child mothers, because relatives are often not willing to support their children, according to Julius, a programme manager at GUSCO. "Our major problem is with the child mothers who often find it difficult to get support from their families. When they are with two or three children, they become an extra burden for the families," he said.
Night commuters
In an attempt to find some improved security, thousands of people from surrounding villages walk long distances at night to shelter in the safety of Kitgum and Gulu towns, where they occupy public spaces such as churches and schools. It is estimated that some 8,500 children commute every night from their villages to stay overnight in Gulu town.
The phenomenon of night commuting is a direct consequence of the fear which the LRA has brought to the community. On some nights, the available shelters are overstretched, forcing most commuters to sleep on pavements and verandas. "We come here out of fear. Our parents do not want us to be abducted. But we are suffering. We only eat one meal at home. We don't get any supper," one child commuter told IRIN.
In Pader, parents have been sending their children to neighbouring Kotido District in the hope they would be looked after, "This illustrates the kind of desperation here. Parents sending their children off to another district where they have no relatives. We have never seen this before," Mads Oyen, the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) child protection officer in Uganda, told IRIN.
Operation Iron Fist
News of the launch of a major military operation to eliminate the LRA, following an agreement signed between the Sudanese and the Ugandan government in March last year, initially raised hopes that the insurgency would be brought to an end and normal life would return to the 1.4 million residents of Acholiland.
The operation, code-named "Operation Iron Fist", prompted humanitarian agencies to make contingency plans to receive an estimated 3,000 children who were to be rescued by the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) from LRA rear bases in southern Sudan.
It was not until June, when Kony's group sneaked back into northern Uganda and intensified attacks on civilians, that it became clear Operation Iron Fist had not succeeded in its mission. The group intensified its attacks on villages and camps for Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs), killing and abducting even more children.
A child abductee who was in the bush under LRA control when Operation Iron Fist [www.updfironfist.co.ug] got under way told IRIN how Kony had ordered the rebels to step up attacks and kill as many civilians as they could. "We extended our operations in Kitgum and Pader. In Agoro, [a county of Kitgum District], we abducted 200 people. Kony ordered us to kill all of them. We did as we were told. After that, we split into smaller groups. Some went to Gulu, but mine stayed in Kitgum," the boy said.
According to Oyen, there had been at least 6,000 abductions since the beginning of 2003. This rate, according to UNICEF is the highest since the insurgency in northern Uganda began. "The abductions that have taken place since the period after June [2002] are the highest levels of abductions ever," Oyen said.
Christopher Arwai believes that many more children have been abducted from northern Uganda during the entire period of the insurgency, most of who may never be accounted for.
Humanitarian Overview
Rebel activities in northern Uganda have forced up to 800,000 people to flee their homes and move into camps protected by the Ugandan army.
