Gulu hosts the bulk of the IDPs, estimated at about 400,000, and in Kitgum camps have spontaneously emerged to accommodate some 100,000 IDPs.
The humanitarian situation in Pader District, where almost the entire population has been displaced and cut off from humanitarian access by insecurity, is considered most dire - an estimated 82 percent of the population in the district are now living in camps. In addition, cattle rustling by armed Karamojong 'warriors' launching raids from neighbouring Kotido district has increased insecurity still further.
A visit to Awer camp, situated on the outskirts of Gulu town, revealed a critical situation. Although the camp's official capacity is 11,000 IDPs, the actual number of displaced persons living in the camp has more than doubled in the past few months, currently standing at 24,000.
Cases of malnutrition have also risen sharply in the region in the past few months, where the capacity of humanitarian agencies to provide emergency food, water, sanitation and health facilities has been overstretched. Attacks by rebels on food stores, who routinely empty granaries and steal domestic animals, only serve to aggravate food shortages further.
Valerie Julliand, head of office for the UN-OCHA Regional Support Office for East and Central Africa, stressed that the special circumstances of children in the northern Uganda conflict required urgent international attention.
"We get the feeling that the situation in northern Uganda is not getting its due attention and needs to be brought back into the international radar. The violence has no limit," she said.
Julliand pointed out that the devastation in Acholiland was already cancelling out much of the progress Uganda had made on the socio-economic front. "We know that wars produce lost generations. These ones are not only lost, they are completely ruined. It is devastating because of the deterioration of the human mind and spirit," Julliand said.
In addition, the nature of the LRA, being a fragmented rebel army made up largely of traumatised children, makes it exceptionally difficult for humanitarian actors to meet with, and to promote humanitarian principles with, the rebels. "It is the purest form of terrorism one can imagine. In some other countries, you can meet rebels and educate them on humanitarian principles, and somehow you don't have the impression that these efforts are in vain," Julliand said.
On-off peace talks
Although both the government and the LRA have expressed commitment to starting serious peace talks, the situation on the ground has often raised questions over the prospects for real progress.
Even after declaring several cease-fires to pave the way for talks, the LRA has sustained attacks on civilians and increased forced recruitment of children into its ranks. For its part, the Ugandan government has indicated that it wants to bring an end to the insurgency by using both diplomatic and military means.
Although parliament passed a law in 1999 offering amnesty to surrendering rebels, few hardcore LRA mebers have taken advantage of it. Most beneficiaries of the amnesty so far have been abducted children who have escaped from LRA captivity, with only a handful of commanders giving themselves up under the terms of the amnesty.
Some observers believe the attitude of the Ugandan military is an obstacle to negotiating peace in northern Ugandan. Carlos Rodriguez, a Catholic missionary in Gulu and a member of the Acholi Religious Leaders Peace Initiative (ARLPI) [www.acholipeace.org], criticised the government's military methods against the LRA which, he argues, have led to the deaths of innocent civilians and the abduction of children.
"The army tells us that they have killed rebels. But when you look at the so-called rebels, most of them are women and children. We don't think the solution is to kill the same people whom we have failed to protect from the rebels in the first place," Rodriguez said. "You don't use helicopter gunships to kill guerrillas. You end up killing many innocent people," he told IRIN.
Mistrust between the government and the rebels has frustrated efforts by the peace team appointed by Museveni to negotiate terms for peace talks, and even to make meaningful contact with the rebel commanders.
Lt-Gen Salim Saleh, a former senior commander in the Ugandan army and currently head of the government's peace team, said the rebels had failed to honour several agreements to meet with the team, but also expressed some concern over the approach of some elements within the Ugandan military. "The president is totally committed to peace. He does not fear to negotiate. The frustration I get is the big pressure from the military. They think they can end this conflict militarily," Saleh told IRIN.
Some aid agencies working in the north have criticised the international community for allowing Museveni's government to keep the humanitarian crisis in the north on the back burner, partly because of his economic successes in other parts of the country.
For example, they have expressed concern over the government's recent decision to re-allocate 23 percent of funds from other ministries to defence, seen by some as indicating a preference for a military solution over a peaceful settlement in the north.
"The government has put almost no resources to humanitarian response, leaving humanitarian organisations to fill the gap. The Ugandan government needs to be reminded of its responsibilities," one aid worker told IRIN.

Original_Search_Title: Horrors of LRA Child Captivity
Headline: Uganda: Horrors of LRA Child Captivity
Body_Text:
[This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United
Nations]
KITGUM, 24 April (IRIN) - Fifteen year-old Patrick Komakech lies on a
bed, grateful for his freedom and more importantly for his life.
He was brought to a rehabilitation centre in the northern Ugandan town
of Kitgum after fleeing his captors, the rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA),
two weeks after they abducted him from his school. He was among the lucky
ones. Others in his group who had tried to escape, were recaptured and
killed in front of their counterparts "as a lesson on what would happen to
us if we tried to run away".
Children are the main target of the 17-year LRA insurgency which has
terrorised civilians in northern Uganda. The group, led by Joseph Kony,
seeks to topple President Yoweri Museveni and replace his government with a
regime governed by the Biblical Ten Commandments.
The group routinely attacks Acholi villages, killing civilians and
abducting children to be forced into its ranks and girls to be used as sex
slaves for commanders.
GRUESOME TALES
Children who have managed to escape from LRA captivity tell horror
stories about the extreme physical and psychological violence with which
they were initiated into rebel ranks.
Peter Ochan, 14, was abducted, when he was asleep and made to walk the
whole night and the whole day, before he and other abducted children were
allowed to rest. During his 10 months in captivity, the clubbing of children
to death by rebel commanders became a daily ritual in the bush, he
says.
Ochan, now accommodated at the Gulu Support the Children Organisation
(GUSCO), a rehabilitation centre set up to help children returning from
captivity, explains how he and others were forced to carry the decomposing
body of a boy killed for trying to escape.
"We carried the body on our shoulders everywhere we went," he said. "It
smelt very bad. We were told it was part of our training. The commander then
told us to scoop out the brains from what was left of the body and show them
around to others."
Another escapee, Tony Ulanya, 16, says he has met LRA leader Joseph Kony
several times and describes him as a "possessed man".
"He would preach to us on Sundays for many hours," he told IRIN. "He
always told us that he spoke with the angel who told him that he would one
day be the president of Uganda, and the young people he has abducted and the
children born in captivity will take over the country. Sometimes he leaves
people and stays on his own for a long time."
FEAR OF ESCAPE
Opportunities to escape from captivity are extremely rare for many of
the abductees. Many do not dare to escape for fear of reprisals, either
towards them or their families.
Relishing his freedom at the Kitgum Concerned Women's Association
(KICWA), Patrick recounts some of his gruesome memories.
"Two boys tried to escape," he says. "They were beaten to death, with
sticks. A child whose feet were too swollen to walk any further was tied up
and left behind to die. He was left there and told to eat soil."
REHABILITATION
Those who run the rehabilitation centres for returning abductees, such
as GUSCO and KICWA, say most of the children who escape are often emaciated,
weak from disease, and severely traumatised.
"In the bush, good food, like chicken and goats was reserved for
commanders, while children only ate sorghum or wild fruits," Patrick
recalled. "In Sudan [where the LRA has its bases], we had to kill people,
steal their animals and take stored grains in order to survive."
The rehabilitation centres are however just a temporary respite for
returning abductees. Once their return to their families has been
facilitated, the children remain under constant risk of re-abduction, which
would mean certain execution by their captors.
Mads Oyen, the UN Children's Fund (UNICEF) child protection officer in
Uganda told IRIN that there had been at least 6,000 abductions since the
beginning of this year, translating to 12,000 a year.
"The abductions that have taken place since the period after June [2002]
are the highest levels of abductions ever [since the insurgency began],"
Oyen said.
Christopher Arwai, who heads the KICWA centre, however believes that
many more children have been abducted from northern Uganda throughout the
insurgency, many of whom may never be accounted for.
[See also Peace Process Crumbling in North at:
http://www.irinnews.org/report.asp?ReportID=33613 ]

Original_Search_Title: Grass: the Fibre of Life
Headline: Zambia: Grass: the Fibre of Life
Body_Text:
From now until mid July, most of the grasslands will remain deep
green.
Immediately after the rains, the wild pastures are so health that a
single acre can produce tonnes of protein rich fodder.
Towards the end of the cold season, the grass will begin to revert to
yellow stubble, hence nature demands that some of the herbivores migrate to
allow the grasslands to rest and replenish.
Others that do not migrate move into the open woodlands that have
islands of grass. It is also a time when the plains are set on fire.
Although fire is usually considered destructive, it is necessary for the
savannah. Fires breakout in the dry season, this encourages new grass to
grow and thus maintaining the savannah.
It is advisable, however, to carry out early rather than late burning,
as this is less destructive.
The savannahs sustain a large variety of game, both large and
small. Some of these are elephant, giraffe, buffalo, zebra and numerous
antelope as well as the tiny grass mouse.
The grazers are mainly antelope, wildebeest, hartebeest, oribi, impala,
gazelle, reedbuck, roan and sable antelope.
In this category there are also buffaloes and zebras represented in good
numbers.
Wildebeests are the main players in most of the grasslands in East and
Southern Africa. When feeding, they always mutter and grunt.
The zebras are a common feature on the plains, they are not ruminant, so
they are able to handle tougher grass. With their sharp cutting teeth, they
can cope with the tough fibrous stems of tall grass.
Buffaloes on the other hand can handle tough grass to an extent. They
are bulk feeders and to help them with the digestion, they drink a lot of
water daily.
It is the savannahs that draw hordes of tourists to view the treasures
that are present in the form of hoofed mammals and their attendant
predators.
There are always the ravenous hunters; the ones that keep a check on the
numbers of herbivores.
Most tourists are actually keen on seeing the predators than the
herbivores.
Among these are packs of wildogs, the slender bodied and fleet footed
cheetahs, that run down their prey with devastating strides.
Leopards, almost invisible in the dappled shade of trees, watch and wait
for the unwary prey to approach the woodland belt.
Also present are lions which are kings of their grassy world; the
plentiful spotted hyenas which keep tabs on the weak, sick, crippled and
young.
Whenever an opportunity avails itself, be sure the hyenas will be
there. These predators determine who would live and who would die.
In general grasslands are the central regions of several continents. On
the edges of these grasslands, they mix with forests.
In addition, scattered clumps of trees often grow in grasslands where
there is enough water.
Tropical grasslands are known as Savannahs which grow in warm lands
nearest the Equator.
Savannah differs from the temperate grasslands, which grow in cooler
parts of the world and are known by several names.
They differ in the length and kinds of grass, which in turn vary with
the amount of rainfall and soil types.
The temperate grasslands of North America are called the Prairies. These
once hosted and boosted millions of bison, a large North American mammal of
the buffalo tribe.
There are numerous species of grass and man uses some of these for
food. Important grass foods for man are maize (corn if your are American),
rice, wheat, sugar cane, millet and sorghum.
Bamboos, the largest species of grass are heavily utilised in building
and furniture making while other wild species of grass are used for
thatching.
It is for this reason that grass must be managed and utilised wisely as
we all depend on it.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Corp Launches Beverages Subsidiary
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Corp Launches Beverages Subsidiary
Body_Text:
Harare — ZIMBABWE Stock Exchange-listed Delta Corporation Limited has integrated its beverage manufacturing divisions under a subsidiary to be known as Delta Beverages, company officials said yesterday.
The move is part of Delta's unbundling process, which started three years ago and has already lead to the disposal of Zimbabwe Sun Limited, Pelhams Limited and OK Zimbabwe Limited.
The unbundling process will enable Delta to concentrate on its core beverage business.
Under the arrangement announced yesterday, United Bottlers, National Breweries and Chibuku Breweries will no longer retain their names and logos but will now be re-branded under the Delta Beverages brand.
Delta Beverages will run a manufacturing division involved in the production of lager, sorghum beer and carbonated soft drinks.
The subsidiary will also have a 100 percent shareholding in its operations division, comprising sales and distribution as well as transport.
Delta Corporation, which has a 28.8 percent interest in African Distillers Limited, will continue to hold 100 percent shareholding in malt producer Kwekwe Malting, 51 percent in Food and Industrial Processors (Pvt) Limited and 51 percent in Megapak (Pvt) Limited, through Delta Beverages.
"We have re-engineered Delta Corporation to provide a synchronised efficiency in its systems, packaging, delivery and training," the group's chief executive, Joe Mutizwa said.
"This integration will help ensure the survival of Delta in an environment which has become increasingly challenging," he added.
Following the restructuring, Delta's customers will be able to procure beverages from a single distribution centre.
Analysts said the consolidation of Delta operations would create a highly focused conglomerate, enhancing shareholder value.
The analysts said the move was likely to increase efficiency and reduce Delta's overhead costs.
They however pointed out that the group might need to increase its distribution centres countrywide to avoid congestion and delays, something Mutizwa said the company was already undertaking.
"I think we are likely to see more efficiency, reduction in costs and the company's income will increase, which will enhance shareholder value," an analyst with Kingdom Stockbrokers said.

Original_Search_Title: New Skirmish in Beer War
Headline: Southern Africa: New Skirmish in Beer War
Body_Text:
A long-standing war of words between Namibia Breweries Limited (NBL) and
SABMiller has intensified, with the Namibian brewery claiming their South
African counterpart's intended launch of a 'clear sorghum beer' represents
unfair competition.
A consignment of Copper Crest heading to Namibia was impounded by
Namibian customs authorities last month for unknown reasons, according to
South African media, possibly for not paying customs duties.
Director of Customs in Namibia's Finance Ministry, GL Kabozu, as well as
the Permanent Secretary in the Ministry of Trade and Industry, Andrew
Ndishishi, could not be reached for comment.
SAB says its Copper Crest sorghum beer should be charged import duties
at the same rate as traditional sorghum beers, but NBL feels it should be
taxed at the higher rate associated with Western beers.
Standard beer is taxed approximately 20 times higher than traditional
sorghum beer, according to NBL.
"SABMiller launched Copper Crest in the Namibian domestic market to try
and abuse a loophole in the Customs and Excise definition of sorghum beer
...
Despite SABMiller's effort to associate Copper Crest with traditional
sorghum beer, it should for all practical purposes, be defined as a clear
beer," said NBL in a press release.
"Copper Crest is a sorghum product containing 70 per cent sorghum.
The customs authorities have formally classified it a sorghum product
under the current excise classification," said Peter McLoughlin, Marketing
Director of SAB, in an official reply yesterday.
He added that SAB was within its rights to respond to the impounding of
Copper Crest with a legal challenge, but would prefer an amicable solution,
and would pursue a non-confrontational course for now.
NBL claimed the Copper Crest importation was one of many "strong-arm
tactics" that SABMiller was employing to eliminate it from the market.
These, it said, included "dumping" beer on the Namibian market by
selling it at below-cost prices and manipulating the retail market.
SAB said this was not so.
"Namibia Breweries continues to make unfounded allegations on dumping
which have absolutely no legal basis.
Castle Brewing in Namibia will from time to time offer price
promotions.
This is accepted competitive practice," said McLoughlin.
The latest series of statements is reminiscent of earlier battles when
NBL successfully lobbied Government in the 1990s to prevent SAB from
establishing a brewery in Namibia.
A SABMiller spokesperson told South Africa's Financial Mail that failure
to launch Copper Crest could well condemn it to becoming SAB's "most
expensive beer" as the group spent more than R20 million on installing the
sorghum process at its Durban brewery.
McLoughlin said Copper Crest had been developed for a specific type of
consumer need that existed in many African markets, including Namibia.
He said South African liquor laws precluded SAB from launching a sorghum
beer in South Africa, but not in Namibia.
NBL quoted industry analysts as saying NBL has been chipping away at
SAB's South African market share, which is said to have dropped from about
98 per cent in the late 1990s to closer to 95 per cent now.
SAB has denied this, quoting the Nielson statistics firm, which said in
November 2002 SAB controlled 97,8 per cent of the market and NBL 1,7 per
cent.
In February 2000, NBL said its biggest challenge in the new millennium
was to break SAB's dominance in the South African market.

Original_Search_Title: Struggling Awethu Breweries Goes After Meatier Market
Headline: South Africa: Struggling Awethu Breweries Goes After Meatier Market
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — AWETHU Breweries, the secondlargest sorghum beer and mageu brewer in SA, plans to shift its focus to the meat industry.
The company has been hard hit by small-scale brewers and illegal home brewers, industry sources say.
Awethu, which has reported a drop in turnover from R8,527m to R821604 for the interim period to December 31, said a bad-debt provision had cost it R1,42m.
The company reported a loss in earnings a share of 2,39c from 0,93c a share. It also reported a net loss of R2m compared to its previous net profit of R786708.
Awethu MD Tony Ford said the directors were in talks to finalise payment from the sale of the Ventersdorp mill and were in litigation to collect an amount owed by RMD Financial Services.
The directors had continued the policy of renting out the Carletonville and Marizaan breweries as well as the Ventersdorp mill.
"Unfortunately, the guarantees for the sale of the Ventersdorp mill have not been met," said Ford. "As soon as these two debtors can be collected, we are sure Awethu will be in a great position to invest in new profitable businesses."

Original_Search_Title: Tracing the Roots of Waragi
Headline: Uganda: Tracing the Roots of Waragi
Body_Text:
Kampala — It has been given all kinds of names. Many people call it waragi. Others call it enguli. There are places where it is called enkangaali. Yet in some places it is known as andiro. It is also referred to as Lira-Lira. In Teso it is called ewaragi.
Some creative folks, claiming that waragi is curative, refer to it as Â¡Â¥choroquineÂ¡oe and sometimes Â¡Â¥quinineÂ¡oe.
At least every ethnic group in Uganda has coined a name for it. In Masaka and some areas of Buganda, the more concentrated brand of the potent gin has been Â¡Â¥baptisedÂ¡oe amandule or Kasese-Kasese, while the less concentrated type is called regular.
So where did this wonder drink originate?
Mzee Wakulira Ssemuju of Masaka says the history of the drink can be traced back to the days just after World War I had ended.
Â¡Â§When the World War I veterans returned to Uganda, they told stories of a Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe which they had tasted and learnt to make,Â¡Â¨ says Mzee Ssemuju.
Since the war attracted men from almost all parts of the country, they started making Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe; oblivious of the fact that they were actually introducing a drink that would forever posses the fair men and women of this land.
Mzee Ssemuju says the locals found the word Â¡Â¥war-ginÂ¡oe a mouthful, too hard to pronounce, and it ended being pronounced as waragi Â¡V a name it has taken up to date.
In neighbouring Kenya it is called changaa, and across Africa it is known by different names.
Today waragi is not only an important drink for low-income earners, it is also a source of income for a sizeable number of families in the country. It is probably the most easily affordable alcoholic drink around.
A tot of waragi goes for at least Shs 100 in most parts of the country, and today jerrycans upon jerrycans of enguli are transported into Kampala and other Ugandan towns from rural areas.
The raw material for its production varies from region to region and this could be the soft landing for its popularity.
In Buganda it is made from banana, in Teso it is made from cassava, in some areas it is made using sorghum and the urban brewers distil it from sugar molasses.
And thus, owing to the different raw materials used in different places, the smell, level of purity and alcohol concentration of the product may vary, but the Â¡Â¥knock-downÂ¡oe effect is more-or-less the same.
At a graduation party in Masaka recently, a woman whose son had just graduated said in her speech, Â¡Â§With five children to look after, it has not been easy to raise money for school fees. I have distilled waragi all my life to raise money to send these children to school.Â¡Â¨ The woman got what Americans would describe as a standing ovation.
Indeed, tales of how whole families have survived on waragi as a source of livelihood are not few.
But distilling enguli, transporting, consuming or being in possession of it or the equipment for making it is illegal, according to Ugandan laws. Chapter 96, Volume 3 of the Enguli (Manufacture and Licensing) Act states in part: Â¡Â§No person shall manufacture, sell or otherwise deal in enguli, have it in his possession, drink or have any apparatus that make enguliÂm.Â¡Â¨
Mr John Matovu, a lawyer in Masaka, told Sunday Monitor recently that any police officer can arrest any person at anytime in any place, if they are found to have broken the Â¡Â¥enguli lawÂ¡oe.
However, for some incomprehensible reason, the law seems to have been put aside. Waragi brewing seems to be on the increase, unabated.
Interestingly, the people who are supposed to arrest the lawbreakers are some of the faithful waragi drinkers.
Mr Joel Okwir, the Southern Regional CID Officer, said the problem is enormous because many police officers also take the forbidden drink.
Mr Okwir, however, speaks of a time when government used to issue licenses to some people to make waragi strictly to be sold to Uganda Distilleries Ltd.
Sunday Monitor has established that this actually happened in 1979 after a tragedy in which a number of people died after consuming some quantities of methanol in waragi.
It had become a habit by some brewers to add some methanol in the distillate to make it more concentrated.
Waragi is associated with a number of health problems. According to Dr Gertrude Nakigozi of Masaka Hospital, continued consumption of waragi can lead to heart and liver diseases like hepatitis, hypertension and bleeding from the gastrointestinal tract.
Dr Nakigozi also says waragi consumption blurs vision and can be responsible for low blood sugar and destruction of the brain.
During the days when law enforcement against distilling waragi was effective, people disappeared into the swamps or forests to prepare it. Today, however, a man will even announce to his friends that he will not attend the village council meeting because he will be distilling waragi.
It has even become normal these days for people to openly pledge a jerrycan or so of waragi as a contribution for a relativeÂ¡oes wedding ceremony. A parent in Buganda may demand some bottles of super as part of the bride price.
Mr Charles Irama, a prosecutor in the state attorneyÂ¡oes office in Masaka told Sunday Monitor, Â¡Â§I have been doing this work for the last six years, but I have not had any Â¡Â¥waragi caseÂ¡oe brought before me.Â¡Â¨
In some villages in Masaka, some micro-finance institutions lend money to women groups who brew waragi as a means of poverty alleviation.
Just as there were no records at Masaka Police Station of how many people have been arrested and prosecuted for distilling waragi during the last five years, there were also no figures of deaths directly linked to waragi consumption at Masaka Hospital.

Original_Search_Title: Pay More to Puff Cigars Or Fly, But Less for a Small Car
Headline: South Africa: Pay More to Puff Cigars Or Fly, But Less for a Small Car
Body_Text:
Cape Town — The Cuban-cigarsmoking, large-car-driving executive who flies a lot is
in trouble Finance Minister Trevor Manuel has imposed a massive increase in
duty on cigars, made small cars cheaper and increased airport departure
tax.
In imposing duty on tobacco products, Manuel has maintained the policy
that the consumption-tax burden on tobacco products should be 50% of the
retail selling price of the most popular brand for each category.
This means that cigarettes go up 38c for a packet of 20, or 11%;
cigarette tobacco R1,08 for 50g, or 21%; and pipe tobacco about 15c for a
25g-packet, or 11%.
Cigars go up a whopping 39%, with the duty on 23g of cigars increasing
from R17,63 to R24,52.
The ad valorem duty on motor cars is to be adjusted through the use of a
formula that distinguishes between the type and weight of vehicles,
translating into a lower excise duty on lower-priced cars. The decision was
taken after discussions with the motor vehicle manufacturing industry.
Airport departure tax has not been adjusted for inflation since its
introduction in 2001. The departure tax for destinations in Botswana,
Lesotho, Namibia and Swaziland will go up 10%, bringing it to R55. For all
other international destinations, a similar 10% increase is applied,
bringing the tax to R110.
The departure taxes will be implemented on July 1.
The duty on all alcoholic drinks with the exception of sorghum beer,
which retains the same duty of 7,8c/l goes up 10%-11%, or roughly last
year's inflation rate. This is about 3% more than the projected 7,7%
inflation rate for this year. It translates into about 4c more on a can of
beer, 9c/l for wine, 18c/l for sherry and port (fortified wines), 25c/l for
sparkling wine and R1,15 more on a bottle of spirits.
Manuel has also moved to close loopholes in the definitions of clear
beer and alcoholic fruit beverages.
"This could potentially be exploited with a view to paying lower excise
duties applicable to traditional sorghum beer in respect of clear lager beer
that contains sorghum as an ingredient.
"The revised definitions for alcoholic fruit beverages will also clarify
any existing uncertainty as to the correct duty applicable for news
alcoholic beverages released on to the market," the budget review says.
With the exception of the departure tax, all of the other duties come
into effect on April 1. All existing ad valorem duties, with the exception
of cars and computers, remain the same.
Distell corporate affairs director Andre Steyn said that while the
higher taxes on alcohol and spirits were almost expected, he applauded the
new taxing formula, which would eliminate the volatility of the price
increases.
Tobacco Institute CEO Francois van der Merwe said an increase of 38c in
excise was regrettable. Over a period of nine years, excise duties had
increased nearly 400%.
He said that although government had not deviated from its excise tax
policy on tobacco products, the increase placed further pressure on the
disposable income of consumers.
This could lead to consumers moving away from legal tobacco products to
illegal, untaxed products. This would not only affect the legal tobacco
industry's volumes negatively, but would slowly erode government's tobacco
tax base.
National Council Against Smoking director Peter Ucko said Manuel did a
"good job" in raising taxes on tobacco products as higher prices were one of
the most useful means to get people to stop smoking.

Original_Search_Title: Smokers, Drinkers Hit Where It Hurts
Headline: South Africa: Smokers, Drinkers Hit Where It Hurts
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Smokers and drinkers have again been hit where it hurts by this
year's Budget - in their pockets.
As has come to be expected from the Finance Ministry, it has targeted
cigarettes and alcohol as increased sources of revenue again in this
year's national Budget. The changes will bring in roughly an extra
R907m to government coffers.
From 1 March, a box of cigarettes will cost the smoker 11% more,
increasing the excise tax to R3.89 per pack of 20. The total tax on
tobacco products has been maintained at 50% of the retail price of
the most popular brand of each of cigarettes, cigarette tobacco,
cigars and pipe tobacco. Among those products, cigars have been
targeted the most heavily, with a 39% hike in the excise duty.
Drinkers of unfortified and sparkling wine will see the highest
increase among the alcohol products - an 11% rise, while sorghum beer
and flour duties have been left unchanged for the second year in a
row.
Taxes on fortified wine and spirits, as well as beer and cider have
been increased by 10%.
The SA Receiver of Revenue (SARS) said the taxation of alcohol
products as it was now structured, was the result of a two-year
process of consultation with stakeholders. On average, 43% of the
retail price of spirits is paid on tax, 33% of beer and 23% of wine.

Original_Search_Title: Smokers, Alcohol Drinkers to Dig Deep Into Their Pockets
Headline: South Africa: Smokers, Alcohol Drinkers to Dig Deep Into Their Pockets
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Finance minister Trevor Manuel has told imbibers and smokers they will
be coughing up more, as taxes on alcohol and tobacco products will be
increased further this year.
In his budget speech in Cape Town today, the minister said increases in
some of the indirect taxes would bring about additional revenue of
approximately R907-million into the country's reserves.
The Minister said the cost of beer and ciders will be raised by ten
percent, bringing the excise duty to 48 cents per 340ml or an average
increase of 4.4 cents per can.
According to Minister Manuel, duties on unfortified and sparkling wine
would be raised by 11 percent and duties on fortified wine would increase by
ten percent while duties on spirits would also increase by ten percent, to
bring the average total duty to R13 per bottle.
Meanwhile, the Minister also revealed that, for the second year running,
sorghum beer and sorghum flour duties would not be increased.
Minister Manuel further explained that taxes on tobacco products would
go up by 11 percent, thus increasing the excise tax to R3, 89 per packet of
20s cigarettes.
The minister said South Africa's economy registered improved growth last
year and that projections indicated that the economy would continue to
experience growth.
'The 2003/2004 budget gives priority to reducing poverty and
vulnerability,' he said.

Original_Search_Title: Kony Ambushes Red Cross Team
Headline: Uganda: Kony Ambushes Red Cross Team
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Uganda Red Cross Society (URCS) has suspended its operations in the northern Uganda districts of Pader, Kitgum and Gulu, following an ambush on its convoy on Saturday at Paiula in Pader district.
Joseph Kony's Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) rebels injured six URCS volunteers.
The rebels took personal effects and communication equipment which included two HF radios and four radio handsets.
In a statement issued yesterday, the URCS chairman, Tom Buruku, said, "the suspension will continue until security is conducive to the resumption of operations."
Buruku said, "The URCS would like to deplore and protest the attack on its clearly marked vehicles and volunteers operating on a humanitarian mission."
The six volunteers were returning from Pader town council after assessing the needs of the recenly displaced persons.
Army spokesman Major Shaban Bantariza regretted the attack and said the LRA were desperately in need of food and medicine.
He said the army had recently advised the URCS to allow their convoys to be escorted by the army.
"We urge the Red Cross to revisit their position and accept to be escorted," Bantariza said yesterday.
"We told them the LRA is a terrorist group which targets anybody, but they have refused to listen to us. We told them that they can operate as a neutral body between belligerents and unescorted only where the rebel group is sensible and has a good cause," he said.
Buruku said URCS was a neutral, independent and impartial organ committed to improving the lives of the vulnerable people in Uganda.
He said Red Cross vehicles and personnel do not carry arms but are protected under the International Humanitarian Law which requires combatants to respect war victims and their property.
Bantariza said on Saturday the rebels were intercepted by the UPDF at Olamunyung in Anaka, Gulu district. He said they fled and dropped bags of peas and sorghum.
Meanwhile, yesterday the rebels ambushed a passenger vehicle at Ligiligi, Kitgum district, and killed 11 people, among them three soldiers. Seven others were injured.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Rams Go Up
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Rams Go Up
Body_Text:
Jigawa — Prices of livestock have risen in Jigawa State in anticipation of the
usual high demand for livestock for the eid- el- kabir festival,
which is less than eight days away.
An investigation by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) at Sara Market
in Gwaram Local Government Area of the state, showed that the prices,
which had been static for several months have risen significantly.
An average bull now attracts N60,000 as against N50,000 in the last
six months, while an average ram, which attracted N6,000 now goes for
more than N8,000.
NAN reports that a medium size sheep is sold for N5,000 and a goat
for less than N4,000, as against N4,000 and N2,600, respectively.
Similarly, the prices of chicken have gone up by about 20 per cent,
selling for about N250, while guinea fowl goes for N300 and cock
sells for N400 as against N200, N250 and N300, respectively.
The prices of food items have, however, remained steady since last
December, with sorghum, millet and maize selling for N2,600 per bag
and N65 per measure.
The prices of rice have, however, skyrocketed to N240 per measure as
against N200, while the local variety goes for N140 as against N110
and flour goes for N140 and sugar, N200 as against N120 and N160 per
measure, respectively.
A cattle dealer at Sara Market, Ibrahim Isa told NAN that the prices
would further go up due to the increase in demand the commodities in
the next few days.
"Though the prices have shown little improvement, but it is
encouraging, compared to the poor patronage in the previous months,"
he said.
